title: Applying Deep Learning for cockpit segmentation in the context of mixed reality
comment: XXV Congresso Brasileiro de Automática - CBA 2024
summary: Computer vision is an area that has been growing continuously. With the advance of technologies with a first-person view, new development opportunities have emerged inside the area. Mixed reality promotes virtual environments with objects from the physical world shown in real time. For that, it's necessary to be concerned with the immersion of the user in this simulated environment, increasingly seeking to bring it closer to a possible desired reality. This paper proposes the development of image processing in order to perform the segmentation of images to identify what is foreground and background in order to facilitate the union of virtual and real images. Thus, the present work obtain real images of the user using the off-highway truck simulator CAT793F, through a camera, to be able to perform the segmentation of such images with artificial intelligence techniques.The convolutional neural network architectures "U-net" and "DeepLabV3+" are applied to perform image segmentation. As a result, metrics with around 90% accuracy were presented and and the best model was determined.

AI.tldr: The paper uses CNN-based segmentation (U-Net and DeepLabV3+) on real-world simulator images to separate foreground/background for mixed reality fusion, achieving ~90% accuracy and selecting the best performing model.
AI.motivation: To enhance immersion in mixed reality by robustly segmenting foreground from background so virtual and real imagery can be fused more accurately, demonstrated with real-world camera data from a truck simulator.
AI.method: Capture real images from the CAT793F off-highway truck simulator with a camera; apply U-Net and DeepLabV3+ architectures to perform image segmentation; evaluate performance and select the best model (reported ~90% accuracy).
AI.result: Segmentation accuracy is reported around 90%; the best model among the evaluated architectures is identified.
AI.conclusion: CNN-based segmentation shows promise for improving real-time fusion of virtual and real content in first-person MR, with the caveat that details on dataset size, training protocol, and runtime performance are not provided in the abstract.

title: Robots Need More than VLA and World Models
summary: Generalist robot intelligence is often framed as a policy-scaling problem: collect more robot demonstrations, train larger Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, and expect broader generalisation. In this position paper, we argue that this framing is incomplete. The central bottleneck is not only policy learning, but the absence of mechanisms that convert the world's abundant unstructured behavioural data into grounded robot supervision. Human motion, internet video, simulation rollouts, and interactive demonstrations contain rich information about tasks, goals, contacts, failures, and physical constraints, yet most of this information is not directly usable by robot policies because it lacks embodiment-specific action labels, task semantics, and reward structure. We identify four missing components for the next generation of robotics: data interfaces for autolabelling unstructured behaviour, embodiment interfaces for retargeting human motion to robot actions, world-model interfaces for physics-grounded 3D reasoning, and reward interfaces for inferring task progress and success from video and language. We survey recent progress in robot foundation models, cross-embodiment datasets, learning from video, world models, and reward modelling, and propose a research agenda for building robotics systems that can learn not only from robot demonstrations, but from the broader physical world.

AI.tldr: Policy scaling alone is insufficient for generalist robot intelligence; the main bottleneck is converting abundant unstructured world data into grounded robot supervision. The authors propose four interfaces (data, embodiment, world-model, reward) and a roadmap to learn from broader physical-world data, surveying current progress and setting a research agenda.
AI.motivation: The central problem is extracting usable, embodiment-specific supervision from unstructured behavioural data (human motion, internet video, simulations, interactive demos). These data lack action labels, task semantics, and reward signals needed for policy learning, creating a gap between data abundance and usable robot supervision.
AI.method: A position/vision paper that surveys recent work on robot foundation models, cross-embodiment datasets, video-based learning, world models, and reward modeling; introduces a four-interface framework and a forward-looking research agenda.
AI.result: Conceptual synthesis without empirical experiments: defines four missing interfaces, surveys related progress, and outlines a roadmap for robotics that learns from unstructured world data in addition to robot demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: To achieve robust generalisation, robotics should leverage the broader physical world by building data, embodiment, world-model, and reward interfaces, enabling learning from diverse, unstructured data sources beyond traditional robot demonstrations.

title: Elmes*: Automated Construction of Fine-Grained Evaluation Rubrics for Large Language Models in Long-Tail Educational Scenarios
summary: Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for education requires measuring how models teach, not only what they know. Existing benchmarks emphasize domain-general correctness or depend on manually designed rubrics that scale poorly to long-tail pedagogical scenarios. We introduce Elmes*, an end-to-end framework for constructing, refining, and applying fine-grained scenario-specific rubrics. Elmes* combines a declarative multi-agent engine for teacher--student--judge interactions with SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from expert-defined pedagogical dimensions. Using Elmes*, we build Edu-330, covering 330 scenarios across 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, and 10 task types, with over 1{,}000 second-level indicators. Experiments on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios show that educational capability is multidimensional: top-tier LLMs differ mainly in creativity and values integration, knowledge-strong models may fail at Socratic scaffolding, and the education-specialized InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges preserve human-comparable rankings with much lower scoring variance, but exhibit judge-specific biases such as self-preference. Ablations show that expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human--LLM alignment, while reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding are model-dependent. Elmes* thus provides scalable diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: Elmes* provides a scalable, end-to-end framework for evaluating LLMs in education via fine-grained, scenario-specific rubrics and a self-evolving data generator, demonstrated on Edu-330.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize general correctness or rely on rubrics that do not scale to diverse pedagogical scenarios; there is a need to measure how well models teach, not just what they know.
AI.method: Declarative multi-agent engine (teacher–student–judge) for interactions combined with SceneGen, a self-evolving module that co-optimizes evaluation criteria and test data from pedagogical dimensions. Built Edu-330 (330 scenarios, 11 subjects, 3 grade bands, 10 task types, >1,000 second-level indicators). Experiments on Edu-330 and four expert-authored gold-standard scenarios.
AI.result: Educational capability is multidimensional: top LLMs differ in creativity and values integration; knowledge-strong models may fall at Socratic scaffolding; education-specialized InnoSpark achieves the best human-evaluated average score. LLM judges match human rankings with lower variance but show judge-specific biases (e.g., self-preference). Expert-scored few-shot anchoring improves human–LLM alignment; reasoning enforcement and greedy decoding effects are model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Elmes* provides a scalable diagnostic infrastructure for pedagogically grounded LLM evaluation.

title: Detecting and Mitigating Bias by Treating Fairness as a Symmetry Operation
summary: Machine learning systems deployed in high stakes socioeconomic settings routinely display bias. We formalize bias as a symmetry breaking operation: a classifier is fair if its outputs remain invariant under the counterfactual operation of switching a sensitive attribute, with merit features held fixed. We implement loss based regularization as a symmetry restoring mechanism and evaluate the framework on four synthetic datasets with varying levels of noise, correlation, and bias. The framework achieves upwards of 90\% violation reduction, with accuracy costs around 5\%. This framework does not require causal graph knowledge, is computationally lightweight, and generalizes to any sensitive attribute definable as a bit-flip, making it suitable for contexts where local sources of discrimination remain absent from mainstream benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A symmetry-based fairness framework that treats bias as a symmetry-breaking operation; uses loss-based regularization to restore invariance to sensitive attribute flips; validated on four synthetic datasets showing substantial bias reduction with minor accuracy loss; does not require causal graphs and is lightweight and generalizes to bit-flip attributes.
AI.motivation: High-stakes ML systems exhibit bias; existing approaches often rely on causal models or heavy supervision. Proposes a causal-graph-free, computationally lightweight fairness mechanism based on counterfactual-like symmetry invariance.
AI.method: Formulate fairness as invariance under a counterfactual sensitive attribute switch with merit features fixed. Implement a loss-based regularization term that penalizes violation of symmetry. Train on four synthetic datasets with varying noise, correlation, and bias; evaluate violation reduction and accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves ≥90% reduction in bias violations; accuracy cost ~5%; no need for causal graph knowledge; computationally lightweight; generalizes to any binary sensitive attribute definable as a bit-flip; applicable where local discrimination sources are not captured by benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed symmetry-regularized framework is a practical, causal-graph-free approach to reduce bias with modest utility loss, suitable for broad deployment; future work could extend to real-world data, multiple sensitive attributes, and continuous attributes, and compare against standard fairness baselines.

title: GOPAgen: Motion-Aware and Efficient Agentic Long-Video Understanding with Structural Memory and Hierarchical Reasoning
summary: Despite significant progress in agentic long video understanding, existing methods still lack detailed motion comprehension coupled with an efficient memory architecture. In this paper, we propose GOPAgen, a novel approach that first integrates video codec into the video understanding framework via a meticulously designed motion agent trained on Groups of Pictures (GOPs) from video codec. We further develop a GOP tree reasoning algorithm, which is naturally aligned with video codec and enhances the model's ability to understand local detailed motions in videos. Additionally, we carefully design a structural memory mechanism that integrates local motion information with detailed captions in structural pages, and propose an efficient coarse-to-fine zoom-in algorithm to fully exploit the structural memory. Furthermore, we incorporate a motion vector database into the framework to enable efficient retrieval of motion vectors at different granularities. Overall, our method achieves superior Video Question Answering (VQA) performance on various video understanding benchmarks, including MotionBench and Egoschema, thereby demonstrating the superiority of our proposed framework.

AI.tldr: GOPAgen integrates video codec GOPs into an efficient motion-aware memory framework for enhanced video VQA, featuring a GOP tree reasoning mechanism, a structural memory with zoom-in, and a motion-vector database; yielding strong results on MotionBench and Egoschema.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in detailed motion understanding and scalable memory for long-form videos; existing methods struggle with fine-grained motion capture and efficient memory integration.
AI.method: 1) Train a motion agent on video GOPs to capture local motion; 2) GOP tree reasoning aligned with GOP structure to reason about motion; 3) Structural memory pages combining local motion with detailed captions; 4) coarse-to-fine zoom-in to exploit structural memory; 5) integrate a motion vector database for multi-granularity retrieval.
AI.result: Achieves superior Video Question Answering performance on MotionBench and Egoschema benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness of the GOPAgen framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively fuses codec-level motion information, structured memory, and retrieval to advance long-video understanding and VQA performance.

title: PhyRoGen: Synthetic Generation of Physical Robot Manipulation Puzzles Using Procedural Content Generation
summary: Robot manipulation of physical puzzles is important for automatic assembly and disassembly tasks. However, to enable robots to solve physical puzzles, manipulation skills need to be learned, which requires large training datasets, the generation of which is often time consuming and tedious. To overcome this problem, we propose the Physical Robot Manipulation Puzzle Generation framework (PhyRoGen), which leverages procedural content generation (PCG) for automated generation of synthetic datasets of manipulation puzzles. PhyRoGen is a general-purpose puzzle generator, which can generate physical puzzles with interlocking object dependencies, where one articulated object must be manipulated before another can be moved. Based upon PhyRoGen, we define six concrete generators which we use to generate 24 physical puzzles. By using a benchmarking framework, we are able to solve all puzzles in 1 to 300 seconds using sampling-based planning algorithms. Finally, we demonstrate that every generated puzzle is manipulatable by using a KUKA LBR iiwa robot in a physical simulation. This shows that our framework is able to procedurally generate unique, solvable robot manipulation puzzles, which is a crucial ingredient to benchmark manipulation algorithms and to develop robust foundation models.

AI.tldr: PCG-based framework PhyRoGen to generate solvable physical puzzle datasets for robot manipulation; 6 generators yielding 24 puzzles; solved with sampling-based planners; validated in simulation with a KUKA LBR iiwa; useful for benchmarking and foundation models.
AI.motivation: Robots require large training datasets to learn manipulation skills, but manually generating such datasets is time-consuming. Procedural content generation can automate and diversify dataset creation, especially for puzzles with interlocking dependencies where one object must be moved before another.
AI.method: PhyRoGen is a general-purpose puzzle generator capable of producing physical puzzles with interlocking dependencies. It defines six concrete generators to create 24 puzzles. A benchmarking framework assesses solvability using sampling-based planning algorithms. Physical manipulability is demonstrated in simulation with a KUKA LBR iiwa robot.
AI.result: All 24 puzzles are solvable within 1 to 300 seconds by sampling-based planners. Each puzzle is manipulatable by the KUKA LBR iiwa robot in physical simulation, confirming the framework produces valid, solvable puzzles suitable for benchmarking and model development.
AI.conclusion: PhyRoGen provides a versatile, procedurally generated suite of unique, solvable manipulation puzzles, enabling robust benchmarking of manipulation algorithms and supporting the development of robust foundation models.

title: FAIR-Calib: Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration for Post-Training Quantization of Diffusion Large Language Models
comment: Accepted as a poster at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) refine tokens iteratively but commit them irreversibly, leading to a "stability lag" where early decisions remain fragile even after being written. We reveal that Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) error easily flips these borderline decisions at the write frontier, which are then permanently locked in and amplified. To address this, we propose Frontier-Aware Instability-Reweighted Calibration (FAIR-Calib), a two-stage PTQ framework for dLLMs. Stage I probes a full-precision teacher to estimate a position prior that combines frontier hits and masked-stage reliability. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE, effectively prioritizing the protection of fragile frontier states without requiring expensive end-to-end diffusion rollouts. We further theoretically justify our weighted objective as a surrogate for output KL divergence. Empirically, FAIR-Calib consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on LLaDA and Dream (W4A4), significantly reducing frontier decision flips and suppressing post-commit mismatches across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Two-stage diffusion LLM quantization (FAIR-Calib) reduces frontier instability by reweighting calibration toward fragile frontier states; theoretically justified and empirically improves stability and reduces post-commit mismatches on LLaDA and Dream.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs commit decisions irreversibly, creating a stability lag. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) errors at the write frontier can flip frontier decisions and lock them in, amplifying errors. A calibration strategy that protects frontier, without costly diffusion rollouts, is needed.
AI.method: Stage I uses a full-precision teacher to estimate a position prior combining frontier hits and masked-stage reliability. Stage II performs off-policy, layer-wise calibration by minimizing a reweighted hidden-state MSE, prioritizing fragile frontier states; provides a theoretical justification that the weighted objective approximates output KL divergence; does not require end-to-end diffusion rollouts.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on LLaDA and Dream (W4A4), significantly reducing frontier decision flips and suppressing post-commit mismatches across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: FAIR-Calib effectively mitigates frontier instability in dLLMs via targeted, theoretically justified calibration, achieving improved stability with efficient, staged calibration.

title: DiBS: Diffusion-Informed Branch Selection
summary: Sudoku is a representative constraint satisfaction problem that requires global structural reasoning under strict discrete constraints. The existing works of solving Sudoku mainly focus on two dominant approaches, i.e., traditional heuristic and deep learning solver. However, they suffer from two complementary limitations: learning-based solvers lack hard correctness guarantees, while complete symbolic solvers are still prone to long-tail search. To address these shortcomings, we propose a novel diffusion model-guided approach, termed as DiBS, for the branch selection search process. Specifically, DiBS keeps the symbolic solver complete and uses the diffusion model as a branch-ordering guide. The core method is ranking candidate values under the current partial assignment and lightweight consistency signal. Furthermore, we provide an in-depth theoretical proof to reveal how it works and why it works. Experiments on the challenging Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark show that our DiBS substantially reduces search cost relative to strong heuristic baselines, especially in nodes, backtracks, and long-tail percentiles. Besides, these results confirm that learned global guidance is effective on hard instances where branch-order mistakes are most expensive. All codes are available at https://github.com/shanxierdan/DiBS.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-model-guided branch ordering approach (DiBS) preserves symbolic completeness while reducing search in Sudoku by using a diffusion model to rank candidate values under partial assignments; shows strong gains on the Royle 17-clue benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing Sudoku solvers fall into two camps: learning-based methods lack hard correctness guarantees, while complete symbolic solvers can suffer from long-tail search. A hybrid method that provides global guidance without sacrificing completeness is desirable.
AI.method: Integrate a diffusion model to guide the branch-ordering process: rank candidate values under the current partial assignment using diffusion-based scores and a lightweight consistency signal, while keeping the symbolic solver complete. The paper provides theoretical analysis explaining why the approach works.
AI.result: Empirical results on the Royle 17-clue Sudoku benchmark show substantial reductions in search cost (nodes, backtracks, long-tail percentiles) compared with strong heuristic baselines; learned global guidance is effective on hard instances where branch-order mistakes are costly. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that learned global guidance via diffusion models can improve the efficiency of complete solvers on hard CSP instances like Sudoku, without sacrificing correctness, and suggests potential applicability to other CSPs.

title: Attention-Guided Autoencoder Fusion for Insulator Defect Detection Using UAV Transmission-Line Imaging
summary: Automated defect detection in high-voltage transmission-line insulators remains challenging due to severe class imbalance, large scale variation, and the small spatial extent of defect instances in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) imagery. To address these challenges, this paper proposes AE-YOLO, an Attention-Guided AutoEncoder-Enhanced YOLO framework for robust insulator defect detection. The architecture integrates lightweight bottleneck autoencoders within a Feature Pyramid Network-Path Aggregation Network (FPN-PAN) neck. This preserves anomaly-sensitive information during multi-scale feature fusion. Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) are used throughout the backbone, enhancing feature discrimination and suppressing background interference. The framework also introduces a variance-maximizing autoencoder regularization strategy, which encourages diverse, defect-discriminative latent representations. The network trains using a unified objective that combines focal loss, Complete IoU (CIoU) loss, and autoencoder regularization to address foreground-background imbalance and improve localization accuracy. During inference, Weighted Boxes Fusion (WBF) combines predictions from YOLOv8, YOLOv10, and YOLO11. An autoencoder-guided confidence boosting mechanism improves sensitivity to rare defect categories. Experiments on the Insulator-Defect Detection dataset show that AE-YOLO with an EfficientNetV2 backbone achieves 95.10 percent mAP at 0.5, 96.40 percent precision, and 93.80 percent recall. This performance surpasses the strongest YOLO-family baseline by 5.0 points in mAP at 0.5 and 6.7 points in recall. These results confirm the effectiveness and adaptability of the framework. The model is a practical and scalable solution for UAV-based transmission-line inspection and defect monitoring.

AI.tldr: A-EYOLO: an attention-guided autoencoder-enhanced YOLO for robust insulator defect detection in UAV imagery, achieving high mAP/precision/recall via FPN-PAN neck with autoencoders, CBAM, and a unified loss; uses WBF across YOLO variants and autoencoder-guided confidence boosting.
AI.motivation: Address severe class imbalance, large scale variation, and small defect instances in UAV images of high-voltage insulators.
AI.method: Embed lightweight bottleneck autoencoders in the FPN-PAN neck; apply CBAM throughout the backbone; adopt a variance-maximizing autoencoder regularization; optimize with a unified objective combining focal loss, CIoU loss, and autoencoder regularization; fuse predictions with Weighted Boxes Fusion across YOLOv8/YOLOv10/YOLOv11; use autoencoder-guided confidence boosting to enhance rare defect detection.
AI.result: On Insulator-Defect Detection dataset, AE-YOLO with EfficientNetV2 backbone attains 95.10% mAP@0.5, 96.40% precision, and 93.80% recall, outperforming the strongest YOLO baseline by 5.0 mAP points and 6.7 points recall.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness and adaptability of the framework for practical, scalable UAV-based insulator defect monitoring.

title: ChronoForest: Closed-Loop Multi-Tree Diffusion Planning for Efficient Bridge Search and Route Composition
summary:
How can we plan long-horizon routes that reach designated goals, visit required waypoints, and remain short when only short-horizon offline trajectories are available? This problem matters in offline navigation because collecting sufficiently rich long-horizon data is difficult, yet real agents must still solve long-range tasks with route-level efficiency rather than mere feasibility. The difficulty is twofold: at the microscopic level, composing many short-horizon segments creates a trade-off between search cost and path quality, while at the macroscopic level, waypoint ordering requires comparing pairwise travel costs among start, goal, and waypoint anchors that are unknown before planning and increasingly unreliable when estimated only from long-range temporal distance.
  In this paper, we propose ChronoForest, a closed-loop planning system that couples local bridge search and online route re-solving through an anchor-chaining tree diffusion planner and an online multi-tree orchestrator. ChronoForest uses temporal distance for short-range guidance and node evaluation, while using search-time bridge evidence to validate long-range anchor connectivity and repeatedly re-solve the route. On OGBench AntMaze-Stitch, ChronoForest achieves 99.8%, 99.3%, and 99.5% success on the medium, large, and giant splits and improves giant-stitch success by up to 34.5 points over prior reported diffusion-based results. On Hamiltonian route-composition benchmarks, online re-solving corrects poor temporal orderings and improves route quality while remaining substantially cheaper than exhaustive planning.

AI.tldr: ChronoForest is a closed-loop planner for long-horizon offline navigation that combines an anchor-chaining diffusion planner with online multi-tree re-solving to maximize route success and quality using short-horizon data.
AI.motivation: Offline navigation data are typically rich for short-horizon behavior but scarce for long-horizon routes. The challenge is to compose many short-horizon segments into a route that visits designated waypoints, minimizes cost, and orders waypoints correctly despite unreliable long-range estimates.
AI.method: ChronoForest couples a bridge-search-based local planner with an online orchestrator. It uses an anchor-chaining tree diffusion planner to propose routes guided by temporal distance for short-range evaluation, and an online multi-tree orchestrator to validate long-range anchor connectivity through bridge evidence and repeatedly re-solve the route to improve plan quality.
AI.result: On OGBench AntMaze-Stitch, ChronoForest attains 99.8%, 99.3%, and 99.5% success on the medium, large, and giant splits, and increases giant-stitch success by up to 34.5 points over prior diffusion-based results. On Hamiltonian route-composition benchmarks, online re-solving corrects poor temporal orderings and enhances route quality while remaining far cheaper than exhaustive planning.
AI.conclusion: ChronoForest demonstrates effective integration of short-range guidance with online long-range validation to enable robust, efficient long-horizon route planning from offline data, achieving strong empirical performance and computational efficiency.

title: Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network for Polymer Classification using THz Dual-Comb Spectroscopy
summary: Reliable polymer identification is essential for ensuring the quality and safety of recycled plastics, yet conventional sorting and spectroscopic techniques often struggle to deliver robust discrimination. Terahertz Dual-Comb Spectroscopy (THz-DCS) offers a promising alternative, providing rapid, high-resolution, and non-destructive measurements. In this work, we leverage THz-DCS to classify 12 types of polymers, including pure polymers, multilayer films, commercial blends, and biopolymers. To handle the complexity of these spectral signals, we propose the Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network (MSFAN), a novel deep learning architecture tailored for THz-DCS data. The framework integrates feature gating for signal recalibration and multi-scale parallel convolutions to capture diverse frequency patterns. These features are further refined through cross-feature attention and attention pooling, enabling the model to intrinsically highlight the most informative THz regions. MSFAN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, reaching a classification accuracy of 85.2%. This study demonstrates the potential of combining THz-DCS with deep learning techniques for effective, scalable, and interpretable polymer classification.

AI.tldr: THz-DCS combined with a Multi-Scale Feature Attention Network (MSFAN) achieves 85.2% accuracy in classifying 12 polymer types, showing promise for robust, non-destructive polymer identification.
AI.motivation: Need robust, rapid, non-destructive polymer identification to improve recycled-plastic sorting; conventional spectroscopy and sorting struggle with complex spectra and layered/blended polymers. THz-DCS offers high-resolution, non-destructive measurements; deep learning can handle complex spectral signals.
AI.method: Collect THz-DCS spectra for 12 polymer types (including pure polymers, multilayer films, blends, biopolymers). Develop MSFAN with feature gating for signal recalibration, multi-scale parallel convolutions, cross-feature attention, and attention pooling to highlight informative THz regions. Train and evaluate MSFAN against baselines; report final accuracy.
AI.result: MSFAN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models with an accuracy of 85.2% across 12 polymer classes, indicating strong discriminative power of THz-DCS features when processed with the proposed architecture.
AI.conclusion: Coupling THz-DCS with MSFAN yields effective, scalable, and interpretable polymer classification, demonstrating potential for improved sorting of recycled plastics and quality control in polymer manufacturing.

title: SafeGene: Reusable Adapters for Transferable Safety Alignment
summary: Open-weight LLMs are increasingly fine-tuned into customized assistants, but downstream fine-tuning can weaken safety alignment and make models more vulnerable to malicious prompts, even when the training data is not intentionally harmful. This creates a recurring safety recovery problem as target models are repeatedly updated with new task data or user interactions. We propose SafeGene, a reusable safety-adapter module designed for cross-task reuse within each architecture-compatible model family. Rather than treating safety recovery as a model-specific repair step, SafeGene treats safety capability as an independent, reusable adapter representation decoupled from task-specific updates. This representation is obtained from aligned--degraded model discrepancies, refined into task-transferable safety vectors through data-aware layer selection, and expressed in each downstream task-adapted model via few-shot layer-wise coefficient recalibration. Experiments across multiple model families, downstream tasks, and safety judges show that SafeGene-enhanced models reduce harmful response rates while maintaining downstream performance, outperforming representative safe adaptation methods in safety--utility trade-off.

AI.tldr: SafeGene introduces a reusable safety-adapter module that decouples safety recovery from task updates, enabling cross-task reuse to reduce harmful responses while preserving downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Downstream fine-tuning can degrade safety alignment and increase vulnerability to malicious prompts. Recurrent safety recovery is needed across model updates, necessitating a reusable solution that works across tasks and model families.
AI.method: Develop SafeGene as an architecture-compatible safety adapter. It captures safety capability from aligned–degraded model discrepancies, refines them into task-transferable safety vectors via data-aware layer selection, and reproduces them in downstream models using few-shot layer-wise coefficient recalibration.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple model families, downstream tasks, and safety judges show SafeGene reduces harmful response rates while maintaining downstream performance, outperforming representative safe adaptation methods in the safety–utility trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Safety capabilities can be made reusable and decoupled from task-specific fine-tuning. SafeGene provides a cross-task safety-adapter approach that improves robustness of safety alignment during continual model updates.

title: WorldBench: A Challenging and Visually Diverse Multimodal Reasoning Benchmark
summary: In real-world applications, models are expected to perform reliably across diverse settings. Yet, many existing multimodal benchmarks expand task types without capturing the visual diversity needed to handle open-ended visual inputs. We present WorldBench, a challenging and visually diverse reasoning benchmark to evaluate Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). We build a taxonomy of thousands of visual concepts across multiple domains (e.g., living things). Guided by this taxonomy, we curate a broad collection of images from search engines and existing datasets to comprehensively represent the visual world. Through structured trial-and-error, we manually design challenging questions that frontier MLLMs fail to answer. On quantitative and human evaluations, WorldBench achieves higher visual diversity than any existing diverse benchmark. Evaluating 15 MLLMs on WorldBench reveals weaknesses in visual understanding: even the strongest model reaches only 64.0% accuracy, while some models perform marginally above chance-level. We hope our work highlights the importance of visual diversity in building multimodal benchmarks.

AI.tldr: WorldBench is a visually diverse multimodal benchmark for evaluating MLLMs; builds a taxonomy of thousands of visual concepts, curates diverse images, and designs challenging questions to expose weaknesses; evaluated 15 MLLMs with the strongest at 64% accuracy, highlighting the need for visual diversity in benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks under-represent visual diversity and open-ended reasoning; need robust evaluation across diverse real-world visuals.
AI.method: Construct a taxonomy of visual concepts across domains; curate images from search engines and datasets guided by taxonomy; manual, trial-and-error design of challenging questions; evaluate 15 MLLMs.
AI.result: WorldBench provides higher visual diversity than prior benchmarks; reveals significant gaps in MLLM visual understanding; best model achieves 64% accuracy, some near chance.
AI.conclusion: Visual diversity is critical in multimodal benchmarking; future benchmarks should emphasize broad visual coverage and challenging reasoning to drive robust MLLM improvements.

title: What Matters When Cotraining Robot Manipulation Policies on Everyday Human Videos?
summary: Human video datasets used for cotraining robot manipulation policies largely consist of curated demonstrations where motions are orchestrated to resemble robot behavior and 3D hand poses are captured with specialized hardware. A more plentiful source of data is everyday Internet video, but it is an open question what factors enable transfer from such videos to robots. We investigate this using a new dataset of 532 human videos with 28 hours of high-quality triangulated hand labels and natural motions. We find that hand pose quality affects transfer, but even with accurate hands, the inherent motion gap hinders transfer unless the vision and policy networks specialize to each embodiment. Our cotraining recipe yields consistent improvements, with an absolute success rate gain of $29.7\%$ in the low-robot-data regime across six manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: Cotrain robot manipulation policies using internet-scale human videos; hand pose quality and embodiment gaps limit transfer; cotraining with embodiment-specific networks yields 29.7 percentage-point improvement in low-robot-data regime across six tasks.
AI.motivation: Understand why transferring from online human videos to robots works; identify factors such as hand pose accuracy and motion gap; leverage cotraining to boost data efficiency.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of 532 human videos with 28 hours of triangulated hand labels; analyze transfer under varying hand pose quality; introduce cotraining recipe with embodiment-specific vision and policy networks; evaluate on six manipulation tasks.
AI.result: Hand pose quality matters; even with accurate hands, a motion gap between human and robot embodiments impedes transfer; specialization of vision/policy networks per embodiment improves transfer; cotraining yields 29.7 percentage-point gain in low-robot-data regime.
AI.conclusion: Transfers from internet videos to robots can be improved via embodiment-aware cotraining; both hand pose quality and motion gap are key; tailoring models to each embodiment is crucial for data-efficient transfer.

title: MacArena: Benchmarking Computer Use Agents on an Online macOS Environment
comment: Accepted to the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) operate graphical user interfaces (GUIs) through vision and control primitives, and their capabilities have advanced rapidly, driven in part by standardized online evaluation benchmarks such as OSWorld, which serve both as evaluation tools and as training environments for reinforcement learning. However, macOS remains underserved in this landscape: the only existing benchmark, macOSWorld, covers a narrow slice of first-party applications with simpler tasks, and runs on x86 virtual machines incompatible with Apple Silicon. We introduce MacArena, a benchmark of 421 manually verified tasks spanning 50 applications that combines a curated port of OSWorld tasks, content sourced from macOSWorld, and 49 new macOS-native tasks, all running on Apple's native Virtualization framework on Apple Silicon. We argue that macOS presents distinct GUI challenges beyond what Linux-based benchmarks capture, and our evaluation supports this claim: strong model performance on existing benchmarks can reflect familiarity with task distributions rather than genuine cross-platform GUI competence. Notably, model rankings invert between ported and macOS-native tasks, with a leading model trailing by over 26% on the MacArena subset, suggesting that macOS poses a genuinely harder environment for current GUI agents.

AI.tldr: MacArena introduces a macOS-native GUI benchmark with 421 tasks across 50 apps on Apple Silicon, showing macOS is a harder environment for GUI agents and that performance on Linux-ported benchmarks does not generalize to macOS-native tasks.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in GUI agent benchmarking for macOS due to platform-specific GUI challenges and the mismatch between Linux-based benchmarks and Apple Silicon/macOS tasks; existing macOSWorld is limited in scope and hardware compatibility.
AI.method: Assembles MacArena by curating a port of OSWorld tasks, incorporating macOSWorld content, and adding 49 new macOS-native tasks. All tasks are validated manually and run on Apple's native Virtualization framework on Apple Silicon.
AI.result: MacArena reveals cross-platform differences: strong performance on existing benchmarks can reflect task familiarity rather than cross-platform GUI competence. Model rankings invert between ported vs. native macOS tasks; the top model on ported tasks underperforms by >26% on MacArena native tasks.
AI.conclusion: macOS presents distinct GUI challenges not captured by Linux-based benchmarks. MacArena is a必要 tool to evaluate GUI agents’ true cross-platform competence and generalization to Apple Silicon/macOS environments.

title: Lean4Agent: Formal Modeling and Verification for Agent Workflow and Trajectory
summary: Equipping Large Language Models (LLMs) to execute reliable multi-step workflows has become a central challenge in artificial intelligence. Despite recent advances in LLMs' agentic capabilities, most agent systems still lack formal methods for specifying, verifying, and debugging their workflow and execution trajectories. This challenge mirrors a long-standing problem in mathematics, where the ambiguity of natural languages (NLs) motivates the development of formal languages (FLs). Inspired by this paradigm, we propose **Lean4Agent**, to the best of our knowledge, the first framework that uses Lean4, a dependent-type FL to model and verify agent behavior. **Lean4Agent** launches **FormalAgentLib**, an extensible Lean4 library for formally modeling and verifying agent workflows' semantic consistency under explicit assumptions, and enabling localization of execution-time failures revealed by trajectories. Building on **FormalAgentLib**, we further develop **LeanEvolve**, which applies results in **FormalAgentLib** to revise workflows to enhance its capability. Extensive experiments on a hard problem subset of SWE-Bench-Verified and a subset of ELAIP-Bench across 5 leading LLMs indicate that the verification-passing workflows outperform the failing ones by an average of **11.94%**, and **LeanEvolve** further improves SWE performance by **7.47%** on average. Furthermore, **Lean4Agent** establishes a foundation for a new field of using expressive dependent-type FL to formally model and verify agent behavior.

AI.tldr: Lean4Agent introduces a dependent-type formal framework in Lean4 to model, verify, and revise LLM agent workflows; FormalAgentLib for semantic verification; LeanEvolve to revise workflows; empirical gains across benchmarks and LLMs.
AI.motivation: Address lack of formal methods for specifying, verifying, and debugging multi-step LLM agent workflows; leverage formal languages to reduce NL ambiguity and guarantee behavior.
AI.method: Develop Lean4Agent atop Lean4; build FormalAgentLib for semantic consistency verification under explicit assumptions; extend with LeanEvolve for workflow revision; evaluate on SWE-Bench-Verified hard subset and ELAIP-Bench subset across 5 LLMs; report verification-passing vs failing improvements and SWE improvements.
AI.result: Verification-passing workflows outperform failing ones by 11.94% on average; LeanEvolve improves SWE performance by 7.47% on average.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a new paradigm for using expressive dependent-type formal languages to model and verify agent behavior, enabling detection of execution-time failures and automated workflow refinement.

title: Synthetic Benchmarks Overstate Forward-Forward Scaling: Real-Data Limits of Layer-Local Training
summary:
Forward-Forward (FF) learning [Hinton, 2022] replaces backpropagation with strictly layer-local goodness updates. Recent FF-CNN work has narrowed the gap to BP on 32x32 benchmarks, raising the question of whether layer-local training is becoming a viable alternative at realistic scale. To probe this rigorously, we develop DTG-FF -- dynamic temperature goodness, decoupled normalization, and multi-layer fusion -- as an instrument that sets FF-family state of the art across nine real-data benchmarks (91.8% CIFAR-10 and the first FF baseline at ImageNet-100 224x224), and use it to audit how far layer-local training actually scales.
  (1) Real-data scaling. Under identical recipe and backbone, an architecture-matched BP-DeepSup baseline beats DTG-FF by 2.40/5.93 pp on CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100, and the gap widens with class count. At 224x224 the same instrument reaches only 49.4% -- the first FF baseline at this scale, versus typical BP above 75% [Tian et al., 2020] -- exposing a real-data ceiling invisible at 32x32.
  (2) Synthetic vs. real K-conflict. DTG-FF increasingly outperforms BP as class count K grows on synthetic teacher-student tasks, yet on real images the FF-BP gap reverses sign and widens with K. A within-dataset CIFAR-100 coarse vs. fine probe isolates label-hierarchy from image distribution: synthetic K-sweeps confound output dimensionality with fine-grained discrimination difficulty and thereby overstate FF transferability.
  (3) Systems audit. FF can be implemented without storing depth-wide activations, but on commodity 8 GB hardware standard BP+gradient-accumulation reaches 4.18 GB / 157 imgs/s versus DTG-FF's 7.90 GB / 138 imgs/s, so a memory-based justification for FF at this scale is not supported under fair baselines.

AI.tldr: DTG-FF introduces dynamic temperature goodness, decoupled normalization, and multi-layer fusion to push Forward-Forward (FF) learning toward real-data scalability; it achieves state-of-the-art among FF methods across nine real-data benchmarks but reveals real-data ceilings relative to backpropagation, and shows that synthetic tasks can overstate FF transferability while memory considerations challenge practical FF use at scale.
AI.motivation: Assess whether layer-local training via FF can scale to realistic datasets and architectures, and rigorously audit claims of FF scalability against backpropagation (BP).
AI.method: Introduce DTG-FF as an instrument to benchmark FF-family learning. Conduct experiments across nine real-data benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet-100 224x224, etc.), comparing DTG-FF with a BP-DeepSup baseline using identical backbones. Analyze (1) real-data scaling results, (2) synthetic vs. real K-conflict using synthetic tasks and a CIFAR coarse-vs-fine probe to separate label hierarchy from image distribution, and (3) systems/memory efficiency (activations storage and throughput) to assess hardware practicality.
AI.result: DTG-FF sets FF-family state-of-the-art on the tested real-data suite. On CIFAR-10/100 with identical backbones, BP-DeepSup outperforms DTG-FF by 2.40 and 5.93 percentage points, respectively. At ImageNet-100 224x224, DTG-FF achieves 49.4% accuracy, whereas typical BP remains above ~75%. Synthetic K-growth favors DTG-FF over BP, but on real images the FF–BP gap reverses and widens with higher K. A CIFAR coarse-vs-fine probe indicates synthetic gains conflate output dimensionality with discriminability. System-wise, BP+gradient accumulation uses about 4.18 GB and achieves 157 images/s, while DTG-FF uses 7.90 GB and 138 images/s, offering no clear memory-based justification for FF under fair baselines.
AI.conclusion: DTG-FF advances Forward-Forward research to real-data benchmarks and highlights critical scaling gaps: significant real-data ceilings relative to BP on large-scale tasks, potential overestimation of transferability in synthetic settings, and lack of clear memory efficiency advantage under fair comparisons. The work provides a rigorous baseline and identifies concrete directions needed to realize FF-scale viability in practical vision workloads.

title: On the Hardness of Optimal Motion on Trees
summary:
This paper presents a simple framework that settles the complexity of Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) on trees across standard objectives--distance, makespan, and flowtime--for both labeled and colored variants. In MAPF, agents occupy the vertices of a graph and must move to target vertices without collisions while optimizing a given objective. In the labeled case, the agents are distinct and have respective targets; in the colored case, agents of the same color are interchangeable. While many MAPF variants are known to be intractable, several basic cases on trees have remained open. We prove NP-hardness on trees for both labeled and 2-colored MAPF under all three objectives. In particular, we resolve the classical Pebble Motion problem, where one pebble moves at a time to an adjacent empty vertex and the goal is to minimize the total number of moves. Despite being one of the most basic discrete motion models, its complexity on trees had remained open for several decades. Moreover, for colored Pebble Motion, we give the first hardness result on any graph class, already with two colors, which is tight.
  All of these results are established through the hardness of Stack Rearrangement, itself posed as an open problem, which asks to optimally rearrange items stored in stacks, and which we also prove to be NP-hard. Notably, the connection to stacks yields hardness already on very simple trees--subdivided stars--across all problems. Together, these results reveal a common tractability barrier that permeates several fundamental motion models, thereby unifying and strengthening prior hardness results.

AI.tldr: MAPF on trees is NP-hard for labeled and 2-colored variants under distance, makespan, and flowtime; Pebble Motion (single-pebble moves) is also NP-hard on trees; colored Pebble Motion with two colors is NP-hard on any graph; all results follow from Stack Rearrangement hardness, with subdivided stars yielding the core barrier.
AI.motivation: Resolve open questions about the complexity of basic MAPF variants on trees and related motion models, unify hardness results, and identify a common tractability barrier across classic models.
AI.method: Reduction-based proofs from Stack Rearrangement, constructing instances on simple graphs (notably subdivided stars) to induce hardness for MAPF (labeled and colored) and Pebble Motion under multiple objectives; establish hardness for both single-color and two-color colored variants.
AI.result: Proves NP-hardness of MAPF on trees for labeled and 2-colored variants under distance, makespan, and flowtime; shows NP-hardness of the classical Pebble Motion problem on trees; first hardness result for colored Pebble Motion with two colors on any graph class; hardness already on subdivided stars; Stack Rearrangement is NP-hard and serves as the central hardness source.
AI.conclusion: Unifies and strengthens prior hardness results across core motion models, revealing a common tractability barrier that persists even on very simple topologies, and provides a cohesive framework linking Stack Rearrangement to MAPF and Pebble Motion hardness.

title: WAV: Multi-Resolution Block Residual Routing for Deep Decoder-Only Transformers
summary: Residual connections are central to training deep Transformers, but standard PreNorm residual streams aggregate sublayer updates with fixed unit weights. Recent Attention Residuals replace this fixed accumulation with content-dependent depth-wise routing, and Block Attention Residuals make the mechanism efficient by routing over block-level residual summaries. However, a single block summary stores only the low-frequency total residual displacement inside a block, discarding directional structure such as attention-vs-MLP imbalance and early-vs-late block dynamics. We propose WAV v1, a lightweight multi-resolution residual routing method for decoder-only Transformers. Instead of representing each block only by its accumulated residual sum, WAV v1 augments every block with two directional detail bases: a phase basis that contrasts attention and MLP updates, and a split basis that contrasts early and late sublayer updates. These bases are routed together with standard block summaries through the same depth-wise softmax mixer, while negative detail-source initialization and detached RMS matching stabilize training. On character-level TinyStories and Text8 language modeling, WAV v1 shows a clear depth-dependent benefit. Although it is not consistently beneficial at 12 layers, it becomes competitive at 24 layers and outperforms all baselines at 48 layers. At 48 layers, WAV v1 reduces validation loss relative to Block AttnRes from 0.4960 to 0.4738 on TinyStories and from 0.9363 to 0.9305 on Text8, with negligible additional parameters. These results suggest that directional residual details, not only block-level sums, are important for scaling residual routing in deeper Transformers.

AI.tldr: WAV v1 adds directional residual detail bases (phase and split) to each block in decoder-only Transformers, routing them alongside standard block summaries via a depth-wise mixer. This multi-resolution residual routing yields depth-dependent gains, stabilizes training, and improves validation loss at very deep scales with negligible parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard PreNorm residual connections use fixed-unit aggregation; existing content-dependent residuals (e.g., Attention Residuals, Block AttnRes) lose directional structure by summarizing blocks with a single residual sum. Preserving attention-vs-MLP dynamics and early-vs-late sublayer dynamics may be crucial for scaling residual routing in deep transformers.
AI.method: Augment each block with two directional detail bases: a phase basis (contrast between attention and MLP updates) and a split basis (contrast between early and late sublayer updates). Route these details along with the standard block summary using the same depth-wise softmax mixer. Stabilize training with negative detail-source initialization and detached RMS matching. Evaluate on decoder-only language models (TinyStories and Text8) across varying depths (12/24/48 layers).
AI.result: WAV v1 shows depth-dependent benefits, becoming competitive at 24 layers and outperforming baselines at 48 layers. At 48 layers, validation loss reductions are from 0.4960 to 0.4738 on TinyStories and from 0.9363 to 0.9305 on Text8, with negligible parameter increases.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating directional residual details alongside block-level sums improves scaling of residual routing in deep Transformers and can yield meaningful gains at large depths without extra parameter cost.

title: CrowdMath: A Dataset of Crowdsourced Mathematical Research Discussions
summary: Large language models have made substantial progress on mathematical reasoning, but existing benchmarks typically evaluate well-specified problems with final answers, step-by-step solutions, or complete proofs. They do not capture collaborative open-problem solving: a setting in which participants propose partial arguments, identify gaps or errors in prior steps, repair flawed reasoning, and gradually synthesize incremental contributions into a proof. We introduce CrowdMath, a dataset of 164 expert-annotated progress chains from the MIT PRIMES--Art of Problem Solving (AoPS) CrowdMath program (2016-2025), a collaborative research initiative whose discussions have led to peer-reviewed publications. Each chain traces a multi-participant forum discussion from an open-problem statement to a completed proof. Posts are labeled by their functional roles in the evolving solution process, including partial progress, proof completion, erroneous reasoning, and error identification. We define evaluation tasks and benchmark six frontier models. Models achieve 83-88% accuracy on next-post prediction, suggesting that they can follow the local flow of mathematical discussion. However, they struggle to identify the functional significance of individual contributions with the best model achieving only 0.42 macro-F1 on post-role classification. CrowdMath exposes a gap between solving well-specified mathematical problems and understanding collaborative mathematical progress as it unfolds.

AI.tldr: CrowdMath provides 164 expert-annotated progress chains from MIT PRIMES AoPS to study collaborative open-problem solving; labels posts by functional roles; benchmarks six frontier models; next-post prediction is strong (83-88%), while post-role classification is weak (macro-F1 ~0.42).
AI.motivation: Fill gap in evaluating LLMs on collaborative, incremental mathematical reasoning and open problems by building a dataset that captures the evolution of a solution with participant roles and error handling.
AI.method: Assemble and annotate 164 progress chains from a real-world collaborative math program; label each post with functional roles (partial progress, error, error identification, proof completion, etc.); define evaluation tasks and benchmark six leading models on next-post prediction and post-role classification.
AI.result: Models achieve 83-88% accuracy on next-post prediction, indicating ability to follow local discourse flow; best macro-F1 for post-role classification is 0.42, showing difficulty in inferring the functional significance of contributions.
AI.conclusion: CrowdMath reveals a gap between solving well-specified problems and understanding collaborative mathematical progress as it unfolds, highlighting the need for models that can interpret and synthesize incremental, collaborative reasoning.

title: Direct 3D-Aware Object Insertion via Decomposed Visual Proxies
summary: Object insertion aims to seamlessly composite a reference object into a specified region of a background image. Recent diffusion-based methods achieve high visual quality but formulate insertion as a simple 2D inpainting task, providing no explicit control over the object's 3D pose and limiting their practical applicability. We propose DIRECT (Decomposed Injection for Reference Composition and Target-integration), a novel framework that integrates interactive pose manipulation with high-fidelity 2D image synthesis to enable pose-controllable object insertion. Our method decomposes the insertion conditions into three complementary components: appearance guidance capturing visual details from the reference object, geometry guidance derived from the user-adjusted 3D proxy, and context guidance from the target background. By injecting them through separate pathways, DIRECT avoids feature entanglement and simultaneously preserves reference appearance, follows the user-specified pose, and adapts the object to the target scene. We also introduce an automated data construction pipeline to improve the diversity and quality of training data. Experiments show that DIRECT outperforms previous methods in both geometric controllability and visual quality.

AI.tldr: DIRECT enables pose-controlled object insertion by decomposing guidance into appearance, geometry, and context streams with separate pathways, achieving high fidelity and improved geometric control.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based insertion methods treat insertion as 2D inpainting and lack explicit control over 3D pose, limiting practical applications.
AI.method: Three-stream injection: appearance guidance from the reference object, geometry guidance from a user-adjusted 3D proxy, and context guidance from the target background; separate pathways prevent feature entanglement; an automated data construction pipeline augments training data.
AI.result: Experiments show that DIRECT outperforms prior methods in both geometric controllability and visual quality.
AI.conclusion: DIRECT provides a pose-controllable object insertion framework with decoupled guidance streams and automated data support, enabling high-fidelity, pose-consistent composites.

title: Optimal Control Approach for Non-prehensile Ball Juggling Using a 7-DoF Manipulator
summary: Non-prehensile object manipulation skills are important for real-world robot interactions, enabling highly dynamic tasks such as balancing a glass on a tray or the controlled sliding of items on a table. Among such tasks, those characterised by high-speed manipulation requirements and general sensitivity of the resulting hybrid dynamics are particularly hard to accomplish. Within these, juggling can be seen as a highly challenging maneuver to be solved. The key to robotic juggling is achieving dynamic stabilisation of an underactuated object. Since the object does not possess the ability of self-correction, its stability is entirely dependent on the forces applied to it. This creates a system that is sensitive to control inputs, where timing is critical to continuously counteract deviations and maintain the desired behavior. We develop a systematic method to control a 7-degree-of-freedom manipulator performing non-prehensile ball juggling with a tool. Our primary contribution is a model-based framework for generating juggling trajectories and stabilizing a periodic juggling motion for this hybrid system. The framework incorporates a two-stage optimal control approach to compute the underlying feasible motion patterns required for stable juggling. Offline-computed trajectories are then organised to enable real-time error correction without solving optimal control problems online. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the resulting controller by first evaluating its performance in a simulation environment and performing an experiment using a Franka Emika Panda robot.

AI.tldr: A model-based, two-stage optimal-control framework for generating and stabilizing periodic juggling trajectories for a 7-DOF manipulator performing non-prehensile ball juggling with a tool; offline trajectory generation paired with real-time error correction; validated in simulation and on a Franka Panda.
AI.motivation: Dynamic, underactuated non-prehensile manipulation (juggling) is highly sensitive to timing and control inputs; achieving stable juggling requires accurate trajectory planning and robust stabilization against perturbations.
AI.method: Two-stage optimal control: first compute feasible motion patterns offline to realize periodic juggling; second stage reorganizes offline trajectories to enable real-time error correction without online re-optimization; experiments include simulation and a real-robot test on a 7-DOF manipulator (Franka Panda).
AI.result: Effective dynamic stabilization of a periodic juggling motion demonstrated; offline trajectories enable real-time correction; validated in both simulation and real hardware, indicating practical viability of the framework.
AI.conclusion: A practical, model-based framework for non-prehensile juggling that combines offline optimization with real-time correction; suitable for high-speed, hybrid-dynamics tasks, with potential extension to other underactuated, non-prehensile tasks.

title: Are you sure? A Comprehensive and Comprehensible Survey of Uncertainty Quantification in Symbolic Regression
summary: Symbolic regression (SR) is a class of methods that systematically explore the space of mathematical functions to discover models that accurately capture the underlying relationships in a dataset. Despite recent advances in the field, a lack of support for uncertainty quantification (UQ) limits its adoption in real-world decision processes. In regression analysis, UQ provides important information about the model reliability, which can both help to avoid overfitting by accounting for uncertainty in the data, and provide insights for decision-making. This survey is the first to clearly address this issue, with the objective of introducing essential UQ concepts and reviewing the current literature on UQ in SR, which can be broadly organized into three research directions: frequentist, Bayesian, and model selection. Despite its importance, UQ in SR is still underexplored, which motivates further research into reliable UQ methods for SR.

AI.tldr: A survey emphasizes the need for uncertainty quantification (UQ) in symbolic regression (SR) and organizes UQ approaches into frequentist, Bayesian, and model-selection categories.
AI.motivation: UQ enhances SR reliability, reduces overfitting, and informs decision-making by accounting for data uncertainty; current SR work lacks integrated UQ.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature survey on UQ in SR, structured around three research directions (frequentist, Bayesian, model selection) and discussion of core concepts, methods, and gaps.
AI.result: Findings indicate that UQ in SR is underexplored with limited adoption of existing UQ techniques in SR workflows.
AI.conclusion: Calls for more robust, reliable UQ methods in SR and for integrating UQ into SR practice to improve decision support and model reliability.

title: Attack Selection in Agentic AI Control Evaluations Meaningfully Decreases Safety
summary: An attacker that strategically chooses when to attack is much harder to catch than one that attacks indiscriminately. AI control is a safety framework for deploying capable but untrusted AI agents under the oversight of a weaker, trusted monitor and a limited human audit budget. Control evaluations stress-test these protocols by pitting a red-team attack policy against the blue-team monitor, but current evaluations typically assume attackers that do not strategically select when to attack. We study this capability, attack selection, in agentic settings by decomposing attack decisions into a start policy, which decides when an attacker should attack, and a stop policy, which decides when an attacker should abort an ongoing attack. Across two agentic settings, BashArena and LinuxArena, both policies substantially lower measured empirical safety without changing the underlying attack capability. At a 1% audit budget, our start policy reduces safety by 20pp on both BashArena and LinuxArena, and our stop policy reduces safety by 20pp on BashArena and 28pp on LinuxArena. These reductions should be interpreted as upper bounds on the effect of attack selection. Existing control evaluations may therefore yield overly optimistic safety estimates against selective attackers. We recommend that future evaluations, system cards, and safety cases elicit attack selection to produce more realistic safety estimates.

AI.tldr: Attack selection (start/stop policies) markedly degrades empirical AI safety in agentic settings, implying current safety evaluations are overly optimistic against selective attackers.
AI.motivation: To understand how strategic attackers who decide when to attack impact safety evaluations of AI control frameworks and to benchmark how much safety is eroded when attackers optimize timing under limited audit budgets.
AI.method: Decompose attacker decisions into a start policy (when to attack) and a stop policy (when to abort) and evaluate them in two agentic environments, BashArena and LinuxArena, under a 1% audit budget.
AI.result: Both policies substantially reduce measured empirical safety: start policy lowers safety by 20 percentage points in both BashArena and LinuxArena; stop policy lowers safety by 20pp in BashArena and 28pp in LinuxArena. These are upper bounds on attack selection effects.
AI.conclusion: Existing control evaluations may yield overly optimistic safety estimates against selective attackers. Future evaluations, safety cards, and risk assessments should explicitly elicit attack selection to produce more realistic safety estimates.

title: From Pixels to Newtons: Predicting In Vivo Joint Contact Forces from Monocular Video
summary: Joint contact forces govern implant longevity, cartilage health, and rehabilitation outcomes, shaping who develops osteoarthritis, who recovers well from joint replacement, and who benefits from biomechanical interventions. Yet they remain measurable only invasively, in a few dozen patients with instrumented implants. I present a physics-free pipeline to predict instantaneous 3D hip and knee contact forces from an uncalibrated monocular video: no markers, force plates, electromyography, subject-specific imaging, or musculoskeletal model. Parametric body meshes are recovered per frame, encoded as kinematic features, and decoded into forces by a transformer whose pose stream is adaptively modulated at every layer by body shape, joint, side, activity text, and self-supervised video tokens (V-JEPA 2), unifying hip and knee in a single model. Under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation across 26 patients and 25 activity categories from the in vivo OrthoLoad database, the pipeline matches the accuracy of subject-specific musculoskeletal simulations ($0.32 \pm 0.08$ BW RMSE for hip; $0.23 \pm 0.03$ BW for knee) and resolves peak force changes smaller than those reported for gait retraining and osteoarthritis progression. Applied zero-shot to an independent instrumented cohort, it rivals or outperforms prior published methods. Even without curated activity labels, video features alone preserve accuracy and enable end-to-end inference on raw footage. Driven by the predictor, a generative motion prior produces biomechanically plausible variants with reduced peak loading, rediscovering strategies from the predictive simulation literature. This pipeline establishes uncalibrated monocular video as a viable modality for estimating joint loading, opening a path toward retrospective analysis of archived clinical recordings, primary-care screening, and at-home rehabilitation tracking.

AI.tldr: A physics-free monocular-video pipeline predicts hip and knee joint contact forces with accuracy comparable to subject-specific simulations, using a transformer-based model (V-JEPA 2) trained on uncalibrated video, enabling end-to-end inference without markers or force plates.
AI.motivation: Noninvasive estimation of joint loading to study OA, rehabilitation, and implant longevity; address invasiveness and limited subject samples in instrumented implant datasets.
AI.method: Recover per-frame parametric body meshes; encode as kinematic features; decode into forces via transformer with pose modulation by body shape, joint, side, activity text and self-supervised tokens; unified hip/knee model; leave-one-subject-out cross-validation on 26 patients and 25 activities from the OrthoLoad database; zero-shot generalization to an independent instrumented cohort; end-to-end inference on raw video; no curated activity labels required; generative motion prior to propose biomechanically plausible variants.
AI.result: HIP RMSE: 0.32 ± 0.08 BW; KNEE RMSE: 0.23 ± 0.03 BW. Peak force changes resolved smaller than those typical in gait retraining and OA progression. Zero-shot to independent cohort rivals or outperforms prior methods. End-to-end inference on raw monocular video; video features alone retain accuracy. Generative motion prior yields plausible variants with reduced peak loading, aligning with predictive simulation literature.
AI.conclusion: Uncalibrated monocular video is a viable modality to estimate hip and knee joint loading, enabling retrospective analysis of archives, primary-care screening, and at-home rehabilitation tracking; unifies hip and knee estimation in a single model; opens avenues for large-scale, noninvasive biomechanical analyses.

title: SCOUT: Semantic scene COverage via Uncertainty-guided Traversal
comment: 2026 ICRA Workshop on Uncertainty in Open World Robotics
summary: Robots that operate over extended periods should not merely visit space; they should progressively understand it. Yet most 3D scene graph pipelines treat perception as a post-processing stage over a fixed dataset, decoupling scene representation from the decisions that determine what is observed in the first place. We present SCOUT, an online semantic exploration framework that closes this loop by coupling active traversal with probabilistic scene graph construction. Given a prior 2D occupancy map and posed RGB-D observations, SCOUT incrementally builds an uncertainty-aware 3D scene graph whose nodes maintain fused geometry and posterior beliefs over open-vocabulary object labels, while edges encode structural relations such as on, inside, belong, and next to. These beliefs are fed back to an uncertainty-guided traversal planner, which selects viewpoints by balancing expected semantic certainty gain, geometric coverage gain, and travel cost. In this way, the robot revisits ambiguous objects when additional evidence matters and expands into unseen free space when the scene remains incomplete. The resulting system treats semantic scene completeness as an operational objective rather than a passive by-product of semantic mapping, moving toward autonomous agents that can patrol, update, and reason about evolving indoor environments with minimal human intervention.

AI.tldr: SCOUT integrates active viewpoint planning with online probabilistic 3D scene graph construction to achieve semantic completeness and efficient exploration in indoor environments.
AI.motivation: Conventional 3D scene graph pipelines treat perception as a post-processing stage on fixed data, decoupling scene representation from decision-making. There is a need for autonomous agents that can patrol, update, and reason about evolving indoor environments with minimal human input, and to treat semantic completeness as an operational objective rather than a passive by-product. Open-vocabulary labeling is desired to generalize beyond predefined categories.
AI.method: From a prior 2D occupancy map and posed RGB-D observations, SCOUT incrementally builds an uncertainty-aware 3D scene graph. Nodes fuse geometry and posterior beliefs over open-vocabulary object labels; edges encode relations such as on, inside, belong, and next to. These beliefs drive an uncertainty-guided traversal planner that selects viewpoints by balancing expected semantic certainty gain, geometric coverage gain, and travel cost. The system revisits ambiguous objects to acquire more evidence and expands into unseen free space when the scene is incomplete, closing the loop between perception and planning.
AI.result: The framework enables a closed-loop perception-planning approach with semantic and geometric updates, allowing iterative refinement of both object labels and scene structure. It demonstrates qualitative capabilities for proactive exploration and mapping with minimal human intervention, toward autonomous operation in evolving indoor environments.
AI.conclusion: SCOUT closes the perception–planning loop by treating semantic completeness as an operational objective, moving toward autonomous agents that patrol, update, and reason about evolving indoor environments with minimal human intervention.

title: Generative Models Erode Human Temporal Learning Through Market Selection
summary: We argue that modern generative models create structural risks for knowledge and cultural production at current, sub-AGI capability levels. We define Human Temporal Learning (HTL) as path-dependent knowledge accumulation through sustained engagement with problems over time. Generative outputs increasingly resemble HTL-intensive work in surface features, so verifying whether a given output reflects genuine human learning grows costly relative to its expected benefit. Once verification loses economic justification, evaluators reward outputs regardless of production mode, and producers who invested years of learning compete on price against outputs that cost almost nothing to generate. We call this pathway value collapse and formalize it through a costly-inspection framework. Cross-domain evidence from academic publishing, legal practice, content platforms, and software security maps onto four stages of verification erosion. Alignment success is orthogonal. Better-aligned models narrow observable gaps between human and AI outputs, making source verification harder and intensifying competitive pressure against HTL-intensive work even when individual AI outputs improve.

AI.tldr: Generative models enable HTL-like outputs, making verification of genuine human learning expensive; this can trigger a costly-inspection dynamic and value collapse, where cheap AI outputs erode incentives for long-term human learning, with alignment not addressing this economic erosion.
AI.motivation: To understand how progress in generative AI could structurally destabilize the economics of knowledge and cultural production by privileging outputs over verified human learning, and to formalize a framework (costly inspection) to model verification erosion across domains.
AI.method: Conceptual synthesis across domains (academia, legal practice, content platforms, software security) and formalization of a costly-inspection framework; define Human Temporal Learning (HTL) as path-dependent learning; trace four stages of verification erosion and analyze how alignment interacts with these dynamics.
AI.result: Proposes a formal, cross-domain framework for verification erosion: HTL is increasingly costly to verify; four stages map how verification degrades; alignment reduces observable gaps but worsens source verification and intensifies competition against HTL-intensive work; predicts a pathway to value collapse under cheap AI generation.
AI.conclusion: Alignment alone cannot prevent the economic erosion of HTL-driven labor; policy and design must address verification costs and incentives, recognizing that better-aligned models may inadvertently intensify pressure on human-learning-based labor.

title: CARVE-Q: Quantum-Proposed, Classically Certified Interactive Driving Repair
summary: The critical question after a correct driving veto is not only whether a maneuver is unsafe, but whether the blocked interaction admits a lawful, auditable, and responsibility-bounded repair. Prediction and game-theoretic planners can suggest plausible cooperation, yet they do not return a proof that the repair respects hard rules, right-of-way, cost allocation, and ego fallback. We introduce CARVE, Certified Affordable Repair of Vetoed maneuvers via Envelopes, a certificate architecture for prediction-free interactive repair. Given a vetoed maneuver, CARVE constructs a finite repair lattice and emits a structured certificate recording the binding rule, selected joint repair, right-of-way-scaled cooperation envelope, responsibility-weighted cost split, and ego-only fallback. This certificate view reveals the algorithmic bottleneck: multi-owner repair induces a product lattice $M = \prod_j |\mathcal{A}_j|$. We therefore introduce CARVE-Q, a verifier-shielded quantum-AI search layer that applies quantum minimum finding only to this black-box lattice while leaving all safety authority classical. In the conservative verifier-oracle model, exact classical minimum finding requires $Θ(M)$ queries in the worst case, whereas Durr-Hoyer/Grover minimum finding uses $O(\sqrt{M})$ oracle queries with high probability. We prove verifier-shielded certificate soundness, priority non-elicitation, black-box query separation, and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility. We then demonstrate state-vector minimum finding on CARVE repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments and validate certificate preservation on Lanelet2-grounded INTERACTION replay with 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives. The result is a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern for certified autonomy: quantum proposes; CARVE certifies.

AI.tldr: CARVE provides a certificate-based framework for repairing vetoed maneuvers with auditable rules, using a finite repair lattice and certificates; CARVE‑Q adds verifier-shielded quantum minimum finding on a product lattice to efficiently identify joint repairs, with soundness guarantees and empirical validation on Lanelet2 INTERACTION.
AI.motivation: Ensure that vetoed autonomous driving maneuvers are repaired in a manner that is lawful, auditable, and cost-responsibility bounded, going beyond mere prediction/planning to provable compliance with hard rules and rights-of-way.
