AI.method: Construct a finite repair lattice for a vetoed maneuver; emit a structured certificate detailing the binding rule, joint repair, right-of-way envelope, cost split, and ego fallback. Analyze the combinatorial product lattice M = ∏_j |A_j|. Introduce CARVE-Q, a verifier-shielded quantum search layer that applies quantum minimum finding to the lattice while keeping safety authority classical. In a verifier-oracle model, compare Θ(M) classical queries with O(√M) quantum queries (Durr–Hoyer/Grover). Prove certificate soundness, non-elicitation, black-box query separation, and finite-precision reversible-oracle constructibility. Demonstrate state-vector minimum finding on up to 65,536 assignments and validate certificate preservation on Lanelet2 INTERACTION replay with perfect right-of-way, blame consistency, and no priority false positives.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration of state-vector minimum finding on repair oracles up to 65,536 assignments; certificate-preservation validation showing 100% right-of-way respect, 100% blame consistency, and zero priority false positives in Lanelet2 INTERACTION replay; establishes a trust-bounded quantum-AI pattern: quantum proposes; CARVE certifies.
AI.conclusion: CARVE and CARVE‑Q offer a rigorously auditable framework for legally compliant, certificate-backed repair of vetoed autonomous maneuvers, leveraging quantum search to scale to combinatorial repair spaces while preserving classical safety authority.

title: Inside the Visual Mind: Neuroscience-Motivated Concept Circuits for Interpreting and Steering Vision Transformers
comment: In Proceedings of the International Conference on Machine Learning, 2026. (acceptance rate 26.6%)
summary: Despite high accuracy, Vision Transformer (ViT) predictions can be driven by spurious cues, raising the need to understand their inner workings before safe deployment. Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) provide a promising lens for decomposing model representations into human-interpretable concepts, yet adapting SAE-based interpretation to ViTs remains challenging due to limited control over concept coverage and subjective, non-scalable feature interpretation. To fill the gaps, motivated by neuroscience-inspired principles, we propose ViSAE, a mechanistic interpretability toolbox for understanding ViT inner workings through concept circuits. ViSAE consists of three components: (1) A probing suite with 64K images and a 16K visually grounded concept vocabulary, improving concept coverage efficiency by 20x over ImageNet and interpretation accuracy by 28.7% over existing concept sets. (2) Top-down concept reading and Bottom-up circuit tracing algorithms that automatically recover ViT inner workings via concept circuits. (3) Applications for auditing and steering ViT behavior. Through concept editing, ViSAE improves the worst-group accuracy on WaterBirds by 48.2%, outperforming existing methods by 23.8%. Our data and code: https://github.com/deep-real/ViSAE.

AI.tldr: ViSAE is a mechanistic interpretability toolbox for ViTs that uses a 64K-image probing suite and a 16K concept vocabulary to recover inner workings via concept circuits, enabling auditing and steering of ViT behavior.
AI.motivation: ViTs can rely on spurious cues; existing interpretability with sparse autoencoders has limited concept coverage and scalability. A neuroscience-inspired, mechanistic approach is needed to understand ViT representations and ensure safe deployment.
AI.method: Three components: (1) probing suite with 64K images and a 16K visually grounded concept vocabulary to improve concept coverage and interpretation accuracy; (2) Top-down concept reading and Bottom-up circuit tracing algorithms to automatically recover ViT inner workings via concept circuits; (3) Applications for auditing and steering ViT behavior, including concept editing.
AI.result: ViSAE achieves 20x improvement in concept coverage efficiency over ImageNet and 28.7% higher interpretation accuracy over existing concept sets. It enables automatic recovery of ViT inner workings via concept circuits and improves worst-group accuracy on WaterBirds by 48.2%, outperforming existing methods by 23.8%. Code and data are available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: ViSAE provides a practical, neuroscience-inspired toolkit for auditing, understanding, and steering ViT behavior through concept circuits, addressing spurious cues and enhancing model robustness.

title: IDDMBSE: Integrating Data-Driven and Model-Based Systems Engineering for Trusted Autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems
comment: 9 pages, 11 figures. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Autonomous cyber-physical systems (CPS) sit at the intersection of Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and data-driven Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence (ML/AI), yet no integrated Systems Engineering (SE) methodology natively spans both. We address this gap with IDDMBSE, an Integrated Data-Driven and Model-Based Systems Engineering methodology that extends the rigorous MBSE V-process with a data-driven loop at every step, anchored in SysML, the autonomy stack, and a hybrid model-based plus data-driven trade-off architecture. We instantiate IDDMBSE as an interoperable, open-source tool chain: PERFECT, which maps SysML system architectures to executable ROS autonomy stacks for scalable performance evaluation; TRADES-X, which decomposes design-space exploration into a model-based optimization stage followed by a data-driven evaluation stage; and VERITAS, which combines formal, data-driven, and runtime verification into a single assurance workflow. We demonstrate IDDMBSE on a Trusted Autonomous Ground Robot across its development lifecycle, spanning sensor-suite selection, risk-sensitive path planning, behavior-tree task verification, conformal-prediction-based robust perception, and assured multi-robot coordination, all exercised in a contested-terrain Isaac Sim test range that we release with the tool chain. We close by sketching how IDDMBSE is being re-formulated on SysML v2 / KerML foundations to enable language-native composability and tighter ML/AI integration.

AI.tldr: IDDMBSE is a data-driven extension of MBSE for autonomous CPS, via an integrated tool chain (PERFECT, TRADES-X, VERITAS) enabling SysML-based design, model-based optimization, data-driven evaluation, and formal/runtime verification; demonstrated on a Trusted Autonomous Ground Robot with multi-domain capabilities; future reformulation on SysML v2/KerML for native composability.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between rigorous MBSE processes and data-driven ML/AI in autonomous CPS; need an integrated, verifiable SE methodology that can handle design, evaluation, and assurance across data- and model-based components.
AI.method: Propose IDDMBSE framework augmenting the MBSE V-process with an iterative data-driven loop at each step, anchored in SysML and the autonomy stack; instantiate as open-source tool chain: PERFECT (SysML-to-ROS mapping for evaluation), TRADES-X (model-based optimization then data-driven evaluation), VERITAS (formal, data-driven, runtime verification). Demonstrate on a Trusted Autonomous Ground Robot across lifecycle tasks and release a test range.
AI.result: Validated across sensor-suite selection, risk-sensitive path planning, behavior-tree verification, conformal-prediction-based perception, and multi-robot coordination within a contested-terrain simulation; released Isaac Sim test range with the tool chain for external use.
AI.conclusion: IDDMBSE can bridge MBSE and ML/AI in autonomous CPS and is being reformulated on SysML v2 / KerML to enable native composability and tighter ML/AI integration.

title: Skip a Layer or Loop It? Learning Program-of-Layers in LLMs
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Substantially extends arXiv:2507.07996. Code: https://github.com/tianyi-lab/PoLar
summary: Large language models (LLMs) perform inference by following a fixed depth and order, non-recurrent execution of all layers. We reveal the wide existence of training-free, flexible, dynamic program-of-layers (PoLar), where pretrained layers can be packed as modules and then skipped or looped to form a customized program for each input. For most inputs, substantially shorter program executions can achieve the same or better accuracy, while incorrect predictions of the original LLM can be corrected by alternative programs with fewer layers. These observations indicate that inference admits multiple valid latent computations beyond the standard forward pass. To efficiently achieve PoLar in practice, we propose a lightweight PoLar prediction network, which learns to generate execution programs that dynamically skip or repeat pretrained layers for each input. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods, often while executing fewer layers, and that these gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation. Our results suggest that fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of an LLM's latent reasoning capacity.

AI.tldr: Dynamic program-of-layers (PoLar) enables input-dependent execution of pretrained layers via a lightweight predictor, skipping or looping layers. This can match or improve accuracy with fewer layers, revealing multiple latent computations beyond the standard fixed-depth forward pass.
AI.motivation: Challenge the notion that LLM inference must follow a fixed-depth, fixed-order forward pass. By enabling dynamic, modular execution, exploit latent computations to improve accuracy and efficiency and enhance robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a PoLar prediction network that outputs execution programs (which layers to skip or repeat) for each input, treating pretrained layers as modular units. The approach is evaluated on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, comparing against standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods.
AI.result: PoLar consistently improves accuracy over standard inference and prior dynamic-depth methods, often with fewer layers. Gains persist under out-of-distribution evaluation, suggesting a broader latent reasoning capacity than fixed-depth execution.
AI.conclusion: Inference in LLMs supports multiple valid latent computations beyond the standard forward pass; fixed-depth execution captures only a narrow subset of latent reasoning. PoLar is a promising direction for efficient, flexible inference.

title: Position: Don't Just "Fix it in Post": A Science of AI Must Study Training Dynamics
comment: Accepted as an oral to the ICML: https://icml.cc/virtual/2026/poster/67142
summary: What would it mean to have a scientific understanding of AI? Models are not static objects: they are snapshots of time-evolving processes shaped by data, objectives, architectures, and optimization dynamics. Yet much of AI research treats models as fixed artifacts, analyzing behaviors after training rather than asking why they emerge. This position paper argues that a science of AI must move beyond post-hoc fixes and study the training dynamics that produce model behavior. Such a science should support progressively stronger forms of understanding: predicting outcomes from early training signals, intervening when trajectories go wrong, and ultimately designing training procedures that more reliably produce desired properties. Scaling laws have made prediction routine for loss; the challenge is extending this success to capabilities, biases, robustness, and safety-relevant behaviors. We articulate requirements for such theories grounded in the history and philosophy of science, examine progress in mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias, and identify concrete open problems.

AI.tldr: Advocates a training-dynamics–driven science of AI, moving beyond post-hoc analyses to predictive, interventionist, and design-oriented understanding of model behavior; aims to extend scaling-law success from loss to capabilities, biases, and safety.
AI.motivation: Models are time-evolving processes shaped by data, objectives, architectures, and optimization; a science of AI must study training dynamics rather than treating models as fixed artifacts to enable prediction, intervention, and design.
AI.method: Proposes a theory-building framework grounded in the history and philosophy of science; surveys progress in mechanistic interpretability, fairness, memorization, and simplicity bias; articulates progressively stronger notions of understanding and outlines concrete open problems.
AI.result: Offers a roadmap for a science of AI that connects learning dynamics to emergent behaviors, identifying where current progress supports the vision and where gaps remain; frames concrete open problems to extend predictive power to safety-relevant properties.
AI.conclusion: A rigorous, dynamics-focused theory is needed to reliably predict, intervene in, and design training procedures that produce desired AI properties, extending scaling-law success to capabilities and safety.

title: Architecture-Adaptive Uncertainty Fusion for Deepfake Detection
summary: Deepfake detection systems achieve near-perfect accuracy on benchmarks, yet forensic deployment demands reliable prediction uncertainty. Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods rely on single sources and ignore that optimal uncertainty composition varies across architectures. We propose Correlation-Optimized Fusion (COF), an architecture-adaptive framework that fuses five complementary uncertainty sources -- epistemic, aleatoric, calibration, conformal, and distributional -- by maximizing Pearson correlation between fused uncertainty scores and prediction errors via constrained optimization on the probability simplex. COF requires no model modifications and only 42 s of weight optimization, compared to 20--45 h for a 5-model Deep Ensemble. Evaluation across eleven architectures on FaceForensics++ reveals a fundamental trade-off: under matched train/evaluation protocol, non-linear methods achieve approximately 5--6% higher in-domain correlation than COF (mean r = 0.438), but this reverses under distribution shift. On CelebDF, COF outperforms Random Forest in 9/11 architectures with up to 7.3x higher correlation (MaxViT-B: r = 0.249 vs. 0.034); RF degrades 85% cross-domain to r = 0.071, whereas COF retains substantially more signal (74% drop to r = 0.116). Cross-dataset evaluation on CelebDF and DFDC reveals catastrophic generalization failure across all methods: in-domain correlations of 0.41--0.47 collapse to near-zero externally (mean degradation 90.7%), with seven of eleven architectures exhibiting uncertainty inversion. These results establish COF as a practical, interpretable framework for controlled-distribution deployment and identify domain-adaptive UQ as the central open challenge for forensic deployment.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, architecture-adaptive uncertainty fusion (COF) that combines five UQ sources to align with detection errors; robust under distribution shift but domain generalization remains problematic.
AI.motivation: Reliable prediction uncertainty is essential for forensic deployment of deepfake detectors. Existing UQ methods are often single-source and architecture-fixed, limiting effectiveness across models and under distribution shift.
AI.method: Correlation-Optimized Fusion (COF) fuses epistemic, aleatoric, calibration, conformal, and distributional uncertainty. It optimizes the Pearson correlation between fused uncertainty scores and prediction errors via constrained optimization on the probability simplex, requires no model changes, and is fast (~42 seconds) compared with training a 5-model Deep Ensemble.
AI.result: COF shows mixed in-domain performance: non-linear methods slightly outperform COF on FaceForensics++ under matched protocol (mean r ~0.438 for COF vs higher for non-linear methods by ~5–6%), but COF gains under distribution shift. On CelebDF, COF outperforms Random Forest in 9/11 architectures with up to 7.3x higher correlation (e.g., MaxViT-B: r=0.249 vs 0.034); RF degrades cross-domain to r=0.071, while COF to 0.116 (74% drop). Cross-dataset evaluation between CelebDF and DFDC shows catastrophic generalization: in-domain r 0.41–0.47 collapses to near-zero externally (mean degradation ~90.7%), with seven of eleven architectures showing uncertainty inversion.
AI.conclusion: COF is a practical, interpretable framework for controlled-distribution deployment in forensic UQ and demonstrates that domain-adaptive UQ is the central open challenge for robust forensic deployment.

title: AxisGuide: Grounding Robot Action Coordinate System in RGB Observations for Robust Visuomotor Manipulation
comment: Accepted to Robotics: Science and Systems (RSS) 2026
summary: Visuomotor manipulation policies trained via large-scale behavior cloning have achieved strong semantic scene understanding, yet often fail to reliably execute correct low-level actions under distribution shifts. For example, even in a simple pickup task with identical scene layouts, camera viewpoints, and illumination, performance can degrade substantially when the object is placed at unseen locations. We argue that this gap arises from insufficient action understanding, namely the inability to interpret the robot's base-frame action coordinate system in image space. To address this issue, we introduce AxisGuide, a lightweight guidance method that bridges semantic scene understanding and action-coordinate interpretation. Using camera parameters and end-effector poses, AxisGuide renders the robot base-frame axes in each camera view and augments RGB observations with a small set of cue channels that explicitly visualize the meaning of the +x, +y, and +z motions in image space. Extensive evaluations in both the LIBERO simulation and real-world environments demonstrate that AxisGuide yields substantial performance gains and improved generalization, highlighting the effectiveness of explicit action-coordinate cues for learning reliable and transferable generalist visuomotor policies.

AI.tldr: AxisGuide augments visuomotor policies with explicit base-frame action cues by rendering robot axis in camera views and adding cue channels, improving generalization under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Identify mismatch between semantic scene understanding and low-level action interpretation as root cause of degradation under distribution shifts; need explicit image-space encoding of action coordinates.
AI.method: Render base-frame axes using camera parameters and end-effector poses; augment RGB with cue channels for +x, +y, +z in image space; apply within LIBERO simulation and real-world tasks; evaluate on pickup tasks with unseen object locations.
AI.result: Substantial performance gains and improved generalization across sim and real environments; AxisGuide yields more reliable actions under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: Explicit action-coordinate cues effectively bridge semantics and action interpretation, enabling more transferable generalist visuomotor policies.

title: Gaussian Process Latent Factor Regression for Low-Data, High-Dimensional Output Problems
comment: 9 pages content + 22 pages appendix/references. Supporting code at https://github.com/edstevenson/GPLFR
summary: In the sciences, regression tasks often require predicting high-dimensional outputs from few training examples. Multi-output Gaussian processes excel in low-data regimes but typically struggle with high-dimensional outputs. Compress-then-predict pipelines such as PCA-GP (principal component analysis plus Gaussian process regression) handle high dimensionality, but rely on bases optimized for reconstruction rather than prediction. To address this gap, we propose a model that represents each output as a linear-Gaussian decoding of a low-dimensional latent state drawn from a Gaussian process prior. By analytically marginalizing the decoder weights, we couple compression and prediction in a single objective that scales to high-dimensional outputs. We refer to this model as Gaussian process latent factor regression (GPLFR). We demonstrate GPLFR by building the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets.

AI.tldr: A GPLFR model unifies dimensionality reduction and regression by treating outputs as linear-Gaussian decodings of a latent GP state, enabling scalable high-dimensional predictions.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where multi-output GPs excel in low-data regimes but struggle with high-dimensional outputs; traditional compression methods optimize reconstruction rather than predictive accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce Gaussian process latent factor regression: a latent state drawn from a GP prior is decoded linearly with Gaussian weights to obtain outputs; by analytically marginalizing the decoder, couple compression and prediction in a single objective that scales to high-dimensional outputs.
AI.result: Demonstrated GPLFR by building the first spatially resolved emulator of global climate models for rocky exoplanets, showing scalability to high-dimensional output spaces.
AI.conclusion: GPLFR integrates compression and predictive modeling in one objective, addressing high-dimensional outputs more effectively than reconstruction-focused methods like PCA-GP.

title: Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT): Fully Realising a GPU-based Symmetric Pseudo-Boolean SAT Solver
summary: We present Accelerated Fourier SAT (AFSAT), a GPU-accelerated solver for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability based on continuous local search (CLS). AFSAT realises the proof-of-concept approach, FastFourierSAT, into a fully-engineered solver supporting any heterogeneous mixture of symmetric constraint types and lengths within a single problem instance. Using the JAX compiler, AFSAT leverages pure function composition, automatic vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and just-in-time (JIT) compilation to perform massively parallel CLS across batches of candidate assignments. We demonstrate substantially improved numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency over the proof-of-concept. We achieve this by way of identifying and addressing various limitations that arise from memory latency and floating-point representation, as well as leveraging automatic parallelisation and compact representations. The inherent representational and stability limitations of floating point are partially addressed by a tailored discrete Fourier transform implementation. We achieve near-linear throughput when scaling to multiple accelerators via JAX array sharding.

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated solver AF SAT (AFSAT) for pseudo-Boolean satisfiability using continuous local search; extends FastFourierSAT with JAX-based, scalable GPU parallelism; addresses numerical stability and memory efficiency via discrete Fourier transform; achieves near-linear scaling across accelerators.
AI.motivation: To provide a practical, scalable solver for PB constraints with heterogeneous types and lengths, overcoming limitations of the proof-of-concept and leveraging GPU acceleration and Fourier-based numerical techniques.
AI.method: Implement continuous local search in a GPU-accelerated pipeline using JAX: pure function composition, vectorisation, automatic differentiation, and JIT; batch processing of candidate assignments; discrete Fourier transform to mitigate floating-point issues; array sharding for multi-accelerator scaling; support for mixed constraint types and lengths.
AI.result: Substantial improvements in numerical stability, runtime performance, and memory efficiency relative to FastFourierSAT; near-linear throughput as accelerators scale via JAX sharding.
AI.conclusion: AFSAT demonstrates a viable, scalable GPU-based solver for PB-SAT using CLS and Fourier-based methods, with effective handling of floating-point constraints and heterogeneous constraints across large problem instances.

title: JA-SIREN: Deterministic Initialization for Sinusoidal Networks via Spectral Matching
summary: Existing implicit neural representation (INR) approaches suffer from stochastic initialization that does not guarantee consistent or high-quality performance across runs, with variations reaching more than 2.5 dB (78%) in image regression. This variation is problematic for scientific computing and simulation, where result reproducibility is crucial. To address this problem, we present Jacobi-Anger Sinusoidal Representation Network (JA-SIREN), a deterministic initialization scheme for sinusoidal networks grounded in classical spectral analysis. By computing the Discrete Sine Transform (DST) of the target signal and leveraging the Jacobi-Anger expansion, we derive closed-form weights for a two-layer sinusoidal MLP that analytically match the network's initial spectral response to the target signal, requiring no random seed or additional hyperparameter tuning. On the Kodak dataset, JA-SIREN achieves a mean PSNR of 67.18 dB, a 21.30 dB improvement over the best baseline. This is achieved with zero run-to-run variance, confirming that spectrally-informed initialization is a more effective and reproducible alternative to stochastic initialization for sinusoidal INRs.

AI.tldr: JA-SIREN introduces a deterministic, spectrally-informed initialization for sinusoidal INRs, using DST and Jacobi-Anger expansion to match target spectral content, yielding zero run-to-run variance and large PSNR gains on Kodak.
AI.motivation: Stochastic initialization in implicit neural representations causes non-reproducible performance across runs, problematic for scientific computing where reproducibility is essential.
AI.method: Compute the Discrete Sine Transform of the target signal and use the Jacobi-Anger expansion to derive closed-form weights for a two-layer sinusoidal MLP that match the desired spectral response without random seeds or extra hyperparameters.
AI.result: On Kodak dataset, achieves mean PSNR of 67.18 dB, a 21.30 dB improvement over the best baseline, with zero run-to-run variance.
AI.conclusion: Spectrally-informed, deterministic initialization provides more reproducible and effective performance for sinusoidal INRs than stochastic initialization.

title: Multi-Robot Planning and Control from CCTV Camera Networks in a Real Warehouse
summary: Off-board control of mobile robots from cameras embedded in the environment offers a practical path to scalable autonomy, moving sensing and compute off the robots. We extend this idea from the single-robot case to coordinated fleets in a real warehouse, driving multiple robots with only a distributed CCTV network and edge compute. The system operates entirely in image space over an uncalibrated, pixel-wise topological camera graph, enabling wide-area operation with flexible camera placement. A hierarchical planner selects a camera sequence per robot and plans its image-space motion through each view, coordinating robots with a prioritised-then-joint strategy and treating overlapping camera regions as shared resources held by one robot at a time to prevent collisions and deadlocks. We validate the approach in a real warehouse with four robots and 30 cameras across six 27 m aisles, reporting mission times and coordination statistics. To our knowledge, this is the first field demonstration of multi-robot planning and coordination using only an external camera network and off-board compute, with robots carrying no task-specific navigation hardware.

AI.tldr: Demonstrates scalable, field-tested multi-robot coordination using only an external CCTV network and edge compute, operating in image space on an uncalibrated topological camera graph; a hierarchical planner manages per-robot camera sequences and resource sharing, validated in a real warehouse with four robots and 30 cameras.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable autonomy by off-loading sensing and computation from robots to an environmental camera network; extend single-robot off-board control to coordinated fleets without task-specific onboard navigation hardware; address real-world warehouse deployment with uncalibrated camera graphs.
AI.method: Operate entirely in image space on a pixel-wise topological camera graph via distributed CCTV and edge compute. Use a hierarchical planner to assign a camera sequence per robot, plan image-space motion through views, and coordinate with a prioritised-then-joint strategy. Treat overlapping camera regions as shared resources held by one robot at a time to avoid collisions/deadlocks.
AI.result: Field demonstration in a real warehouse with four robots and 30 cameras across six aisles; reports mission times and coordination statistics; demonstrates feasibility of multi-robot planning and coordination using only external cameras and off-board compute without robot navigation hardware.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility and practicality of external-camera-based multi-robot autonomy for warehouse environments, enabling scalable coordination without onboard navigation hardware and with uncalibrated topological camera graphs; lays groundwork for broader deployment.

title: Principles and Practice of Deep Representation Learning: or a Mathematical Theory of Memory
comment: version 2; TeX source and supplementary material at https://ma-lab-berkeley.github.io/deep-representation-learning-book/
summary:
In the current era of deep learning and especially generative models, there is significant investment in training very large generative models. Thus far, such models have been "black boxes" that are difficult to understand in the sense that they have opaque internal mechanisms, leading to difficulties in interpretability, reliability, and control. Naturally, this lack of understanding has led to both hype and fear.
  This book is an attempt to "open the black box" and understand the mechanisms of large deep networks, through the perspective of representation learning, which is a major factor - arguably the single most important one - in the empirical power of deep learning models. A brief outline of this book is as follows. Chapter 1 will summarize the threads that underlie the whole text. Chapters 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 will explain the design principles of modern neural network architectures through optimization and information theory, reducing the process of architecture development (long having been described as a sort of "alchemy") to undergraduate-level linear algebra and calculus exercises once the underlying principles are introduced. Chapters 7 and 8 will discuss applications of these principles to solve problems in more paradigmatic ways, obtaining new methods and models which are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, and yet no less - sometimes even more - powerful than the black-box models they resemble. Chapter 9 will discuss potential future directions for deep learning, the role of representation learning, as well as some open problems.

AI.tldr: A book that demystifies large deep networks by grounding them in representation learning, using optimization and information theory to convert architecture design from an art to principled practice, with a chapter-driven agenda toward interpretable, controllable, and efficient models.
AI.motivation: To address opacity and mistrust in large generative models by providing a principled, representation-learning-based framework that makes architectures interpretable, reliable, and controllable.
AI.method: Present a unified design principle for modern neural networks anchored in optimization and information theory; reduce architecture development to undergraduate-level linear algebra/calculus; structure content across chapters to connect theory (principles) with practical applications.
AI.result: A structured expository framework (nine chapters) that outlines design principles and applications leading to models that are efficient, interpretable, and controllable by design, potentially surpassing black-box counterparts in some settings.
AI.conclusion: The work argues for the central role of representation learning in future deep learning progress, outlines future directions and open problems, and envisions architectures built from first-principles rather than alchemy.

title: A Study of Parallel Continuous Local Search
summary: We study parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) as a solution approach for Boolean satisfiability problems with symmetric pseudo-Boolean (PB) constraints. Here, the $n$-variable PB-satisfiability problem is relaxed to a continuous optimisation problem with a differentiable objective function on an $n$-dimensional hypercube. For satisfiable instances, the global minimisers of this optimisation problem correspond to satisfying assignments of the SAT problem at hand. We present several novel findings via empirical experiments: (i) redundant constraints can inhibit rather than accelerate convergence; (ii) CLS shows promise as a sub-solver in hybridised settings, quickly completing partial assignments; and (iii) local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality (i.e., degree of satisfaction), due to saddle-dense objectives where additional solver steps yield diminishing returns. Our findings inform practical uses of CLS for SAT on modern accelerator hardware.

AI.tldr: Parallel Continuous Local Search (CLS) applied to Boolean satisfiability with symmetric pseudo-Boolean constraints via a continuous relaxation on the n-dimensional hypercube. Empirical study reveals (1) redundant constraints can hinder convergence, (2) CLS can serve as a fast sub-solver in hybrid architectures, and (3) local search quickly stabilizes into a distribution of solution quality due to saddle-dense objective landscapes, with implications for accelerator-based implementations.
AI.motivation: Investigate the viability and practical characteristics of parallel CLS as a solution approach for PB-SAT problems, and to assess its behavior and potential benefits on modern accelerator hardware.
AI.method: Relax PB-SAT to a differentiable continuous optimisation problem on the hypercube; use parallel CLS to optimize the objective; conduct empirical experiments to observe the effects of redundant constraints, sub-solver utility, and convergence dynamics; evaluate convergence behavior and solution quality distribution.
AI.result: Key empirical findings: redundant constraints can slow convergence; CLS is effective as a quick sub-solver for partial assignments; local search rapidly converges to a stable distribution of solution quality due to saddle-dense objectives and diminishing returns from further solver steps.
AI.conclusion: CLS shows promise as a scalable approach for SAT with PB constraints, with practical design considerations for accelerator-based implementations; redundant constraints should be managed, and CLS can serve as a fast component in hybrid solvers, leveraging the rapid stabilization of solution quality in this problem class.

title: Adaptive Band Selection for Hyperspectral Classification with Spatially Disjoint Evaluation
summary: Hyperspectral band selection methods based on differentiable selectors can be sensitive to initialization and to extracting a final discrete subset, while prescribed band counts limit flexibility. We propose SGBR-HC (Spectral-Group Band Ranking with Hard-Concrete initialization), a two-stage method that uses a supervised spectral ranking to initialize trainable sparse gates rather than treating ranking as a fixed selection rule, letting the number of selected bands be determined by training. Stage-1 scores candidate bands from training pixels by class discriminability and spectral diversity; this ranking seeds the gate logits for Stage-2, which trains the sparse gates jointly with a spatial classifier. Under spatially disjoint evaluation on Pavia University and Houston 2013, verified by retraining a fresh classifier on the selected bands, SGBR-HC achieves the highest mean overall accuracy and Cohen's kappa with approximately twenty bands. Bypassing Stage-1 degrades OA by 8.84 pp on Pavia University and 22.15 pp on Houston 2013, confirming the ranking prior's role. Random pixel splits inflate OA on Pavia University by 30.56 pp, underscoring spatial leakage as a critical evaluation confound.

AI.tldr: A two-stage hyperspectral band selection method (SGBR-HC) uses a supervised spectral ranking to initialize differentiable sparse gates and jointly trains them with a spatial classifier, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy with ~20 bands while revealing the importance of a ranking prior and proper evaluation to avoid spatial leakage.
AI.motivation: To overcome sensitivity of differentiable band selectors to initialization and to avoid fixing a discrete subset of bands a priori; allow the selected band count to be determined by training while maintaining high discriminability.
AI.method: Stage-1: compute a supervised spectral ranking based on class discriminability and spectral diversity to seed gate logits. Stage-2: train differentiable sparse gates (Hard-Concrete) jointly with a spatial classifier, enabling the number of selected bands to be determined during training.
AI.result: Under disjoint spatial evaluation on Pavia University and Houston 2013, with retraining a classifier on the selected bands, SGBR-HC achieves the highest mean OA and Cohen’s kappa with ~20 bands. Ablating Stage-1 reduces OA by 8.84 pp (Pavia) and 22.15 pp (Houston). Random pixel splits inflate OA by 30.56 pp on Pavia, indicating spatial leakage.
AI.conclusion: A ranking prior is crucial for reliable joint sparsification and performance; the proposed two-stage framework yields flexible band counts and robust results, while highlighting the need for careful spatially-disjoint evaluation to avoid leakage.

title: Learning All-Terrain Locomotion for a Planetary Rover with Actively Articulated Suspension
summary: This paper presents ERNEST, a four-wheeled planetary rover concept equipped with a two-degree-of-freedom Active Gimbal Suspension that combines yaw and roll actuation to enable wheel reconfiguration, steering, and active load redistribution. A single neural network controller, trained to track a desired path across challenging terrain, fully unlocks the capabilities of this actuated suspension system for autonomous obstacle negotiation. A reinforcement learning framework is developed using the high-fidelity DARTS simulation engine, which combines rigid-contact dynamics and Bekker-Wong terramechanics, enabling the emergence of locomotion strategies adapted to loose-soil conditions. To obtain a single unified controller across heterogeneous terrains, a policy consolidation strategy merges the experience of terrain-specialized agents into one neural network, eliminating the need for explicit terrain classification and controller switching. The resulting controller operates on a combination of proprioceptive and exteroceptive feedback, including sparse stereo-derived terrain elevation, chassis attitude, joint states, and force-torque measurements. Zero-shot transfer to the physical rover is achieved through domain randomization, sensor noise injection, and model-to-real system identification. Experimental results demonstrate autonomous traversal of rock fields, a bump trap, a wheel-high step, sand ripples, and sandy slopes. On a 20° sandy slope, the learned controller reduces the cost of transport by 37% on dry sand despite the additional actuation, and achieves superior performance on wet sand where the passive suspension becomes completely immobilized.

AI.tldr: A rover with an active gimbal suspension learns a single neural controller in high-fidelity terramechanics simulations to autonomously traverse diverse terrains, achieving zero-shot transfer to hardware and notable efficiency gains.
AI.motivation: Enable robust autonomous traversal over heterogeneous, loose-soil terrains without terrain-type classification or controller switching, and improve energy efficiency with active suspension.
AI.method: Design of ERNEST rover with 2-DOF active gimbal suspension (yaw and roll), a single neural network controller trained via reinforcement learning in the DARTS simulation environment that couples rigid-contact dynamics with Bekker–Wong terramechanics; policy consolidation to merge terrain-specialized policies into one policy; sensor suite combining proprioceptive and exteroceptive feedback; domain randomization and model-to-real identification for transfer.
AI.result: Autonomous traversal of rock fields, bump traps, wheel-high steps, sand ripples, and sandy slopes. On a 20° sandy slope, cost of transport on dry sand reduced by 37% despite extra actuation; superior performance on wet sand where passive suspension immobilizes.
AI.conclusion: A unified, terrain-agnostic controller trained in realistic simulations with an actively actuated suspension can achieve robust, efficient locomotion across diverse terrains and transfer to real hardware without terrain-specific controllers.

title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
comment: 28 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Code available at https://github.com/Jimmy110101013/fmscope
summary:
Objective. EEG foundation models (FMs) report strong accuracy on clinical resting-state EEG. However, high accuracy under subject-disjoint cross-validation remains ambiguous: it can reflect a genuine clinical biomarker, or subject-identity features that correlate with the label. We name this the Identity Trap and ask whether it can be diagnosed at the representation level before fine-tuning.
  Approach. We propose FMScope, a frozen-representation protocol packaging five diagnostics: variance decomposition, subject-axis erasure, aperiodic 1/f ablation, layer-wise label probing, and within-subject direction consistency. We apply it to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout: subject relation of label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker.
  Main results. (i) The Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null in 12/12 pairs, rising in all 12 under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 pp). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves label decoding where the label varies within subject (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). (ii) Aperiodic 1/f is one subject carrier: removing it drops the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. (iii) Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
  Significance. The Identity Trap is a physically-grounded instance of shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a measurable physiological component, and subject-disjoint splitting alone cannot rule it out. FMScope separates gains reflecting a biological marker from those reflecting subject identity.

AI.tldr: Identity Trap in EEG foundation models; FMScope diagnoses whether high subject-disjoint accuracy reflects biology or subject identity; frozen representations harbor strong subject cues, with aperiodic 1/f carrying identity; fine-tuning increases label-variance where cross-subject markers exist; FMScope separates biological markers from identity cues.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether high cross-subject performance in resting-state EEG foundation models arises from genuine clinical biomarkers or subject-specific cues, and diagnose representation-level causes prior to fine-tuning.
AI.method: FMScope protocol with five diagnostics: (1) variance decomposition, (2) subject-axis erasure, (3) aperiodic 1/f ablation, (4) layer-wise label probing, (5) within-subject direction consistency. Applied to three pretrained FMs (LaBraM, CBraMod, REVE) across four datasets in a 2x2 layout (label x presence of a consensus cross-subject EEG marker).
AI.result: Identity Trap is universal: frozen subject-variance is 13-89x a random null across 12/12 pairs, increasing for all 12 pairs under fine-tuning (+10 to +63 percentage points). This dominance is a removable linear axis: erasing it improves within-subject label decoding (+6 to +12 pp in primary cells; +4 to +27 pp across external cohorts). Aperiodic 1/f is a subject carrier: removing it reduces the subject probe by 9-19 pp on LaBraM and CBraMod. REVE saturates subject identity without measurable aperiodic dependence. Fine-tuning amplifies label-variance only in cells with a literature-established cross-subject marker.
AI.conclusion: The Identity Trap embodies shortcut learning: the preferred cue has a physiological component, and subject-disjoint splits cannot rule it out. FMScope provides a representation-level diagnostic to separate gains due to a true biological marker from those due to subject identity.

title: AEGIS: A Backup Reflex for Physical AI
summary: Long-horizon robot manipulation tends to fail gradually: one bad step degrades the state, and the policy spirals into a basin from which it cannot recover. The failure is often visible before it happens. We introduce AEGIS (Activation-probe Early-warning, Gated Inference Switching), a selective escalation method that uses a lightweight probe on a weak policy's frozen activations to detect high-risk steps while there is still time to act. When the probe flags a step, control switches to a stronger separate policy, but only for the steps that need it. On LIBERO-Spatial, AEGIS recovers 10.1% of the trajectories the weak policy alone loses, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for a random-trigger placebo. These gains are significant under one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment over three pre-registered contrasts: +5.4pp over blind escalation, p=8.5e-6; +5.0pp over random triggering, p=1.0e-4; paired-trajectory bootstrap CIs exclude zero. AEGIS activates the stronger policy on only 38% of steps, so the lever is timing rather than compute. The probe clears its precondition with an early-window AUROC of 0.764, 95% CI [0.70, 0.84], read from the weak-policy path over the first 30% of trajectory steps before any handoff. We pre-register the full analysis plan, including a conditional recovered-task-rate estimand and explicit kill criteria, and confirm the result on 700 common-random-number episodes per arm, with nA-fail=646.

AI.tldr: AEGIS uses a lightweight activation-probe to detect high-risk steps in long-horizon robotic manipulation and gate switching to a stronger policy, yielding timing-based gains with minimal compute; notable recovery improvements on LIBERO-Spatial and a pre-registered, robust evaluation.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon manipulation failures unfold gradually and are detectable before the catastrophe; early, selective escalation can prevent degraded states without overusing compute.
AI.method: Freeze a weak policy's activations and run a probe to predict high-risk steps; switch to a stronger policy only for those steps; pre-register the analysis plan; evaluate on LIBERO-Spatial with 700 common-random-number episodes per arm; compare against budget-matched blind escalation and random-trigger baselines using one-sided exact paired McNemar tests with Holm-Bonferroni adjustment; report AUROC, recovered-task-rate, and bootstrap CIs.
AI.result: AEGIS recovers 10.1% of trajectories lost by the weak policy, versus 4.6% for budget-matched blind escalation and 5.1% for random-trigger placebo. Activation occurs on 38% of steps. Early-window AUROC on the weak-policy path (first 30% of trajectory) is 0.764 (95% CI [0.70, 0.84]). Statistical tests show +5.4 percentage points over blind escalation (p=8.5e-6) and +5.0 pp over random triggering (p=1.0e-4); paired-trajectory bootstrap CIs exclude zero. Analysis was pre-registered with explicit kill criteria, and results replicated on 700 episodes per arm (nA-fail=646).
AI.conclusion: Selective, time-efficient escalation via a probe-driven gating mechanism yields tangible recovery gains with modest compute, supported by a preregistered, robust evaluation framework.

title: RigPAPR: Rig-Based Animation of Static Neural Point Clouds from a Fixed-Viewpoint Video
comment: An overview video is available at https://youtu.be/up3BwRHYWG8
summary: Static neural point reconstructions capture a subject at high fidelity from posed images. Given such a reconstruction, we aim to animate it to follow a monocular fixed-viewpoint driving video of the subject, whether captured or produced by image-to-video (I2V) generation, and to recover a rigged, re-posable 3D asset. Existing methods deform Gaussian splats through direct linear blend skinning (LBS) or mesh proxies, both of which are prone to joint-boundary artifacts under articulation, even with per-primitive corrections. We trace the artifact to the representation: each splat carries an individual shape calibrated in the canonical pose to tile with its neighbours. Under rigid LBS, each splat moves with its bone but cannot bend, so the canonical tiling breaks at joint boundaries into gaps and spikes. Proximity attention point rendering (PAPR) instead carries no per-primitive shape; each pixel is recomposed at render time from the deformed primitives' positions, so the surface re-forms naturally with the articulation. We present RigPAPR, which auto-rigs a static PAPR cloud and drives it under direct LBS from a single fixed-viewpoint video, without mesh proxy, pose-dependent correction, or category template. On synthetic subjects, RigPAPR matches the strongest baseline at the supervised view and exceeds mesh-based and Gaussian-splatting baselines at novel views by 3+dB PSNR, with cleaner joint-boundary renderings of both synthetic and real subjects.

AI.tldr: RigPAPR introduces a rendering approach that eliminates per-primitive shapes by recomposing pixels from deformed primitives at render time, enabling auto-rigged, LBS-driven animation from a single fixed-viewpoint video without meshes, and achieving stronger novel-view performance and cleaner joint boundaries than mesh-based or Gaussian-splat baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing static neural point reconstructions animated via LBS or mesh proxies suffer from joint-boundary artifacts because each splat has a fixed canonical shape that tilts and gaps at joints. A representation that avoids per-primitive shapes and re-forms the surface at render time should yield natural articulation without calibration or templates.
AI.method: Proximity Attention Point Rendering (PAPR) removes per-primitive shapes; rendering recomposes each pixel from the deformed primitives. RigPAPR auto-rigs a static PAPR cloud and animates it under direct LBS from a single fixed-viewpoint video, without mesh proxies, pose-dependent corrections, or category templates.
AI.result: On synthetic subjects, RigPAPR matches the strongest baseline at the supervised view and exceeds mesh-based and Gaussian-splatting baselines at novel views by 3+dB PSNR, with cleaner joint-boundary renderings for both synthetic and real subjects.
AI.conclusion: RigPAPR provides auto-rigged, artifact-resistant 3D avatars from static neural point clouds by leveraging render-time reassembly (PAPR) and LBS-driven animation, enabling high-fidelity articulation from monocular driving videos without mesh proxies.

title: Lane Change Trajectory Planning for Personalized Driving Comfort and Mobility Efficiency
comment: Accepted by the IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IEEE IV 2026), Detroit, MI, United States, June 22_25, 2026
summary: Lane changing entails simultaneous longitudinal and lateral motions that affect driving comfort and mobility efficiency. Because these motions are tightly coupled and subject to substantial inter-vehicle variability, trajectory planning for lane-change maneuvers is characterized by a highly personalized nature. This study proposes a neural network-driven planner that integrates a third-order polynomial trajectory generator with a learning module that infers optimal trajectory parameters across diverse driving conditions. Using a shared backbone with dual heads, one head ensures all-condition operational guarantees, while the other captures driver-specific preferences for comfort or mobility efficiency. A head-gated switching mechanism, realized through a statistical gate based on error-winner logistic regression, adaptively selects the appropriate head under varying driving conditions, which enables context-aware lane-change trajectory planning. Representative cases and Monte Carlo simulations show that the proposed planner achieves personalized comfort and mobility during lane changes, while the baseline ensures feasible trajectories under driving conditions where personalized data are insufficient or inaccessible.

AI.tldr: A neural network-based lane-change planner with a dual-head architecture and a head-switching gate delivers personalized comfort and mobility while maintaining feasibility, evaluated via representative cases and Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.motivation: Lane-change maneuvers exhibit tightly coupled longitudinal-lateral dynamics with substantial inter-vehicle variability, making personalized trajectory planning essential for comfort and efficiency, while guaranteeing feasible trajectories under diverse conditions.
AI.method: A neural-network-driven planner uses a shared backbone with two heads: (1) an all-condition feasibility head and (2) a driver-preference head for comfort or mobility efficiency. A head-gated switching mechanism, implemented via an error-winner logistic regression gate, selects the appropriate head based on driving conditions. A third-order polynomial trajectory generator provides the trajectory parameterization. The learning module infers optimal trajectory parameters across diverse conditions; evaluations include representative cases and Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: The planner achieves personalized comfort and mobility during lane changes, while a baseline ensures feasible trajectories when personalized data are unavailable or insufficient.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables context-aware lane-change trajectory planning by combining a dual-head learning architecture with a gate-based head selection and a polynomial trajectory generator, offering personalized performance with safety-feasible behavior across conditions; its effectiveness relies on data availability and gate reliability, suggesting directions for real-world validation and robustness improvements.

title: Capturing non-Markovian dynamics in non-equilibrium stochastic systems using flow matching
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, Accepted to 2026 Conference on Physics and AI (PAI26)
summary: Hydrodynamic models of stochastic particle systems represented by coarse-grained stochastic partial differential equations (SPDE), such as the regularized Dean-Kawasaki (DK) equation, do not accurately capture the short-time system dynamics that is dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where the distributions are highly non-Gaussian. We develop a generative flow matching method that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations that explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects. As a demonstration, we use this method to simulate the Kramers first passage time problem for a system of non-interacting Brownian particles. We show the model accurately captures the short-time behavior and provides better predictions of the statistical moments of the number density when compared against the solution of the Markovian baseline, regularized DK equation.

AI.tldr: Generative flow-matching framework to model non-Markovian, non-Gaussian flux distributions from particle simulations; demonstrates improved short-time dynamics and density-moment predictions over a regularized Dean-Kawasaki SPDE for Kramers first-passage in non-interacting Brownian particles.
AI.motivation: Hydrodynamic SPDE closures (e.g., Dean–Kawasaki) inadequately capture short-time dynamics dominated by non-Markovian effects and low-density, non-Gaussian regimes; a data-driven approach is needed to faithfully represent flux statistics.
AI.method: Introduce a generative flow-matching model that learns the probability distribution of particle fluxes from simulations, explicitly incorporating non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects in the learned dynamics.
AI.result: The model accurately reproduces short-time behavior and yields improved predictions for the moments of the number density compared to the Markovian baseline (regularized DK).
AI.conclusion: Flow-based generative modeling can robustly capture non-Markovian, non-Gaussian short-time stochastic dynamics in coarse-grained particle systems, with potential applicability beyond the studied Brownian-particle setup.

title: A Geometric Account of Activation Steering through Angle-Norm Decomposition
summary: Linear activation steering has gained popularity as a simple and empirically effective way to control language model behavior. More recently, spherical steering paradigms have been proposed to address limitations of additive interventions, often motivated by the assumption that hidden-state norm does not carry concept-relevant information. In this work, we revisit this assumption through a controlled empirical study designed to disentangle the roles of angular and radial components. We show that steering methods differ mainly in how they couple two geometric effects: changing a token's angular alignment with a concept direction and changing its hidden-state norm. Across seven language models, we find that concepts are represented primarily in angular structure, supporting the motivation for spherical methods, but that norm remains important for the stability and downstream effects of steering. Our results explain why interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently, and suggest that activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components of the intervention, rather than by a single additive coefficient that entangles these two effects.

AI.tldr: Concepts in language-model representations are encoded primarily in angular structure; norm (radial component) affects stability and downstream effects of activation steering; steering should be parameterized by separate angular and radial components rather than a single additive coefficient.
AI.motivation: To test the claim that hidden-state norm carries little concept information and to disentangle angular versus radial contributions in activation steering, comparing linear and spherical approaches across multiple models.
AI.method: Controlled empirical study that separates angular alignment with a concept direction from changes in hidden-state norm; analyzes coupling of these geometric effects; evaluates interventions across seven language models to explain variability in steering outcomes.
AI.result: Concepts are largely represented in angular structure; spherical steering is well-motivated by this finding, but the hidden-state norm remains important for stability and downstream effects; interventions with similar concept-level effects can behave differently due to angular–radial coupling.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering should be parameterized by interpretable angular and radial components, improving interpretability, stability, and transfer of steering interventions across models.

title: RPC-GS: Gaussian Splatting with native RPC Rendering for Satellite Imagery
summary: We present RPC-GS, the first Gaussian Splatting framework for satellite imagery that operates natively with Rational Polynomial Camera (RPC) models. The RPC model is the de facto standard for representing the complex imaging geometry of modern pushbroom satellite sensors. To simplify rendering, prior satellite Gaussian Splatting methods replace the RPC model with perspective or affine camera approximations, leading to geometric errors during reconstruction. RPC-GS avoids these approximations by projecting Gaussian means and covariances directly through the RPC model during the splatting process. We embed the RPC model in a chain of carefully selected geo-coordinate transformations representing a mapping from splatting-suitable scene coordinates to image coordinates. To map the Gaussian covariance matrices, we derive a numerically robust Jacobian-based covariance projection for the (partially nonlinear) coordinate transformations. Since RPCs lack an explicit notion of camera depth, we integrate a metric ray-based depth formulation. We benchmark RPC, perspective, and affine camera models in a unified framework, with our native RPC renderer consistently achieving the lowest reconstruction error on leading satellite benchmark datasets, improving mean altitude error over perspective and affine approximations by 29.6% and 63.8% on DFC2019, and by 9.9% and 37.9% on IARPA2016. We release our code to support future research of Gaussian Splatting in the satellite imaging domain.

AI.tldr: RPC-GS is the first Gaussian Splatting framework that operates directly with Rational Polynomial Camera (RPC) models for satellite imagery, avoiding projection approximations and achieving lower reconstruction error than perspective/affine methods; it introduces a Jacobian-based covariance projection, metric ray-depth, and releases code.
AI.motivation: RPCs model accurate satellite imaging geometry but prior Gaussian Splatting used simpler camera models, causing geometric errors; native RPC integration aims to reduce error and improve reconstruction fidelity.
AI.method: Integrates RPC projection into the splatting pipeline; uses a chain of geo-coordinate transforms from splatting scene coords to image coords; derives a numerically robust Jacobian-based covariance projection under partially nonlinear transforms; introduces a metric ray-based depth; evaluates RPC, perspective, affine within a unified framework.
AI.result: Native RPC renderer yields the lowest reconstruction error on DFC2019 and IARPA2016, with mean altitude error reductions of 29.6% and 63.8% over perspective and affine on DFC2019, and 9.9% and 37.9% on IARPA2016; code released.
AI.conclusion: Native RPC integration in Gaussian Splatting significantly improves geometric fidelity for satellite imagery, with robust covariance handling and depth formulation; the work enables future extension and benchmarking in satellite imaging with Gaussian Splatting.

title: Three-dimensional hydro-cluttered locomotion by an undulatory robot
summary: Aquatic robots have expanded human access to underwater environments, yet many underwater spaces contain obstacles that can disrupt open-water locomotion. In "hydro-cluttered" environments, water is interspersed with rigid and flexible clutter, making body-obstacle contact unavoidable. Operating in these spaces requires robots that can regulate and exploit contact, but this regime remains difficult to model or simulate. Building on recent advances in mechanical intelligence in terradynamically capable limbless robotics, we develop principles for 3D aquatic locomotion using AquaMILR, an elongate limbless robot that combines bilateral cable-driven actuation, programmable body compliance, distributed depth regulation, corrosion-resistant enclosures, and onboard power and electronics for untethered field operation. Systematic robophysical experiments reveal that programmable body compliance regulates body deformation and converts body-environment interactions into fast, robust, forward progression across increasing hydro-clutter constraint strength. Depth regulation provides three-dimensional access, allowing the robot to bypass clutter, recover from obstruction, and continue through otherwise inaccessible routes. In potential jamming scenarios, emergent inertia-induced rolling acts as a spontaneous recovery mechanism, freeing the robot from clutter that would otherwise lead to failure and allowing locomotion to continue without additional control. Tests of the robot in an aquatic mangrove field demonstrate that these principles transfer to practical operation, enabling navigation and onboard visual inspection of inaccessible root zones. These results establish principles for hydro-cluttered locomotion and a design paradigm in which aquatic robots exploit environmental complexity as a locomotor resource.

AI.tldr: AquaMILR demonstrates a 3D, limbless aquatic robot that exploits hydro-clutter using programmable body compliance, depth control, and robust onboard systems to navigate cluttered environments and recover from jams, with practical field validation in mangroves.
AI.motivation: Underwater, cluttered environments introduce frequent body-obstacle contact. Traditional open-water locomotion struggles in hydro-clutter; there's a need for robots that regulate and exploit contact as a locomotor resource to expand navigable spaces.
AI.method: Design of AquaMILR: an elongate limbless robot with bilateral cable-driven actuation, programmable body compliance, distributed depth regulation, corrosion-resistant enclosures, and onboard power/electronics for untethered operation. Systematic robophysical experiments assess how body compliance, depth control, and emergent behaviors (inertia-induced rolling) contribute to locomotion in increasing clutter. Field validation in an aquatic mangrove environment demonstrates practical navigation and onboard inspection capabilities.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) programmable body compliance modulates deformation and converts body-environment interactions into rapid forward progress under higher clutter; (2) depth regulation enables 3D access, bypassing clutter and enabling recovery from obstructions; (3) inertia-induced rolling emerges as a recovery mechanism in jam situations, allowing continued locomotion without extra control; (4) mangrove-field tests show real-world applicability for navigation and onboard inspection of root zones; (5) the study proposes general principles for hydro-cluttered locomotion and a design paradigm that treats environmental complexity as a locomotor resource.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes design principles for hydro-cluttered aquatic locomotion by leveraging programmable body compliance, depth control, and autonomous hardware to navigate, recover from obstruction, and exploit environmental complexity as a resource, with demonstrated field applicability.

title: Explainable Runtime Dependency Tracking for AI-RAN Conflict Monitoring
summary:
Future AI-integrated Radio Access Networks (AI-RAN) will combine open programmability with learning-enabled xApps, rApps, and control functions that act on shared parameters and key performance indicators (KPIs). For conflict monitoring, it is not enough to know which applications are deployed; the system must also know whether the parameter--KPI dependencies assumed by runtime diagnosis remain valid under the current operating regime. This paper studies a lightweight monitoring primitive for that purpose: tracking an interpretable dependency representation from streaming telemetry events.
  We represent active dependencies by a Boolean matrix and use Boolean matrix multiplication to check whether recent parameter-activity and KPI-response events are consistent with the current estimate. We propose a sliding-window inference procedure that reuses the estimate when it remains consistent and recomputes it when recent observations indicate structural change. The tracker is intended as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not as an autonomous mitigation mechanism. Experiments on controlled Boolean event streams show efficient and accurate tracking under dependency changes and Boolean observation noise.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, interpretable tracker for AI-RAN dependency validity using a Boolean dependency matrix and sliding-window inference to monitor parameter–KPI relations under noise and regime shifts; emphasizes explainability over mitigation.
AI.motivation: In AI-RAN, multiple xApps/rApps depend on shared parameters and KPIs. To diagnose conflicts and ensure runtime diagnosis remains valid, a compact, interpretable monitor of dependency structure is needed, capable of signaling when assumptions fail.
AI.method: Represent active dependencies as a Boolean matrix. Use Boolean matrix multiplication to test consistency between recent parameter-activity and KPI-response events. Employ a sliding-window inference procedure that reuses the current estimate when consistent, and recomputes upon detection of structural change. Operates on streaming telemetry events; intended as an explainable signal for conflict diagnosis and slow-loop model refresh, not autonomous mitigation.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficient and accurate tracking of dependency changes under Boolean observation noise in controlled event streams; provides a lightweight, explainable input signal for conflict diagnosis and model refresh cadence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Boolean dependency tracker offers a transparent, computationally lightweight mechanism to monitor the validity of dependency assumptions in AI-RAN, enabling explainable diagnostics and timely model refresh without autonomous control actions.

title: OpenSkill: Open-World Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures and 8 tables. Code is avalable at https://github.com/OpenLAIR/OpenSkill
summary: Self-evolving agents requires adaptation after deployment, but existing approaches assume a usable learning loop, such as curated skills, successful trajectories, or verifier signals. Real open-world deployments may provide none of these, offering only a task prompt. In this work, we study open-world self-evolution, where an agent must build both its skills and its own verification signals from scratch, using open-world resources but no target-task supervision. We propose OpenSkill, a framework that bootstraps this loop: it acquires grounded knowledge and verification anchors from documentation, repositories, and the web, synthesizes them into transferable skills, and refines those skills against self-built virtual tasks grounded in the anchors rather than in target answers. The open world thus supplies both the knowledge to be learned and a supervision-independent practice environment, with target-task supervision reserved for final evaluation. Across three benchmarks and two target agents, OpenSkill attains the best automated pass rate while satisfying the no-supervision constraint. Analysis shows its skills transfer across models without model-specific adaptation, and its self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes despite never accessing them.

AI.tldr: OpenSkill enables open-world self-evolution by bootstrapping skills and verifiers from documentation, repositories, and the web, without target-task supervision; it achieves transferable skills and self-built verification, leading to best automated pass rates across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Open-world deployments lack curated learning signals; agents must bootstrap their own skills and verification from open resources without target-task supervision.
AI.method: OpenSkill acquires grounded knowledge and verification anchors from docs, code repositories, and the web; synthesizes them into transferable skills; refines skills using self-built virtual tasks grounded in the anchors; final evaluation uses target-task supervision only.
AI.result: Three benchmarks and two target agents show OpenSkill achieves best automated pass rate; skills transfer across models without model-specific adaptation; self-built verifier aligns with ground-truth outcomes without access to them.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates feasible open-world self-evolution by bootstrapping knowledge and verification from open resources, yielding transferable skills and alignment between self-built verification and true outcomes, without target-task supervision.

title: S23DR 2026 Winning Solution
summary: This text presents the winning solution to the S23DR 2026 challenge for structured 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse SfM, fitted depth, and semantic segmentations. The method treats vertices as a conditional set and denoises 64 vertex tokens with a flow-matching DiT conditioned on Perceiver-style scene tokens. A global pass predicts the coarse structure, a hull-cropped second pass refines it, and a small multi-sample consensus step keeps the stochastic sampler well behaved. The final system ranked first on the private leaderboard, achievingHSS = 0.654.

AI.tldr: A neural pipeline for structured 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse SfM, depth, and semantics, denoising vertex tokens with a flow-matching DiT conditioned on Perceiver-style scene tokens; a coarse global pass followed by hull-cropped refinement and a small multi-sample consensus stabilizer, achieving first place with HSS 0.654.
AI.motivation: Tackle robust 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse geometric cues and semantic segmentations by modeling vertices as a conditional set and applying staged denoising and refinement to improve geometric fidelity.
AI.method: Treats vertices as a conditional set and denoise 64 vertex tokens with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer conditioned on Perceiver-style scene tokens. A global pass predicts coarse structure, a hull-cropped second pass refines it, and a small multi-sample consensus step stabilizes the stochastic sampler.
AI.result: The method ranked first on the private leaderboard of the S23DR 2026 challenge, achieving a Housekeeping Score (HSS) of 0.654.
AI.conclusion: The combination of conditional vertex-token denoising, staged refinement (global then hull-cropped), and a probabilistic consensus mechanism yields state-of-the-art performance for structured 3D wireframe reconstruction from sparse cues and segmentation data.

title: STRIPS-WM: Learning Grounded Propositional STRIPS-style World Models from Images
summary: Robots performing long-horizon visual manipulation observe high-dimensional images, but successful plans depend on action-relevant facts: what can be done now and what changes afterward. A useful planning representation should discard irrelevant visual details while preserving action applicability and effects. Classical task planners exploit this structure through symbolic operators with preconditions and effects, but obtaining such representations from raw visual experience remains challenging. We study a visual task-planning setting in which a robot receives only image transitions: the current image, executed high-level action, and the resulting image. At test time, given a start image and a goal image, the robot must produce a sequence of high-level actions that reaches the goal. To address this problem, we introduce STRIPS-WM, a framework for learning image-grounded STRIPS-style world models directly from visual transitions. STRIPS-WM first induces a finite abstract transition graph from images, then learns latent binary predicates and one grounded propositional operator per action label. The learned operators form a symbolic action model with sparse preconditions and add/delete effects. Finally, the learned predicates are distilled into a visual encoder, enabling classical planning directly from novel start and goal images. Experiments on visual rearrangement tasks show that STRIPS-WM improves image-to-plan success over the tested visual rollout, latent graph-search and latent-symbolic baselines.

AI.tldr: STRIPS-WM learns image-grounded STRIPS-style operators from visual transitions to enable planning from start/goal images in long-horizon visual manipulation.
AI.motivation: Bridge perception and symbolic planning by extracting action-relevant, sparse preconditions/effects from high-dimensional visuals to enable classical planning.
AI.method: 1) Construct an abstract transition graph from image transitions; 2) Learn latent binary predicates and derive a grounded propositional operator per action label; 3) Distill predicates into a visual encoder to plan directly from new images.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on visual rearrangement tasks shows STRIPS-WM improves image-to-plan success against visual rollout, latent graph-search, and latent-symbolic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Learning STRIPS-style world models from visual transitions is viable and enables planning directly from images for long-horizon manipulation, effectively combining learning with classical planning.

title: Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping for Sparse-Reward Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IEEE CAI). 6 pages, 3 figures. Code available at: https://github.com/USD-AI-ResearchLab/uncertainty-aware-llm-rl
summary: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences remain persistent challenges in Reinforcement Learning (RL), often resulting in slow convergence, weak generalization, and inefficient exploration. We propose Uncertainty-Aware LLM-Guided Policy Shaping (ULPS), a novel framework that integrates a calibrated Large Language Model (LLM) into the RL training loop to provide structured, uncertainty-modulated behavioral guidance. ULPS employs an A*-based oracle to synthesize optimal symbolic trajectories, which are used to fine-tune a BERT-based language model. During training, this model supplies action suggestions whose influence is conditioned on epistemic uncertainty estimated via Monte Carlo (MC) dropout. An entropy-based blending mechanism adaptively balances LLM guidance and the learned policy (via Proximal Policy Optimization, PPO), allowing the agent to prioritize reliable priors while preserving adaptability. We evaluate ULPS on the MiniGridUnlockPickup benchmark and observe consistent improvements in success rate, reward efficiency, and sample complexity over unguided, uncalibrated, and standard RL baselines. ULPS achieves more than 9% improvement in execution accuracy after fine-tuning, requires fewer environment interactions, and yields higher reward AUC. Our results demonstrate that integrating symbolic A* trajectories, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control offers a principled and effective approach to multi-task reinforcement learning in sparse-reward domains, with potential extensibility to partially observable and multi-agent settings.

AI.tldr: ULPS uses A*-generated symbolic trajectories to fine-tune a BERT-based LM, whose uncertainty-aware action suggestions are blended with PPO guidance to improve learning in sparse-reward RL; evaluated on MiniGridUnlockPickup with positive gains in success rate, sample efficiency, and reward metrics.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards and heterogeneous task sequences impede RL progress, causing slow convergence and poor generalization; incorporating structured priors and uncertainty-aware guidance can improve exploration and adaptability.
AI.method: An A*-based oracle synthesizes optimal symbolic trajectories, which are used to fine-tune a BERT-based language model. During training, the LM provides action suggestions whose influence is modulated by epistemic uncertainty estimated via MC dropout. The suggestions and the learned policy are blended using an entropy-based mechanism, balancing prior guidance with PPO optimization.
AI.result: ULPS yields consistent improvements over unguided, uncalibrated, and standard RL baselines on MiniGridUnlockPickup, including >9% gains in execution accuracy after fine-tuning, reduced environment interactions, and higher reward AUC.
