AI.conclusion: Integrating symbolic priors, pretrained language priors, and uncertainty-aware control provides a principled framework for multi-task RL in sparse-reward domains, with potential extensions to partially observable and multi-agent settings.

title: AdMem: Advanced Memory for Task-solving Agents
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise as tool-using agents but remain limited in long-horizon tasks that require remembering, organizing, and reusing knowledge. Prior memory approaches aim to resolve the situation, but mainly focus on storing factual information. Recent work on procedural memory improves task reuse, yet often reduces to replaying past successes without addressing failure cases or online scalability. We introduce a unified and automatic memory framework that integrates semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level design combining short-term and long-term stores. A multi-agent architecture with actor, memory, and critic agents enables automatic memory generation, reward annotation, and adaptive retrieval. Long-term memory is managed through reward-based evaluation, merging, and pruning, ensuring scalability and continual improvement. Experiments across various environments show that our approach improves robustness and success on long multi-turn tasks compared to existing baselines. This work highlights the importance of comprehensive, adaptive memory for advancing LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified, automatic memory framework for LLM agents integrating semantic, episodic, and procedural memory in a bi-level architecture with multi-agent components to improve long-horizon task performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with long-horizon tasks due to memory limitations; existing memory approaches focus on factual storage or replay past successes; need integrated, scalable memory that handles failures and online adaptation.
AI.method: Bi-level memory design with short-term and long-term stores; semantic/episodic/procedural memory integration; multi-agent system (actor, memory, critic) for automatic memory generation, reward annotation, adaptive retrieval; long-term memory curated by reward-based evaluation, merging, pruning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse environments shows improved robustness and success on long multi-turn tasks relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Comprehensive, adaptive memory is key to advancing LLM-based agents and enabling scalable, continual improvement in long-horizon tasks.

title: MMBU: A Massive Multi-modal Biomedical Understanding Benchmark to Probe the Perception Capabilities of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision and language models (VLMs) hold immense promise to transform biomedical imaging workflows, from detecting lesions in chest X-rays to profiling cellular features in microscopy. Realizing this potential, however, requires robust and fine-grained visual perception. Models need to correctly interpret subtle features in images, and they must do so across diverse biomedical modalities, scales, and contexts. Nevertheless, current benchmarks remain limited. To address these gaps, we introduce the Massive Multimodal Biomedical Understanding (MMBU) benchmark. It is the largest biomedical vision and language benchmark to date, covering 35 submodalities with rich structured metadata. It includes both open and closed versions of ungrounded classification, grounded classification, and object detection, enabling systematic evaluation of model performance across biological scales, clinical settings, and imaging modalities. Evaluating 15 open-weight and 2 frontier VLMs, we find that while medical adaptation provides measurable gains for some models, the high accuracy often reported on established benchmarks can mask deficiencies in visual perception and domain generalization.

AI.tldr: MMBU benchmark: largest biomedical VLM benchmark across 35 submodalities with open/closed tasks (un/no-grounded), including classification and object detection; evaluates 15 open-weight and 2 frontier VLMs; reveals gains from medical adaptation but hides perception/domain-generalization gaps in reported accuracies.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing benchmarks by enabling robust, fine-grained evaluation of vision-language models across diverse biomedical modalities, scales, and clinical contexts.
AI.method: Construct Massive Multimodal Biomedical Understanding benchmark with 35 submodalities and rich metadata; provide both open and closed variants for ungrounded/classification, grounded/classification, and object detection; systematically evaluate multiple VLMs (15 open-weight, 2 frontier).
AI.result: Medical adaptation yields measurable gains for some models, but high accuracy on established benchmarks can mask deficiencies in visual perception and domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: MMBU offers a comprehensive, diverse evaluation suite for biomedical VLMs, exposing gaps between benchmark scores and real-world robustness; emphasizes need for improved visual perception and domain generalization in biomedical VLMs.

title: Think Like a Pilot: Fine-Grained Long-Horizon UAV Navigation
summary: Language-guided UAV agents must execute long-horizon semantic instructions while producing smooth, physically feasible continuous flight commands, yet existing Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) benchmarks typically use discrete or coarse actions and existing UAV Vision-Language-Action (VLA) tasks focus on short, atomic maneuvers. To address this gap in UAV task settings, we introduce \textbf{FLIGHT}, a \textbf{F}ine-grained \textbf{L}ong-horizon \textbf{I}nstruction-\textbf{G}uided benchmark for \textbf{H}ybrid UAV navigation and reasoning \textbf{T}asks, which combines multi-stage instructions with dense 6-DoF trajectory annotations across two dataset splits: Fine-grained VLN and Long-horizon Flow. To endow the UAV agent with the capability of real-time in-flight reasoning over task execution status and mission planning, while simultaneously accommodating high-frequency, real-time precise control, we further propose \textbf{FLIGHT VLA}, an asynchronous architecture that decouples a low-frequency Streaming Pilot Vision-Language Model (VLM) for task-state reasoning from a high-frequency diffusion action model for continuous control, supervised by explicit \textbf{Pilot Reasoning} texts that summarize the current flight state and anticipate the next subgoal. In closed-loop evaluation, FLIGHT VLA consistently surpasses representative VLN and VLA baselines on our FLIGHT benchmarks, achieving stronger multi-stage completion, subgoal adherence, and terminal control. Its trained Streaming Pilot Reasoning VLM further improves UAV video reasoning, validating the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: FLIGHT introduces a fine-grained, long-horizon UAV benchmark and an asynchronous VLA architecture to enable continuous 6-DoF control with multi-stage guidance. It pairs a low-frequency streaming Pilot VLM for reasoning with a high-frequency diffusion action model for control, using Pilot Reasoning texts to summarize state and anticipate subgoals; results show improved multi-stage completion, subgoal adherence, and terminal control over baselines.
AI.motivation: VLN benchmarks for UAVs lack long-horizon, fine-grained, continuous-action formulations and realistic in-flight reasoning. Existing VLN and VLA setups either use discrete actions or short maneuvers, hindering real-world applicability for UAV navigation and planning.
AI.method: Present FLIGHT, a benchmark with Fine-grained VLN and Long-horizon Flow datasets that provide multi-stage instructions and dense 6-DoF trajectory annotations. Propose FLIGHT VLA, an asynchronous architecture decoupling a low-frequency Streaming Pilot VLM (state reasoning) from a high-frequency diffusion action model (continuous control), guided by Pilot Reasoning texts. The system enables real-time in-flight reasoning and precise control across two dataset splits.
AI.result: In closed-loop evaluations, FLIGHT VLA outperforms representative VLN and VLA baselines on FLIGHT benchmarks, achieving better multi-stage completion, subgoal adherence, and terminal control. The Streaming Pilot Reasoning VLM also improves UAV video reasoning, validating the effectiveness of the design.
AI.conclusion: The proposed benchmark and asynchronous VLA architecture effectively address the gap in long-horizon, fine-grained UAV navigation tasks, providing improved multi-stage planning, adherence to subgoals, and precise terminal control, while also facilitating more capable in-flight reasoning through the Streaming Pilot Reasoning VLM.

title: Spatiotemporal Imputation with Graph-Informed Flow Matching
summary: Missing data is a common challenge in spatiotemporal systems, arising in applications such as air quality monitoring and urban traffic management. Traditional machine learning approaches, like recurrent and graph neural networks, rely on iterative propagation, which tends to accumulate errors over time and space. Recent diffusion-based methods mitigate error propagation but require iterative sampling and often depend on problem-agnostic Gaussian priors, limiting both efficiency and effectiveness. To address these limitations, we propose GiFlow, a Graph-Informed Flow Matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation. GiFlow replaces the typical Gaussian prior with a graph-informed prior constructed via spatiotemporal filtering of observable signals, which better aligns the source distribution to the target and thereby simplifies the generation trajectory. The flow field is parameterized by a hybrid vector field model that integrates spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation, enabling joint modeling of spatial and temporal dependencies. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed GiFlow outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches in spatiotemporal imputation. The code is available at https://github.com/zepengzhang/GiFlow.

AI.tldr: GiFlow introduces a Graph-Informed Flow Matching framework for spatiotemporal imputation, replacing Gaussian priors with a graph-informed prior derived from spatiotemporal filtering; it uses a hybrid vector field combining spatial and temporal attention with spatiotemporal propagation; it outperforms state-of-the-art methods on synthetic and real datasets; code is released.
AI.motivation: To overcome two main limitations: (1) error accumulation in iterative propagation used by recurrent and graph neural networks, and (2) inefficiency and limited effectiveness of diffusion-based approaches that rely on problem-agnostic Gaussian priors. A graph-informed prior aligned with task structure can reduce trajectory complexity and improve accuracy and efficiency in imputation.
AI.method: Construct a graph-informed prior by applying spatiotemporal filtering to observable signals. Parameterize the generative flow with a hybrid vector field model that integrates spatial attention, temporal attention, and spatiotemporal propagation to jointly model spatial and temporal dependencies. Train via a flow-matching objective to map from the graph-informed prior to the target data, enabling non-iterative generation trajectories.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show GiFlow outperforms state-of-the-art approaches for spatiotemporal imputation.
AI.conclusion: Graph-informed priors that leverage observable spatiotemporal structure, together with a hybrid attention-based flow field, yield more accurate and efficient imputation than Gaussian-prior diffusion or recurrent/graph-based methods; code is publicly available.

title: Evidence-Based Intelligent Diagnostic and Therapeutic Visualization System with Large Language Models: Multi-Turn Interaction and Multimodal Treatment Plan Generation
comment: 29 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables, including supporting information
summary:
Aim: Existing AI-assisted traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic tools suffer from opaque reasoning processes, passive interaction, and limited treatment plan presentation. This
  study proposes a knowledge-enhanced visual diagnostic system to improve the transparency and interpretability of syndrome differentiation and treatment.
  Methods: The system is built upon a Neo4j knowledge graph comprising 241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, and 2,485 relations. It incorporates a four-stage symptom matching pipeline
  (exact, semantic, fuzzy, and large language model verification), an information gain-driven proactive questioning strategy optimized with genetic algorithms, and a multimodal
  treatment presentation integrating artificial intelligence-generated illustrations, three-dimensional meridian-acupoint models, and evidence-based literature.
  Results: Knowledge graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%. Case studies validated the effectiveness of the interactive workflow across patient self-assessment,
  clinician-assisted diagnosis, and traditional Chinese medicine education. Automated paired-comparison evaluation across 30 cases further demonstrated significant improvements in
  diagnostic trust (Cohen's d = 1.82, p < 0.001), reduced cognitive load (improvements in four of five dimensions), and higher credibility of evidence-based references (4.21 vs.
  2.95).
  Conclusions: The proposed system enhances the transparency of traditional Chinese medicine diagnostic reasoning and the interpretability of treatment plans through knowledge
  graph-driven visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a practical solution for trustworthy artificial intelligence-assisted traditional Chinese medicine applications.

AI.tldr: A knowledge-graph–driven, four-stage symptom matching and multimodal treatment presentation system for AI-assisted traditional Chinese medicine diagnosis, improving transparency and diagnostic credibility.
AI.motivation: Current AI-assisted TCM tools suffer from opaque reasoning, passive interaction, and limited treatment-plan presentation, hindering trust and adoption. A transparent, interpretable system leveraging structured knowledge and multimodal outputs is needed.
AI.method: Neo4j-based knowledge graph with 241 syndromes, 1,263 symptoms, 2,485 relations. A four-stage symptom matching pipeline (exact, semantic, fuzzy, LLM verification), information-gain-driven proactive questioning optimized via genetic algorithms, and multimodal treatment presentation including AI-generated illustrations, 3D meridian–acupoint models, and evidence-based literature.
AI.result: Knowledge-graph constraints reduced non-standard outputs by 32%. Case studies validated the interactive workflow for patient self-assessment, clinician-assisted diagnosis, and TCM education. In a 30-case automated paired-comparison evaluation, diagnostic trust increased (Cohen’s d = 1.82, p < 0.001), cognitive load decreased across four of five dimensions, and credibility of evidence-based references rose (4.21 vs. 2.95).
AI.conclusion: The system enhances transparency in TCM diagnostic reasoning and interpretability of treatment plans via knowledge-graph visualization and multimodal interaction, offering a practical, trustworthy AI-assisted TCM solution.

title: USU-Corn-WeedDB: A UAV RGB Image Dataset for Multi-Species Weed Detection in Forage Corn
summary: Weed pressure in forage corn production causes yield losses of up to 31.5%, yet site-specific weed management (SSWM) systems built on UAV imagery and deep learning remain constrained by the scarcity of field-representative training datasets. We present USU-Corn-WeedDB, a publicly available UAV RGB image dataset collected from a commercial forage corn field in Cache Valley, Utah, designed to support multi-class weed detection under both supervised and semi-supervised learning frameworks. RGB imagery was acquired on 27 June 2025 using an Autel EVO II Dual 640T V2 drone at ~10m above ground level, yielding a ground sampling distance of approximately 0.48 cm/pixel. A total of 366 full-resolution images were tiled into 8,800 patches at 640 x 640-pixel resolution. Of these, 800 images were manually annotated for three weed species; common lambsquarters (Chenopodium album), redroot pigweed (Amaranthus retroflexus), and green foxtail (Setaria viridis) comprising 10,539 bounding-box instances, with the remaining 8,000 tiles retained as an unlabeled pool for semi-supervised experiments. This dataset reflects a natural class imbalance where redroot pigweed constitutes 53.86% of annotated instances, which was preserved intentionally to mirror real field conditions. To validate dataset utility, we trained 28 object detection models spanning five architecture families including YOLOv8, YOLOv9, YOLOv10, YOLO11, YOLO26, and RT-DETR under identical conditions without hyperparameter tuning. Test set mAP@0.5 ranged from 0.773 to 0.840, with lightweight models achieving competitive performance relevant to edge-deployed UAV systems. USU-Corn-WeedDB is publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20044178.

AI.tldr: A public UAV RGB weed-detection dataset for forage corn (USU-Corn-WeedDB) with 800 annotated images for three weed species and 8,000 unlabeled tiles, enabling supervised and semi-supervised learning; evaluated 28 detectors across several YOLO variants and RT-DETR with mAP@0.5 0.773–0.840; dataset emphasizes real-field class imbalance and edge-friendly models.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of field-representative datasets for site-specific weed management (SSWM) in forage corn and to enable robust supervised and semi-supervised learning and edge deployment through diverse model benchmarking.
AI.method: Data collection via a drone at ~10 m AGL yielding ~0.48 cm/pixel GSD; 366 full-res images tiled into 8,800 640x640 patches; 800 images annotated for three species (Chenopodium album, Amaranthus retroflexus, Setaria viridis) with 10,539 bounding boxes; 8,000 unlabeled patches reserved for semi-supervised experiments; evaluation of 28 object detectors across five architecture families including YOLOv8/9/10/11/26 and RT-DETR under identical conditions without hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: Test set mAP@0.5 ranged from 0.773 to 0.840; lightweight models performed competitively for edge deployment; dataset provides a realistic class imbalance (redroot pigweed ~53.86% of instances); publicly available at Zenodo.
AI.conclusion: USU-Corn-WeedDB fills a critical data gap for forage-corn weed detection, enabling supervised and semi-supervised learning research and benchmarking of diverse architectures, with practical relevance for field-scale weed management and UAV-based deployment.

title: What Is My Robot Thinking? Design Considerations for Transparent and Trustworthy Shared Autonomy
comment: 9 pages, 5 Figures, Code and videos are available at https://sites.google.com/view/design-t2-sa/home. Under review at IROS 2026
summary: Assistive robots operating under shared autonomy must balance user control with autonomous assistance. Because robot actions depend on internal intent inference that is not directly observable, mismatches between inferred and intended goals can undermine coordination and trust. We investigate how interface-level transparency, including feedback modality (visual vs. auditory) and information richness (sparse vs. rich), shapes interaction in a vision-based shared autonomy system. In a user study with N=25 participants across two assistive manipulation tasks, we evaluate how these designs influence coordination and trust. Providing feedback significantly improves intent alignment and reduces corrective intervention, indicating that making the inferred goal legible accelerates convergence in shared control. Participants preferred visual over auditory feedback, while preferences for sparse versus rich information depended on task complexity. We also found that revealing the full belief distribution did not consistently improve alignment or trust. Together, these findings indicate that effective transparency enhances coordination primarily through goal legibility, while trust depends on task-appropriate information exposure rather than maximal disclosure. Based on these results, we outline guidelines for designing transparent shared autonomy systems.

AI.tldr: Interface-level transparency in vision-based shared autonomy improves intent alignment and reduces corrective interventions by making the inferred goal legible; visual feedback is preferred, information richness preference depends on task complexity, and revealing full belief distributions does not always help. Guidelines for designing transparent shared autonomy are proposed.
AI.motivation: To enhance coordination and trust in assistive robots by making the system's inferred user intent legible through transparency, and to understand how feedback modality and information richness affect interaction.
AI.method: Experimental user study (N=25) across two assistive manipulation tasks, testing feedback modalities (visual vs. auditory) and information richness (sparse vs. rich); additionally tested disclosure of the full belief distribution; evaluated coordination, trust, and need for corrective intervention.
AI.result: Providing feedback significantly improves intent alignment and reduces corrective intervention; participants preferred visual feedback over auditory; sparse vs. rich information preferences depended on task complexity; full belief-distribution disclosure did not consistently improve alignment or trust.
AI.conclusion: Transparency that emphasizes goal legibility facilitates coordination in shared autonomy; trust is influenced by task-appropriate information exposure rather than maximal disclosure; guidelines for transparent shared autonomy design are proposed.

title: Towards Serverless Semi-Decentralized Federated Learning with Heterogeneous Optimizers
comment: Under review at IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking
summary: We investigate cluster formation, involving the number and composition of clusters, in decentralized federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous machine learning (ML) optimizers. While clustering in centralized FL has enabled scalability and resource savings, its value and development in fully decentralized environments have yet to be explored. Optimizing cluster formation in such environments is challenging, especially due to the complex coupling between network graph structures, local data heterogeneity, and different local ML model optimizers. To address these challenges, we propose serverless semi-decentralized FL (SSD-FL), a methodology requiring no persistent server infrastructure. In SSD-FL, cluster formation occurs via a lightweight, one-time device-to-device (D2D) initialization phase, after which actual ML model training (alongside consensus and convergence processes) is fully serverless. Functionally, SSD-FL segments global rounds into intra-cluster and inter-cluster regimes, ensuring global convergence and consensus through novel "effective loss functions" that integrate device-specific ML optimizers with network graph-based regularization. Next, SSD-FL leverages the consensus gap via the Cheeger inequality to develop an iterative clustering algorithm evaluated against our derived convergence and consensus bounds, which incorporate a unique scoring metric to quantify data and optimizer heterogeneity across devices. Finally, experimental evaluation against three categories of decentralized FL methodologies validate that SSD-FL improves both convergence speeds and communication efficiency across various network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer regimes.

AI.tldr: Proposes SSD-FL, a serverless, semi-decentralized FL framework that forms device clusters via a one-time D2D initialization and achieves global convergence with intra- and inter-cluster phases, Cheeger-inequality-based clustering, and novel effective loss functions; shows improved convergence and communication efficiency.
AI.motivation: Decentralized FL lacks principled clustering and convergence guarantees; coupling of network topology, data heterogeneity, and heterogeneous optimizers makes training challenging in a fully serverless setting.
AI.method: One-time device-to-device initialization to form clusters; split global rounds into intra-cluster and inter-cluster phases; introduce effective loss functions that integrate device-specific optimizers with graph-based regularization; develop a clustering algorithm based on consensus gap and Cheeger inequality using a heterogeneity-aware scoring metric; evaluate against baseline decentralized FL methods.
AI.result: SSD-FL improves convergence speed and communication efficiency across various network graphs, datasets, and local optimizer regimes, validated against three categories of decentralized FL methodologies.
AI.conclusion: Serverless semi-decentralized FL with clustering and effective loss functions can achieve global convergence and consensus with improved efficiency, demonstrating robustness to heterogeneity in data and optimizers.

title: Workflow-to-Skill: Skill Creation via Routing-Workflow-Semantics-Attachments Decomposition
summary: Large language model agents increasingly rely on Skills to encode procedural knowledge, yet high-quality Skills remain costly to hand-write. This paper studies automatic Skill construction from heterogeneous interaction evidence, including demonstrations, agent trajectories, tool traces, and execution logs. We argue that trace-to-skill construction is not simple summarization tasks, because traces are fragmented, redundant, and may miss rare but safety-critical behaviors. To address this, we introduce RWSA, a workflow-oriented intermediate representation that decomposes Skills into Workflow structure, execution Semantics, and runtime Attachments, capturing task decomposition, control flow, verification, safety, rollback, and state management. Building on RWSA, we propose W2S, a framework that segments traces, induces local Skill drafts, aligns shared structures, reconciles branches, and compresses redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations. Experiments on 70 Skills show that W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization- and prompting-based baselines, highlighting the need to treat traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text.

AI.tldr: W2S uses RWSA to convert heterogeneous traces into executable, workflow-based Skills, improving replay fidelity by 10.5% on 70 Skills.
AI.motivation: Hand-writing high-quality Skills is costly; traces are fragmented, redundant, and may miss rare safety-critical behaviors. Automatic, evidence-preserving Skill construction from demonstrations, trajectories, tool traces, and logs is needed.
AI.method: Introduce RWSA—a workflow-oriented intermediate representation with Workflow structure, execution Semantics, and runtime Attachments. Build W2S to segment traces, induce local Skill drafts, align shared structures, reconcile branches, and compress redundancy while preserving evidence and confidence annotations.
AI.result: Experiments on 70 Skills show W2S improves behavioral replay consistency by 10.5% over summarization- and prompting-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treat traces as executable runtime specifications rather than compressible text; structured representations (RWSA) enable more robust Skill construction and better safety-conscious behavior replay.

title: Anchored, Not Graded: Vision-Language Models Fail at Slant-from-Texture Perception
summary: Human perception of surface slant from texture exhibits systematic, graded biases that emerge reliably in psychophysical experiments. Prior work showed that unsupervised CNNs reproduce several human-like biases, while supervised CNNs do not. Do Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit similar competences? Across multiple VLM families and model scales, zero-shot and in-context prompting both produce distinctive failures: slant is predicted at only a small set of anchors (e.g., 0\degree, $\pm$25\degree, $\pm$45\degree) with little dependence on stimulus field of view, optical slant, or surface curvature. Supervised fine-tuning partially remediates the failure, but residual anchoring persists. While success in high-level vision-language benchmarks might not require sensitivity to low-level geometric cues, we interpret anchoring as a failure at the representation-to-output language interface: Not necessarily an absence of geometric encoding, but a failure to express it in a graded form.

AI.tldr: VLMs show human-like but limited biases in texture-based surface slant perception. Zero-shot and in-context prompts anchor slant to few angles (0°, ±25°, ±45°), largely independent of stimulus field of view, optical slant, or curvature. Supervised fine-tuning mitigates anchors but does not remove them. The issue is framed as a representation-to-output interface failure rather than a lack of geometric encoding—geometry may be encoded but not expressed in graded language outputs.
AI.motivation: Assess whether Vision-Language Models encode low-level geometric cues for surface slant, and whether they exhibit human-like biases. Compare unsupervised vs supervised training and prompting strategies across model families and scales.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple VLM families and scales. Test zero-shot and in-context prompting. Systematically vary stimulus field of view, optical slant, and surface curvature. Record predicted slant from model outputs and analyze anchoring patterns. Compare with human psychophysics data and assess effect of supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Slant predictions exhibit anchoring to a small set of angles (0°, ±25°, ±45°) with little dependence on FOV, optical slant, or curvature. Zero-shot and in-context prompting reveal distinctive failures across models; supervised fine-tuning reduces but does not eliminate anchoring. High-level vision-language benchmarks may not require sensitivity to low-level cues; authors interpret anchoring as a failure in the representation-to-output interface rather than a complete absence of geometric encoding.
AI.conclusion: Anchoring reveals a bottleneck at mapping geometric representations to graded language outputs. VLMs may encode geometry, but the interface truncates continuous, graded information into discrete anchors. Addressing this requires training objectives or architectural changes that align representation with continuous, language-grounded geometry, potentially via calibrated outputs, alternative prompting strategies, or multimodal objectives.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Learning for Long-Horizon Task Planning Under Complex Logical Constraints
summary: Task planning often suffers from severe efficiency bottlenecks when robots must reason over long-horizon action sequences under complex logical constraints, including object affordances, spatial relationships, and sequential action dependencies. Recent neuro-symbolic methods improve planning efficiency by learning object-importance scores to prune task-irrelevant objects, but they typically rely on fixed offline supervision generated from full search spaces. This creates a train-test mismatch: at deployment, the planner operates in pruned search spaces induced by the model's own imperfect predictions, leading to exposure bias and degraded planning performance. To address this challenge, we formulate object-importance learning for task planning as an imperative learning-based bilevel optimization problem. The upper level optimizes a neural scorer, while the lower level solves a symbolic planning problem in the score-pruned search space. To stabilize this learning process, we introduce a 3R strategy into the lower-level planning, using parallel Repair, Restart, and Rollback recovery to provide reliable and adaptive feedback for upper-level learning. Experiments on three challenging benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, including an 80.04% reduction in failure rate and a 57.14% reduction in planning time. We further validate the framework on a quadruped-based mobile manipulator in simulation and the real world, demonstrating its potential for efficient and deployable neuro-symbolic task planning.

AI.tldr: An imperative bilevel learning framework for object-importance in task planning, with a 3R stabilization strategy, yielding major improvements in failure rate and planning time and validated on simulated/real robots.
AI.motivation: Address train-test mismatch and exposure bias in neuro-symbolic task planning caused by offline supervision from full search spaces, which harms deployment performance when pruning is driven by imperfect predictions.
AI.method: Upper-level neural scorer learns object-importance; lower-level symbolic planner operates in the score-pruned search space. A 3R (Repair, Restart, Rollback) strategy stabilizes lower-level planning to provide reliable feedback for upper-level learning in a bilevel optimization loop.
AI.result: Benchmark experiments show state-of-the-art performance: ~80.04% reduction in failure rate and ~57.14% reduction in planning time. Validated on a quadruped-based mobile manipulator in simulation and in the real world.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables efficient, deployable neuro-symbolic task planning by integrating learned pruning with symbolic planning, with the 3R strategy ensuring stable, adaptive training; promising for long-horizon, constrained planning in robotics.

title: The Geography of Algorithmic Judgment: LLM Intermediaries, Place Identity, and Racial Steering in Housing Search
comment: 13 pages with supplemental tables and figures, AIES '26 Submission
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are rapidly assuming an intermediary role in housing search through the integration of listing platforms within conversational interfaces, mediating access to information, search, and recommendations within urban settings. We expand on prior work on racial steering in LLMs by conducting a behavioral audit of seven open-weight and closed-source LLMs across four U.S. cities, testing location recommendations across three iterative prompting conditions that progressively add lifestyle preference context and reflect fair housing paired-testing methodologies. We find that steering is an emergent behavior of the model's interpretive license rather than primarily a static property. Steering results from the interaction of a user's identity, preference articulation, and the spatial logic that a model has internalized about learned representations of place, preference, and opportunity in a given city, and how different types of users relate to it. While steering was present, it was not uniform in direction or magnitude across evaluated conditions. Preference-conditioned testing often increased or reconfigured the number of models that exhibited steering behaviors relative to baseline conditions, suggesting that LLMs may interpret what the same housing preference means differently depending on the racial identity of the user. Our findings also demonstrate that the city is not a neutral testing unit for LLM evaluation in place-based sectors, and results from one local market cannot be assumed to generalize to another. Local and domain expertise will be required in the housing sector to ensure that legal and institutional commitments to fair housing are not undermined while adopting AI tools that mediate spatial access.

AI.tldr: Steering in LLM-based housing recommendations is emergent and context-dependent, varying with user identity and prompting, not a static property.
AI.motivation: Assess potential discrimination risk in AI-mediated housing search; extend prior work on racial steering; inform fair housing in AI deployment.
AI.method: Behavioral audit of seven open-weight and closed-source LLMs across four U.S. cities; evaluate location recommendations under three iterative prompting conditions that add lifestyle preferences and fair housing paired-testing methodologies.
AI.result: Steering emerges from the interaction of user identity, articulated preferences, and the model's internalized spatial logic about place, opportunity, and locality; not uniform in direction or magnitude; preference-conditioned testing can increase or reconfigure steering; city-level generalization is limited; local/domain expertise is required for fair housing in AI tools.
AI.conclusion: Local and domain expertise are necessary to ensure fair housing commitments are upheld when adopting AI tools that mediate spatial access.

title: Declarative Skills for AI Agents in Knowledge-Grounded Tool-Use Workflows
summary: We study orchestration mechanisms for tool-using AI agents in realistic customer-service workflows over an unstructured knowledge base. We argue that declarative agents -- AI agents equipped with natural-language skill files appended to the system prompt -- are an effective orchestration paradigm. Concretely, we compare (i) a DeclarativeAgent that reads three domain-specific skill files at inference time and decides its own control flow, (ii) an ImperativeAgent based on a programmatic state machine with explicit phases, and (iii) an unscaffolded baseline agent modeled after the $τ$-Knowledge benchmark agent. Our ImperativeAgent is motivated by externalised-control inference as in Recursive Language Models and graph-based orchestration frameworks. We formalise the three agents as policy classes within a decentralised partially-observable Markov decision process and analyse their information-theoretic and structural properties; we then test the predicted differences empirically on five language models and two retrieval regimes. Our results show that retrieval quality is a dominant bottleneck for AI agents: when evidence is incomplete or skewed, all agents degrade substantially, and skill files cannot recover lost performance. Under high-quality retrieval, however, declarative skills consistently improve accuracy on procedural tasks and reduce orchestration errors, while the imperative state machine's brittleness does not reliably improve task success or compliance.

AI.tldr: DeclarativeAgent with domain-specific skill files improves performance under good retrieval; ImperativeAgent is brittle; retrieval quality is the dominant bottleneck for tool-using AI agents in customer-service settings.
AI.motivation: Understand how to orchestrate tool-using AI agents in realistic, unstructured knowledge bases and compare declarative vs imperative orchestration paradigms.
AI.method: Formalize three agent types as policy classes within a decentralized partially-observable Markov decision process; empirically evaluate across five LMs and two retrieval regimes; analyze information-theoretic and structural properties of each approach.
AI.result: Retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck; with incomplete or skewed evidence, all agents degrade and skill files cannot compensate; with high-quality retrieval, declarative skills reduce orchestration errors and improve procedural task accuracy, while imperative state machines are brittle and do not reliably improve task success or compliance.
AI.conclusion: Skill-file–based declarative orchestration is advantageous in well-supported retrieval settings; retrieval reliability is the critical determinant of performance; imperative orchestration offers no consistent gains and is fragile.

title: MedSIGHT: Towards Grounded Visual Comprehension in Medical Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical large vision-language models (Med-LVLMs) have recently achieved remarkable progress in vision-language comprehension and medical image segmentation. However, existing models still struggle to unify these two capabilities, which is essential for achieving clinically reasoning that connects visual findings with semantic interpretation. We present MedSIGHT, a unified framework that equips Med-LVLMs with structured, pixel-level understanding for grounded visual comprehension. MedSIGHT introduces a novel Region Perceiver module that produces region-centric tokens, encoding spatial information directly into representation space of the language model. We further propose a medical region codebook into the LLM vocabulary, allowing the model to generate discrete region codes as symbolic representations of anatomical and pathological regions. These codes are decoded through the Region Perceiver to reconstruct segmentation mask, achieving end-to-end spatial grounding. Lastly, MedSIGHT combines Region Perceiver, Codebook and LLM using our proposed progressive training strategy to gradually aligns these modules stably. Trained on only 72K multimodal instruction pairs, MedSIGHT achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse imaging modalities on both medical comprehension and segmentation tasks.

AI.tldr: MedSIGHT is a unified medical large vision-language model (Med-LVLM) that achieves end-to-end spatial grounding by introducing a Region Perceiver to produce region-centric tokens, a medical region codebook integrated into the LLM vocabulary for discrete region codes, and a progressive training strategy to align components, enabling state-of-the-art medical comprehension and segmentation with 72k multimodal instruction pairs.
AI.motivation: The unification gap in Med-LVLMs between vision-language understanding and precise pixel-level segmentation hinders clinically meaningful reasoning. A unified framework that jointly reasons about visual findings and semantic interpretation—grounded in spatial regions—is needed to improve clinical decision support.
AI.method: Introduce Region Perceiver module that outputs region-centric tokens encoding spatial information into the language model's representation space. Incorporate a medical region codebook into the LLM vocabulary to generate discrete region codes representing anatomical/pathological regions. Allow codes to be decoded via the Region Perceiver to reconstruct segmentation masks, enabling end-to-end spatial grounding. Employ a progressive training strategy to gradually align Region Perceiver, Codebook, and LLM modules. Trained on 72k multimodal instruction pairs.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse medical imaging modalities on both medical comprehension and segmentation tasks, despite the modest training data size (72k pairs).
AI.conclusion: MedSIGHT provides a cohesive architecture that unifies visual understanding and pixel-level grounding in Med-LVLMs, enabling explicit region-level reasoning and end-to-end segmentation generation, with potential to enhance clinical radiology workflows.

title: A Cross-view Fusion Framework for Robust 6-DoF Grasp Pose Estimation
summary: In this paper, we propose a cross-view fusion framework that enhances the robustness of 6-DoF grasp pose estimation in corner views. Our framework alleviates occlusion by incorporating an auxiliary view and avoids the time-consuming, task-agnostic multi-view reconstruction through a post-fusion strategy. To enhance cross-view fusion, we propose a self-supervised contrastive learning strategy that leverages cross-view associations to regularize point cloud features. In brief, a cross-view point pair is considered a match if the two points correspond to the same 3D location, and a non-match if they represent distinct grasp directions. The learning strategy significantly enhances the spatial consistency and direction distinctiveness of point features, thereby facilitating cross-view fusion and improving estimation robustness. Furthermore, we propose a cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module to fuse grasp-relevant geometry into a comprehensive representation. Specifically, the module first aligns the cross-view points and features according to their similarity to enhance the robustness against noise. Subsequently, these points are registered into the cylindrical coordinate frame, emphasizing the rotation-symmetric geometry which is important for grasping. Finally, local self-attention and seed cross-attention layers are alternately employed, respectively enabling interactions within single views and across views, which supports fine-grained representation of grasp-relevant geometry. Our framework achieves strong performance on the GraspNet-1Billion benchmark and in real-world applications. Code is available at https://github.com/KJZhuAutomatic/Cross-view-Grasp.

AI.tldr: A cross-view fusion framework with an auxiliary view, self-supervised contrastive learning, and a cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module to improve 6-DoF grasp pose estimation robustness against occlusion; achieves strong results on GraspNet-1Billion and real-world tasks; code released.
AI.motivation: Occlusion and view variability hinder robust 6-DoF grasp pose estimation. The method aims to enhance cross-view information fusion without costly multi-view reconstruction, improving robustness and representation quality.
AI.method: 1) Introduce an auxiliary view to mitigate occlusion. 2) Propose a self-supervised contrastive learning strategy leveraging cross-view correspondences to regularize point features: cross-view point pairs are matches if they map to the same 3D location, non-matches if they imply different grasp directions, improving spatial consistency and direction discrimination. 3) Propose a cross-view-aligned cylinder integration module: align cross-view points by similarity, register into cylindrical coordinates to emphasize rotation-symmetric geometry, and use alternating local self-attention (within-view) and seed cross-attention (across-view) for fine-grained geometry representation.
AI.result: Excellent cross-view fusion performance with improved robustness; strong results on the GraspNet-1Billion benchmark and in real-world use. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively leverages auxiliary views and cross-view regularization to enhance feature consistency and geometry fusion, improving 6-DoF grasp pose estimation robustness through improved cross-view alignment and rotation-symmetric geometry handling.

title: RECAP: Regression Evaluation for Continual Adaptation of Prompts
summary: Production agentic systems routinely face evolving constraints and must comply from the very next interaction. Scenarios like a tool-call notification changing a compliance threshold or a policy update adding disclosure requirements fit this criteria, having close to no room for errors in production. This proactive adaptation setting is common in deployment, but absent from current benchmarks, which assume either static constraint sets or reactive protocols with evaluation feedback. We introduce RECAP, a benchmark that measures continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level under a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol: prompt optimization methods receive only the constraint specification and must generalize before seeing any test data. Evaluating six methods across four LLMs and three schedules with evolving constraints, we find that these methods show no significant improvement in performance, even after incurring a higher latency. These methods, designed for offline or reactive settings, are inadequate for the proactive paradigm. Our work emphasizes the growing need for designing proactive prompt adaptation methods, where the models must remain robust to evolving needs in deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes RECAP benchmark to evaluate proactive, constraint-driven continual learning in prompt optimization; findings show existing offline/reactive methods underperform in proactive settings.
AI.motivation: Production agentic systems face evolving constraints requiring adaptation from the very first interaction; current benchmarks are static or reactive and fail to test proactive generalization.
AI.method: Introduce RECAP benchmark and evaluation protocol, assessing continual-learning phenomena (forgetting, regression, forward transfer) at the constraint level under a strictly proactive adapt-then-test protocol; tests involve prompt optimization methods that must generalize before any test data, across six methods, four LLMs, and three constraint-evolution schedules.
AI.result: Across configurations, methods show no significant performance gains and incur higher latency; methods designed for offline/reactive settings are inadequate for proactive adaptation.
AI.conclusion: There is a growing need for designing proactive prompt-adaptation methods that remain robust to evolving deployment needs.

title: Quantum-Inspired Trace-Augmented Evidence Selection for Reasoning over Structured Hypothesis Spaces
summary: Large language models (LLMs) now solve a wide range of expert-level exams at or above human level, yet remain brittle on specialised, evidence-intensive domains such as law. On these tasks, errors arise not only from gaps in world knowledge but also from subtle distinctions between pieces of evidence and inconsistent use of supporting evidence. The most common aggregator over sampled chain-of-thought (CoT) traces, majority vote, returns the most popular answer regardless of whether its evidence is actually strongest. We propose to treat the selection of CoT reasoning fragments into a set of evidence as an explicit combinatorial optimisation problem, allowing well-supported but minority hypotheses to override noisy majorities, and to evaluate the approach on legal-reasoning benchmarks that are particularly sensitive to evidence quality. We introduce EP-HUBO (Evidence Pool Higher-Order Binary Optimisation), which generates multiple CoT traces with a small local model, parses fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools, solves a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation per pool with quality-derived weights (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness), and delegates a single adjudication call per question to a frontier model. We evaluate EP-HUBO on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks using both simulated annealing on classical hardware and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine from Quantum Computing Inc. HUBO-style optimisation gives a principled way to aggregate reasoning fragments while preserving minority-but-correct hypotheses, and is most valuable in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not already absorbed the benchmark material.

AI.tldr: EP-HUBO reframes CoT evidence selection as a higher-order binary optimization over evidence pools to improve legal reasoning by leveraging minority, high-quality evidence fragments, evaluated on legal benchmarks with classical simulated annealing and a quantum photonic device.
AI.motivation: Majority-vote aggregation over chain-of-thought traces often discards strong but minority evidence. In evidence-intensive domains like law, evidence quality and distinctiveness are crucial for correct adjudication.
AI.method: Generate multiple CoT traces with a small local model; parse fragments into per-hypothesis evidence pools; solve a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization (HUBO) per pool with weights based on quality metrics (relevance, specificity, distinctiveness); conduct a single adjudication call to a frontier model. Evaluation uses simulated annealing on classical hardware and the Dirac-3 photonic entropy-quantum machine.
AI.result: HUBO-style optimization provides a principled aggregation of reasoning fragments, preserving minority-but-correct hypotheses and outperforming simple majority-vote approaches on two evidence-intensive legal benchmarks; validation on both classical SA and a quantum photonic device.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a principled, scalable method to aggregate CoT fragments in domains where evidence quality is paramount, with particular strength in low-contamination domains where frontier models have not fully absorbed the benchmark material.

title: Breaking the Lock-in: Diversifying Text-to-Image Generation via Representation Modulation
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026. Code is available at: https://github.com/daheekwon/DAVE
summary: Recent text-to-image models built on large-scale Transformer backbones and flow-based objectives deliver strong text-image alignment and high visual quality, yet often produce overly similar samples under a fixed prompt. Existing diversity-enhancement methods alleviate this issue, but typically require expensive sampling or auxiliary optimization, incurring non-trivial overhead. To investigate the root cause of this homogeneity, we examine intermediate Transformer features and observe that the zero-frequency spatial average (DC) component rapidly converges across seeds early in generation, causing early trajectory lock-in that limits downstream variation. Building on this observation, we propose DC Attenuation for diVersity Enhancement (DAVE), a training-free representation-level intervention that selectively attenuates this component in the early regime. DAVE preserves the sampling pipeline with negligible overhead, improving prompt-consistent diversity while maintaining competitive image quality.

AI.tldr: DC attenuation (DAVE) identifies the zero-frequency (DC) spatial component in intermediate Transformer features as the driver of sample homogeneity, and introduces a training-free, representation-level intervention to attenuate this component early in generation, boosting diversity with negligible overhead while preserving image quality.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image models with Transformer backbones achieve strong text-image alignment and quality but generate overly similar samples under a fixed prompt. Existing diversity methods are costly, calling for a low-overhead solution that addresses the root cause of homogeneity.
AI.method: Analyze intermediate Transformer features and find that the zero-frequency (DC) spatial average converges across seeds early in generation, leading to early trajectory lock-in. Propose DAVE, a training-free representation-level intervention that selectively attenuates the DC component in the early regime without altering the sampling pipeline.
AI.result: DAVE improves prompt-consistent diversity across seeds while maintaining competitive image quality, with negligible overhead thanks to its training-free, representation-level nature.
AI.conclusion: DC attenuation is an effective, lightweight strategy to mitigate early trajectory lock-in in diffusion-like text-to-image generation, offering a practical route to richer diversity without sacrificing quality.

title: ActionMap: Robot Policy Learning via Voxel Action Heatmap
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have advanced rapidly across backbones, training recipes, and data scale, yet the action decoder, which converts the backbone's hidden state into a continuous control signal, has barely changed and remains a single-point predictor across the majority of current VLAs. Whether implemented via autoregressive token bins, L1 regression, or flow-matching denoising, the resulting decoder treats the action space as unstructured, leaving the geometric proximity of neighboring actions unexploited during training. To advance this, we introduce ActionMap, a voxel heatmap action head that drops into an existing VLA in place of its native action decoder. For each new action, the head predicts a voxel heatmap over the action space, where each voxel directly stores the probability of the corresponding action. Across LIBERO simulation and real-world Franka manipulation, our heatmap head surpasses two architecturally distinct backbones at matched training steps (e.g., +8.2% over OpenVLA-OFT's L1 regression head on the LIBERO four-suite average), converges at comparable or faster rates on both backbones, and remains markedly more data-efficient at low training data. The cross-backbone consistency indicates that action representation is a real lever for VLA performance, distinct from further backbone or recipe scaling. Project Page: https://github.com/showlab/ActionMap.

AI.tldr: ActionMap introduces a voxel heatmap action head that replaces the standard action decoder in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, enabling structured, probabilistic action representations and improving data efficiency and cross-backbone performance.
AI.motivation: Current VLA decoders predict actions as unstructured, single-point outputs (autoregressive bins, L1, or flow-matching) and do not exploit geometric proximity of neighboring actions, limiting data efficiency and cross-model transfer.
AI.method: Integrate a voxel heatmap head into existing VLA architectures in place of the native action decoder. For each new action, predict a voxel-wise probability heatmap over the action space. Evaluate on LIBERO simulation and real Franka manipulation using two backbone backbones, comparing against L1 regression and flow-matching baselines at matched training steps.
AI.result: The heatmap head outperforms both backbones at the same training steps (e.g., +8.2% over OpenVLA-OFT's L1 regression on LIBERO four-suite average), converges at comparable or faster rates, and is markedly more data-efficient at low training data.
AI.conclusion: Structured action representations via voxel heatmaps are a robust lever for VLA performance, yielding cross-backbone gains and better data efficiency independent of backbone scaling or training recipe changes.

title: ShallowBench: Benchmarking Generative Drug Design Models on Shallow-Pocket Targets
summary: While generative AI models have demonstrated remarkable success in structure-based drug design, they predominantly rely on deep binding pockets and struggle to sample effective ligands for challenging low-pocketability targets, such as the historically "undruggable" oncology targets KRAS and MYC. To address this gap, we introduce ShallowBench, a strictly curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets extracted from CrossDocked2020. By computing the difference between an Alpha Shape "lid" volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume, we successfully isolated targets with low concavity while ensuring sufficient surface area for binding. Evaluating various state-of-the-art generative models reveals weaker predicted binding affinity on these low-concavity interfaces. ShallowBench therefore provides a rigorous benchmark for generative biology models and highlights the necessity of new architectural innovations or loss functions capable of navigating these challenging targets.

AI.tldr: ShallowBench: a strictly curated benchmark of 5,780 shallow-pocket targets extracted from CrossDocked2020, defined via a difference between an Alpha Shape lid volume and the underlying protein atom voxel volume; demonstrates weaker predicted binding affinities of current generative models on low-concavity targets and motivates new architectural or loss-function innovations.
AI.motivation: Current generative AI models for structure-based drug design perform well on deep binding pockets but struggle with low-pocketability targets (e.g., KRAS, MYC). A rigorous benchmark for shallow pockets is needed to drive methodological progress and to assess model limitations on challenging targets.
AI.method: Identify low-concavity pockets by computing the difference between an Alpha Shape lid volume and the corresponding protein atom voxel volume, isolating targets with low concavity yet sufficient binding surface; curate 5,780 targets from CrossDocked2020; evaluate state-of-the-art generative models on these targets to assess predicted binding affinity.
AI.result: Low-concavity interfaces exhibit weaker predicted binding affinity by current generative models, indicating a significant challenge and validating ShallowBench as a discriminative benchmark for evaluating generative biology models and identifying model limitations on shallow pockets.
AI.conclusion: ShallowBench provides a rigorous benchmark to stimulate development of new architectural innovations or loss functions tailored to sampling ligands for shallow pockets. It highlights the need for methodological advances to handle challenging targets, though the evaluation relies on docking/prediction metrics and static structures which may limit generalization.

title: Accounting for Context: Shaping Moral Credences for Value Alignment
summary: Ensuring that agent behaviours are aligned with human moral values inevitably raises the problem of how to account for the plurality of moral perspectives that societies -- and even individuals -- typically adopt. Work on moral uncertainty proposes mechanisms to fairly and democratically aggregate evaluations of actions across different moral theories. However, this paper argues that one needs to account for contextual factors when aggregating moral evaluations. For example, consequentialist perspectives assume an ability to accurately determine how an agent's actions change the world; an assumption that often does not hold in real world settings. We, therefore, formalise agent decision making under moral uncertainty, while also accounting for these kinds of contextual factors. We thereby show that a seemingly commonsensical property -- the weak Pareto principle -- is violated. We argue that this apparent problem is, in fact, a variation of Simpson's paradox, and hence reveals the limitations of aggregation mechanisms that ignore the impact of contextual factors.

AI.tldr: Formalizes agent decision making under moral uncertainty with contextual factors and shows weak Pareto can fail due to a Simpson's paradox variant, highlighting limits of context-ignorant aggregation.
AI.motivation: Addresses how to fairly aggregate diverse moral evaluations while recognizing that real-world context influences judgments; existing approaches often assume accurate outcome prediction and ignore context.
AI.method: Formal modeling of decision making under moral uncertainty that includes contextual factors affecting moral evaluation (e.g., accuracy of determining consequences); analytical demonstration that the weak Pareto property can be violated; interpretation as a Simpson's paradox variant.
AI.result: Demonstrates violation of the weak Pareto principle within the context-aware moral uncertainty model; identifies the phenomenon as a variant of Simpson's paradox, illustrating aggregation limitations when ignoring context.
AI.conclusion: Aggregation schemes for moral evaluation must incorporate contextual factors to avoid misleading outcomes; the observed Pareto violation underscores fundamental limits of context-agnostic aggregation in moral philosophy and AI alignment.

title: VideoSEG-O3: A Multi-turn Reinforcement Learning Framework for Reasoning Video Object Segmentation
summary: Reasoning Video Object Segmentation (RVOS) demands a sophisticated integration of temporal dynamics, spatial details, and linguistic reasoning to achieve precise pixel-level localization. Existing methods are limited to reasoning over fixed initial inputs and lack the capacity to actively acquire further visual evidence, which is often essential for resolving complex references in long or intricate videos. To address this, we propose \textbf{VideoSEG-O3}, the first multi-turn reinforcement learning framework for RVOS that emulates the human \textit{``coarse-to-fine''} cognitive process. It employs a \textit{multi-turn temporal-spatial chain-of-thought} to capture fine-grained details by iteratively pinpointing critical intervals and keyframes. Additionally, to enable the policy to perceive segmentation quality beyond mere text probability of \texttt{[SEG]} during the RL stage, we introduce \textit{SEG-aware logit calibration}, which integrates pixel-wise segmentation feedback directly into the token-level logits. Furthermore, we design a \textit{decoupled thinking trace} to hierarchically decompose the reasoning process into temporal, spatial, and linguistic dimensions, and construct \textbf{VTS-CoT}, a specialized cold-start dataset featuring comprehensive reasoning trajectories. The code and models will be released at https://github.com/Dmmm1997/VideoSEG-O3.

AI.tldr: VideoSEG-O3 introduces a multi-turn reinforcement learning framework for RVOS with coarse-to-fine reasoning, SEG-aware logit calibration, and a decoupled thinking trace, plus a reasoning-trajectory dataset (VTS-CoT).
AI.motivation: To overcome fixed-input reasoning and passive evidence gathering in RVOS, enabling active, iterative planning over long/intricate videos through a coarse-to-fine, temporal-spatial–linguistic multi-turn process.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-turn temporal-spatial chain-of-thought in a reinforcement learning framework for RVOS; introduces SEG-aware logit calibration to couple segmentation feedback with token logits; employs a decoupled thinking trace to separate temporal, spatial, and linguistic reasoning; releases a cold-start dataset VTS-CoT for comprehensive reasoning trajectories; code and models to be released.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The work outlines a novel RL-based, interpretable reasoning framework for RVOS that could enable active evidence gathering and finer segmentation, contingent on thorough empirical validation and comparison with strong baselines.

title: T-GMP: Terrain-conditioned Generative Motion Priors for Versatile and Natural Humanoid Locomotion
summary: Achieving both anthropomorphic naturalness and robust terrain traversal remains a fundamental challenge in humanoid locomotion. Existing Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches typically rely on fixed motion priors, limiting their adaptability to varying environments. We propose Terrain-conditioned Generative Motion Priors (T-GMP), a module that captures a terrain-conditioned latent motion manifold from a few expert state-terrain demonstrations using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). The learned priors enable smooth style transitions, facilitating a unified policy that adapts to terrain variations. We integrate T-GMP into an adversarial learning pipeline with our proposed Foothold Penalty, where a discriminator dynamically modulates naturalness constraints conditioned on local terrain features, guiding the generation of versatile and human-like motions. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing baselines in traversal success rate and motion smoothness, while preserving biomimetically natural and physically coordinated motions.

AI.tldr: Terrain-conditioned generative motion priors (T-GMP) learned via CVAE to adapt humanoid motion to varying terrains, integrated with adversarial learning and a Foothold Penalty to improve traversal and naturalness.
AI.motivation: Current RL approaches rely on fixed motion priors, limiting adaptability to diverse environments; a terrain-conditioned latent motion manifold can enable unified, robust locomotion across terrains.
AI.method: Learn a terrain-conditioned latent motion manifold using a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) from limited expert state–terrain demonstrations; integrate into an adversarial pipeline where a discriminator modulates naturalness constraints based on local terrain features; apply a Foothold Penalty to guide generation toward versatile, human-like foothold strategies and smooth motion.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in traversal success rate and motion smoothness while maintaining biomimetic naturalness and physically coordinated motions.
AI.conclusion: Terrain-conditioned priors via CVAE and adversarial naturalness control improve humanoid locomotion across terrains, enabling smooth style transitions and robust, natural movements.

title: MSAIC-Net: A Multi-Scale Attention and Imbalance-Aware Contrastive Network for ECG-Based Myocardial Substrate Abnormality Detection
summary: Myocardial substrate abnormalities, such as myocardial scar and myocardial infarction (MI), are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes. Electrocardiography (ECG) provides a low-cost and widely available tool for detecting these abnormalities, but ECG-based detection remains challenging due to heterogeneous lead-dependent manifestations, high-dimensional multi-lead signals, class imbalance, and the limited interpretability of deep learning models. We propose a multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network (MSAIC-Net) for ECG-based myocardial substrate abnormality detection. MSAIC-Net employs parallel atrous convolutional branches to extract ECG features across multiple temporal receptive fields. %, enabling the model to capture both local and longer-range temporal patterns. Channel attention is then used to adaptively reweight informative lead-wise and feature-channel representations. To address class imbalance and improve feature separability, we introduce a novel imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy that encourages samples from the same class to form compact representations while increasing separation between abnormal and normal samples. Lead-wise permutation importance is further incorporated to quantify the contribution of each ECG lead and improve model interpretability. The proposed method was evaluated on two complementary datasets: a low-data institutional cohort from the University of Virginia (UVA) Health System for myocardial scar classification and the large-scale public PTB-XL dataset from PhysioNet for MI identification. Experimental results show that MSAIC-Net outperforms baseline models, with particularly pronounced improvements in the low-data UVA cohort. Overall, the proposed framework provides an effective and interpretable approach for ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities.

AI.tldr: MSAIC-Net combines multi-scale atrous convolution, channel attention, and imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning with lead-wise interpretability for ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities, validated on UVA (low-data) and PTB-XL datasets, showing superior performance especially in low-data settings.
AI.motivation: Detecting myocardial substrate abnormalities (scar/MI) from ECG is challenging due to heterogeneity across leads, high-dimensional multi-lead signals, class imbalance, and limited interpretability of deep models. The work seeks improved accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: A multi-scale attention-enhanced convolutional network (MSAIC-Net) with parallel atrous convolution branches to capture multi-temporal features. Channel attention reweights lead-wise and feature-channel representations. An imbalance-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy improves feature separability and handles class imbalance. Lead-wise permutation importance is used for interpretability. Evaluated on UVA low-data scar classification and PTB-XL MI identification.
AI.result: MSAIC-Net outperforms baseline models on both datasets, with particularly notable improvements in the low-data UVA cohort, and provides improved interpretability through lead-wise permutation importance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework offers an effective and more interpretable approach for ECG-based detection of myocardial substrate abnormalities, with demonstrated generalization across a low-data institutional cohort and a large-scale public dataset.

title: Exploring Agentic Tool-Calling Decisions via Uncertainty-Aligned Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents often make suboptimal tool-use decisions, including unsupported tool invocation and hallucinated direct responses, which may accumulate errors throughout multi-step interactions. Existing approaches mainly improve these behaviors through inference-time correction or coarse-grained reward signals based on decision outcomes and structured checklists, leaving the uncertainty characteristics of agent decisions underexplored. We observe that decision-oriented reinforcement learning tends to weaken the uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions, resulting in overconfident mistakes and weaker exploration signals. Therefore, we propose TRUST, which incorporates uncertainty quantification into reward design as a repulsive force for maintaining uncertainty separation, and labels lightweight key-turn annotations for unified post-training of multi-turn trajectories. Experimental results across diverse tool-use benchmarks show that TRUST consistently enhances both decision quality and agent performance while maintaining more reliable uncertainty estimates during optimization.

AI.tldr: TRUST introduces uncertainty-aware reward shaping to preserve uncertainty separation between correct and incorrect actions in LLM-based multi-turn tool-use, plus lightweight key-turn annotations for post-training trajectories; it improves decision quality and overall performance while maintaining reliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on inference-time correction or coarse reward signals and do not explicitly manage the uncertainty characteristics of decisions. Decision-oriented RL often collapses uncertainty separation, leading to overconfident mistakes and weaker exploration. There is a need to explicitly quantify and leverage uncertainty during training for better tool-use decisions.
AI.method: Integrate uncertainty quantification into the reward design as a repulsive force that maintains separation between correct and incorrect actions. Use lightweight key-turn annotations to enable unified post-training of multi-turn trajectories.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse tool-use benchmarks show TRUST consistently improves decision quality and agent performance while preserving more reliable uncertainty estimates during optimization.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating uncertainty-aware reward shaping and lightweight trajectory annotations enables more reliable decision-making and better performance in multi-turn tool-use scenarios, addressing overconfidence and exploration shortcomings observed in standard decision-oriented RL.

title: AdaGRPO: A Capability-Aware Adaptive Enhancement for Flow-based GRPO
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has demonstrated remarkable success in aligning text-to-image (T2I) flow models with human preferences. However, we have identified that the learning loop of current flow-based GRPO is fundamentally decoupled from the learner's current capability, suffering from critical blind spots at both prompt selection and advantage estimation: (i) Existing methods sample prompts randomly, overlooking the substantial impact of data selection on reinforcement learning (RL) efficacy--a factor proven crucial in GRPO for large language models; (ii) They evaluate sample quality solely relying on intra-group statistics, lacking a global perspective to accurately measure true policy improvement. To address these issues, we propose Adaptive GRPO (AdaGRPO), a novel capability-aware RL algorithm tailored for flow models. Specifically, AdaGRPO consists of two principal components: (i) Online Curriculum Filtering Strategy: Dynamically tracks the model's proficiency and adaptively selects prompts that best match its current learning boundary; (ii) Cross-Level Advantage Fusion: Synergistically integrates fine-grained intra-group advantages with macro-level global advantages, providing a comprehensive and unbiased policy evaluation. As a lightweight, plug-and-play module, AdaGRPO can be seamlessly integrated with existing frameworks such as Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO, and Flow-CPS. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AdaGRPO consistently drives performance gains while significantly stabilizes GRPO training for flow models.

AI.tldr: AdaGRPO introduces capability-aware RL for flow-based GRPO with (i) Online Curriculum Filtering to adapt prompt sampling to the model's learning boundary, and (ii) Cross-Level Advantage Fusion to combine intra-group and global policy advantages, yielding improved performance and training stability and offering plug-and-play integration with Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO, and Flow-CPS.
AI.motivation: Current GRPO loops for text-to-image flow models are decoupled from the learner's capability, relying on random prompt sampling and intra-group evaluation, which hurts RL efficacy and bias reduction. There is a need for data-selection-aware training and unbiased, global policy evaluation.
AI.method: Two main components: (i) Online Curriculum Filtering Strategy that dynamically tracks the model's proficiency and adaptively selects prompts aligned with its current learning boundary; (ii) Cross-Level Advantage Fusion that fuses fine-grained intra-group advantages with macro-level global advantages for unbiased policy evaluation. Lightweight, plug-and-play module that can be integrated into Flow-GRPO, DanceGRPO, Flow-CPS.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AdaGRPO consistently improves performance and significantly stabilizes GRPO training for flow models.
AI.conclusion: AdaGRPO provides a capability-aware enhancement to GRPO for flow models, improving data efficiency, stability, and overall performance while remaining easily integrable with existing frameworks.

title: LIMMT: Less is More for Motion Tracking
summary: We argue that high-quality motion data can steer tracking policies toward better optimization trajectories early in training. In this work, we introduce LIMMT (Less Is More for Motion Tracking). To our knowledge, this is the first data-centric study for physics-based humanoid motion tracking. We go beyond simply removing low-quality and erroneous clips, but define motion data quality through three dimensions: physics feasibility, diversity, and complexity. We show that even training with under 3% of AMASS yields better tracking performance than training with the full dataset. We further conduct data cleaning on the estimated web-sourced mocap data. Extensive experiments and analyses validate the effectiveness of our framework.

AI.tldr: Data-centric motion-tracking study showing high-quality data can steer optimization trajectories; LIMMT leverages limited data with physics feasibility, diversity, and complexity; even ~3% of AMASS can outperform full AMASS; data cleaning of web mocap improves results; extensive experiments validate the approach.
AI.motivation: Improve humanoid motion tracking by using data quality to guide training trajectories early in optimization.
AI.method: Define data quality along three axes (physics feasibility, diversity, complexity); train using limited, high-quality AMASS data; apply data cleaning to web-sourced mocap; present LIMMT framework.
AI.result: Training with under 3% of AMASS yields better tracking performance than using the full AMASS dataset; extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric approach that emphasizes physics-feasible, diverse, and appropriately complex motion data can steer tracking policies toward better optimization trajectories in physics-based humanoid motion tracking.

title: Flatland: The Adventures of Gradient Descent with Large Step Sizes
comment: Accepted for the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: The training of neural networks often entails objective functions that are not globally $L$-smooth. For these functions, it is both theoretically and practically difficult to reply to the question: what is the largest possible step size that ensures the convergence of gradient descent (GD)? We address this longstanding open question in deep learning by providing a unifying definition of "large" step sizes that requires only local Lipschitz (or even Hölder) continuity of the gradient. We design first-order adaptive methods that provably yield large step sizes and show that they operate at the edge of stability (EoS) right from the start of the training.
