AI.method: During meta-training, WIZARD learns a mapping from task evidence (language instruction + demonstration video) to expert LoRA updates for a frozen VLA policy, enabling a single forward pass to predict adaptation weights. At test time, it does not rely on target-task action labels or optimization. The approach leverages LoRA adapters to incur minimal parameter changes in weight space.
AI.result: On LIBERO, WIZARD yields up to ~2x improvement on unseen dataset collections and up to ~14x on unseen tasks. On a real Franka Emika Panda, it consistently outperforms a real-domain adapted baseline, indicating task-level specialization beyond simulation.
AI.conclusion: Weight-space meta-learning of adapters enables scalable, task-specific specialization for VLA policies without fine-tuning or task-specific demonstrations at deployment, bridging simulation and real-world performance.

title: Product units in gated recurrent units improve nuclear-mass prediction
summary: The prediction of masses of atomic nuclei using machine learning can complement theoretical models and advance the exploration of poorly known domains of the nuclear chart. We propose a machine learning technique based on gated recurrent units (GRU), which have demonstrated competitive performance in nuclear-mass prediction by exploiting long-term dependencies. By integrating multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations within recurrent units, we report significant improvements in nuclear-mass prediction. Computations are performed in the complex domain to jointly capture amplitude and phase dynamics. For interpolation and temporal-extrapolation tasks based on the atomic mass evaluation (AME2016 and AME2020), the complex additive-multiplicative product-unit gated recurrent unit (AM-PU-GRU) model consistently achieves the lowest prediction errors, with an interpolation RMSE of 0.227 $\pm$ 0.004 MeV and an extrapolation RMSE of 0.179 $\pm$ 0.015 MeV. These results surpass other state-of-the-art machine learning models and also outperform the real-valued GRU baseline and product-unit ablation variants, while remaining robust to different theoretical priors, including WS4 and SEMF. Our findings establish complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks as a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction.

AI.tldr: A complex-valued product-unit GRU (AM-PU-GRU) with multiplicative interactions improves nuclear-mass predictions: achieves lowest RMSE on interpolation (0.227±0.004 MeV) and extrapolation (0.179±0.015 MeV) against AME2016/2020, outperforming real-valued GRU, ablations, and priors (WS4/SEMF); establishes a new benchmark.
AI.motivation: Enhance predictive accuracy for atomic-nucleus masses, especially in poorly known regions, by leveraging complex-domain dynamics and multiplicative product-unit interactions within recurrent networks to capture long-range dependencies.
AI.method: Develop complex-valued additive-multiplicative product-unit GRU (AM-PU-GRU) that performs computations in the complex domain, integrating multiplicative interactions and product-unit transformations into recurrent units. Train and evaluate on AME2016 and AME2020 datasets for interpolation and temporal-extrapolation tasks; compare against state-of-the-art ML models, real-valued GRU, and ablation variants; assess robustness to nuclear mass priors like WS4 and SEMF.
AI.result: AM-PU-GRU achieves interpolation RMSE = 0.227 ± 0.004 MeV and extrapolation RMSE = 0.179 ± 0.015 MeV, consistently outperforming other ML models, the real-valued GRU baseline, and product-unit ablations; robust to different theoretical priors (WS4, SEMF).
AI.conclusion: Complex-valued product-unit recurrent networks establish a new benchmark for sequence-based nuclear-mass prediction, offering improved accuracy and robustness over prior models.

title: A Geometric Gaussian Mixture Representation of Plane Curves
summary:
We introduce a user defined probabilistic polygonal representation for plane curves. Given a curve, we select vertices on the curve and connect consecutive vertices by line segments to obtain a polygonal approximation. Each segment is equipped with a user defined uncertainty parameter in the normal direction. This yields a collection of thin probabilistic geometric primitives that retain the geometrz of the underlying curve while extending it beyond the idealized deterministic one dimensional formulation.
  For each segment, we define a Random Variable that is uniform distributed in the tangent direction of the segment and Gaussian distributed in the normal direction of the segment. By matching the first and the second central moments, this construction induces a Gaussian component whose mean lies at the segment midpoint and whose covariance encodes both tangential and normal uncertainty. Combining the segment wise components with appropriate weights yields a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) representation of the user defined probabilistic polygonal representation of the plane curve.
  The proposed framework provides an analytically tractable probabilistic model that preserves local geometry, and uncertainty in the normal direction. It applies to smooth, closed, open, non regular, and self intersecting plane curves, allows adaptive discretization and varying uncertainty in the normal direction, and as a result supports uncertainty aware geometric modeling. Experiments on a collection of canonical plane curves show that the resulting GMM capture local tangent, local normal, and local arc length; resulting in the global shape of the underlying curves to be truthfully captured as well. The representation is particularly relevant for applications in uncertainty aware CAD and digital twins, probabilistic obstacle modeling in robotics, and probabilistic trajectory planning.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic polygonal representation for plane curves using segment-wise tangent-uniform and normal-Gaussian uncertainties, yielding a Gaussian Mixture Model that preserves local geometry and supports uncertainty-aware CAD, robotics, and planning.
AI.motivation: Address uncertainty in geometric representations of plane curves while retaining local geometric fidelity. Allow adaptive discretization and varying normal uncertainty to model real-world imprecision and noise in curves (smooth, open, closed, non-regular, self-intersecting).
AI.method: Construct polygonal chain by selecting vertices; for each segment, define a random variable with tangential component uniform along the segment and normal component Gaussian. Match first and second moments to obtain a Gaussian with mean at the segment midpoint and covariance encoding tangential and normal uncertainties. Combine segment-wise Gaussians with weights to form a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) representing the probabilistic polygonal curve. Applicable to various curve types, with adaptive discretization and varying normal uncertainty.
AI.result: The resulting GMM captures local tangent, local normal, and local arc length, leading to faithful recovery of the curve's global shape. Empirical demonstrations on canonical plane curves show the method preserves geometry and uncertainty information. The framework yields an analytically tractable probabilistic model.
AI.conclusion: A tractable probabilistic framework for plane curves that preserves local geometry and encodes normal-direction uncertainty. It supports uncertainty-aware geometric modeling and has potential applications in CAD, digital twins, probabilistic robotics, and trajectory planning, with flexible discretization and robustness to curve irregularities.

title: polyDAG: Polynomial Acyclicity Constraints for Efficient Continuous Causal Discovery in Visual Semantic Graphs
summary: Modern image-analysis pipelines often convert images into structured semantic variables, such as facial attributes, object concepts, and scene descriptors. Learning directed dependencies among these variables can produce interpretable visual semantic graphs, but continuous directed acyclic graph learning is limited by the cost of enforcing acyclicity. We present polyDAG, a polynomial acyclicity framework for efficient continuous causal discovery in visual semantic graphs. polyDAG replaces the matrix-exponential acyclicity constraint with a finite polynomial trace constraint and proves that the new constraint is zero exactly for acyclic graphs. We further derive a geometric-series implementation that avoids the explicit summation loop while preserving the same acyclicity condition. Experiments on synthetic Erdos-Renyi graphs and CelebA facial visual attributes show that polyDAG improves efficiency and structure recovery. Averaged over the revised synthetic protocol with d in {100, 200, 500}, polyDAG reduces mean structural Hamming distance from 318.4 to 285.4 and improves mean F1 score from 0.725 to 0.756. At 100 nodes, the geometric variant runs in 3.44 seconds compared with 5.16 seconds for the exponential baseline, corresponding to a 33.4 percent speedup. Code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/wenhaoz-fengcai/polyDAG.

AI.tldr: polyDAG introduces a polynomial acyclicity constraint for continuous DAG learning in visual semantic graphs, achieving faster training and improved structure recovery compared to the matrix-exponential baseline.
AI.motivation: Enabling efficient continuous DAG learning for visual semantic graphs by replacing the expensive matrix-exponential acyclicity constraint with a finite polynomial trace constraint, ensuring exact acyclicity.
AI.method: Develop a polynomial acyclicity constraint and a geometric-series implementation to avoid explicit summation. Train DAGs on synthetic Erdos-Renyi graphs and CelebA facial attributes; compare against exponential baseline using SHD and F1; report runtime.
AI.result: PolyDAG improves SHD from 318.4 to 285.4 and F1 from 0.725 to 0.756; at 100 nodes, geometric variant is 3.44s vs 5.16s (33.4% speedup). Code available.
AI.conclusion: The polynomial acyclicity framework provides both improved structure recovery and computational efficiency for continuous causal discovery in visual semantic graphs, with publicly available code for reproduction.

title: Beyond Waypoints: A Trajectory-Centric Waypointing Paradigm for Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments (VLN-CE) requires agents to follow natural-language instructions while navigating in real-world-like environments. Most VLN-CE approach\-es adopt a three-stage framework: a waypoint predictor proposes navigable waypoints, and a navigator selects the best waypoint, with a low-level controller executing the movement to it. However, this decoupled paradigm often leads to unreachable waypoints or inconsistencies between planning and control. In this work, instead of predicting isolated waypoints, we introduce a novel paradigm called Trajectory Waypoint, which grounds each candidate waypoint in an executable trajectory. To realize this, we design a Trajectory Waypoint Predictor formulated as a TSDF-guided diffusion policy, which steers trajectory generation away from obstacles, inherently ensuring the reachability of the predicted waypoints. We further propose a trajectory-enhanced navigator that injects the associated trajectory as additional information for planning, enabling strict consistency between high-level semantic decisions and low-level execution. Extensive experiments on the VLN-CE benchmark show that our Trajectory Waypoint paradigm achieves superior performance over the baselines.

AI.tldr: Grounds each candidate waypoint in an executable trajectory via a TSDF-guided diffusion policy, improving reachability and planning-execution consistency in VLN-CE.
AI.motivation: Decoupled waypoint prediction and navigation lead to unreachable waypoints and planning-control inconsistencies; integrate trajectory-grounded planning to ensure realism and feasibility.
AI.method: Trajectory Waypoint Predictor using TSDF-guided diffusion to generate obstacle-avoiding trajectories; trajectory-enhanced navigator that uses the associated trajectory to guide planning.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on VLN-CE benchmark, demonstrating improved reachability and consistency.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory Waypoint paradigm offers a more coherent and effective framework for VLN-CE; potential trade-offs include computational cost and TSDF quality.

title: Evidence-Grounded Ensemble Diagnosis of 802.11 Packet Captures: A Multi-Stage Pipeline with Deterministic Reliability Scoring
summary:
Diagnosing 802.11 packet captures requires expert protocol knowledge, is slow, inconsistent across engineers, and unscalable. LLM-based approaches sound plausible but fabricate protocol events absent from captures (especially truncated traces), produce uncalibrated confidence scores, and suffer evaluation bias when golden references are co-produced by the model under test. We introduce PROBE (Protocol Reasoning Over evidence-Based Ensembles), a multi-stage pipeline addressing all three failures. It integrates (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability, (ii) multi-run, multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinion and progressive obfuscation, (iii) a verdict-aware evidence framework treating absence of failure evidence as contributing evidence, and (iv) a fully deterministic composite reliability score from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement without LLM self-assessment.
  On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures (104 capture-reviewer pairs), single-pass LLM analysis raises weighted evidence F1 from 0.871 (expert baseline) to 0.912 but misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops below baseline (0.842) as majority voting amplifies conservative verdicts: 50% of confirmed failures are misclassified as 'no issue' or 'insufficient evidence.' Adding evidence-grounded reconciliation achieves 0.957 F1, a 96% auto-accept rate, and a worst-case floor above 0.70. LLM self-reported confidence clusters at 0.95 regardless of difficulty (71% report exactly 0.95), confirming it is uninformative. We also introduce a model-agnostic evaluation framework using per-field assertion matching, eliminating circular bias from model-co-produced golden references.

AI.tldr: PROBE is a multi-stage, evidence-based pipeline for diagnosing 802.11 traces with LLMs, addressing fabrication, calibration, and evaluation bias; it uses deterministic normalization, multi-run ensembles, verdict-aware evidence, and a model-agnostic evaluation framework to achieve high F1 and auto-accept rates.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing 802.11 captures with LLMs is error-prone due to fabricated protocol events, uncalibrated confidence, and biased evaluation when golden references are model-produced; a robust, calibration-free framework is needed for scalable, accurate diagnostics.
AI.method: Four-part pipeline: (i) deterministic PCAP-to-text normalization with frame-level verifiability; (ii) multi-run, multi-candidate ensembles with optional cross-model second opinions and progressive obfuscation; (iii) verdict-aware evidence framework treating absence of failure evidence as evidence; (iv) fully deterministic composite reliability score from evidence validity, run-to-run stability, and cross-model agreement without LLM self-assessment.
AI.result: On 87 enterprise Wi-Fi captures (104 capture-reviewer pairs), single-pass LLM analysis raises weighted evidence F1 from 0.871 (expert baseline) to 0.912 but misses critical frames in 35% of cases. Naive ensemble voting drops below baseline (0.842) due to majority voting bias toward conservative verdicts. Evidence-grounded reconciliation achieves 0.957 F1, a 96% auto-accept rate, and a worst-case floor above 0.70. LLM self-reported confidence clusters at 0.95 regardless of difficulty, confirming it is uninformative. A model-agnostic evaluation framework using per-field assertion matching eliminates circular bias from model-co-produced golden references.
AI.conclusion: PROBE effectively addresses fabrication, calibration, and evaluation bias in LLM-based protocol diagnosis. It delivers higher accuracy and reliability than single-pass or naive ensemble approaches, supports scalable evaluation without model self-assessment biases, and introduces a model-agnostic, field-level evaluation framework for robust benchmarking.

title: DRIFT: From Robustness Gaps to Invariance Manifolds for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of generative image models challenges existing AI-generated image detectors, particularly in open-world settings with unseen generators. Recent training-free approaches measure robustness gaps in frozen vision foundation models (VFMs), detecting fakes via perturbation-induced embedding drift. However, these methods rely on fixed invariance geometry inherited from pretraining and lack principled adaptation to the detection task. We instead formulate AI-generated image detection as learning a structured invariance manifold of real images under one-class supervision. Building upon a frozen VFM, we introduce lightweight projection heads that decompose representation space into complementary robust and fragile subspaces. The robust subspace is explicitly trained to suppress variations induced by physically plausible imaging transformations, approximating tangent directions of a real-image manifold, while the fragile subspace retains sensitivity to edit-like perturbations. A structured ordering margin enforces hierarchical separation between physical invariance and edit-induced variability, enabling detection as a margin-violation test relative to the learned manifold. At inference, multi-scale patch-wise drift under both transformation families yields a dual-channel invariance signature and interpretable localization. Extensive experiments demonstrate strong open-world generalization across unseen generators and resolutions, consistently outperforming training-free robustness-based baselines while providing interpretable invariance-violation maps.

AI.tldr: A principled, training-free-style approach to AI-generated image detection framed as learning a structured invariance manifold of real images under one-class supervision. It decomposes the representation into robust and fragile subspaces using a frozen vision foundation model, enforces a margin-based separation, and uses multi-scale patch-wise drift for open-world detection and localization.
AI.motivation: Open-world AI-generated image detection remains difficult due to unseen generators and distribution shifts. Existing training-free methods rely on fixed invariance geometry and lack task-adaptive principled modeling. Astructured, learned manifold with explicit invariances can improve generalization and interpretability.
AI.method: Leverage a frozen vision foundation model (VFM) with lightweight projection heads that partition representation space into a robust subspace (invariance to physically plausible imaging transformations) and a fragile subspace (sensitive to edit-like perturbations). Train with one-class supervision to shape the robust subspace as tangent directions of the real-image manifold; enforce a structured ordering margin to separate invariance from perturbation-induced variability; perform inference via multi-scale patch-wise drift under transformation families to obtain a dual-channel invariance signature and localization maps.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show strong open-world generalization to unseen generators and resolutions, outperforming training-free robustness-based baselines, and providing interpretable invariance-violation maps.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a principled, structured invariance framework for AI-generated image detection built on a frozen VFM with learned robust/fragile subspaces and margin-based separation, enabling open-world detection and interpretable localization.

title: CAPE: Contrastive Action-conditioned Parallel Encoding for Embodied Planning
summary: Embodied agents need to predict the future consequences of candidate actions in order to plan effectively before execution. Existing visual dynamics models learn by reconstructing future visual states or rolling out dense latent representations, which spreads learning capacity across visually salient but planning-irrelevant content rather than the action-conditioned changes that drive manipulation outcomes. We propose CAPE, a Contrastive Action-conditioned Parallel Encoding framework that learns visual dynamics by distinguishing the future outcomes induced by different action sequences. Given an initial observation and a candidate action sequence, CAPE decodes the full future latent trajectory in a single forward pass and is trained with a Goal-Convergent Contrastive Objective that aligns predictions corresponding to the same future outcome while separating those corresponding to different outcomes. On real-world DROID and zero-shot transfer to RoboCasa, CAPE substantially outperforms prior baselines on future-state retrieval, offline action matching, and closed-loop planning, while notably reducing planning-time inference cost at long prediction horizons.

AI.tldr: CAPE introduces a contrastive, action-conditioned, single-pass future trajectory predictor for embodied agents, improving planning and transfer while reducing inference cost.
AI.motivation: Standard visual dynamics models allocate capacity to reconstruct or roll out visually rich but planning-irrelevant content, hindering action-conditioned manipulation planning; need goal-aligned, efficient future prediction.
AI.method: Proposes CAPE: given initial observation and candidate action sequence, decodes full future latent trajectory in a single pass; trains with a Goal-Convergent Contrastive Objective to align same-outcome predictions and separate different-outcomes; uses parallel encoding to condition on actions; evaluation on real-world DROID and zero-shot RoboCasa transfer.
AI.result: Significant gains on future-state retrieval, offline action matching, and closed-loop planning; improved planning efficiency at long horizons; successful zero-shot transfer to RoboCasa.
AI.conclusion: Action-conditioned, contrastive visual dynamics with single-pass decoding offers robust, transferable, and computationally efficient planning-ready representations for embodied agents.

title: GlucoFM-Bench: Benchmarking Time-Series Foundation Models for Blood Glucose Forecasting
summary: Blood glucose forecasting models are foundational for modern diabetes management systems, as reliable short-term predictions can enable proactive interventions, support automated insulin delivery, and reduce the risk of hypo- and hyperglycemic events. From a modeling perspective, glucose forecasting poses unique challenges due to heterogeneous physiological dynamics across diabetes populations. Traditional machine learning and deep learning models have been extensively evaluated for glucose prediction, yet recent time-series foundation models (TSFMs) remain much less studied in this setting. To bridge this gap, we present GlucoFM-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark evaluating state-of-the-art TSFMs alongside supervised deep learning models for blood glucose forecasting. We assess eight representative architectures, including pre-trained TSFMs, time-series large language models, and task-specific deep learning models, across 15 publicly available diabetes-relevant datasets comprising 1,117 individuals with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, prediabetes, and no diabetes. Models are evaluated under zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols, with systematic variation in context length and prediction horizon. Across datasets, pre-trained TSFMs, especially Chronos-2 and TimesFM, show strong zero-shot and few-shot transfer, with the best zero-shot model performing within 5% of the best full-shot supervised model. Yet, when task-specific data are abundant, a lightweight LSTM remains strongest, outperforming TSFMs by 4--21% under full-shot training. Stratified analyses reveal persistent challenges in T1D cohorts and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges, highlighting the need for evaluation beyond aggregate error metrics. Together, GlucoFM-Bench provides a standardized and reproducible foundation for evaluating, comparing, and improving foundation models for blood glucose forecasting.

AI.tldr: Pre-trained time-series foundation models (TSFMs) show strong zero-shot and few-shot glucose forecasting across diverse datasets, but with abundant task-specific data, a lightweight LSTM can outperform TSFMs; GlucoFM-Bench standardizes evaluation of TSFMs and supervised models for blood glucose forecasting across 15 datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of evaluation and benchmarking for time-series foundation models in blood glucose forecasting, given heterogeneity across diabetes populations and the need for robust, generalizable predictions.
AI.method: Evaluate eight architectures (including pre-trained TSFMs, time-series LLMs, and task-specific DL models) across 15 public diabetes-relevant datasets (1,117 individuals: T1D, T2D, prediabetes, non-diabetic). Use zero-shot, few-shot, and full-shot protocols with varying context lengths and prediction horizons; compare performance and stratify by cohorts and glycemic ranges.
AI.result: Pre-trained TSFMs (notably Chronos-2 and TimesFM) deliver strong zero-shot/few-shot transfer; the top zero-shot model is within 5% of the best full-shot supervised model. However, with ample task-specific data, a lightweight LSTM remains strongest, beating TSFMs by 4–21% under full-shot training. T1D cohorts and hypo-/hyperglycemic ranges remain challenging; aggregate metrics may mask these difficulties. The work provides a standardized framework for benchmarking foundation models in glucose forecasting.
AI.conclusion: GlucoFM-Bench offers a reproducible, standardized platform to evaluate, compare, and enhance foundation models for blood glucose forecasting, highlighting when TSFMs are advantageous (low data regimes) and when task-specific models prevail (ample data) while underscoring persistent clinical challenges.

title: SVHighlights: Towards Extremely Long Sport Video Highlight Detection
comment: Accepted to KDD 2026 (Datasets and Benchmarks Track). Project Page: https://leedongkyu2019.github.io/SVHighlights/
summary: While highlight detection for long-form videos is of great practical importance, most existing methods remain limited to short-form content, largely due to the absence of a suitable benchmark. To bridge this gap, we introduce SVHighlights, to the best of our knowledge, the first benchmark for highlight detection in extremely long sports videos, each exceeding one hour in duration, across multiple sports categories. SVHighlights is constructed from pairs of full-length sports videos and their corresponding official highlight videos using a dataset generation pipeline, enabling scalable label generation without conventional per-clip saliency annotation. The benchmark comprises 320 videos with an average duration of 2.00 hours and a total of 640.18 hours, substantially exceeding previous datasets. Existing methods also face fundamental challenges on long videos: models trained on short clips fail to generalize to hour-long content, and their clip-level scoring lacks the broader context needed to identify highlights. To address this and provide a strong baseline, we present TF-SELECTOR, a training-free segment-based approach that divides each video into context-aware segments by merging adjacent shots sharing the same semantic content, and predicts segment-level saliency scores using a large language model with multimodal inputs including visual captions, transcripts, and audio volume. Experiments demonstrate that TF-SELECTOR achieves superior performance across most metrics compared to Video Temporal Grounding (VTG)-tuned baselines, with improvements of +3.12 in HIT@1, +4.06 in HIT@K, and +2.95 in IoU. These results establish SVHighlights as a challenging testbed for long-form highlight detection and demonstrate that a simple segment-based strategy can effectively scale to hour-long videos.

AI.tldr: Introduces SVHighlights, the first benchmark for highlight detection in extremely long sports videos (>1h) and TF-SELECTOR, a training-free, segment-based baseline using multimodal LLMs to predict segment saliency.
AI.motivation: Long-form highlight detection suffers from lack of benchmarks and generalization gaps when models trained on short clips are applied to hour-long videos; need scalable label generation without per-clip annotations.
AI.method: SVHighlights couples full-length videos with official highlights via a dataset generation pipeline. TF-SELECTOR segments videos into context-aware segments by merging adjacent shots with the same semantic content and uses a multimodal LLM (visual captions, transcripts, audio volume) to assign segment-level saliency scores.
AI.result: SVHighlights comprises 320 videos (~2.0 hours avg; total ~640.18 hours), substantially larger than prior datasets. TF-SELECTOR achieves superior performance over VTG-tuned baselines with +3.12 HIT@1, +4.06 HIT@K, and +2.95 IoU on key metrics.
AI.conclusion: SVHighlights is a challenging testbed for long-form highlight detection; a simple, training-free segment-based approach can scale to hour-long videos and yield strong performance, establishing a robust baseline for long-form highlight detection.

title: RhinoVLA Technical Report
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for robotic manipulation, but real-time deployment on edge hardware remains challenging. In this work, we identify VLM visual and context tokens as a major source of deployment latency: for GEMM-dominated projection operators, computation grows linearly with the number of input tokens when model dimensions are fixed. Motivated by this observation, we propose RhinoVLA, a deployment-oriented VLA model co-designed with the Huixi R1 edge SoC. RhinoVLA adopts a token-efficient Qwen3-VL backbone and a continuous Action Expert, reducing the VLM-side token and computation burden while preserving pretrained multimodal capability. To support cross-robot learning, RhinoVLA further introduces a unified interface that combines View Registry, 72D physical state-action slot space, and robotinstance LoRA, allowing heterogeneous robot observations and action schemas to be aligned under a shared policy. On the deployment side, RhinoVLA is optimized through hardware-aware compilation, mixed-precision execution, and parallel visual encoding. Experiments show that RhinoVLA achieves downstream performance comparable to π0.5 at a similar parameter scale, while reaching 11.69 Hz end-to-end inference on Huixi R1, meeting the 10 Hz real-time closedloop control target. The project will be open-sourced at https://github.com/HuixiAI/RhinoVLA.

AI.tldr: RhinoVLA is a deployment-optimized Vision-Language-Action model co-designed with an edge SoC, achieving token-efficient multimodal reasoning and real-time control on Huixi R1 (11.69 Hz), with cross-robot learning via a unified interface.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time, edge-deployed VLA for robotic manipulation by addressing token-related latency in VLM components and enabling cross-robot policy sharing.
AI.method: Co-design with Huixi R1: token-efficient Qwen3-VL backbone; continuous Action Expert; unified cross-robot interface (View Registry, 72D state-action slot space, robotinstance LoRA); hardware-aware compilation, mixed-precision execution, parallel visual encoding; deployment-oriented optimizations.
AI.result: End-to-end inference at 11.69 Hz on Huixi R1, meeting 10 Hz real-time requirement; performance comparable to π0.5 at similar parameter count; open-sourced at the project link.
AI.conclusion: A deployment-oriented VLA system that retains multimodal capabilities while reducing token- and computation-load, enabling real-time edge robotics and cross-robot policy alignment.

title: Data-Constrained Language Model Pretraining: Improved Regularization and Scaling Laws
summary: Classical scaling laws for language model pretraining balance model size against training dataset size under a fixed compute budget, assuming abundant data and a single pass over the corpus. As training compute grows faster than the supply of natural language data, pretraining is likely to enter a data-constrained, compute-rich regime where models train for multiple epochs over a finite dataset. We study data-constrained pretraining along two axes, regularization and scaling. For regularization, we study masked-input regularization (MIR), an auxiliary next-token prediction loss on randomly masked inputs. MIR tests whether the random masking central to diffusion language models can benefit autoregressive pretraining without architectural changes or inference overhead. Across 72M to 1.4B parameter models, we find that MIR added on top of strong weight decay improves validation loss over autoregressive strong-weight-decay-only models, with downstream gains at 1.4B. For scaling, we propose SoftQ, a scaling law that couples model size and data size to capture their interaction under repeated data. Classical alternatives such as the Chinchilla law use an additive form that decouples these terms, making them misspecified in the data-constrained regime. We find that SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than these alternatives, and estimates MIR's gains as equivalent to roughly 1.3 times as much unique training data. We release our code at https://github.com/yixinw-lab/dc_pretrain.

AI.tldr: In data-constrained pretraining, introducing masked-input regularization (MIR) improves autoregressive models with strong weight decay, and a coupled model/data scaling law (SoftQ) better captures performance than additive laws like Chinchilla; MIR equates to ~1.3x more data; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the regime where training compute outpaces data supply, leading to multi-epoch pretraining on finite datasets; understand how regularization and joint model/data scaling influence performance.
AI.method: Empirical study across 72M–1.4B parameter models. Compare autoregressive pretraining with strong weight decay (baseline) to MIR (auxiliary masked-input next-token loss). Evaluate validation loss and downstream performance. Propose SoftQ, a scaling law coupling model size and data size under repeated data; compare against additive laws (e.g., Chinchilla).
AI.result: MIR added on top of strong weight decay improves validation loss and yields downstream gains at 1.4B params. SoftQ fits data-constrained experiments substantially better than additive laws, and MIR gains are equivalent to about 1.3× more unique training data.
AI.conclusion: In data-constrained pretraining, MIR is beneficial for regularization without changing architecture or inference, and the SoftQ scaling law provides a better model-data interaction description than traditional additive laws, quantifying MIR’s data-equivalence benefit.

title: When CLIP Sees More, It Fights Back Harder: Multi-View Guided Adaptive Counterattacks for Test-Time Adversarial Robustness
summary: Vision-language models such as CLIP have achieved remarkable zero-shot recognition capabilities, yet their robustness against adversarial perturbations remains limited. Test-time counterattack (TTC) was recently proposed to improve CLIP's robustness by perturbing an input image to steer it away from a corrupted state during inference. However, TTC remains fragile under strong attacks because its counterattack relies on a directly corrupted original view and employs a noise-driven hard-gating scheme that cannot adapt to varying corruption severity. To address these limitations, we introduce Multi-view guided Adaptive Counterattack (MAC), which performs counterattacks for multi-view with corruption-aware soft weighting. Specifically, MAC first constructs augmented views of an input image to obtain diverse embeddings. It then performs counterattacks to refine corrupted embeddings of views. Next, MAC adaptively scales the counterattack intensity for each view based on its estimated corruption degree. Finally, the adaptively counterattacked views are aggregated to yield a robust final prediction. Extensive experiments across 20 datasets and diverse attack scenarios demonstrate that MAC substantially improves robustness while preserving high inference speed and memory efficiency with its tuning-free design. Our code is available at https://github.com/sunoh-kim/MAC.

AI.tldr: MAC improves CLIP robustness to adversarial perturbations by using multi-view augmented inputs and corruption-aware adaptive counterattacks, outperforming TTC with better robustness, speed, and memory efficiency; code available.
AI.motivation: Address the fragility of test-time counterattack (TTC) under strong attacks due to reliance on a corrupted original view and a fixed gating, by proposing a robust, efficient defense for vision-language models like CLIP.
AI.method: 1) Construct augmented views of the input image to obtain diverse embeddings. 2) Perform counterattacks to refine corrupted embeddings of the views. 3) Adaptively scale the counterattack intensity for each view based on its estimated corruption degree. 4) Aggregate the adaptively counterattacked views for the final prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 20 datasets and diverse attack scenarios show that MAC substantially improves robustness while preserving high inference speed and memory efficiency, and it is tuning-free.
AI.conclusion: MAC provides a robust, efficient defense for CLIP-like models via multi-view adaptive counterattacks, bridging the gap left by TTC; code is released for reproducibility.

title: Spline Policy: A Structured Representation for Robot Policies
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Modern imitation-learning policies for robot manipulation often represent actions as fixed-resolution action chunks, which are simple and effective but expose limited geometric and temporal structure before execution. This paper studies Spline Policy (SP), a structured representation that replaces action chunks with spline parameters while keeping the policy backbone unchanged. The predicted spline can be decoded as a compact continuous trajectory, queried at different temporal resolutions, constrained or edited in parameter space, and passed to downstream controllers. For quadratic spline outputs, the same representation can also be converted into a state-dependent vector field through an analytical distance-field construction. Under the regularity and projection assumptions of this construction, the induced dynamics do not increase the distance to the generated spline, yielding a principled local corrective mechanism around the predicted motion. The spline output further supports uncertainty propagation from observations to spline parameters, trajectories, and flow fields, and can be combined with classical control mechanisms such as null-space collision avoidance without retraining the policy backbone. We instantiate SP with diffusion, flow-matching, transformer-based, and vision-language-action backbones. Experiments in low-dimensional motion learning, simulated manipulation under matched backbones, dexterous manipulation, and real-robot case studies show that SP remains compatible with modern policy learners while exposing useful motion-structure properties, including compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction around predicted motions, uncertainty evaluation, and controller compatibility.

AI.tldr: Spline Policy (SP) replaces fixed-action chunks with spline parameters to capture geometric/temporal structure in imitation-learning policies for robot manipulation, enabling compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction, and seamless controller integration while remaining compatible with backbones like diffusion, transformers, and vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Current imitation-learning policies use fixed-resolution action chunks, which limit geometric and temporal structure exposure before execution. SP aims to provide a structured, continuous-motion representation that can be decoded into trajectories, queried at different temporal resolutions, and integrated with downstream controllers without retraining the backbone.
AI.method: Introduce Spline Policy (SP) by substituting action chunks with spline parameters while keeping the policy backbone intact. Spline outputs can be decoded into compact continuous trajectories, resampled temporally, edited in parameter space, and fed to controllers. For quadratic splines, obtain a state-dependent vector field via an analytical distance-field construction, ensuring regularity/projection conditions yield dynamics that do not increase distance to the spline. Propagate uncertainty from observations to spline parameters, trajectories, and flow fields. Combine with classical control (e.g., null-space collision avoidance) without retraining. Instantiate SP with diffusion, flow-matching, transformer-based, and vision-language-action backbones.
AI.result: SP achieves compact, continuously decodable trajectories with flexible temporal resolution, provides a local corrective mechanism around predicted motions, supports uncertainty propagation, and remains compatible with diverse policy backbones. Demonstrated across low-dimensional motion learning, simulated manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and real-robot case studies.
AI.conclusion: Spline Policy offers a structured, backbone-compatible motion representation that imparts motion-structure properties—compact decoding, temporal resampling, local correction, uncertainty evaluation, and controller compatibility—without sacrificing the strengths of modern policy learners and enabling richer geometric/temporal control.

title: GRASP: Geometry-aware Residual Alignment for Scalable Pretraining Data Attribution
summary: Scalable data attribution methods typically assign isolated utility scores to individual training examples. This prevalent additive assumption fundamentally fails to capture critical subset dynamics, including data redundancy and complementary coverage. In this work, we reframe attribution as subset-level counterfactual utility prediction and introduce GRASP, an interaction-aware surrogate. Grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound, GRASP explicitly models subset interactions through a quadratic geometric penalty. To achieve pretraining-scale efficiency without relying on hidden oracle tuning, we couple low-dimensional feature sketches with a strictly finite lower-confidence bound selection protocol. Extensive subset-retraining evaluations demonstrate that GRASP decisively outperforms existing scalable baselines. It more than doubles the task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity while reducing upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude. Downstream diagnostics further show that this scoring mechanism transfers to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection, establishing a robust foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora.

AI.tldr: GRASP: an interaction-aware data attribution method that moves beyond additive per-example scores to subset-level counterfactual utility, using a quadratic penalty to model interactions for scalable, efficient pretraining-scale attribution with strong cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Additive attribution fails to capture subset dynamics like data redundancy and complementary coverage; modeling interactions among training examples is essential for accurate data valuation and scalable curation.
AI.method: Introduces GRASP, an interaction-aware surrogate that uses a quadratic geometric penalty to encode subset interactions, grounded in a theoretical smoothness lower bound. To achieve pretraining-scale efficiency without oracle tuning, it combines low-dimensional feature sketches with a finite lower-confidence bound (LCB) selection protocol.
AI.result: Extensive subset-retraining evaluations show GRASP outperforms existing scalable baselines, more than doubling task-level rank correlation for counterfactual subset fidelity, while reducing upfront artifact construction costs by nearly an order of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields a robust, transferable scoring mechanism that generalizes to language model curation and cross-domain vision selection, providing a solid foundation for optimizing massive pretraining corpora.

title: SS-TPT: Stability and Suitability-Guided Test-Time Prompt Tuning for Adversarially Robust Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP achieve strong zero-shot recognition but remain highly fragile under adversarial perturbations. Recent test-time adaptation defenses improve robustness by leveraging many augmented views, but this leads to impractical slowdown and a clear robustness-throughput trade-off. To address this challenge, we present Stability and Suitability-guided Test-time Prompt Tuning (SS-TPT), evaluating the quality of each augmented view via two complementary scores: (1) stability, measuring prediction invariance to weak augmentations, and (2) suitability, measuring feature-space density among views. These stability and suitability (SS) scores guide both adaptation and inference through an SS-guided consistency loss and an SS-weighted prediction, amplifying trustworthy views while suppressing corrupted ones. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SS-TPT significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior robustness-throughput trade-offs across diverse datasets and varying numbers of views, thereby demonstrating both strong practicality and generality. Our code is available at https://github.com/sunoh-kim/SS-TPT.

AI.tldr: SS-TPT introduces stability and suitability scores to guide test-time prompt tuning for vision-language models, improving robustness with fewer augmented views and better throughput.
AI.motivation: To overcome the robustness-throughput trade-off in test-time adaptation of vision-language models, which typically relies on many augmented views, causing slowdown.
AI.method: The method computes two complementary scores per augmented view: stability (prediction invariance to weak augmentations) and suitability (feature-space density across views). These SS scores guide an SS-guided consistency loss during adaptation and an SS-weighted prediction during inference, enabling selective use of trustworthy views.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows SS-TPT outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods across diverse datasets and varying numbers of views, achieving a favorable robustness-throughput trade-off; code is released.
AI.conclusion: SS-TPT provides a practical, generalizable approach to robustified VLMs by selectively leveraging augmented views through SS-guided optimization, reducing computational overhead while maintaining or improving robustness.

title: Simulation-Driven Imitation Learning for Biosignals-Free Shared-Autonomy Prosthetic Grasping
summary: Biosignals-free shared-autonomy control of upper-limb prosthetic hands aims to enable natural and low-effort manipulation without relying on EMG or other physiological signals. Recent imitation-learning-based approaches have shown promising results, but their scalability is limited by the cost and variability of collecting large amounts of real-world human demonstration data. In this work, we present a scalable simulation framework that automatically generates diverse reach-to-grasp demonstrations from a wrist-mounted virtual camera. The framework combines physically feasible grasp synthesis, natural reaching trajectories retargeting, and reach--grasp--lift execution in procedurally generated indoor environments. It records wrist-view observations, proprioception, and actions to build a large-scale demonstration dataset for imitation learning. Through extensive simulation benchmarks, we evaluate object and scene generalization and compare several representative state-of-the-art imitation learning methods. Results show that the simulated demonstrations are sufficiently rich and consistent for effective policy learning. In three realistic settings, the learned sim-to-real policy achieves over 90\% grasp success, surpasses baseline methods, and exhibits stronger generalization, highlighting the promise of simulation-driven training for biosignals-free shared-autonomy prosthetic grasping. The demonstrations are available at \href{https://sites.google.com/view/sim-prosthetic-grasp/home}{https://sites.google.com/view/sim-prosthetic-grasp/home}.

AI.tldr: A scalable simulation-based pipeline generates diverse wrist-camera reach-to-grasp demonstrations to train biosignals-free shared-autonomy prosthetic grasping via imitation learning, achieving high sim-to-real performance.
AI.motivation: The main bottleneck for imitation-learning-based prosthetic control is acquiring large, varied real-world demonstration data; a scalable, label-free simulation approach can overcome data collection costs and variability.
AI.method: Develop a scalable framework that (1) synthesizes physically feasible grasps, (2) retargets natural reaching trajectories from wrist-mounted viewpoints, and (3) executes reach–grasp–lift in procedurally generated indoor environments, recording wrist-view observations, proprioception, and actions to create a large demonstration dataset. Then train and evaluate imitation-learning policies in simulation and transfer to real hardware.
AI.result: Simulated demonstrations are rich and consistent, enabling effective policy learning. In three realistic settings the sim-to-real policy achieves >90% grasp success, outperforms baselines, and shows stronger generalization.
AI.conclusion: Simulation-driven training is a promising path for biosignals-free shared-autonomy prosthetic grasping, with publicly available demonstrations enabling scalable, transferable learning.

title: TALAN: Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks for Targeted Post-Training of Large Language Models
summary:
Targeted post-training aims to improve reasoning, math, and code without degrading strengths. Low-rank adapters are efficient but task-global; activation interventions are input-aware but often require separate probes, vectors, or inference-time steering. We introduce TALAN (Task-Aligned Latent Adaptation Networks), a sequence-conditioned latent side path inserted into a transformer's residual stream and co-trained with a low-rank adapter in one SFT loop. TALAN compresses the active sequence into latent memory, remixes it into token-level perturbations, and writes them back through a controlled residual update. It is configured along six axes: insertion location, memory size, mixer, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale.
  Across four Qwen3-family backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks, TALAN improves matched LoRA and DoRA baselines. With LoRA, it yields a +1.41 point cross-model mean gain, positive on all four backbones and non-negative on all 16 model-benchmark cells. With DoRA, it yields a +1.85 point mean gain, positive on all backbones and on 13 of 16 cells. Paired seed checks support positive average effects but show nontrivial variance, so we treat them as sensitivity checks. Cost is small: <1% trainable parameters relative to the backbone and 1.01-1.02x inference overhead versus matched LoRA. A Llama-3.2-1B transfer probe is also positive under LoRA and rsLoRA across seven paired seeds, supporting a transfer beyond Qwen.
  Internal-state analyses suggest TALAN is a small complementary activation intervention. The matched adapter update is 80-1,700x larger than the TALAN perturbation, yet their directions have near-zero cosine; per-layer measurements show this small orthogonal perturbation propagates and amplifies through depth. TALAN offers a practical platform for studying steerable activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training.

AI.tldr: TALAN is a sequence-conditioned latent side path that complements low-rank adapters (LoRA/DoRA) by compressing the active sequence into latent memory, remixing it into token-level perturbations, and writing back through a controlled residual update, yielding consistent gains with minimal cost.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of task-global low-rank adapters and input-aware activation interventions by introducing a compact, latent, activation-level adapter that can be co-trained with LoRA/DoRA in a single fine-tuning loop, enabling task-aligned perturbations without degrading base strengths.
AI.method: Introduce TALAN, a latent activation module inserted in the transformer's residual stream that conditions on the input sequence. It co-trains with a low-rank adapter in one SFT loop, compresses the active sequence into latent memory, remixes into token-level perturbations, and writes back via a controlled residual update. Six configuration axes are explored: insertion location, memory size, mixer, writeback rule, trainability scope, and gradient scale.
AI.result: Empirical gains across four Qwen3 backbones and four STEM/code benchmarks: +1.41 cross-model mean gain with LoRA, positive on all four backbones and 16/16 cells; +1.85 mean gain with DoRA, positive on all backbones and 13/16 cells. Seed-based sensitivity checks show positive averages with some variance. Cost is low (<1% trainable params, 1.01-1.02x inference overhead vs matched LoRA). A transfer probe (Llama-3.2-1B) corroborates positive transfer under LoRA/rsLoRA across seven seeds.
AI.conclusion: TALAN acts as a small, complementary activation intervention largely orthogonal to adapter updates. The prominent adapter update (80-1,700x larger) has near-zero cosine similarity with TALAN perturbations, yet TALAN perturbations propagate and amplify through depth. TALAN offers a practical platform for steerable activation-level adaptation within standard adapter-based post-training.

authors: Donald Johann Jr. and Fred Prior
title: When is 3D Worth It? A Resource-Performance Frontier for CNNs and Transformers in Lung CT
summary: Three-dimensional models are widely assumed preferable for volumetric medical imaging, yet their practical value depends on whether performance gains justify added computational cost and complexity. Rather than proposing a new architecture, we study how input dimensionality (2D, 2.5D, 3D) affects model behavior across convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) under a fixed training protocol. Using a leakage-free NLST cohort (n = 1,977) with supporting LIDC-IDRI data, we find that the 2.5D CNN offers the most favorable discrimination-stability trade-off in our comparison (ROC-AUC 0.682, 95% CI [0.546, 0.799]) with a stable operating point. In contrast, 3D CNNs show threshold instability, and transformers exhibit degenerate predictions, such as all-positive predictions. Confidence intervals are wide and overlapping, so we present these results as a controlled resource-performance frontier and a failure-mode taxonomy rather than as definitive superiority claims. For class-imbalanced lung cancer screening classification, 2D and 2.5D inputs provide a more reliable trade-off between performance, stability, and computational efficiency than full 3D representations.

AI.tldr: Across 2D, 2.5D, and 3D input representations for CNNs and ViTs in lung cancer classification, 2.5D CNN offers the best discrimination-stability trade-off; 3D CNNs show threshold instability; Vision Transformers produce degenerate predictions; overall 2D/2.5D favored over 3D for class-imbalanced screening, with results framed as a resource-performance frontier and a failure-mode taxonomy rather than definitive superiority.
AI.motivation: To determine whether higher input dimensionality (3D) yields meaningful performance gains in volumetric medical imaging and how this interacts with architecture (CNNs vs ViTs) under a fixed training protocol, informing practical choices via a frontier of resource-performance and failure modes.
AI.method: Comparative evaluation of 2D, 2.5D, and 3D input representations across CNN and Vision Transformer architectures using a leakage-free NLST cohort (n=1,977) with supporting LIDC-IDRI data. A fixed training protocol and a single-task lung cancer screening classification were used. Performance was assessed with ROC-AUC and stability (threshold behavior); confidence intervals were reported and results framed as a resource-performance frontier and a failure-mode taxonomy rather than asserting definitive superiority.
AI.result: 2.5D CNN achieved the most favorable discrimination-stability trade-off (ROC-AUC 0.682, 95% CI [0.546, 0.799]); 3D CNNs exhibited threshold instability; Vision Transformers yielded degenerate predictions (e.g., all-positive). Wide, overlapping CIs; overall recommendation favors 2D/2.5D inputs for class-imbalanced screening in terms of reliability and computational efficiency over full 3D representations.
AI.conclusion: For class-imbalanced lung cancer screening, using 2D or 2.5D inputs provides a more reliable balance of performance, stability, and efficiency than full 3D representations. The study emphasizes a resource-performance frontier and a failure-mode taxonomy rather than claiming 3D superiority, guiding practical choices in volumetric imaging tasks.

title: Rapid co-design of Buoyancy-assisted robots for Challenging Locomotion using Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists
summary: Designing high-performance legged robots requires jointly optimizing morphology and control. Model-free Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers an alternative to model-predictive control for developing robust controllers without explicitly specifying robot dynamics. Thus, we have seen theuse of RL to train controllers and evaluate designs for robot morphology optimization. While RL has shown success inlocomotion, using it in the co-design inner loop is expensive due to repeated policy training. Universal policies conditioned on morphology offer a promising alternative, but suffer from behavioral diversity collapse, converging to a single strategy that performs sub-optimally across designs. On the other hand, end-to-end Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures fail due to a collapse in its representation. We propose Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists (GES), a framework that decouples design-space partitioning from policy learning to capture diverse behaviors explicitly. GES assigns specialist policies to evolving Gaussian regions and iteratively refines them via training, probing, and territory expansion. The resulting specialists are integrated into a design sampling loop, replacing costly re-training with direct evaluation. When tested on the Buoyancy-Assisted Light Legged Unit (BALLU), GES discovers designs with 5 - 25% higher performance than naive universal policies. On hardware, a GES optimized design overcomes a 24 cm tall obstacle - 3x improvement over the baseline BALLU design. Moreover, GES curtails design optimization time by 37%.

AI.tldr: GES decouples design-space partitioning from policy learning using evolving Gaussian regions to generate diverse specialist policies for morphology–control co-design, improving performance and reducing retraining costs.
AI.motivation: Joint morphology–control co-design with model-free RL is costly due to inner-loop policy retraining and behavioral diversity collapse in universal policies; end-to-end MoE approaches also fail due to representation collapse; need an approach that explicitly maintains behavioral diversity while enabling efficient evaluation within design loops.
AI.method: Gaussian Evolutionary Specialists (GES) assigns specialist policies to evolving Gaussian regions in the design space, iteratively refining them through training, probing, and territory expansion. The specialists are integrated into a design sampling loop to evaluate designs directly, replacing costly re-training with direct evaluation of diversified policies.
AI.result: GES discovers BALLU designs with 5–25% higher performance than naive universal policies. A hardware demonstration shows a GES-optimized design overcoming a 24 cm tall obstacle, achieving ~3× improvement over the baseline BALLU design. Overall, GES reduces design-optimization time by ~37%.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling design-space partitioning from policy learning with evolving Gaussian specialists enables explicit behavioral diversity, accelerates co-design workflows, and yields higher-performing morphologies and controllers than naive universal policies or end-to-end MoE approaches.

title: The Fine-Tuning Trap: Evaluating Negative Transfer and the Role of PEFT in Sub-1B Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Deploying Small Language Models (SLMs) on edge devices requires efficient fine-tuning strategies that adapt models to new tasks without degrading their general capabilities. In this study, we benchmark five sub-1B models (135M-1B) on mathematical reasoning tasks and uncover a critical vulnerability: Full Fine-Tuning (Full FT) actively harms performance in models under 300M parameters, often dropping accuracy below zero-shot baselines. This "negative transfer" makes Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) not just an efficiency preference, but a stability requirement. We find that while Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Weight-Decomposed LoRA (DoRA) perform comparably, their strengths vary by task; DoRA excels in complex reasoning (GSM8K), while LoRA dominates pattern matching (OrcaMath). In particular, Full FT is outperformed by LoRA on aligned models (Qwen2.5-0.5B) and even by simple 5-shot In-Context Learning on the smallest architectures (SmolLM2-135M). Based on these findings, we recommend defaulting to PEFT for all aligned sub-1B models and caution against Full FT for any architecture smaller than 500M parameters to prevent catastrophic forgetting. Reproduction of this work can be found at https://github.com/gulguluu/tiny-slm-finetune-compare.

AI.tldr: Small models (<300M) suffer from negative transfer when fully fine-tuned, making PEFT methods essential for stability; LoRA and DoRA perform differently across tasks; for sub-1B aligned models, PEFT should be default; avoid full fine-tuning on models <500M.
AI.motivation: Assess how different fine-tuning strategies impact the task performance and stability of very small language models on edge devices, particularly for mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Benchmark five sub-1B models (135M–1B) on GSM8K and OrcaMath; compare Full Fine-Tuning (FT), LoRA, and Weight-Decomposed LoRA (DoRA); evaluate across aligned models (e.g., Qwen2.5-0.5B) and small architectures (SmolLM2-135M); include 5-shot In-Context Learning (ICL); analyze transfer effects.
AI.result: Full Fine-Tuning degrades performance for models under 300M (negative transfer), often below zero-shot baselines. LoRA and DoRA perform comparably with task-dependent strengths: DoRA excels in GSM8K (complex reasoning); LoRA excels in OrcaMath (pattern matching). On aligned models, FT is outperformed by LoRA; even 5-shot ICL can beat FT on the smallest architectures. PEFT is recommended over FT for sub-1B aligned models; avoid FT for any model <500M to prevent catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Default to Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for all aligned sub-1B models; exercise caution with Full Fine-Tuning for architectures smaller than 500M due to catastrophic forgetting. Reproduction: GitHub link provided.

title: MVSegNet: A Lightweight Boundary-Aware Network for Fetal Lateral Ventricle Segmentation and Atrial Width Estimation in Prenatal Ultrasound
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Code and trained models will be released upon acceptance. Supplementary material available upon request
summary: Fetal ventriculomegaly is assessed by measuring the atrial width of the lateral ventricle in prenatal ultrasound. Accurate segmentation is essential for this measurement, but acoustic shadowing, speckle noise, and poor contrast make it difficult. We developed MVSegNet, a lightweight encoder-decoder network combining multi-scale feature extraction and boundary-aware refinement. The model was trained and evaluated on 584 expert-annotated transventricular ultrasound frames using a 70/15/15 split. Performance was compared against six segmentation baselines using overlap, boundary, and measurement metrics. MVSegNet achieved a Dice score of 80.79%, IoU of 68.47%, Hausdorff distance of 4.07 mm, and atrial width mean absolute error of 3.40 mm. The model contains 2.31 million parameters and runs at 165.6 frames per second on an NVIDIA T4 GPU. MVSegNet outperformed all evaluated baselines on boundary and measurement metrics while maintaining low computational cost, supporting its use in automated fetal ultrasound analysis.

AI.tldr: MVSegNet is a lightweight encoder-decoder network for fetal ventricle segmentation in ultrasound that achieves strong boundary and measurement accuracy with low computational cost, enabling automated atrial width assessment.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate, robust segmentation of fetal lateral ventricles under challenging ultrasound conditions (shadowing, speckle noise, low contrast) to reliably measure atrial width for ventriculomegaly assessment.
AI.method: A lightweight encoder-decoder architecture called MVSegNet with multi-scale feature extraction and boundary-aware refinement. Trained and evaluated on 584 expert-annotated transventricular ultrasound frames with a 70/15/15 train/val/test split, and compared against six segmentation baselines using overlap, boundary, and measurement metrics.
AI.result: MVSegNet achieved Dice 80.79%, IoU 68.47%, Hausdorff distance 4.07 mm, atrial width MAE 3.40 mm. It has 2.31 million parameters and runs at 165.6 frames/s on an NVIDIA T4. It outperformed all baselines on boundary and measurement metrics while maintaining low computational cost.
AI.conclusion: MVSegNet is effective and efficient for automated fetal ultrasound analysis of ventriculomegaly, offering strong segmentation performance with a small model and fast inference, suitable for clinical deployment.

title: Re-imagining ISO 26262 in the Age of Autonomous Vehicles: Enhancing Controllability through Transferability and Predictability
summary: The ISO 26262 standard defines functional safety for road vehicles through risk assessments based on Severity, Exposure, and Controllability, grounded in a human-driven vehicle paradigm. In the context of autonomous vehicles (AVs), the absence of a human driver necessitates revisiting these principles. This paper decomposes the Controllability placeholder into two auditable evidence dimensions of ISO 26262 by introducing two measurable sub-concepts: Transferability and Predictability. Transferability extends Controllability to capture AV systems' ability to hand off control to dedicated fallback safety mechanisms, while Predictability captures how easily external agents can anticipate AV behavior. Predictability is formally defined from human-robot interaction-inspired principles, and a mathematical framework is provided to quantify it. A designed-versus-achievable gap is introduced to distinguish architectural fallback claims from scene-conditioned achievable fallback capability. The proposed metrics align with ISO 26262 and ISO/PAS 21448 (SOTIF), rendering fallback and interaction claims falsifiable and traceable across ODD slices. These dimensions complement rather than replace existing standards, and the enhancements preserve the structure of ISO 26262 while extending its applicability to driverless automated systems operating at SAE Levels 4 and 5.

AI.tldr: Proposes to extend ISO 26262 for autonomous vehicles by decomposing Controllability into Transferability and Predictability, with a formal framework and a gap metric; aims for falsifiability and traceability across ODD slices; complements existing standards for SAE Levels 4-5.
AI.motivation: Address the absence of a human driver in AVs by creating auditable, measurable evidence for fallback and interaction capabilities; align safety standards (ISO 26262 and SOTIF) to driverless systems.
AI.method: Define two auditable sub-concepts (Transferability and Predictability) as refinements of Controllability; formalize Predictability using human-robot interaction-inspired principles; develop a mathematical framework to quantify Predictability; introduce a designed-versus-achievable gap to separate architectural claims from scene-conditioned capabilities; map these metrics to ISO 26262 and ISO/PAS 21448 (SOTIF) and analyze applicability across operating design domains (ODD).
AI.result: Presents a theoretical framework with formal definitions and metrics; enables falsifiable and traceable evaluation of fallback and interaction claims; demonstrates alignment with ISO 26262 and SOTIF and applicability to SAE Levels 4–5; empirical validation is not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The approach complements existing safety standards rather than replacing them, extending ISO 26262 to driverless automated systems by introducing Transferability and Predictability as evidence dimensions for fallback and interaction across ODD slices.

title: From Sampled Outcomes to Capability Distributions: Rethinking Supervision for LLM Routing
summary: Existing LLM routing methods typically treat a model's single response to a query as its capability label for training routers. However, because LLM generation is inherently stochastic, such single-shot supervision provides only a noisy observation of a query-model pair's behavior rather than a reliable capability estimate. We show that this assumption introduces systematic noise into routing supervision, making learned routing policies less reliable. To address this issue, we propose DARS (Distribution-Aware Routing Supervision), a framework that constructs routing supervision from a distributional view of model behavior. Instead of relying on a single generated response, DARS considers uncertainty from both the input side and the output side, capturing how semantically equivalent query formulations and stochastic generations affect model performance. Based on these distribution-aware observations, DARS builds more reliable supervision signals for routing. Experiments across diverse tasks show that single-shot labels can be misleading for model selection, while distribution-aware supervision provides more stable labels and improves learned routing behavior. Our results suggest that reliable LLM routing should move beyond single-response observations and be grounded in query-level model capability distributions.

AI.tldr: A distribution-aware routing supervision (DARS) framework for LLM routing that treats model behavior as a distribution rather than single-shot labels, accounting for input and output uncertainty, improving routing stability and reliability.
AI.motivation: Single-shot supervision for routing is noisy due to stochastic LLM generation; a distributional view can yield more reliable routing signals by capturing uncertainty in both inputs and outputs.
AI.method: DARS constructs routing supervision from distributional observations: it incorporates input-side (semantically equivalent query formulations) and output-side (stochastic generations) uncertainty, and derives supervision signals from model capability distributions instead of single responses.
AI.result: Experiments show that single-shot labels can mislead model selection, while distribution-aware supervision yields more stable labels and improves learned routing behavior across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Reliable LLM routing should move beyond single-response observations and be grounded in query-level model capability distributions.

title: From Vision to Text: A Compact Multimodal Approach for Robust, Cross-Domain Presentation Attack Detection on ID Cards
comment: Publication under the revision process on IEEE
summary: Cross-domain shifts challenge Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) on ID Cards, given the restricted data available due to privacy concerns. This work proposes a compact multimodal model, based on new generative and discriminative blocks, which combines visual and textual data for PAD on genuine and synthetic ID images. While multimodal models exhibit strong generalisation after supervised fine-tuning, they fail in zero-shot settings. Our findings underscore that model capacity and real-world data are essential for reliable PAD, while existing synthetic datasets may not reflect real-world challenges. We argue for a re-evaluation of synthetic data as a benchmark and emphasise the need for more realistic, diverse datasets to advance PAD research.

AI.tldr: Compact multimodal PAD model for ID cards; cross-domain shifts; supervised fine-tuning aids generalisation but zero-shot fails; synthetic data may not reflect real challenges; calls for realistic datasets and re-evaluation of synthetic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address cross-domain generalisation under privacy constraints with limited data; assess whether multimodal fusion improves PAD over unimodal visual methods; evaluate usefulness of synthetic data.
AI.method: Propose a compact multimodal architecture integrating visual and textual cues via new generative and discriminative blocks for PAD on authentic and synthetic ID images; evaluate in cross-domain and zero-shot settings; compare with unimodal baselines.
AI.result: Multimodal models generalise well after supervised fine-tuning but struggle in zero-shot; model capacity and access to real-world data are crucial; current synthetic datasets may not reflect real-world PAD challenges.
AI.conclusion: Re-evaluate synthetic data as a benchmark; prioritize realistic, diverse datasets to advance PAD; address cross-domain shifts by improving model capacity and data realism.

title: Planning-aligned Token Compression for Long-Context Autonomous Driving
summary: Monolithic vision-action models represent an emerging paradigm in autonomous driving. However, this architecture produces token sequences that quickly exceed real-time computational budgets when encoding extended temporal context for complex interactions. While approaches like linear transformers and external memory try to make the context lightweight, token compression is most compatible with the architecture as it requires no backbone modifications. Yet existing compression adopts rule-based heuristics like temporal decay, decoupled from planning, risking loss of decision-critical information. We propose COMPACT-VA, a planning-aligned working memory framework built on conditional VQ-VAE, compressing extended context into bounded representations. Compression is conditioned on both historical trajectory and a learned planning intent that the posterior encoder distills from future trajectories during training, while the prior encoder learns to predict it from compressed observations. The compressed memory, concatenated with the predicted latent, feeds the policy for end-to-end optimization, planning with retained decision-critical information. We evaluate on high-signal dynamic scenarios where historical context is most critical for behavior correctness (e.g., stop, yield, or proceed), and accordingly design behavioral metrics. Under comparable token budgets, we achieve $>$6% improvement (68.3%) on success rates with consistent gains across metrics. Ablations validate planning-aligned coupling effectiveness. Closed-loop evaluation confirms that COMPACT-VA maintained general driving performance with 3.3* speedup and 2.7* memory reduction over uncompressed processing.

AI.tldr: COMPACT-VA introduces a planning-aligned working memory for monolithic vision-action models using conditional VQ-VAE to compress extended temporal context, achieving better success rates under token budgets with substantial speedup and memory reductions.
AI.motivation: Monolithic vision-action models suffer from rapidly growing token sequences when encoding extended temporal context, straining real-time budgets. Rule-based compression is decoupled from planning and risks losing decision-critical information.
AI.method: A conditional VQ-VAE framework with a posterior encoder conditioned on future trajectories to distill planning intent, and a prior encoder that learns to predict that intent from compressed observations. The compressed memory, concatenated with the predicted latent, feeds the policy for end-to-end optimization, aligning compression with planning.
AI.result: Under comparable token budgets, achieved >6% improvement (68.3% success) with consistent gains across metrics. Ablations validate the planning-aligned coupling. Closed-loop evaluation shows 3.3× speedup and 2.7× memory reduction compared with uncompressed processing.
AI.conclusion: Planning-aligned memory compression via COMPACT-VA preserves critical decision information while reducing compute and memory overhead, enabling robust end-to-end optimization in autonomous driving.

title: Uniform Stability and Generalization Error of GD and SGD on Fixed-Point Parameters
summary: We analyze generalization error, uniform stability, and uniform argument stability of gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) over discrete parameter spaces, where each update involves deterministic or stochastic rounding. We show that deterministic rounding degrades the generalization error of GD on convex, Lipschitz, and smooth loss functions, increasing the rate from $O(T/n)$ to $O(T/\sqrt{n})$, and establish matching lower bounds. We further prove that uniform stability of GD becomes $Ω(T)$, showing that stability-based generalization bounds are vacuous in this setting. In contrast, for the same losses, stochastic gradient descent with deterministic rounding admits nontrivial uniform stability guarantees, which differ qualitatively from the real-valued case and exhibit distinct dependencies on the number of iterations and the dimension: we prove tight bounds $O(T/n)$ for one dimension and $O(T^2/n)$ for higher dimensions. We also show that stochastic rounding can introduce generalization error that increases with the dimension; such a phenomenon is absent in standard real-valued optimization and in the deterministic rounding case. Finally, we provide upper bounds on uniform argument stability for stochastic rounding schemes and show that these bounds are tight when the loss can be represented as a sum of coordinate-wise functions.

AI.tldr: Deterministic rounding hurts generalization for gradient descent in quantized discrete parameter spaces; stability-based bounds collapse for GD, whereas SGD with deterministic rounding retains nontrivial stability with dimension- and iteration-dependent bounds; stochastic rounding introduces new generalization phenomena that can worsen with dimension; uniform argument stability bounds exist for stochastic rounding, tight for coordinate-sum losses.
AI.motivation: Understand how quantization/rounding in discrete parameter spaces affects generalization and stability of gradient-based optimization (GD/SGD), with implications for quantized hardware and practical learning.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis under convex, Lipschitz, and smooth loss assumptions.
