Derives upper and lower bounds on generalization error and stability for GD with deterministic rounding, showing degraded rates; shows Ω(T) stability for GD; analyzes SGD with deterministic rounding to obtain nontrivial bounds O(T/n) in 1D and O(T^2/n) in higher dimensions; investigates stochastic rounding revealing distinct dependencies on iterations and dimension and potential dimension-induced generalization increase; provides upper bounds on uniform argument stability for stochastic rounding, tight for sum of coordinate-wise losses.
AI.result: Key results include: (1) deterministic rounding degrades GD generalization from O(T/n) to O(T/√n) with matching lower bounds; (2) GD uniform stability is Ω(T), rendering stability-based generalization vacuous; (3) SGD with deterministic rounding yields nontrivial uniform stability—tight bounds O(T/n) in 1D and O(T^2/n) in higher dimensions; (4) stochastic rounding can cause generalization error to grow with dimension; (5) upper bounds on uniform argument stability for stochastic rounding, tight when loss splits into coordinate-wise components.
AI.conclusion: Rounding strategy critically impacts generalization and stability in gradient-based optimization: deterministic rounding harms GD generalization and yields vacuous stability; SGD with deterministic rounding preserves some stability with dimension-aware rates; stochastic rounding introduces distinct, dimension-sensitive generalization behavior and qualitative differences from real-valued optimization; bounds are tight in several regimes and inform quantized optimization design and theory.

title: CL-CLIP: CLIP-Based Continual Learning Framework with Cost-Volume Category Decoupling for Object Detection
summary: Continual Object Detection (COD) requires a detector to acquire new categories over time while preserving previously learned ones. This goal is closely related to open-vocabulary detection, since both settings require reasoning over categories that are not fully covered by the annotations available at the current training stage. Recent CLIP-based open-vocabulary detectors have shown strong zero-shot generalization, and frameworks such as F-ViT demonstrate that vision-language pretraining can provide powerful zero-shot detection ability for unseen categories. However, real-world deployments cannot remain purely zero-shot: once these detectors are continually updated on newly introduced categories, they suffer severe catastrophic forgetting and quickly lose their previously calibrated detection ability. We therefore propose CL-CLIP, a CLIP-based COD framework that equips open-vocabulary detectors with better continual learning ability through cost-volume-guided category decoupling. Specifically, following CAT-Seg, we compute a CLIP image-text similarity cost volume, defined as dense category-wise response maps between visual tokens and class text embeddings. This zero-shot spatial prior decomposes shared region features into class-specific pathways, which are then processed by a Multi-Expert RoI head. Extensive experiments on PASCAL VOC and MS-COCO show that CL-CLIP substantially improves the F-ViT baseline under continual fine-tuning and achieves competitive performance with existing continual object detectors, especially in adapting to newly introduced categories while preserving competitive base-class performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces CL-CLIP, a CLIP-based continual object detector using cost-volume similarity maps to decompose features by class and a Multi-Expert RoI head, achieving better continual fine-tuning on VOC/COCO.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in open-vocabulary detectors during continual learning; leverage zero-shot priors to maintain base-class performance while adapting to new categories.
AI.method: Compute dense image-text similarity cost volume between visual tokens and class text embeddings (in the spirit of CAT-Seg); use this zero-shot spatial prior to decompose shared region features into class-specific pathways, processed by a Multi-Expert RoI head, then trained under continual fine-tuning.
AI.result: On PASCAL VOC and MS-COCO, CL-CLIP substantially improves the F-ViT baseline under continual finetuning and achieves competitive performance with existing continual object detectors, particularly in adapting to newly introduced categories while preserving base-class performance.
AI.conclusion: Cost-volume guided category decoupling enables open-vocabulary detectors to better withstand catastrophic forgetting in continual object detection, leveraging zero-shot priors to maintain and extend performance during continual updates.

title: Affordance-Based Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Quadruped Pedipulation
comment: This paper is submitted to Wiley Journal of Field Robotics
summary: The object manipulation capabilities of quadruped robots is an open research challenge. While previous studies have focused on low-level policy learning, task execution still relies on expert-designed high-level trajectories. Autonomous selection of both an affordable interaction point on the target object and an affordable robot base pose removes the need for pre-designed trajectories. This study proposes a three-level hierarchical reinforcement learning (RL) framework that utilizes pose affordances to guide the navigation policy, while the navigation policy drives the locomotion policy. In addition, the pedipulation policy is guided by interaction-point affordances, enabling object-centric pose alignment of the quadruped robot and effective end-effector manipulation planning. We train the proposed framework in the IsaacSim ecosystem and evaluate it in both simulation and real-world settings. We investigate the effectiveness of pose affordance across multiple scenarios in simulation while various object interaction tasks are validated on real-world setting forming an object-interaction dataset. The results show that the proposed framework can autonomously identify candidate poses based on their affordance and successfully execute object manipulation tasks in the real world without human guidance.

AI.tldr: A three-level hierarchical RL framework enables autonomous pose and interaction-point selection for quadruped manipulation, guided by pose and interaction affordances, trained in IsaacSim, validated in simulation and real world, achieving autonomous manipulation without human guidance.
AI.motivation: To move beyond expert-designed high-level trajectories in quadruped manipulation by enabling autonomous selection of both an affordable object interaction point and a suitable robot base pose through pose affordances.
AI.method: A three-level hierarchical reinforcement learning framework where pose affordances guide the navigation policy, which in turn drives the locomotion policy; a pedipulation policy is guided by interaction-point affordances for object-centric pose alignment and manipulation planning. Training occurs in the IsaacSim ecosystem with evaluation in both simulation and real environments. A real-world object-interaction dataset is built to validate various tasks.
AI.result: The framework autonomously identifies candidate poses based on affordances and successfully executes object manipulation tasks in the real world without human guidance; evaluation shows effectiveness of pose affordances across multiple simulated scenarios and real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Pose affordances enable autonomous pose and interaction-point selection, reducing reliance on pre-designed trajectories and enabling autonomous pedipulation for quadrupeds; results support feasibility of sim-to-real transfer and real-world generalization.

title: GenPO++: Generative Policy Optimization with Jacobian-free Likelihood Ratios
summary: Generative policies provide expressive and multimodal action distributions, making them attractive for reinforcement learning (RL) in complex continuous-control tasks. Among them, flow-based policies are especially appealing because they generate actions through deterministic transport maps. However, applying such generative policies to likelihood-based on-policy learning remains limited by the difficulty of evaluating the probability of executed actions. Existing flow RL methods either replace the true action-density ratio with approximate surrogates, which can introduce biased updates, or recover exact likelihoods through dummy-action augmentation, which enlarges the policy space and increases computation. In this work, we propose GenPO++, a reversible generative policy optimization framework that uses history states as auxiliary memory in a high-order reversible ODE solver, yielding exact inversion without changing the original action dimension. The resulting generative policy map has a log-determinant determined only by fixed solver coefficients, enabling exact and Jacobian-free likelihood-ratio computation. This design preserves the expressiveness of generative flow policies while avoiding both action ratio bias and dummy-action overhead. We evaluate GenPO++ on large-scale simulated control, fine-tuning, and real-world robotic manipulation tasks, where it achieves competitive or superior performance over state-of-the-art on-policy RL methods, while improving training stability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: GenPO++ offers an exact, memory-guided reversible flow policy for on-policy RL, enabling Jacobian-free likelihood ratios and removing surrogate/dummy-action overhead while preserving expressiveness.
AI.motivation: Flow-based policies enable expressive multimodal actions but on-policy likelihood evaluation is hard; existing fixes cause bias or inefficiency.
AI.method: Use history states as auxiliary memory within a high-order reversible ODE solver to achieve exact inversion without expanding action dimension; log-determinant fixed by solver coefficients; enable likelihood-ratio computation without Jacobian.
AI.result: Empirical results show competitive or superior performance to on-policy baselines across large-scale simulated control, fine-tuning, and real robotic manipulation; improved stability and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: GenPO++ maintains expressive generative flows while avoiding bias and overhead, making likelihood-based on-policy RL with flow policies practical at scale.

title: Don't Pause: Streaming Video-Language Synchrony for Online Video Understanding
summary: Online Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have advanced toward seamless human-AI interaction through frame-by-frame processing and proactive responding. However, a critical challenge remains in streaming scenarios: existing models typically pause video perception while generating responses, breaking real-time video-language synchrony and causing stutters. To address this, we introduce a novel paradigm for online video understanding: Streaming Video-Language Synchrony (SVLS), and present LyraV, a live streaming assistant built upon a hierarchical control framework with two core innovations. First, the Frame-Driven Transition Controller (FDTC), a training-free verification-based finite-state machine, makes high-level semantic decisions on when to continue speaking, start a new response, or stay silent. Second, the Streaming Token Pacer (SToP), a plug-and-play lightweight predictive module, dynamically adapts the language generation rate to match the pace of the visual content. Concretely, LyraV performs \emph{per-frame incremental, sub-budget decoding}: within each frame interval it emits only a small chunk of tokens that fits the real-time budget, so perception is never blocked for a full sentence. Together, these components enable LyraV to seamlessly interleave incoming video frames with generated word tokens, achieving a fine-grained synchrony. Extensive experiments conducted on five online and three offline benchmarks demonstrate that LyraV preserves the backbone's general understanding ability while substantially improving streaming synchrony and narrative fluency, delivering a 98.29\% synchrony with video playback and a real-time processing speed of 3.89 FPS. Interestingly, we observe an empirical capability in LyraV: dynamic reasoning over streaming tokens, enabling continuous interpretation and "thinking" alongside visual input.

AI.tldr: LyraV enables real-time streaming video-language interaction by per-frame incremental decoding using a Frame-Driven Transition Controller (FDTC) and a Streaming Token Pacer (SToP). It achieves high synchrony with video (98.29%) and real-time processing (3.89 FPS) while preserving understanding, and exhibits dynamic token-based reasoning during streaming.
AI.motivation: To fix the stopping-and-starting mismatch in online Video-LLMs where perception is paused during response generation, breaking real-time video-language synchrony and causing stutters.
AI.method: A hierarchical control framework: (1) FDTC, a training-free verification-based finite-state machine that decides when to continue speaking, start a new response, or stay silent; (2) SToP, a plug-and-play lightweight predictive module that adjusts language generation rate to the pace of visual content; plus per-frame incremental, sub-budget decoding to emit small token chunks within each frame interval.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five online and three offline benchmarks show LyraV preserves backbone understanding while substantially improving streaming synchrony and narrative fluency, delivering 98.29% synchrony with video playback and 3.89 FPS; empirical evidence of dynamic reasoning over streaming tokens.
AI.conclusion: LyraV advances online video understanding by tightly coupling perception and generation for real-time streaming, achieving high synchrony without blocking perception, and revealing emergent dynamic token reasoning during streaming.

title: Accelerating Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimisation via Predictive-Gradient Catalysts
summary: This paper presents a general acceleration mechanism for multi-objective Bayesian optimisation (MOBO) that leverages Gaussian process predictive gradients as auxiliary signals. Rather than replacing existing Pareto-compliant acquisition functions, the proposed approach augments them with local stationarity information derived from surrogate-derived gradients, enabling faster convergence toward the global Pareto set under limited evaluation budgets. Two catalyst instantiations are investigated: an adaptive Multiple-Gradient Descent Algorithm-Based Catalyst (MGDA) and a predefined-weight variant that enables focused exploration when budgets are tight. Experiments on the DTLZ benchmark suite (using 2 objectives and 10 decision variables) show that predictive gradient catalysis can deliver significant acceleration compared to other acquisition functions (EHVI, AugTch, tMPoI, SAF) when surrogates are accurate, particularly for stationary problems.

AI.tldr: Gradient-catalyzed MOBO accelerates convergence by using GP predictive gradients as auxiliary signals to augment Pareto-compliant acquisitions; MGDA-based and fixed-weight variants offer flexible catalysts under limited budgets.
AI.motivation: Enhance sample efficiency and speed of convergence to the global Pareto set by injecting local stationarity information from surrogate-gradient signals without discarding established Pareto front acquisitions.
AI.method: Introduce predictive-gradient catalysis that augments existing Pareto-compliant MOBO acquisitions with GP-predicted gradients. Two catalyst instantiations are explored: (i) an adaptive MGDA-based catalyst leveraging multiple-gradient descent directions, and (ii) a predefined-weight variant for targeted exploration when budgets are tight.
AI.result: Demonstrates accelerated convergence to the Pareto set compared to standard acquisitions (EHVI, AugTch, tMPoI, SAF) when surrogates are accurate, especially for stationary problems; validated on DTLZ with 2 objectives and 10 decision variables under limited evaluation budgets.
AI.conclusion: Predictive gradient catalysis is an effective, parameter-efficient augmentation to MOBO that leverages surrogate gradient information to accelerate optimization toward the Pareto front; MGDA and predefined-weight catalysts provide practical options depending on problem stationary properties and budget constraints.

title: GuideCAD: A Lightweight Multimodal Framework for 3D CAD Model Generation via Prefix Embedding
summary: Multi-modal approaches used for 3D CAD generation require substantial computational resources, necessitating efficient training. To address this, we propose GuideCAD, which leverages semantically rich visual-textual representations having only a small number of trainable parameters to generate 3D CAD models. Specifically, GuideCAD uses a mapping network that converts image embeddings into prefix embeddings, enabling a pretrained large language model to integrate visual and textual information. As a result, a transformer-based decoder predicts the construction sequence using the visual-textual embeddings in order to generate the 3D CAD model. For experimental evaluation, we construct a new dataset, referred to as GuideCAD, which consists of text-image pairs. Each pair includes a text prompt that represents a 3D CAD construction sequence and its corresponding 3D CAD image. Our experimental results show that GuideCAD generates comparably high-quality 3D CAD models while using approximately four times fewer parameters and achieving twice the training efficiency compared to fine-tuning approaches. We have released the source code and dataset for our method at: https://github.com/mskimS2/GuideCAD

AI.tldr: GuideCAD leverages a mapping network to convert image embeddings into LLM-friendly prefix tokens, enabling a pretrained large language model to fuse visual and textual cues for 3D CAD construction. A transformer decoder then predicts construction sequences to generate 3D CAD models, achieving comparable quality with ~4x fewer trainable parameters and ~2x training efficiency versus fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost of multimodal 3D CAD generation by reusing pretrained LLMs to integrate visual and textual signals, minimizing trainable parameters while maintaining model quality.
AI.method: A mapping network turns image embeddings into prefix embeddings that a pretrained LLM consumes. A transformer-based decoder uses visual-textual embeddings to predict a 3D CAD construction sequence, generating the 3D model. Evaluation is performed on a newly created GuideCAD text-image dataset (text prompts of construction sequences paired with their 3D CAD images).
AI.result: GuideCAD achieves comparable quality 3D CAD models while using ~4× fewer trainable parameters and about 2× faster training than fine-tuning baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates efficient multimodal 3D CAD generation by reusing pretrained LLMs with a lightweight mapping to visual cues. The authors provide code and the GuideCAD dataset for reproducibility.

title: LARA: Latent Action Representation Alignment for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Visual-language action (VLA) models enable robots to predict actions directly from observations and language instructions, but their performance depends on large-scale, high-quality data and is limited by the scarcity of real-world robot action datasets. To facilitate VLA model learning with abundant unlabeled human videos, Latent Action Models (LAM) learn latent action representations from visual dynamics to provide additional supervision for VLA learning. However, LAM and VLA are typically trained separately, leaving LAM ungrounded during VLA training and VLA models constrained by frozen LAM representations. To address these issues, we propose Latent Action Representation Alignment (LARA), a plug-and-play framework that jointly optimizes LAM and VLA via representation alignment. This enables reciprocal benefits where LAMs learn with action trajectories to avoid spurious visual changes, while VLAs are regularized by forward dynamics learned within LAMs to reduce hallucinations of functionally ineffective trajectories. We demonstrate LARA versatility and effectiveness for pre-training, post-training enhancement of pre-trained VLA models, and LAM refinement, achieving an average of ~10%, ~5%, and ~15% improvement over 3 simulation and 1 meticulously designed real-world robotic manipulation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: LARA jointly optimizes latent-action models (LAM) and visual-language action (VLA) models by aligning latent action representations with VLA dynamics, enabling data-efficient learning from unlabeled videos and reducing hallucinations. It enables pre-training, post-training enhancement, and LAM refinement, with reported gains on simulation and real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LAMs trained on visual dynamics from unlabeled data are often ungrounded during VLA training when used as fixed representations, limiting VLA performance and causing spurious changes. A joint, aligned representation can ground LAMs during VLA learning and regularize VLA predictions with forward dynamics.
AI.method: A plug-and-play framework (LARA) that jointly optimizes LAM and VLA via representation alignment. LAM learns action trajectories from visual dynamics; VLA learns from language-conditioned observations; LARA aligns latent action representations to forward dynamics learned in LAM, allowing reciprocal feedback to improve both.
AI.result: LARA achieves ~10% improvement on 3 simulation benchmarks, ~5% on another set, and ~15% on 1 meticulously designed real-world robotic manipulation benchmark, across pre-training, post-training enhancement of pre-trained VLA models, and LAM refinement.
AI.conclusion: Joint optimization and representation alignment between LAM and VLA yield reciprocal benefits: LAM benefits from action-trajectory supervision to avoid spurious changes, while VLA performance is regularized by LAM’s forward dynamics to reduce hallucinations. LARA is versatile across tasks (pre-training, post-training, refinement) and shows meaningful performance gains on both simulation and real-world data.

title: Heterogeneous Effects of Green Finance on Urban Decarbonization: Evidence from 285 Cities in China
summary: While green finance has become a key instrument for low-carbon city transitions, its actual decarbonization effects and transmission mechanisms remain unclear. This study employs econometric models and machine learning-based analysis to examine whether and how green finance reduces city-level carbon intensity. Results show that green finance significantly lowers carbon intensity, with green bonds and green investment having the strongest impacts and evident spatial spillovers. The effects vary by development level, being most pronounced in Fourth- and Fifth-tier cities. Mediation analysis reveals that green finance operates mainly through energy structure optimization, followed by industrial upgrading, foreign direct investment, and technological innovation. SHAP analysis confirms substantial differences across financial instruments, with green bonds, funds, and credit contributing most to decarbonization. Moreover, the marginal impact is stronger in cities with low technological capacity, high industrial dependency, and coal-based energy mixes. These findings provide theoretical support and policy guidance for building a multi-level, regionally differentiated green finance system to promote inclusive low-carbon transitions. Keywords: Green Finance; Carbon Intensity; Decarbonization Effect; Machine Learning; City

AI.tldr: Green finance lowers city carbon intensity; strongest effects from green bonds and investments with spatial spillovers; effects vary by city development level (strongest in Fourth/Fifth-tier cities); mediated by energy structure optimization, industrial upgrading, FDI, and technological innovation; SHAP shows instrument differences; larger marginal impacts in low-tech, coal-based, industrially dependent cities; supports regionally differentiated green finance policy.
AI.motivation: To resolve ambiguity about whether and how green finance drives decarbonization at the city level, and through which channels and heterogeneity.
AI.method: Econometric modeling complemented by machine learning analyses; mediation analysis to identify transmission channels; SHAP analysis to attribute instrument-level contributions; assessment of spatial spillovers and heterogeneity across city tiers and development contexts.
AI.result: Green finance significantly reduces city-level carbon intensity; green bonds and green investment yield the strongest reductions; spatial spillovers observed; effects stronger in less technologically capable, more industrially dependent, coal-dominant cities; energy structure optimization is the main channel, followed by industrial upgrading, FDI, and technological innovation; SHAP indicates substantial instrument-specific differences, with bonds, funds, and credit as key contributors.
AI.conclusion: Supports building a multi-level, regionally differentiated green finance system to promote inclusive low-carbon transitions; implications for targeting instruments and cities based on development level and energy/industrial structure.

title: Never Seen Before: Benchmarking Genuine Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval with Consistent Video-Sourced Datasets
summary: Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval (ZS-CIR) aims to retrieve a target image based on a query composed of a reference image and a relative caption without training samples. Existing ZS-CIR datasets often suffer from complete irrelevance between reference and target images due to noisy image sources, and do not achieve a true zero-shot scenario as they use public image datasets that models like CLIP have been trained on. To tackle these challenges, we introduce ZeroSight, a novel benchmark for ZS-CIR. It includes a dataset with consistent reference-target pairs sourced from videos, a data construction pipeline, and evaluation methods that consider the ranking of multiple positive and negative target images. We ensure visually and semantically consistent reference-target pairs by extracting frames from a single video and generating relative captions using LLM-assisted methods. To ensure a true zero-shot scenario, we use video data published after March 31, 2022, ensuring it was not included in CLIP's pre-training data. Additionally, we propose a training-free MLLM-driven method, SC4CIR (Symmetric Consistency for CIR), which can effectively identify hard negative targets through 3 symmetric consistency checks. This method is plug-and-play, seamlessly integrating with various CIR methods and significantly improving performance. Our experimental results from 27 methods reveal that current ZS-CIR datasets and evaluation metrics result in inflated retrieval performance, exaggerating the capabilities of CIR methods. Our benchmark and models can be accessed at https://github.com/sotayang/ZeroSight.

AI.tldr: Introduces ZeroSight, a true zero-shot ZS-CIR benchmark with video-based reference-target pairs and LLM-generated relative captions, plus a training-free SC4CIR method that uses symmetric consistency to identify hard negatives; shows existing benchmarks inflated performance and provides a reusable benchmark and method.
AI.motivation: Current ZS-CIR datasets suffer from irrelevance between reference and target and are not truly zero-shot since they draw from CLIP-trained public datasets. A benchmark with post-2022 data and consistent pairs is needed to assess true zero-shot CIR capabilities.
AI.method: Create a dataset with frames from a single video to ensure visual consistency; generate relative captions via LLM-assisted methods; enforce evaluation that ranks multiple positives/negatives; define SC4CIR, a training-free, MLLM-driven method using three symmetric consistency checks to identify hard negatives and be plug-and-play with CIR models.
AI.result: Experiments across 27 methods show that existing ZS-CIR datasets and metrics inflate performance. ZeroSight provides a more reliable evaluation and demonstrates the effectiveness of SC4CIR in improving retrieval by addressing hard negatives; code and data available at the project repository.
AI.conclusion: ZeroSight offers a robust, zero-shot CIR benchmark and a versatile, training-free method that enhance performance assessments and encourage re-evaluation of CIR techniques on more rigorous, post-2022 data.

title: Does Appearance Help? A Systematic Study of Image-Based Re-Identification in Online 3D Multi-Pedestrian Tracking
comment: Accepted for publication at the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026)
summary: LiDAR-based 3D Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) typically relies solely on geometric information, which is often insufficient to distinguish between targets during prolonged occlusions or in crowded human-populated environments. While integrating RGB-based Re-Identification (ReID) offers a theoretical solution for preserving identity context, existing approaches often rely on computationally expensive parallel detectors that hinder real-time robot responsiveness. This work presents a systematic study of image-based ReID in online 3D MOT, utilizing a lightweight projection-based framework to decouple geometric and appearance modeling for mobile robots. A comprehensive analysis of feature extraction architectures is conducted, employing lightweight CNNs and Vision Transformers, and evaluating various multi-modal data association strategies to balance computational latency with robust tracking. Experiments on the Pedestrian class of the KITTI dataset reveal that naive linear fusion, of appearance and motion costs, degrades performance due to visual noise. Conversely, a cascaded matching strategy successfully recovers occluded tracks without compromising overall precision, effectively preventing identity switches to maintain human-robot interaction continuity. We show that lightweight architectures can offer an optimal trade-off between the low latency required for safe navigation and the discriminative power needed for social awareness.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, projection-based RGB-LiDAR fusion framework for online 3D MOT that uses cascaded appearance–motion data association to preserve identities under occlusions with low latency.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations ofLiDAR-only MOT in occlusions and crowded scenes, and avoid expensive parallel detectors by enabling real-time, appearance-informed tracking for mobile robots.
AI.method: Decouple geometry and appearance via a projection-based framework; explore lightweight feature extractors (CNNs and Vision Transformers); assess multi-modal data association strategies; implement cascaded matching to preserve identity and handle occlusions.
AI.result: Naive linear fusion of appearance and motion harms performance due to visual noise; cascaded matching recovers occluded tracks without sacrificing precision, preventing identity switches; lightweight architectures provide favorable latency–discrimination trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: A projection-based, lightweight appearance integration with cascaded data association enables robust, real-time 3D MOT for mobile robots, maintaining identity continuity in challenging scenes.

title: Accelerating Reproducible Research in Synthetic EHR Generation
summary: The generation of high-fidelity synthetic Electronic Health Records (EHR) is crucial for advancing medical research while preserving patient privacy. However, head-to-head comparison of existing generative models is hindered by disjointed codebases, incompatible data loaders, conflicting library dependencies, and inconsistent evaluation protocols. To address these gaps, we introduce a lightweight, end-to-end benchmarking framework for reproducible synthetic EHR evaluation, organized as a unified pipeline spanning data ingestion, standardized model training, and architecture-agnostic evaluation. Our current implementation targets the generation of longitudinal ICD diagnosis codes -- the most commonly studied modality in this literature -- and is built on the community-maintained PyHealth library. We reimplement and unify strong baselines (MedGAN, CorGAN, PromptEHR, HALO) under full ICD-9 vocabulary granularity, and add a lightweight GPT-2 baseline from the general-purpose sequence-modeling literature. We contribute a rigorous, architecture-agnostic privacy-utility evaluation suite that applies identically to GAN- and transformer-based generators, and report bootstrapped confidence intervals across all metrics. We further analyze the poor long-tailed performance of existing models and discuss the extensibility of our framework beyond diagnosis codes. By lowering the engineering barrier to running, extending, and evaluating under a single pipeline, we introduce a starting point for community-driven reproducibility and benchmarking synthetic EHR models.

AI.tldr: A unified, end-to-end benchmarking framework for reproducible synthetic EHR evaluation, focusing on longitudinal ICD diagnosis codes, unifying baselines, adding GPT-2, and providing architecture-agnostic privacy-utility metrics with bootstrapped uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Fragmented codebases, incompatible data loaders, conflicting dependencies, and inconsistent evaluation protocols hinder fair comparison of synthetic EHR models—creating a barrier to reproducibility and progress.
AI.method: Develop a lightweight, end-to-end pipeline spanning data ingestion, standardized model training, and architecture-agnostic evaluation. Built on PyHealth; reimplement/unify baselines (MedGAN, CorGAN, PromptEHR, HALO) at ICD-9 granularity and add a GPT-2 baseline. Introduce a tractable privacy-utility evaluation suite applicable to both GANs and transformers, with bootstrapped confidence intervals. Analyze long-tail performance and discuss extensibility beyond diagnosis codes.
AI.result: A unified benchmarking framework and reimplemented baselines are provided, plus a GPT-2 baseline and an architecture-agnostic evaluation suite with bootstrapped CIs. Demonstrates improved reproducibility and enables fairer comparisons across models, while revealing long-tail challenges and outlining extensibility to other EHR modalities.
AI.conclusion: The framework lowers engineering barriers to running, extending, and evaluating synthetic EHR models within a single pipeline, establishing a starting point for community-driven reproducibility and benchmarking in synthetic EHR research.

title: ForensicConcept: Transferable Forensic Concepts for AIGI Detection
summary: AI-generated image detectors achieve high accuracy on in-distribution data but often fail on unseen generators. A key obstacle to understanding this failure is the black-box nature of current detectors: they do not reveal which evidence drives their decisions. We propose ForensicConcept, a framework that extracts explicit forensic concepts from detectors and enables their transfer across backbones. Our method localizes decision-critical patches via Transformer attribution, clusters them into a compact concept codebook, and uses a concept-aligned projection to produce auditable evidence readouts. Motivated by prior studies showing that DINO representations can guide diffusion generation and exhibit concept-level correspondence with diffusion features, we introduce a generation-trace reference based on CleanDIFT diffusion features and quantify backbone-trace alignment via neighborhood-structure consistency (CKNNA). We further propose concept codebook injection to transfer diffusion-derived concepts into target backbones. Experiments on GenImage, GAN-family, and Chameleon benchmarks show consistent improvements over prior methods. We also find that CKNNA alignment predicts transfer effectiveness, providing a principled explanation for why some backbones yield more transferable forensic evidence than others.

AI.tldr: ForensicConcept extracts auditable forensic concepts from detectors using transformer-based attribution, builds a diffusion-informed concept codebook, and transfers concepts across backbones to improve generalization to unseen generators; a CKNN alignment metric predicts transferability.
AI.motivation: Black-box detectors often fail on unseen generators, hindering understanding of their decisions. There is a need for explicit, transferable forensic evidence that can be audited and transferred across backbones to explain and improve generalization.
AI.method: Localize decision-critical patches via Transformer attribution, cluster them into a compact concept codebook, and apply a concept-aligned projection to produce auditable readouts. Use a generation-trace reference based on CleanDIFT diffusion features (DINO-guided diffusion feature correspondence). Quantify backbone-trace alignment with neighborhood-structure consistency (CKNNA). Introduce concept codebook injection to transfer diffusion-derived concepts into target backbones.
AI.result: Experiments on GenImage, GAN-family, and Chameleon benchmarks show consistent improvements over prior methods. CKNNA alignment predicts transfer effectiveness, offering a principled explanation for why some backbones yield more transferable forensic evidence than others.
AI.conclusion: ForensicConcept enables interpretable, transferable forensic evidence across detectors by aligning diffusion-derived concepts with backbone representations; the CKNN alignment serves as a predictor of transfer success and the concept codebook injection facilitates cross-backbone transfer.

title: Dash2Sim: Closed-Loop Driving Simulation from in-the-wild Dashcam Videos
summary: Self-driving simulations typically rely on data collected in a small number of cities or on hand-authored synthetic scenarios. Dashcam videos cover a far broader range of locations and situations, including rare or long-tailed scenarios. They are considered less usable for simulation because it is difficult to recover accurate 4D scenes from monocular in-the-wild videos. Work zones are one such class of long-tailed situations that dashcams capture. We present Dash2Sim, a framework that turns in-the-wild monocular dashcam videos into metric, geo-referenced 4D driving logs compatible with existing simulators, and verifies eachone against an independently maintained map without annotations. We apply Dash2Sim to a large video corpus to create the ROADWork4D benchmark dataset, which spans 4,244 scenes with 2.7M 3D objects across 17 cities. On a verified subset ROADWork4D-CL (2,201 scenes), we study privileged closed-loop planners and find that work zone scenarios are difficult: while rule-based and hybrid planners generalize better than learning-based ones, all fall short, failing to make the lane changes that temporary work zone channels require. Beyond planning, dense depth recovered by Dash2Sim improves novel-view synthesis quality by up to 19% on perceptual metrics, suggesting its potential to provide rich conditioning for closed-loop sensor simulation from monocular videos.

AI.tldr: Dash2Sim converts monocular dashcam videos into metric, geo-referenced 4D driving logs for simulators, enabling ROADWork4D and exposing planning gaps in work zones; dense depth also benefits novel-view synthesis.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and long-tailed, rare work-zone scenarios in driving simulators; leverage abundant dashcam footage with map-verified 4D reconstruction to create scalable, annotation-free datasets.
AI.method: A pipeline that recovers metric 3D structure and depth from monocular video, generates geo-referenced 4D logs, and validates them against independent maps without annotations; builds ROADWork4D (4,244 scenes, 2.7M objects, 17 cities); defines ROADWork4D-CL subset for closed-loop planner evaluation.
AI.result: ROADWork4D established; work-zone scenarios prove challenging for planners; rule-based and hybrid planners generalize better than learning-based, but all struggle with temporary lane changes; dense depth from Dash2Sim improves novel-view synthesis by up to 19% on perceptual metrics.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and value of monocular dashcam-to-4D logs for simulation, highlights planner gaps in work zones, and shows richer depth information can enhance perception tasks and sensor simulation conditioning.

title: RASFT: Rollout-Adaptive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Reasoning
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is a prevailing method for adapting large language models to reasoning tasks by imitating offline expert demonstrations, often treating a single expert trajectory as the target behavior. However, reasoning is not simple path imitation: rigidly following one demonstrated solution may overfit to surface forms and suppress the model's own reasoning distribution. We propose Rollout-Adaptive Supervised Fine-Tuning (RASFT), a policy-aware SFT framework that calibrates expert supervision according to problem-level solvability estimated from verified on-policy rollouts. For each problem, RASFT strengthens expert guidance when the current policy struggles, while relaxing rigid imitation and incorporating correct self-generated trajectories when the model already exhibits reliable reasoning behavior. To preserve useful reasoning priors, RASFT further introduces a clipped inverse ratio between the frozen reference model and the current policy to constrain excessive policy drift. Experiments across multiple models on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks and two code reasoning benchmarks show that RASFT achieves better overall performance than SFT, SFT variants, and representative RL methods. The code is available at https://github.com/zjd1sq/RASFT.

AI.tldr: Rollout-Adaptive SFT (RASFT) uses policy-aware supervision by adapting expert guidance with on-policy rollout solvability estimates and clips the policy drift via a ratio constraint, yielding stronger reasoning performance than standard SFT and RL baselines on math and code reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: The standard SFT paradigm often overfits to a single expert trajectory and misaligns with the model's own reasoning distribution. By adapting supervision to problem solvability, RASFT aims to harmonize imitation with the model's evolving reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: For each problem, estimate solvability from verified on-policy rollouts; increase expert guidance when the policy struggles and relax imitation when reasoning is reliable; apply a clipped inverse ratio between the frozen reference model and the current policy to constrain excessive policy drift.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple models and six math reasoning benchmarks plus two code reasoning benchmarks show RASFT achieves better overall performance than SFT, SFT variants, and representative RL methods; code is released.
AI.conclusion: RASFT provides a policy-aware, drift-constrained SFT framework that better preserves useful reasoning priors while leveraging model improvements through adaptive supervision.

title: STREAM: Stochastic Riemannian Flow Matching with Anisotropic Decoder for Digital Histopathology Image Generation
summary: Synthetic histopathology image generation addresses critical challenges in computational pathology, including patient privacy and the growing need for large-scale training data for foundation models. Latent diffusion models have dominated the image generation domain, with recent works emphasizing that the choice of latent space is critical to the quality of generated images. Existing state-of-the-art generative models in histopathology use pretrained Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) as conditioning signals, and we observe that this leads to "conditioning collapse," where the conditioning signal dominates the latent space and lowers the quality and diversity of generated samples. Therefore, we instead use pretrained histopathology VFMs as the latent space itself, leveraging their patch-token features that encode rich semantic information. We empirically show that these features are $\ell_2$-normalized and lie on the unit hypersphere $\mathcal{S}^{d-1}$ with strong angular dominance and intrinsic curvature, making them naturally suited for a Riemannian formulation. We therefore present STREAM, the first framework to apply Riemannian flow matching in the pathology domain. STREAM consists of two stages: 1) a bridge-type stochastic perturbation that establishes per-token rectifiability on $\mathcal{S}^{d-1}$ for training a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) in latent space, and 2) a novel anisotropic decoder that allocates robustness to low-energy directions of the velocity-field Jacobian while preserving fidelity along its high-energy directions. Together, STREAM achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction and generation performance on breast and colorectal cancer datasets. The code will be publicly released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: STREAM leverages unit-sphere histopathology features from pretrained VFMs as a diffusion latent space, uses Riemannian flow matching with a two-stage training and an anisotropic decoder to avoid conditioning collapse, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction and generation on breast and colorectal cancer datasets.
AI.motivation: Synthetic histopathology image generation must address patient privacy and the need for large-scale data for foundation models. Using pretrained VFMs as conditioning can cause conditioning collapse, where the conditioning signal dominates the latent space and harms quality and diversity.
AI.method: Use pretrained histopathology VFMs to supply the latent space via per-token patch-token features that are l2-normalized to lie on the unit sphere. Apply a bridge-type stochastic perturbation to establish per-token rectifiability on S^{d-1} for training a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) in latent space. Introduce an anisotropic decoder that emphasizes robustness to low-energy directions of the velocity-field Jacobian while preserving fidelity along high-energy directions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction and generation performance on breast and colorectal cancer datasets.
AI.conclusion: STREAM is the first framework to apply Riemannian flow matching in the pathology domain, enabling high-quality, diverse synthetic histopathology images without conditioning collapse; code will be released upon acceptance.

title: A Geometric View for Understanding Concept Learning and Neuron Interpretation in Sparse Autoencoders
summary: We propose a unified mathematical framework for a geometric understanding of concept learning and neuron interpretation in sparse autoencoders (SAEs). While SAEs improve interpretability of neural networks by learning sparse feature representations, a principled definition of ''concept'' and ''learning'' remains unclear. We formalize concepts as sets of data points and cast concept learning as a set-alignment problem between human-defined and model-induced concepts. This formulation distinguishes three increasingly strong notions of learning -- detection, separation, and approximation -- and yields geometric conditions, error bounds, and capacity constraints for when concepts can be represented by individual neurons or multi-neuron units. It also provides a set-theoretic account for common SAE phenomena, including feature splitting, feature absorption, feature families, and hierarchical concepts. Finally, we connect concept learning and neuron interpretation through formal concept analysis, showing that the two directions need not agree and that their many-to-many structure can be organized by concept lattices. Experiments on synthetic data with ReLU and Top-$K$ SAEs illustrate the theory and reveal the effects of SAE size and sparsity on concept learning.

AI.tldr: A unified geometric/set-theoretic framework for concept learning and neuron interpretation in sparse autoencoders; formalizes concepts as data-sets, analyzes set-alignment with human concepts; introduces detection/separation/approximation, gives geometric/error/capacity bounds; uses formal concept analysis to relate concepts and neurons; validated on synthetic data with ReLU/Top-K SAEs; insights on the effect of size and sparsity.
AI.motivation: Interpreting neural networks via sparse representations remains ill-posed; a principled, formal framework is needed to define 'concept' and 'learning', and to connect human concepts with model-induced concepts; explains observed SAE phenomena (feature splitting/absorption, etc.) and provides error/bound analyses.
AI.method: Formalize concepts as sets of data points; set-alignment between human-defined concepts and model-induced concepts; define three learning levels (detection, separation, approximation); derive geometric conditions, error bounds, and capacity constraints for neuron-based representations; provide a set-theoretic account of SAE phenomena; connect to formal concept analysis to build concept lattices; empirical validation on synthetic data with ReLU and Top-K SAEs.
AI.result: Geometric conditions, error bounds, and capacity constraints for concept representations; a set-theoretic explanation of SAE phenomena; a formal link between concept learning and neuron interpretation via concept lattices; empirical demonstration showing how SAE size and sparsity affect concept learning.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled, unified basis for understanding concept learning and neuron interpretation in SAEs, clarifying when single versus multi-neuron representations are possible, and revealing the lattice-structured relation between human and model concepts; practical implications for designing sparse autoencoders; future work includes evaluation on real data and extension to broader architectures.

title: TrioPose: Native Triple-Stream Diffusion Transformers for Pose-Guided Text-to-Image Generation
comment: 15 pages (9 pages main body, 6 pages references and appendix), 3 figures, 5 tables
summary: Pose-guided text-to-image generation often suffers from limb distortions and feature crosstalk in complex multi-person scenarios. While existing UNet-based adapters struggle with long-range spatial dependencies, emerging Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiTs) offer superior global modeling. However, naive signal concatenation in MM-DiTs severely disrupts pre-trained latent distributions. To address this, we propose TrioPose, a native pose-driven framework built upon the SD3.5M architecture. Specifically, we introduce a Triple-Stream Pose-Aware DiT (TSPA-DiT) that treats pose as an independent modality. It employs layer-wise activation and zero-initialized dual-residual injection to smoothly enforce geometric constraints while preserving pre-trained latent stability. To resolve severe multi-instance occlusions, we design a Learnable Relational Bias Mask that categorizes topological connectivity into fine-grained physical states, mapping them into continuous attention soft constraints to effectively decouple inter-instance interference. Furthermore, a Pose-Guided Spatial Loss Weighting strategy modulates the native diffusion objective using heatmap-derived error maps, focusing anatomical supervision strictly on distortion-prone regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TrioPose achieves state-of-the-art performance across challenging benchmarks, including Human-Art, CrowdPose, and OCHuman. Notably, it attains an AP of $64.33$ on Human-Art, representing a $30\%$ improvement over prior arts, while setting new standards for visual fidelity and text-image semantic alignment in complex multi-human generation.

AI.tldr: TrioPose introduces a native pose-driven diffusion framework for multi-person text-to-image generation (T2I) that treats pose as an independent modality via a Triple-Stream Pose-Aware DiT (TSPA-DiT). It uses a Learnable Relational Bias Mask to decouple inter-instance interference and a Pose-Guided Spatial Loss Weighting to focus supervision on distortion-prone regions, achieving state-of-the-art results on Human-Art, CrowdPose, and OCHuman (e.g., AP 64.33 on Human-Art, ~30% improvements).
AI.motivation: Pose-guided T2I in multi-person scenarios suffers from limb distortions, feature crosstalk, and limited long-range dependencies in UNet adapters. MM-DiTs offer strong global modeling but naive signal concatenation destabilizes pretrained latent distributions. Occlusions and inter-instance interference remain challenging.
AI.method: Propose TrioPose built on SD3.5M with a Triple-Stream Pose-Aware DiT (TSPA-DiT) that treats pose as an independent modality. Introduce layer-wise activation and zero-initialized dual-residual injection to enforce geometric constraints while preserving latent stability. Employ a Learnable Relational Bias Mask to categorize topological connectivity into fine-grained physical states, mapping them to continuous attention soft constraints to decouple inter-instance interference. Apply Pose-Guided Spatial Loss Weighting using heatmap-derived error maps to focus supervision on distortion-prone regions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on Human-Art, CrowdPose, and OCHuman. Achieves AP 64.33 on Human-Art, about 30% better than prior arts, with improved visual fidelity and semantic alignment in complex multi-human generation.
AI.conclusion: TrioPose effectively enforces geometric consistency and reduces inter-instance interference without disrupting pretrained latent distributions, yielding robust performance in challenging multi-person T2I tasks and demonstrating strong generalization to realistic benchmarks.

title: CF-JEPA: Mask-free forward prediction with asymmetric encoder utilization for time-series representation learning
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) for time-series representation learning is dominated by two paradigms: contrastive methods, which face challenges in constructing positive or negative pairs, and masking-based methods, which disrupt the temporal continuity of time-series signals. Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) offers a promising alternative by predicting in representation space rather than reconstructing raw inputs. However, existing time-series JEPA variants still rely on masking and therefore inherit its continuity problem. Crop-based Forward JEPA (CF-JEPA) is proposed as an innovative mask-free framework that replaces masking with multi-horizon forward prediction: random crops serve as context views, and short-, mid-, and long-horizon future representations are predicted in the forward temporal direction, directly leveraging the inherent temporal ordering of time-series data as a learning signal. A strong asymmetry is also identified between the online encoder and the exponential moving average (EMA) target encoder, both produced from a single training run: the online encoder develops higher-rank discriminative features, while the EMA target encoder develops smoother, lower-rank temporal features. Exploiting this asymmetry, classification is routed to the online encoder and forecasting or anomaly detection to the EMA target encoder, achieving a 27% reduction in multivariate forecasting mean squared error (MSE) at no additional training cost. Across 126 University of California, Riverside (UCR) and 26 University of East Anglia (UEA) classification datasets, eight electricity transformer temperature forecasting benchmarks, and Key Performance Indicator /Yahoo anomaly detection, CF-JEPA achieves the highest average accuracy and rank on UCR and UEA among self-supervised baselines and ranks second on univariate forecasting and k-nearest neighbors-scored anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: CF-JEPA: a mask-free JEPA for time-series using multi-horizon forward prediction with random crops; exploits online/EMA encoder asymmetry to route tasks, achieving strong cross-dataset SSL performance.
AI.motivation: Time-series SSL is dominated by contrastive and masking-based methods. Contrastive SSL struggles with positive/negative pair construction; masking-based methods disrupt temporal continuity. JEPA predicts in representation space but often relies on masking. There is a need for a mask-free JEPA that leverages temporal order as supervision.
AI.method: Crop-based Forward JEPA (CF-JEPA) uses random crops as context views and predicts short-, mid-, and long-horizon future representations in the forward temporal direction. It is mask-free and exploits the inherent temporal ordering. An online encoder and an EMA target encoder emerge from a single training run, with a demonstrated asymmetry: online encoder learns higher-rank discriminative features while EMA target encoder learns smoother, lower-rank temporal features. Downstream tasks are routed: classification via the online encoder; forecasting or anomaly detection via the EMA target encoder.
AI.result: CF-JEPA achieves 27% reduction in multivariate forecasting MSE. Across 126 UCR and 26 UEA classification datasets, eight electricity transformer temperature forecasting benchmarks, and KPI/Yahoo anomaly detection, CF-JEPA attains the highest average accuracy and rank on UCR/UEA among self-supervised baselines and ranks second on univariate forecasting and kNN-based anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: CF-JEPA provides a strong mask-free JEPA framework for time-series SSL that leverages multi-horizon forward prediction and the online/EMA asymmetry to route tasks, delivering superior performance on standard benchmarks and robust cross-domain results.

title: AsyncPatch Diffusion: spatially-flexible image generation
summary: Standard diffusion models corrupt an entire sample with a single shared noise level, forcing all spatial regions to follow the same denoising trajectory. We introduce AsyncPatch Diffusion, a joint-diffusion framework that assigns distinct noise levels to different input dimensions, such as image pixels, or latent tokens. We show how this asynchronous corruption defines a valid generative process while supporting a richer family of spatially heterogeneous denoising trajectories, and prove the first valid ELBO for this process. We show that a single pretrained model can perform spatially adaptive generation, where different regions are denoised on different schedules. A key challenge is training: naive independent noise-level sampling overemphasizes highly heterogeneous configurations and underrepresents homogeneous noise levels, that are crucial during sampling. We address this with a controlled noise-level sampler that regulates both the average corruption level and its spatial variability. AsyncPatch achieves generation quality comparable to conventional diffusion on ImageNet 256 and LSUN, while being natively suited for inpainting without task-specific fine-tuning. We further introduce input guidance, which uses clean or partially corrupted regions to guide the generation of unknown regions, improving local consistency and texture matching. Finally, we demonstrate adaptive generation strategies including uncertainty-guided acceleration and autoregressive sampling.

AI.tldr: AsyncPatch Diffusion introduces per-dimension (e.g., per-pixel or per-token) noise levels in a joint diffusion process, enabling spatially heterogeneous denoising trajectories with a single pretrained model, validated by an ELBO, and extended with input guidance and adaptive sampling for inpainting and adaptive generation.
AI.motivation: Standard diffusion uses a single shared noise level, forcing identical denoising paths across the sample. This limits regional control and inpainting capabilities. Per-dimension noise schedules offer richer expressivity and practical benefits for localized generation and guidance.
AI.method: Define a joint-diffusion process with distinct noise levels across input dimensions (pixels/latent tokens) and asynchronous corruption. Prove the first valid ELBO for this process. Train with a controlled noise-level sampler that balances average corruption and spatial variability. Enable spatially adaptive generation with a single pretrained model. Introduce input guidance to steer unknown regions using clean or partially corrupted regions. Demonstrate adaptive strategies including uncertainty-guided acceleration and autoregressive sampling.
AI.result: AsyncPatch achieves generation quality comparable to conventional diffusion on ImageNet 256 and LSUN. It is natively suited for inpainting without task-specific fine-tuning. Input guidance improves local texture matching and consistency. The framework supports adaptive generation strategies, such as uncertainty-guided acceleration and autoregressive sampling.
AI.conclusion: Per-dimension diffusion is a viable, flexible generalization of diffusion models. A single model can realize spatially adaptive generation with proper training, and the controlled noise sampler is key to balancing homogeneous and heterogeneous configurations. The approach enables effective inpainting, input-guided generation, and dynamic sampling strategies.

title: Hierarchical Forecast Reconciliation for Urban Rail Transit Demand Prediction under Operational Disruptions
summary: Accurate and coherent passenger demand forecasting is essential for Urban Rail Transit (URT) operations. Passenger demand has a hierarchical structure in which origin-destination (OD) flows aggregate to station-level inflows and outflows through conservation constraints. In practice, station-level and OD-level forecasts are often generated independently, producing incoherent predictions that violate these constraints and introduce inconsistencies into operational decision-making. Such issues become more severe during disruptions, when forecasting reliability is most critical. This paper presents the first hierarchical forecast reconciliation framework for joint station-level and OD-level URT demand prediction. A neural Fully Connected Reconciler (FCR) learns a non-linear mapping from incoherent base forecasts to coherent hierarchical predictions while guaranteeing exact structural consistency by construction. The method is benchmarked against OLS, WLS, and Minimum Trace (MinT) variants using Rejsekort smart-card data from the Copenhagen S-train network under one-step, multi-step, and disruption forecasting scenarios. Results show that reconciliation consistently improves OD forecasting accuracy while ensuring hierarchical coherence. Under normal conditions, FCR performs competitively with MinT-based methods. An oracle analysis indicates that perfect station-level forecasts could reduce OD prediction error by up to 34 percent, highlighting the value of improved base forecasts. Under severe disruptions, FCR outperforms classical methods, reducing OD forecasting error by up to 17.45 percent in multi-step destination-side delay scenarios. These findings establish hierarchical reconciliation as an effective mechanism for improving forecast robustness, with the largest benefits occurring under the most challenging operating conditions.

AI.tldr: Neural Fully Connected Reconciler (FCR) for URT hierarchical demand: learns a non-linear mapping to produce coherent station-level and OD-level forecasts, outperforming classical methods under disruptions, with strong coherence guarantees; normal-case competitiveness with MinT; disruption gains substantial.
AI.motivation: In URT, origin-destination (OD) flows aggregate to station-level inflows and outflows through conservation constraints. Independent forecasts often violate these constraints, causing incoherence and unreliable decisions, especially during disruptions. Hierarchical reconciliation enforces coherence and improves robustness.
AI.method: Train a neural Fully Connected Reconciler that maps incoherent base forecasts to coherent hierarchical forecasts with exact structural consistency by construction. Evaluate against OLS, WLS, MinT variants on Copenhagen Rejsekort data for one-step, multi-step, and disruption scenarios.
AI.result: Forecast reconciliation improves OD accuracy and coherence; FCR competitive with MinT under normal conditions; strong improvement under disruptions (OD error reductions up to 17.45% in multi-step destination-side delay scenarios; an oracle analysis suggests up to 34% OD error reduction if station forecasts were perfect).
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical forecast reconciliation is effective for URT demand, enhancing robustness, especially under disruption; FCR provides a robust alternative to classical linear reconciliation methods.

title: An Adaptive Data cleaning Framework for Noisy Label Detection
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) excel in computer vision tasks given large annotated datasets. In real-world applications, however, labels are often corrupted by ambiguity, human error, or dynamic environments. Over-parameterized DNNs easily memorize these noisy labels during training, degrading model accuracy and generalization. Existing data-cleaning and sample-selection strategies often rely on manually specified thresholds, prior knowledge of the noise ratio, or a single metric (either learning dynamics or geometric structure), making them unstable in complex data regimes. This paper proposes a self-adaptive data-cleaning framework that integrates local, global, and learning dynamics cues for robust noisy-label detection. Samples are mapped into a unified low-dimensional feature space through a modular feature concatenation paradigm. We provide two instantiations: a 2D metric integrating class-adaptive KNN-based local disagreement with k-means-based global centroid distance, and a 3D multi-metric that additionally incorporates a z-normalized score. Unlike conventional 1D Gaussian Mixture Models applied to a single scalar metric, our framework performs multi-metric clustering on the feature space to adaptively partition samples into clean-dominant and noise-dominant components without requiring manual thresholds or noise priors. Experiments on CIFAR-10, MNIST, and ImageNet-100 with 5% to 40% symmetric label noise show high recall across settings, including near-perfect recall (>=98%) on ImageNet-100 at 40% noise. Subsequent training yields accuracy gains across evaluated settings, especially under severe corruption on ImageNet-100. These findings suggest that multi-metric integration provides a threshold-free, practical, and low-tuning strategy for noisy label detection.

AI.tldr: A threshold-free, multi-metric data-cleaning framework for noisy labels that merges local and global cues with learning dynamics in a unified low-dimensional space, using 2D and 3D instantiations for robust detection and improved training performance under high label noise.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels hamper DNN training; existing cleaning strategies rely on manual thresholds, noise priors, or a single metric, making them unstable in complex data regimes. A unified, adaptive approach leveraging multiple cues could robustly identify clean vs. noisy samples.
AI.method: Create a unified low-dimensional feature space via modular feature concatenation. Two instantiations: (2D) a metric combining class-adaptive KNN local disagreement with k-means global centroid distance; (3D) adds a z-normalized score. Perform multi-metric clustering (instead of a single 1D Gaussian) to partition samples into clean-dominant and noise-dominant components without manual thresholds or noise priors. Validate by removing noisy samples or down-weighting them during training.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10, MNIST, and ImageNet-100 with 5%–40% symmetric label noise show high recall, including ≥98% recall on ImageNet-100 at 40% noise. Subsequent training yields accuracy gains, especially under severe corruption, indicating robustness of the multi-metric approach.
AI.conclusion: Multi-metric integration provides a threshold-free, practical, low-tuning framework for noisy-label detection that is robust across data regimes by integrating local, global, and learning-dynamics cues in a unified representation.

title: Constructing VAE Latent Spaces with Prescribed Topology
summary: Variational autoencoders (VAEs) learn low-dimensional latent representations of high-dimensional data. When the data lies on a manifold with non-Euclidean topology, the standard Gaussian prior introduces a topological mismatch that degrades reconstruction quality and prevents faithful representation. We present a constructive mathematical framework that resolves this mismatch for all manifolds that admit a product covering space. These are manifolds expressible as products of elementary factors (circles, intervals, or lines) or as quotients of such products by a finite symmetry group. The class includes cylinders, tori, Möbius strips, Klein bottles, and real projective spaces. Factorized distributions over the elementary factors yield product topologies with closed-form, decoupled KL divergences, so that each latent factor can be shaped independently while keeping training tractable. We catalogue reparametrizable encoder-prior pairs for periodic, bounded, and unbounded supports, and provide coordinate transformations that allow standard neural networks to output non-Euclidean parameters with smooth gradients. For quotient manifolds, the decoder receives group-invariant features of the covering-space coordinates, so that identified points produce identical outputs. Anchor constraints fix the coordinate system relative to the data or create soft topological holes. Experiments on synthetic manifolds and real-image datasets (rotated and cyclically shifted MNIST) confirm that a topology-matched prior aligns KL regularization with the data manifold. The resulting topology-aware models outperform the Gaussian baseline at all practically relevant regularization strengths. The code is available at https://github.com/JvHulst/VAE-Topology.

AI.tldr: A topology-aware VAE framework that matches priors to data manifolds with product covering spaces, addressing topological mismatch of Gaussian priors. It supports factorized latent factors, quotient manifolds via group-invariant decodings, and anchor constraints; demonstrates improved reconstruction/regularization on synthetic manifolds and rotated/cyclic MNIST; code released.
AI.motivation: Gaussian priors introduce a topological mismatch when data lie on non-Euclidean manifolds, impairing reconstruction and latent representations. The goal is to develop priors and encoders that respect manifold topology for accurate, tractable variational learning.
AI.method: Constructive framework for manifolds with product cover spaces; use factorized priors over elementary factors (circles, intervals, lines) to obtain decoupled KL terms; provide coordinate transforms to produce non-Euclidean parameters with smooth gradients; enable reparametrizable encoder-prior pairs; for quotient manifolds, decode from group-invariant covering-space features; anchor constraints to fix coordinates or create soft topological holes.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic manifolds and rotated/cyclic MNIST show topology-matched priors align KL with the data manifold and outperform Gaussian baselines across practical regularization strengths.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware VAEs enable faithful latent representations on non-Euclidean manifolds by matching the prior topology to the data manifold; the approach remains tractable and generalizable to a broad class of manifolds, with code available.

title: Detecting Temporally Localized Manipulations in Authentic Video Streams
summary: The rapid advancement of video editing and generative artificial intelligence technologies has made realistic video manipulation increasingly accessible. Although existing datasets have significantly advanced research in deepfake detection, object removal, and video inpainting, they do not adequately model scenarios in which a short manipulated segment is inserted into an otherwise authentic video and the original video continues afterward. In this study, we review representative datasets from the literature, analyze their characteristics, and discuss their limitations with respect to temporally localized realistic manipulation detection. Based on this analysis, we motivate the need for a new dataset specifically designed for authentic videos containing short and highly realistic manipulated intervals. Finally, we evaluate two complementary approaches on our custom-curated test set to establish an initial benchmark for this challenging scenario. The first employs a linear probe on DINOv3 features, assessed under three thresholding strategies. The second leverages DINOv3 features with a consecutive frame similarity-based method to detect temporal manipulation boundaries. Together, these experiments provide an initial benchmark for partially manipulated video detection and highlight the need for content-adaptive thresholding mechanisms. The dataset, code, and supplementary materials are publicly available at https://github.com/OkanUmur/temporally-localized-video-manipulation-detection.

AI.tldr: Introduces a dataset and benchmarks for temporally localized video manipulation, focusing on short, realistic manipulated intervals within authentic videos; benchmarks two feature-based detection approaches; highlights need for content-adaptive thresholds.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets do not adequately model short, temporally localized manipulations within authentic video streams, hindering robust detection of brief manipulated segments.
AI.method: (1) Review representative datasets and analyze their characteristics and limitations for temporally localized manipulation detection; (2) curate a new dataset designed for authentic videos containing short, highly realistic manipulated intervals; (3) evaluate two complementary detection approaches on the custom test set: a linear probe on DINOv3 features with three thresholding strategies, and a consecutive frame similarity-based method using DINOv3 features; (4) provide an initial benchmark and public resources.
AI.result: Publication of a custom dataset and accompanying code and materials; initial benchmark results establish a baseline for partially manipulated video detection and reveal the need for content-adaptive thresholding.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for temporally localized manipulation benchmarks and methods; the proposed dataset enables realistic evaluation and the initial approaches offer a starting point, with results suggesting adaptive thresholding and possibly more robust, content-aware techniques as future directions.

title: Bias in Filter Feature Selection Evaluation: A Meta-Analysis of Datasets, Baselines, and Experimental Design Choices
summary:
Background: Since 1990 many feature selection methods have been proposed across heterogeneous applications. To validate the usefulness of a new method, it needs to be compared against at least one baseline method from the existing literature on a feature selection task using at least one dataset. Recent developments in tabular Deep Learning (DL) and data valuation in Machine Learning (ML) suggest that the evaluation of new methods, algorithms, and models may be consciously or unconsciously biased. We hypothesise that a similar trend exists in feature selection (FS), particularly in filter feature selection (FFS). The aim of this study is therefore to examine FFS studies to identify factors that influence the evaluation and that might consist entry point for biases in order to recommend stronger principles for FFS evaluation.
  Methods: We analyse a sample of 28 high profile FFS studies published between 1994 and 2025. The analysis provides reflections on how to examine FFS studies, highlights lessons learned throughout the process, and gives five evidence-based recommendations for future FFS evaluation.
  Results: Multivariate Linear Regression analysis achieved a score of $R^2=0.33$. It means that 33% of the variance in the performance of new methods against chosen baselines (win rate) is explained by the number of datasets (#Datasets), the number of baselines (#Baselines), and the number of new methods (#NewMethods).
  Discussion: $R^2=0.33$ is considered medium explanation; which is promising given that this is the first such study. The medium explanation result is due to the fact that win rate is influenced by additional factors such as the maturity of the feature selection domain, the type of datasets and baselines, and the simplicity of the regression model used to explain the relationship.

AI.tldr: The study audits evaluation practices in filter feature selection (FFS) and finds only moderate explanatory power (R^2 = 0.33) for what determines whether a new method beats baselines, based on 28 high-profile studies; it identifies biases and provides five recommendations to strengthen FFS evaluation.
AI.motivation: To uncover potential biases in FS evaluation practices, particularly in filter feature selection, and to propose evidence-based principles to improve how new methods are evaluated against baselines.
AI.method: Literature analysis of 28 high-profile FFS studies published between 1994 and 2025. The study examines how evaluations are conducted, uses multivariate linear regression to relate win rate (new methods vs baselines) to predictors (number of datasets, number of baselines, number of new methods), and discusses lessons learned and five recommendations.
AI.result: The regression yields R^2 = 0.33, indicating that 33% of the variance in win rate is explained by the predictors. The authors note that this is a medium effect size for an inaugural study and that other factors (domain maturity, dataset/baseline type, and model simplicity) likely influence outcomes.
AI.conclusion: This is the first study to quantify evaluation biases in FFS practices. The moderate explanatory power suggests the need for stronger, standardized evaluation principles in FFS, guided by the five recommendations proposed by the authors to mitigate bias and improve comparability across FS studies.

title: SlimSearcher: Training Efficiency-Aware Web Agents via Adaptive Reward Gating
summary: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in complex information-seeking tasks, yet this power comes at a steep computational cost. Driven by accuracy-focused training paradigms, current models adopt brute-force strategies characterized by blind tool dependency and performative reasoning-generating long, redundant trajectories that are far from necessary for resolving these tasks, leading to wasteful tool calls and excessive token consumption. To overcome this efficiency trap, we propose SlimSearcher, a principled framework that pushes the Pareto frontier between accuracy and computational cost across both Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). In the SFT stage, SlimSearcher employs Pareto-efficient filtration to distill trajectories that are both successful and economical, guiding the model toward inherently efficiency-aware search behaviors. During RL, we introduce Adaptive Reward Gating, a dynamic reward-shaping mechanism that evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within a sampled cohort. By cascading these adaptive efficiency metrics with a strict correctness gate, our approach effectively avoids the brevity bias associated with absolute penalties and mitigates reward hacking. Extensive experiments on long-horizon benchmarks, including GAIA, BrowseComp, and XBenchDeepSearch, demonstrate that SlimSearcher reduces average tool-call rounds by 17%-58% while maintaining or improving accuracy.

AI.tldr: SlimSearcher balances accuracy and compute in deep research agents by Pareto-efficient SFT filtration and Adaptive Reward Gating in RL, cutting tool-call rounds by 17-58% while preserving or improving accuracy on GAIA, BrowseComp, and XBenchDeepSearch.
