AI.motivation: Current accuracy-driven training leads to brute-force, long and redundant tool use and performative reasoning, causing wasted computation and high token costs; there is a need to achieve similar or better accuracy with substantially lower compute.
AI.method: SFT: Pareto-efficient filtration to distill trajectories that are both successful and economical, guiding the model toward efficient search behavior. RL: Adaptive Reward Gating—dynamic reward shaping that evaluates relative tool and token efficiency within a sampled cohort, cascaded with a strict correctness gate to avoid brevity bias and reward hacking.
AI.result: Empirical results show reduced average tool-call rounds by 17%–58% across long-horizon benchmarks (GAIA, BrowseComp, XBenchDeepSearch) with maintained or improved accuracy.
AI.conclusion: SlimSearcher expands the Pareto frontier for accuracy vs. computational cost in deep research agents, mitigating brevity bias and reward hacking, and demonstrating efficiency gains across multiple benchmarks.

title: GP-Adapter: Gaussian Process CLIP-Adapter for Few-Shot Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: We propose GP-Adapter, a training-free framework that augments CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) with Gaussian Process (GP) uncertainty modeling for few-shot classification and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. While CLIP achieves strong zero-shot recognition, it yields deterministic similarity scores and offers limited uncertainty information, which is critical under distribution shift and data scarcity. GP-Adapter constructs modality-specific, class-wise one-class GPs on top of frozen CLIP embeddings using an RBF kernel for image features and a linear kernel for text prompts and fuses their predictive statistics to produce a variance-aware confidence score for OOD detection. The method requires no fine-tuning of the CLIP backbone and relies only on a small $K$-shot cache and lightweight hyperparameter selection, with memory cost scaling as $O(CK^2)$ for $C$ classes and $K$ shots. Experiments on ImageNet and multiple OOD benchmarks show that GP-Adapter provides competitive few-shot performance and consistently improves OOD detection when combined with prompt-learning baselines, highlighting the complementarity between GP-based uncertainty modeling and prompt learning. Overall, our results suggest that integrating probabilistic inference with large pre-trained vision-language models can improve reliability in low-data and distribution-shifted settings. Code is available at https://github.com/tms-byte/GP-Adapter

AI.tldr: GP-Adapter adds training-free Gaussian Process (GP) uncertainty modeling on frozen CLIP embeddings to improve few-shot classification and OOD detection by fusing image (RBF kernel) and text (linear kernel) uncertainties, without fine-tuning; competitive in few-shot and improves OOD with prompt-learning baselines.
AI.motivation: CLIP provides strong zero-shot performance but yields deterministic similarity scores with limited uncertainty information. Uncertainty modeling is essential under distribution shift and data scarcity. The work aims to enable probabilistic reasoning atop fixed CLIP features with minimal additional data and computation.
AI.method: For each class, construct modality-specific, class-wise one-class GPs on CLIP embeddings: image features with an RBF kernel; text prompts with a linear kernel. Compute predictive statistics (mean/variance) per modality and fuse them to produce a variance-aware confidence score for OOD detection. No backbone fine-tuning; relies on a small K-shot cache and lightweight hyperparameters. Memory scales as O(CK^2).
AI.result: GP-Adapter achieves competitive few-shot performance on ImageNet and consistently improves OOD detection when combined with prompt-learning baselines, highlighting the complementarity between GP uncertainty modeling and prompt learning.
AI.conclusion: Integrating probabilistic inference with large pre-trained vision-language models enhances reliability under data scarcity and distribution shift, enabling training-free, uncertainty-aware classification and OOD detection; code is available for replication.

title: On the Geometry of On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is increasingly used to improve large language model reasoning, but its training dynamics remain poorly understood. We characterize the trajectory of OPD updates in parameter space and compare it with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). A suite of parameter-space diagnostics consistently places OPD in a relaxed off-principal regime: compared with SFT, its updates affect fewer weights and avoid principal directions more strongly, while compared with RLVR, they remain less tightly constrained. Beyond this static localization, OPD exhibits subspace locking: its cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional channel. Constraining training to the update subspace formed early in training preserves OPD performance but substantially degrades SFT, indicating that the locked subspace is functionally sufficient for OPD. Control experiments further show that sparsifying the update tokens and shifting rollout generation off-policy preserve the rank dynamics, whereas mixing the OPD objective with RLVR changes them. Overall, these results suggest that OPD is not merely an intermediate point between SFT and RLVR, but induces its own update geometry in parameter space.

AI.tldr: OPD has a distinct update geometry with sparse, low-rank updates and early-subspace locking; the early subspace is sufficient for OPD, indicating it is not merely between SFT and RLVR.
AI.motivation: To elucidate the dynamic trajectory of OPD in parameter space and its relation to SFT and RLVR.
AI.method: Parameter-space diagnostics across OPD, SFT, RLVR; analysis of weight participation, principal directions, subspace locking; ablations: update token sparsification, off-policy rollout, mixing with RLVR; subspace-constraining experiments.
AI.result: OPD updates affect fewer weights and avoid principal directions more than SFT; less tightly constrained than RLVR; cumulative updates rapidly enter a narrow low-dimensional subspace (subspace locking); constraining to this subspace preserves OPD performance but degrades SFT; update tokens sparsification and off-policy rollouts preserve rank dynamics; mixing with RLVR changes rank dynamics.
AI.conclusion: OPD creates its own update geometry, not simply an intermediate between SFT and RLVR; the early-locked subspace is functionally sufficient for OPD.

title: 3DMorph: Single-Image-Guided Local 3D Shape Editing and Morphing
summary: Despite recent progress in 3D generation, intuitive editing of existing shapes remains limited. Unlike images, which benefit from well-established inpainting tools, general 3D objects such as meshes still lack simple and effective methods for local shape editing. Existing approaches are often global, domain-specific, require complex user interaction, or focus on appearance (color and texture) rather than geometry. We introduce 3DMorph, a training-free framework for single-image-guided local 3D shape editing and morphing. Given an edited image showing a desired shape modification, our method automatically localizes the relevant 3D region and transfers 2D modifications to 3D while preserving unmodified areas. 3DMorph also enables intermediate shape generation between the original and edited objects, facilitating design exploration. To benchmark editing quality, we introduce Delta3D, an image-guided local 3D editing benchmark with paired ground-truth edits. Experimental results show that 3DMorph translates intuitive 2D edits into 3D, outperforming state-of-the-art generative and editing methods.

AI.tldr: 3DMorph is a training-free framework enabling single-image-guided local 3D shape editing and morphing by localizing edit regions and transferring 2D modifications to 3D, with a new Delta3D benchmark and results surpassing prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current 3D editing tools are often global, domain-specific, require complex interaction, or focus on appearance rather than geometry, leaving intuitive local editing for 3D objects underdeveloped compared to image inpainting.
AI.method: A training-free pipeline that, given an edited image, localizes the relevant 3D region and transfers 2D edits to the 3D model while preserving unmodified areas. It supports intermediate morphs between original and edited shapes and introduces Delta3D, a benchmark with paired ground-truth edits for image-guided local 3D editing.
AI.result: Experimental results show that 3DMorph effectively translates intuitive 2D edits into 3D geometry and outperforms state-of-the-art generative and editing methods.
AI.conclusion: 3DMorph provides a practical, training-free solution for local 3D editing guided by images and introduces Delta3D as a standardized benchmark for such edits.

title: Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning for Stochastic Constrained Decision-Making
summary: Stochastic constrained decision-making requires optimizing performance objectives while enforcing statistical requirements such as safety or fairness. However, standard primal--dual methods struggle to update multipliers robustly under stochastic mini-batch feedback, as the noise of mini-batch gradients and constraint estimates can be directly accumulated into the multiplier memory. To address this issue, we propose Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning (RCML), which reformulates multiplier updating as projected-pressure feedback. The central idea is to decompose the projected multiplier into an effective pressure signal for primal descent and a pressure-memory residual for finite-gain multiplier tracking. To handle heterogeneous and noisy observations, we further augment this residual-integral backbone with modular stochastic stabilization components. For the convex-affine backbone, we establish finite-gain convergence, derive a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback, and show that the residual feedback law admits a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Experiments across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks show that RCML improves feasibility control and multiplier stability while maintaining competitive objective performance. Code is available here.

AI.tldr: RCML offers a robust multiplier-update mechanism for stochastic constrained optimization by separating a projected multiplier into a primal-descent pressure and a finite-gain residual memory, with stochastic stabilization; it achieves finite-gain convergence for convex-affine problems, provides a stochastic residual bound, and shows local KKT-consistency near regular points, with empirical gains in feasibility and stability.
AI.motivation: Stochastic constrained decision-making is hampered by noise in mini-batch gradients and constraint estimates, causing multiplier updates to accumulate error and degrade feasibility control.
AI.method: Introduce Residual-Controlled Multiplier Learning (RCML) that reframes multiplier updates as projected-pressure feedback. Decompose the projected multiplier into an effective pressure signal driving primal descent and a pressure-memory residual for finite-gain tracking. Augment the residual backbone with modular stochastic stabilization components to handle heterogeneity and noise.
AI.result: For the convex-affine backbone, proves finite-gain convergence and derives a stochastic residual bound under mini-batch feedback. Shows the residual feedback law has a local KKT-residual interpretation near regular KKT points of nonconvex problems. Empirically, RCML improves feasibility control and multiplier stability while maintaining competitive objective performance across optimization, allocation, and fair-ranking tasks.
AI.conclusion: RCML provides a robust, theoretically grounded multiplier update mechanism for stochastic constrained optimization, improving feasibility control and stability without sacrificing objective performance. Code is available.

title: Native3D: End-to-End 3D Scene Generation via Unified Mesh-Texture Modeling and Semantic Alignment
summary: This paper presents Native3D, the first end-to-end 3D scene generation framework that completely bypasses 2D intermediate representations. Traditional approaches typically require adapting 3D representations to the 2D domain to leverage pre-trained diffusion models, which inevitably introduces domain adaptation issues including geometric structural distortion and texture detail degradation. To address these limitations, we design a unified mesh-texture joint representation that simultaneously models both geometric structures and texture features through a Transformer-based scene encoder, effectively maintaining spatial relationships and visual consistency among objects within scenes. We further propose the 3D Representation Alignment Loss (3D REPA Loss), which employs an improved contrastive learning mechanism to align multi-level semantic representations in the latent space, significantly enhancing geometric and textural fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that Native3D outperforms existing methods in both generation quality and editing flexibility, providing a novel solution for 3D scene editing.

AI.tldr: Native3D proposes end-to-end 3D scene generation without 2D intermediates, using a mesh-texture joint representation and 3D REPA Loss to align multi-level semantics via a Transformer encoder, achieving higher fidelity and editability.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of 2D-projected diffusion modeling for 3D scene generation, which can cause geometric distortion and texture degradation; enable true end-to-end 3D generation with preserved geometry and texture and improved editing capabilities.
AI.method: Introduce a unified mesh-texture joint representation processed by a Transformer-based scene encoder to preserve spatial relationships; propose 3D Representation Alignment Loss (3D REPA Loss) using an enhanced contrastive objective to align multi-level semantic representations in latent space.
AI.result: Native3D outperforms existing methods in generation quality and editing flexibility, with improved geometric fidelity and texture preservation.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable end-to-end 3D scene generation framework that bypasses 2D intermediates, leveraging a mesh-texture joint representation and 3D REPA Loss to enhance fidelity and editability.

title: The discovery of the effects of women employment participation on the fertility of developing countries: A panel data approach
summary: The fertility trend in developing countries has experienced a significant decline in the last few decades; at the same time, the role of women in the workplace has improved. To have a better insight of the causality of the rate of women participation in the labor market on the total fertility rate in developing world, this paper divides the dataset of 115 developing countries in the period of 1991-2018 into four continents group (Africa, North/South America, Asia/Pacific, Europe) and then applies a data-driven panel data econometric procedure to mitigate omitted bias. The results suggest that the fertility behaviors of women in the North/South America continents are influenced by their career choice; meanwhile in society of other regions, other factors might be more important to women when thinking of having children. In conclusion, policymakers can reference to the paper and formulate policies to have more incentives in making reproductive decisions and further research in the field needs to consider family policies and patrilocality of developing countries as important data.

AI.tldr: Regionally heterogeneous effect of female labor participation on fertility in developing countries (1991–2018): significant influence in North/South America; other regions show different dominant factors; policy implications point to family policies and cultural context (patrilocality).
AI.motivation: To understand whether women's labor market participation causally affects total fertility rate in the developing world and to identify regional heterogeneity to inform targeted policy-making.
AI.method: Panel data analysis on 115 developing countries over 1991–2018, with continental grouping (Africa, North/South America, Asia/Pacific, Europe) and a data-driven approach intended to mitigate omitted-variable bias.
AI.result: Fertility behavior in North/South America appears influenced by women's career choices; in Africa, Asia/Pacific, and Europe, other factors may dominate fertility decisions; results suggest substantial regional heterogeneity in the labor–fertility nexus.
AI.conclusion: Policymakers could tailor reproductive and labor policies to regional contexts; future research should incorporate family policies and cultural factors such as patrilocality when analyzing fertility determinants in developing countries.

title: Consistent-Inversion: Reverse Consistency Guidance for Structure-Preserving Visual Editing
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Multimedia; 10 pages, 4 figures
summary: Text-guided diffusion models have become effective tools for real-image visual editing, where the edited image must follow a target instruction while preserving editing-irrelevant structure. Most training-free editors rely on inversion: a source image is mapped to a noisy latent trajectory and the terminal latent is reused for target-prompt denoising. This reuse is useful for preservation, but it also couples source reconstruction and target editing. The resulting trajectory mismatch may either damage background/layout details or over-constrain the intended edit. This paper presents Consistent-Inversion, a training-free reverse consistency guidance framework for structure-preserving visual editing. Instead of treating the inverted source latent as a fixed initialization, Consistent-Inversion checks whether an intermediate target trajectory can be reversed toward the source inversion trajectory under the source prompt. To make this check well-defined, we construct an auxiliary target-side noise representation, perform source-guided reverse denoising, and use the resulting reverse consistency discrepancy as a correction signal for selected early target denoising steps. The method does not update model parameters, is compatible with inversion-based editors, and introduces only a small inference overhead when applied sparsely. Experiments on PIE-Bench show that Consistent-Inversion improves background and structural fidelity under a unified SD3.5 protocol while maintaining target-prompt alignment, and compatibility experiments further verify the same correction principle on classical Stable-Diffusion inversion pipelines.

AI.tldr: Training-free Consistent-Inversion improves structure-preserving edits by enforcing reverse-consistency during diffusion inversion, using an auxiliary target-side noise and selective correction of early denoising steps, without model updates.
AI.motivation: Inversion-based editors reuse the terminal latent from the source image to enable target-guided edits, which couples reconstruction and editing and can cause trajectory mismatch that harms background/structure or over-constrains edits. A method is needed to decouple reconstruction from editing while preserving content.
AI.method: Introduce Consistent-Inversion. Treat the inverted source latent as non-fixed and check whether an intermediate target trajectory can be reversed to align with the source inversion under the source prompt. Construct an auxiliary target-side noise representation, perform source-guided reverse denoising, and use the resulting reverse-consistency discrepancy as a correction signal for selected early target-denoising steps. No parameter updates; compatible with inversion-based editors; introduces small inference overhead when applied sparsely.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on PIE-Bench under a unified SD3.5 protocol shows improved background and structural fidelity while maintaining target-prompt alignment; additional compatibility experiments verify the same correction principle on classical Stable-Diffusion inversion pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Consistent-Inversion provides a lightweight, training-free correction mechanism that preserves structure during text-guided diffusion edits. It demonstrates a general reverse-consistency principle for inversion-based editing pipelines and is compatible with existing editors with minimal overhead.

title: OffQ: Taming Structured Outliers in LLM Quantization by Offsetting
summary: Low-bit quantization has been widely adopted to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) by significantly reducing computational cost and memory usage. However, activation outliers pose a major challenge to effective quantization, often leading to notable performance degradation. In this paper, we introduce OffQ, a method designed to mitigate activation outliers in low-bit quantization through a novel offsetting mechanism. Specifically, OffQ first identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace in the activations using a proposed top-1 PCA, and then concentrates high-magnitude activations into 1 channel via rotation. OffQ then absorbs this concentrated outlier channel by converting its magnitude into a shared offset, thereby reducing the standard deviation of the activations. This offsetting strategy enables effective W4A4KV4 quantization of LLMs using deployment-friendly uniform-grid and uniform-precision quantization. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that OffQ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, consistently improving model accuracy while preserving low-bit efficiency.

AI.tldr: OffQ mitigates activation outliers for low-bit LLM quantization by identifying a low-dimensional outlier subspace with top-1 PCA, rotating to concentrate high-magnitude activations into a single channel, and absorbing that channel into a shared offset to reduce activation std, enabling W4A4KV4 quantization with uniform-grid/precision. Empirically, it yields consistent accuracy gains over baselines while preserving low-bit efficiency.
AI.motivation: Activation outliers significantly degrade quantized LLM performance; there is a need for deployment-friendly, low-bit quantization that maintains accuracy.
AI.method: Identify the activation outlier subspace via top-1 PCA; rotate activations to concentrate high-magnitude values into one channel; convert the magnitude of that channel into a shared offset (offsetting); reduce activation standard deviation; apply W4A4KV4 quantization using uniform-grid/uniform-precision quantization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks show OffQ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, delivering consistent accuracy improvements while maintaining low-bit efficiency.
AI.conclusion: OffQ provides an effective activation outlier mitigation strategy that enables robust deployment-friendly low-bit quantization for LLMs without sacrificing efficiency.

title: TraRA: Trajectory-level Recognition Aggregation for Video Text Spotting in Urban Surveillance
comment: 22nd IEEE International Conference on Advanced Visual and Signal-Based Systems
summary: Video Text Spotting (VTS) is essential for urban surveillance and intelligent transportation systems, enabling automated reading of street signs, vehicle markings, and scene text in video streams. However, reliable recognition remains challenging due to dynamic video factors common in surveillance scenarios, including motion blur, occlusion, and scale variation, which degrade frame-level recognition. Existing VTS methods typically perform recognition independently on each frame, leading to inconsistent and inaccurate results across sequences. To address these limitations, we propose TraRA (Trajectory-level Recognition Aggregation for VTS), a plug-and-play method that performs trajectory-level text recognition by leveraging temporal and multimodal consistency. TraRA integrates two key modules: (1) the Temporal Clustering and (2) the Vision-Language Aggregation. The former refines noisy trajectories by grouping temporally and visually coherent text instances, while the latter employs a Low-Rank Adaptation-enhanced Vision-Language model to fuse visual cues with linguistic context across frames. By aggregating information over entire text trajectories, TraRA achieves robust text recognition even under challenging surveillance conditions. Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks, including road and urban scene datasets (RoadText, BOVText, ArTVideo, and ICDAR15), demonstrate that TraRA consistently improves tracking and recognition performance over state-of-the-art VTS methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/trid2912/TraRA.

AI.tldr: TraRA introduces trajectory-level recognition aggregation for video text spotting (VTS) by refining text trajectories with temporal clustering and fusing cross-frame cues via a Low-Rank Adaptation-enhanced Vision-Language model, improving robustness under surveillance challenges and outperforming state-of-the-art methods on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Frame-level VTS recognition suffers from motion blur, occlusion, and scale variation in surveillance videos; independent-frame recognition yields inconsistent results. A trajectory-level, temporally and multimodally coherent approach can leverage cross-frame consistency to improve accuracy and stability.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) Temporal Clustering to group temporally and visually coherent text instances into refined trajectories; (2) Vision-Language Aggregation that uses a Low-Rank Adaptation-enhanced Vision-Language model to fuse visual cues with linguistic context across frames, enabling robust trajectory-level recognition.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on RoadText, BOVText, ArTVideo, and ICDAR15 show TraRA consistently improves tracking and recognition performance over state-of-the-art VTS methods.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-level aggregation with temporal clustering and vision-language fusion yields robust, plug-and-play VTS performance, validating the benefit of aggregating across entire text trajectories; source code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Beyond Linear and Overcomplete Regimes: A Mean-Field Analysis of Bottleneck Autoencoders
summary: Autoencoders (AEs) learn low-dimensional representations by mapping data into a latent space while minimizing reconstruction error. Despite their empirical success, theoretical understanding remains limited and largely restricted to linear models or settings without a bottleneck. In this work, we study nonlinear AEs with a fixed finite-dimensional bottleneck in the mean-field (MF) regime. We derive explicit MF learning dynamics for both encoder and decoder, providing a tractable characterization of training in the nonlinear setting. We show that, over finite time horizons, the empirical risk of finite-width networks trained with stochastic gradient descent closely tracks the MF risk trajectory with high probability. At optimality, we further establish that the finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum, demonstrating that finite networks are sufficiently expressive to approximate the infinite-width solution.

AI.tldr: Nonlinear autoencoders with a fixed finite bottleneck are analyzed in the mean-field regime. Explicit MF dynamics for encoder/decoder are derived. Finite-width networks trained with SGD track the MF risk trajectory over finite time and, at optimality, converge to the MF optimum, implying finite networks can approximate the infinite-width solution.
AI.motivation: To extend theoretical understanding of nonlinear autoencoders with bottlenecks beyond linear models, by deriving tractable mean-field dynamics and establishing finite-width convergence to the infinite-width limit.
AI.method: Derive explicit mean-field learning dynamics for both encoder and decoder in nonlinear AEs with a fixed finite-dimensional bottleneck. Compare finite-width SGD trajectories to the MF risk over finite time horizons.
AI.result: Finite-width empirical risk closely tracks the MF risk trajectory with high probability. At optimality, finite-width risk converges to the MF optimum, showing finite networks are expressive enough to approximate the infinite-width solution.
AI.conclusion: MF theory provides a tractable, rigorous description of training nonlinear AEs with bottlenecks and indicates that practical finite networks can realize the infinite-width optimum, guiding architecture and training expectations.

title: When Recovery Matters: The Blind Spot of Surrogate Privacy in MLLM Editing
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) enable flexible instruction-driven image editing, but privacy risks arise when user images expose diverse and user-specific private content. Canonical privacy protection strategies typically substitute sensitive regions with surrogate content before cloud editing. Yet, the resulting output is often an edited surrogate rather than the desired edited source image, neglecting the local recovery in both design and evaluation scope. To this end, we introduce SPPE (Surrogate-based Privacy-Preserving Editing), the first recovery-oriented benchmark covering 36 fine-grained privacy categories and 65 editing instructions. It defines two complementary tasks: 1) editability assessment, which estimates before cloud interaction whether a surrogate can induce an edit consistent with the original image; and 2) surrogate-to-source edit recovery, which evaluates whether the edited surrogate can be transferred back to the private source with the edit effect preserved. We address each task with a dedicated method: ERMA predicts surrogate editability through instruction-aware multimodal relation modeling, while \method performs cycle-consistent recovery by using the surrogate editing pair as visual edit evidence and the source image as a source-preserving anchor. Experiments on SPPE and InstructPix2Pix show consistent improvements on both tasks. For editability assessment, ERMA improves over the best-performing baselines by 13.9% in SRCC and 12.3% in PLCC. For surrogate-to-source edit recovery, C2E-S2SER outperforms SOER across all 8 source integrity and edit consistency metrics on SPPE.

AI.tldr: Introduces SPPE, a recovery-oriented benchmark for surrogate-based privacy-preserving editing in multimodal LLMs. Defines two tasks—editability assessment and surrogate-to-source edit recovery—and proposes two methods: ERMA and C2E-S2SER. Demonstrates significant improvements over baselines on SPPE and InstructPix2Pix.
AI.motivation: Current privacy protections for MLLMs substitute sensitive regions with surrogates, but edits applied to surrogates may not reflect on the private source image. The work addresses the lack of recovery-oriented evaluation and methods to transfer edits back to the source while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Editability: ERMA uses instruction-aware multimodal relation modeling to predict whether a surrogate edit can be enacted consistently with the original image. Recovery: C2E-S2SER performs cycle-consistent recovery using the surrogate edit pair as visual evidence and the source image as an anchor to preserve source integrity while transferring edits.
AI.result: ERMA achieves 13.9% higher SRCC and 12.3% higher PLCC over best baselines for editability. C2E-S2SER outperforms SOER across all 8 source integrity and edit-consistency metrics on SPPE, with consistent gains on SPPE and InstructPix2Pix datasets.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes a recovery-oriented benchmark (SPPE) and two tailored methods (ERMA and C2E-S2SER) that improve the alignment and recoverability of edits in privacy-preserving MLLM workflows, highlighting the importance of local recovery in evaluation and design.

title: Learning Explicit Behavioral Models with Adaptive Questions and World-Model Probes
summary: Interactive agents trained only against task return can achieve high scores while failing to represent the mechanisms that make their actions succeed. This makes brittle behavior difficult to diagnose and limits adaptation when environment dynamics change. Existing LLM reflection and policy-code repair can revise behavior from failed trajectories, but questions and world-understanding tests are usually used only after training. We introduce an Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM), a trainable behavioral model that couples task performance with evidence-grounded question answering and executable mechanism prediction. An ESBM represents behavior through typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options and mechanism memory; the mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards and terminal consequences under action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes convert score failures, QA errors and transition-prediction errors into constraints for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected by a multi-criterion rule that jointly evaluates task score, answerability and active world-model consistency. Under the tested Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions, indicating that adaptive questions can serve as both training pressure and reusable benchmarks for mechanistic policy learning in this setting.

AI.tldr: ESBM couples task performance with evidence-grounded QA and executable mechanism prediction to enable explainable, repairable policies that achieve high scores on Atari-like tasks.
AI.motivation: Agents optimized solely for task return often develop brittle, opaque behaviors. Existing reflection/repair methods are post-hoc; there is a need for ongoing mechanistic reasoning and explainability during learning.
AI.method: Introduce an Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM) with typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options, and mechanism memory. A mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards, and terminal outcomes under interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and world-model probes generate constraints for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected via a multi-criterion rule combining task score, answerability, and world-model consistency.
AI.result: On Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions, showing adaptive questions can train and benchmark mechanistic policy learning.
AI.conclusion: ESBM provides a framework for explainable, mechanistic policy learning that couples performance with symbolic reasoning and verifiable world-models, enabling diagnosis and adaptation when dynamics change.

title: Textual Supervision Enhances Geospatial Representations in Vision-Language Models
summary: Geospatial understanding is a critical yet underexplored dimension in the development of machine learning systems for tasks such as image geolocation and spatial reasoning. In this work, we analyze the geospatial representations acquired by three model families: vision-only architectures (e.g., ViT), vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), and large-scale multimodal foundation models (e.g., LLaVA, Qwen, and Gemma). By evaluating across image clusters, including people, landmarks, and everyday objects, grouped based on the degree of localizability, we reveal systematic gaps in spatial accuracy and show that textual supervision enhances the learning of geospatial representations. Our findings suggest the role of language as an effective complementary modality for encoding spatial context and multimodal learning as a key direction for advancing geospatial AI.

AI.tldr: The abstract compares geospatial representations across vision-only, vision-language, and multimodal models, revealing gaps in spatial accuracy and showing that textual supervision improves geospatial encoding; multimodal learning is a promising direction for geospatial AI.
AI.motivation: Geospatial understanding is essential for tasks like image geolocation and spatial reasoning, yet its representation in modern ML models remains underexplored. The work seeks to diagnose how different model families encode geospatial information and how language supervision affects it.
AI.method: Evaluate geospatial representations across three model families (vision-only ViT; vision-language CLIP; and multimodal foundation models such as LLaVA, Qwen, Gemma) on image clusters grouped by localizability (people, landmarks, everyday objects), then compare spatial accuracy and the influence of textual supervision.
AI.result: Textual supervision enhances learning of geospatial representations across models; there are systematic gaps in spatial accuracy, particularly for categories with lower localizability. Multimodal learning and language provide complementary spatial context that improves geospatial encoding.
AI.conclusion: Language acts as an effective complementary modality for encoding spatial context, and multimodal foundation models are a key direction for advancing geospatial AI.

title: A machine-learning-assisted progressive digit-randomness screening framework for detecting non-random patterns in raw numerical research data
summary: Raw numerical datasets remain less systematically examined in integrity screening than images, plagiarism, or summary-statistic inconsistencies. We developed the Fabrication-risk Digit Randomness Screening model (FDRS), a statistical and machine-learning framework for detecting non-random digit-pattern irregularities in numerical research data. FDRS integrates single- and joint-decimal-digit tests, Cramer's V, entropy metrics, Kullback-Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling, and semi-supervised risk scoring. It was evaluated using an instrument-derived enzymatic absorbance dataset (RawData, n=253) and a blinded manually simulated irregular dataset (ErrData, n=255). RawData showed no significant deviation in single third-decimal-digit analysis, whereas ErrData showed a significant deviation. In joint third-fourth decimal digit analysis, ErrData showed higher Cramer's V, lower normalized entropy, higher KL divergence, and a more persistent progressive-subsampling deviation signal. In internal validation, Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved the highest AUC (0.98395) and lowest Brier score (0.048439), while Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (0.926667) and balanced accuracy (0.935). RawData received a low ensemble risk score of 0.124627 and was classified as Grade 0; ErrData received a score of 0.740760 and was classified as Grade 3. External real-world benchmarks supported graded risk stratification: three datasets without identified public post-publication concerns were classified as Grade 0 or 1, whereas two datasets from publicly questioned or institutionally handled articles were classified as Grade 2 or 3. FDRS can prioritize raw numerical datasets for further review by integrating interpretable statistical and machine-learning features. It is an auxiliary digit-structure screening tool, not standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised, multi-feature screening framework (FDRS) detects non-random digit-pattern irregularities in raw numerical data to flag fabrication risk, demonstrating strong internal performance and graded external validation while serving as an auxiliary screening tool.
AI.motivation: Raw numerical datasets are underexamined for integrity compared to images or textual statistics; there is a need for automated, interpretable screening to identify fabrication-risk in numerical data.
AI.method: FDRS integrates single- and joint-decimal-digit tests, Cramer's V, entropy metrics, Kullback–Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling, and semi-supervised risk scoring. Evaluation used RawData (n=253) and ErrData (n=255); models included Elastic-net Logistic Regression and Random Forest; internal and external validation performed.
AI.result: ErrData showed significant digit irregularities in both single and joint decimal analyses; higher Cramer’s V, lower entropy, higher KL divergence, and persistent deviation signals in joint 3rd-4th digits. Elastic-net LR achieved AUC 0.984 and Brier 0.048; RF achieved accuracy 0.927 and balanced accuracy 0.935. RawData scored low risk and Grade 0; ErrData scored high risk and Grade 3; external datasets aligned with graded risk.
AI.conclusion: FDRS can prioritize raw numerical datasets for review by integrating interpretable statistical and ML features; it is an auxiliary screening tool, not standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

title: Seeing Without Exposing: Adaptive Privacy Control for Open-World, Context-Hungry MLLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have raised new privacy challenges. On the data side, user-provided inputs often include unpredictable sensitive information; while on the downstream task side, model reasoning depends on rich visual context that may itself be privacy-sensitive. Existing privacy protection methods, however, rely on predefined sensitive categories and fixed obfuscation strategies, struggling to tackle such challenges in MLLMs. To address this dilemma, we propose Anchored Privacy Drifting (APD), a training-free method that drifts privacy-sensitive elements toward semantically equivalent alternatives while anchoring contextual cues to the source image. To systematically evaluate this dual objective of privacy protection and contextual preservation, we introduce AdaptShield, a comprehensive benchmark covering 22 privacy categories, which combines conventional privacy metrics with MLLM-based assessments of contextual utility. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves balanced improvements in both privacy sanitization and content retention, with average gains of 10.4% on textual categories and 8.5% under MLLM-based evaluation across four MLLM series, i.e., Qwen2.5, Qwen3, InternVL3, and InternVL3.5.

AI.tldr: APD is a training-free privacy drift method for multimodal LLMs that obfuscates sensitive text while anchoring context to the source image; evaluated with AdaptShield across 22 privacy categories and four MLLMs, achieving balanced privacy and content retention.
AI.motivation: MLLMs expose privacy risks from user-provided inputs and privacy-sensitive visual context; existing sanitization relies on predefined categories and fixed obfuscation, which is inadequate for flexible, multimodal privacy protection.
AI.method: Anchored Privacy Drifting (APD): a training-free approach that drifts privacy-sensitive elements toward semantically equivalent alternatives and anchors contextual cues to the source image. Introduces AdaptShield, a benchmark spanning 22 privacy categories, combining conventional privacy metrics with MLLM-based assessments of contextual utility.
AI.result: APD yields balanced improvements in privacy sanitization and content retention, with average gains of 10.4% on textual categories and 8.5% under MLLM-based evaluation across four MLLM series (Qwen2.5, Qwen3, InternVL3, InternVL3.5).
AI.conclusion: APD provides a practical solution for privacy protection in multimodal LLMs, delivering improved privacy and contextual preservation without retraining, validated on a comprehensive benchmark (AdaptShield) and multiple MLLMs.

title: $α$-PFN: Fast Entropy Search via In-Context Learning
summary: Information-theoretic acquisition functions such as Entropy Search (ES) offer a principled exploration-exploitation framework for Bayesian optimization (BO). However, their practical implementation relies on complicated and slow approximations, i.e., a Monte Carlo estimation of the information gain. This complexity can introduce numerical errors and requires specialized, hand-crafted implementations. We propose a two-stage amortization strategy that learns to approximate entropy search-based acquisition functions using Prior-data Fitted Networks (PFNs) in a single forward pass. A first PFN is trained to be conditioned on information about the optima; second, the $α$-PFN is trained to predict the expected information gain by training on information gains measured with the first PFN. The $α$-PFN offers a flexible learned approximation, which replaces the complex heuristic approximations with a single forward pass per candidate, enabling rapid and extensible acquisition evaluation. Empirically, our approach is competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search implementations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, while accelerating the different entropy search variants across all our experiments, with speed ups over 50x. Source code: https://github.com/automl/AlphaPFN.

AI.tldr: A two-stage PFN-based approach (AlphaPFN) learns to approximate entropy-search acquisition functions, enabling fast, single-pass evaluation and strong speedups (~50x) while remaining competitive with ES.
AI.motivation: Entropy-search acquisitions rely on costly Monte Carlo estimation, which hampers practical Bayesian optimization. There is a need for scalable, accurate, and generalizable surrogates to replace hand-crafted approximations.
AI.method: Train a first PFN conditioned on information about the optima; train a second, α-PFN to predict the expected information gain by learning from the first PFN's information gains. The α-PFN enables a single forward pass per candidate for rapid acquisition evaluation; code provided.
AI.result: Empirically competitive with state-of-the-art entropy search on synthetic and real-world benchmarks; accelerates entropy-search variants across all experiments with speedups over 50x.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage PFN-based amortization offers a flexible, efficient alternative to Monte Carlo-based entropy searches, enabling rapid and scalable acquisition evaluation in Bayesian optimization.

title: EvoGS: Constructing Continuous-Layered Gaussian Splatting with Evolution Tree for Scalable 3D Streaming
summary: Streaming 3D Gaussian Splatting requires highly scalable, progressive representations. Existing progressive methods rely on \textit{discrete layering}, accumulating separate splat sets for each level of detail. This structural independence between layers inherently leads to error accumulation, severe splat redundancy, and uncontrolled quality transitions. We propose EvoGS, the first \textit{continuous-layering} representation. Organized as an Evolution Tree, EvoGS generates finer details via an explicit, wavelet-inspired parent-child refinement. This empowers child nodes to structurally correct ancestral errors, yield inherently sparse and highly compressible inter-layer signals. Extensive experiments show EvoGS eliminates splat redundancy from over 65\% to under 25\%. Compared to state-of-the-art baselines, it reduces transmission payload and GPU VRAM footprint by up to 2.4$\times$ and 5.5$\times$, respectively, and achieves smooth quality transitions optimal for real-time adaptive streaming. Project page: https://yuang-ian.github.io/evogs/

AI.tldr: EvoGS introduces continuous-layering for streaming 3D Gaussian splatting via an Evolution Tree with wavelet-inspired refinement, reducing inter-layer redundancy and enabling efficient, stable real-time streaming.
AI.motivation: Discrete layering in progressive 3D splatting causes error accumulation, splat redundancy, and unstable quality transitions; a continuous, inter-layer refinement mechanism could yield sparse, highly compressible inter-layer signals and improved streaming performance.
AI.method: EvoGS builds a Continuous-layering Representation organized as an Evolution Tree. It uses wavelet-inspired parent-child refinement to generate finer details, allowing child nodes to correct ancestral errors and produce sparse inter-layer signals that compress well, reducing redundancy and enabling smooth quality transitions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows splat redundancy drops from over 65% to below 25%. Compared with baselines, payload and GPU VRAM footprint decrease by up to 2.4x and 5.5x, respectively, with smooth quality transitions suitable for real-time adaptive streaming.
AI.conclusion: This work presents the first continuous-layering approach for streaming 3D Gaussian splatting, achieving substantial redundancy reductions and transmission/memory efficiency while enabling stable, real-time quality adaptation.

title: Explaining Unsupervised Disease Staging in Huntington's Disease: Insights into Model Representations and Clusters
comment: Accepted for oral presentation and as a full-length paper at the International Conference on AI in Healthcare 2026 (26-28 August 2026, Imperial College London) and will be published by Springer in the Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS) series
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects motor, cognitive, and behavioral functions, where accurate characterization of disease progression remains essential to improve patient outcome and quality of life. Unsupervised machine learning (ML) approaches have demonstrated the ability to uncover disease progression trajectories and meaningful latent stages from longitudinal data; however, their limited interpretability restricts clinical trust and translation. We extend a previously proposed ML-based disease staging framework by applying an explainability analysis to the extracted feature representations and discovered disease stages. Applied to the Enroll-HD dataset, we first project the learned representations into a lower-dimensional space to intuitively assess whether the resulting clusters align with the progression of established clinical measures. We then use saliency maps to identify the clinical features that most strongly contribute to the learned embeddings over time. Finally, we train a surrogate classifier and apply SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignments and to analyze which clinical variables drive transitions between disease stages. The explainability analysis indicates that the learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure, aligning with established motor and functional severity scores and exhibiting progressive deterioration across clusters. Within this analysis, SHAP reveals a stratification of disease stages, ranging from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known clinical progression patterns, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised Huntington's disease (HD) staging using learned embeddings validated with explainability analyses (saliency maps and SHAP) on Enroll-HD, showing alignment with clinical measures and revealing progression and intra-stage variability.
AI.motivation: HD progresses along multiple clinical domains; while unsupervised ML can uncover latent disease stages and trajectories, interpretability is essential for clinical trust and translation.
AI.method: Extend an ML-based disease staging framework by applying explainability analyses to the learned embeddings and stages. Project representations to a low-dimensional space to assess cluster alignment with established clinical measures. Use saliency maps to identify features driving embeddings over time. Train a surrogate classifier and apply SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignments and transitions between stages. Evaluation performed on the Enroll-HD longitudinal dataset.
AI.result: Learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure that aligns with motor and functional severity scores and show progressive deterioration across clusters. SHAP reveals a stage stratification from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency and highlights intra-stage variability.
AI.conclusion: Explainability analyses corroborate the clinical relevance of the unsupervised staging framework, supporting its potential for interpretable patient stratification and monitoring; further work could address external validation and robustness across cohorts.

title: OPTIMUS-Prime: Minimal and Sufficient Concept Explanations for Deep Vision Models
summary: The growing demand for transparency in automated decision-making has propelled eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to the forefront of machine learning research. In computer vision, however, existing explanation methods often prioritize end-user accessibility at the expense of formal guarantees, leaving a critical gap between practical utility and theoretical rigor. In this paper, we address this gap by introducing OPTIMUS, a novel framework for generating concept-based visual explanations for deep classification models. OPTIMUS explanations take the form of visual heatmaps that not only remain interpretable to end users, but are grounded in the well-established theory of prime implicants, providing formal guarantees that have been largely absent from existing saliency-based methods. Specifically, OPTIMUS explanations satisfy two desirable properties: sufficiency, ensuring that the highlighted concepts provably guarantee the classifier's prediction, and minimality, ensuring that no strict subset of those concepts retains this guarantee. Together, these properties yield explanations that are both logically tight and visually coherent. We validate our approach on a visual classification benchmark, demonstrating that OPTIMUS heatmaps naturally and faithfully surface the decision-relevant concepts underlying model predictions.

AI.tldr: OPTIMUS offers concept-based, heatmap explanations for deep classifiers with formal guarantees via prime implicants: sufficiency and minimality, producing interpretable and provably faithful representations validated on a visual classification benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between practical explainability in vision and theoretical guarantees; existing saliency approaches lack formal certifiability.
AI.method: Introduce OPTIMUS framework that maps predictions to a set of human-interpretable concepts and constructs visual heatmaps grounded in prime implicants; enforces sufficiency (the concepts guarantee the prediction) and minimality (no smaller subset suffices).
AI.result: Empirical validation on a visual classification benchmark shows that OPTIMUS heatmaps faithfully surface decision-relevant concepts and align with model predictions.
AI.conclusion: OPTIMUS provides a principled, interpretable, and provably faithful explanation framework for deep vision models, bridging practicality and theory.

title: REMEDI: A Benchmark for Retention and Unlearning Evaluation in Multi-label Clinical Disease Inference
summary: Language models trained for clinical disease inference are trained on patient data, which may include sensitive and private information, and data owners may request the removal of their data from a trained model due to privacy or copyright concerns. However, exactly unlearning patient-specific data is intractable, and retraining with minor data removal is resource-intensive. While there exists several machine unlearning methods that can be used, their utility is generally restricted to non-medical domains. Moreover, the existing benchmarks for evaluating such unlearning methods primarily utilize synthetically curated datasets, which are not truly representative of real-world systems. Hence, the effectiveness of these unlearning methods in the medical domain is largely unclear. To this end, we introduce REMEDI, an extensive benchmark for machine unlearning tailored to multi-label and multiclass clinical disease inference, where label correlations, longitudinal structure, and safety constraints make unlearning particularly challenging. Unlike the existing benchmarks, REMEDI considers: (1) a relevant application domain (medical), (2) comprehensive unlearning setups involving diverse sets of forget instances, (3) challenging unlearning scenarios including multi-label and multi-class classification tasks, and (4) evaluation metrics involving performance both in terms of utility and extent of unlearning achieved. REMEDI is developed using the MIMIC-III clinical database that contains comprehensive clinical data of patients. Experiments with existing unlearning methods indicate that there exists a trade-off between utility and unlearning performance. They are also largely unsuited to multi-label classification tasks. To facilitate reproducibility, we make our benchmark publicly available.

AI.tldr: REMEDI is a medical-domain benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning in multi-label/multi-class clinical disease inference, using MIMIC-III, revealing utility–unlearning trade-offs and gaps in existing methods, and enabling reproducible evaluation.
AI.motivation: To address privacy and copyright concerns when training clinical language models, and to evaluate unlearning methods in realistic medical settings where existing benchmarks (often synthetic) fail to capture multi-label, longitudinal, and safety constraints.
AI.method: Construct REMEDI with diverse forget scenarios in a medical multi-label/multi-class task, leveraging MIMIC-III data. Includes evaluation metrics for utility and extent of unlearning and releases the benchmark publicly to enable reproducibility.
AI.result: Experiments with existing unlearning methods show a clear trade-off between utility and unlearning performance; existing methods are largely unsuitable for multi-label tasks within medical data.
AI.conclusion: REMEDI fills a critical gap by providing a realistic, reproducible benchmark tailored to medical unlearning challenges, particularly for multi-label/multi-class clinical inference with label correlations and longitudinal structure.

title: AdaTok: Self-Budgeting Image Tokenization with Quality-Preserving Dynamic Tokens
summary: Image tokenizers, from 2D grids to recent 1D sequences, typically encode every image with the same fixed number of tokens. Yet visual complexity is highly heterogeneous, so a uniform budget overspends on simple inputs and underserves complex ones. Existing elastic tokenizers expose variable-length reconstructions, but often leave token length as a deployment-time operating point, a search target, or an external prediction rather than an output of the tokenizer itself. In this work, we ask whether a discrete visual tokenizer can budget itself in one pass. Our central finding is that actionable elasticity requires a representation--allocation co-design: prefixes must remain decodable across budgets, and the tokenizer must learn which prefix each image needs. We propose AdaTok, a self-budgeting discrete 1D tokenizer. AdaTok combines Prioritized Representation Learning, which orders tokens with nested tail masking and resolves budget-dependent semantic shift through Multi-Head LoRA decoder heads, with Adaptive Token Allocation, which trains a lightweight deterministic-group GRPO policy over candidate budgets. Dynamic Pareto Weighting balances fidelity and efficiency during policy training without manual trade-off sweeps. On ImageNet-1K, AdaTok-Full reaches rFID 1.31 at 256 tokens, while AdaTok-Adaptive attains rFID 1.50 using only ~118 tokens on average, outperforming discrete 1D baselines at comparable budgets. In autoregressive image generation, the shorter adaptive representation yields ~2.1x throughput over a fixed 256-token decode, suggesting that visual token count can be learned as a content-conditioned output rather than set as a fixed hyperparameter.

AI.tldr: AdaTok enables self-budgeting discrete 1D image tokenization via representation-allocation co-design, yielding adaptive budgets per image with strong accuracy and throughput gains compared to fixed-budget baselines.
AI.motivation: Visual content is heterogeneous in complexity; fixed token budgets waste capacity on simple inputs and underserve complex ones. Existing elastic tokenizers often treat token length as a fixed deployment-time parameter or external predictor. The paper seeks a tokenizer that outputs an adaptive, decodable token budget in one pass.
AI.method: AdaTok combines (1) Prioritized Representation Learning with nested tail masking and Multi-Head LoRA decoder heads to align semantics across budgets, and (2) Adaptive Token Allocation using a lightweight deterministic-group GRPO policy over candidate budgets. Dynamic Pareto Weighting balances fidelity and efficiency during policy training without manual trade-offs.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K, AdaTok-Full achieves rFID 1.31 at 256 tokens; AdaTok-Adaptive achieves rFID 1.50 with ~118 tokens on average, outperforming discrete 1D baselines at similar budgets. In autoregressive generation, the adaptive representation yields ~2.1x higher throughput than decoding a fixed 256-token budget.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a discrete tokenizer can learn content-conditioned budgets via a co-designed representation-allocation strategy, enabling efficient, decodable, variable-length representations in one pass, with strong empirical gains on image compression-like metrics and faster generation.

title: Decision-Aware Evaluation of Physics-Informed Surrogates
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/Dyniel/pinn-gym
summary:
Physics-informed machine learning is often assessed by curve error, although engineering use depends on downstream decisions: ranking candidates, avoiding infeasible designs and limiting regret. We introduce pinn-gym, an open benchmark for material-conditioned lattice design that couples a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle with five printable polymer cards, dimensionless force-response targets and a protocol spanning curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval and mass regret.
  Across per-material, pooled and cross-material settings, low nRMSE is frequently insufficient to identify useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter trade-offs rather than monotonically improving all metrics, and dimensionless conditioning improves comparability without making transfer symmetric. The benchmark is not a certified material model; within the released oracle, candidate generator and material cards, pinn-gym provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems rather than curve predictors alone.

AI.tldr: Pinn-gym is an open benchmark for evaluating physics-informed ML in material-conditioned lattice design, focusing on downstream decision metrics rather than sole curve accuracy.
AI.motivation: Curve-based evaluation often fails to capture engineering usefulness; there is a need for decision-oriented benchmarks to assess PIML surrogates in ranking, feasibility, and regret minimization.
AI.method: Introduce pinn-gym with a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle, five printable polymer cards, dimensionless force-response targets, and a protocol covering curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval, and mass regret; evaluate across per-material, pooled, and cross-material settings.
AI.result: Low nRMSE does not reliably identify useful designs; physics-informed losses shift trade-offs rather than universally improving metrics; dimensionless conditioning aids comparability but does not guarantee symmetric transfer; the benchmark is not a certified material model but a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems.
AI.conclusion: Pinn-gym offers a reproducible platform to assess PIML surrogates in decision tasks, enabling evaluation beyond curve predictors alone.

title: DualGate-Net: A Prior-Gated Dual-Encoder Framework for Histopathology Cell Detection
summary: Cell detection in histopathology images strongly depends on surrounding tissue context, where visually similar cells may belong to different classes under different microenvironments. Recent tissue-aware methods incorporate contextual priors, but often rely on static fusion strategies that may propagate noisy information. In this work, we propose DualGate-Net, a prior-aware dual-encoder framework that combines a ConvNeXtV2-based local encoder and a SegFormer-based global encoder through a learnable prior-gated fusion mechanism. The proposed module adaptively regulates the influence of tissue priors across spatial locations, while an auxiliary foreground reconstruction branch preserves high-frequency cellular structures during training. In addition, auxiliary cellness-guided cues are incorporated to further improve localization robustness. Experiments on the OCELOT benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements, achieving macro F1-scores of 0.7722 on the validation set and 0.7345 on the test set, highlighting the effectiveness of adaptive prior integration for robust histopathology cell detection.

AI.tldr: A dual-encoder architecture (ConvNeXtV2 local + SegFormer global) with a learnable prior-gated fusion and auxiliary branches improves tissue-context-aware histopathology cell detection, achieving stateful macro F1 on OCELOT.
AI.motivation: Cell detection in histopathology is highly context-dependent; static fusion of context can propagate noise. A dynamic prior integration aims to adapt to local tissue context and improve robustness.
AI.method: Two encoders (ConvNeXtV2 local, SegFormer global) feed a learnable prior-gated fusion module that modulates tissue priors by spatially adaptive gates. An auxiliary foreground reconstruction branch preserves high-frequency cellular details, plus cellness-guided cues to reinforce localization.
AI.result: On the OCELOT benchmark, macro F1-scores achieved 0.7722 on validation and 0.7345 on test, indicating consistent improvement via adaptive prior integration.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, learnable fusion of local and global tissue priors enhances robustness and accuracy of histopathology cell detection, outperforming static fusion baselines.

title: Geodesics of Dynamic Graphs for Regime Change Detection
summary: Traditional change point detection in dynamic networks assumes abrupt transitions between stationary states, overlooking scenarios of continuous evolution which arise in most real-world applications, such as social networks or physical systems. We address this gap by formally defining regimes as periods of coherent dynamics in temporal graphs, which we characterize as trajectories along geodesics in a suitably defined graph space. This original perspective allows us to define regime changes as significant drifts in dynamics, either toward new trajectories or with pace changes. We leverage graph regression methods to measure the cumulative distance of sequences of observed graphs from the estimated geodesics between their endpoints, in the relevant graph space, which we can combine with change point detection algorithms. We present experiments on dynamic networks, with changing trajectories and varying speeds, in which we outperform state of the art change point detection models. Then, we analyse mobility data during the Covid-19 pandemic, and show that our assumptions on regular network evolution lead to change points that are more aligned to external events compared to the outcomes of baseline methods. Our work is the first to model and detect changes between evolving regimes in graph space, providing a realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data.

AI.tldr: Regime-based change point detection in temporal graphs via geodesic trajectories in graph space, capturing continuous evolution and outperforming abrupt-change methods, validated on synthetic data and Covid-19 mobility.
AI.motivation: Traditional change point detection in dynamic networks assumes abrupt transitions between stationary states, ignoring continuous evolution common in real-world networks. The paper defines regimes as coherent dynamics along trajectories (geodesics) in a graph space, enabling detection of drifts toward new trajectories or speed changes.
AI.method: Regimes are modeled as trajectories along geodesics in a graph space. Endpoints of trajectories are estimated, and graph regression measures the cumulative distance of observed graphs to the geodesic between endpoints. This distance is combined with change point detection algorithms to identify regime changes.
AI.result: Experiments on dynamic networks with changing trajectories and speeds show superior performance to state-of-the-art CPD models. Analysis of Covid-19 mobility data yields change points more aligned with external events than baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces a novel framework for modeling and detecting changes between evolving regimes in graph space, offering a realistic and powerful tool for analyzing complex temporal graph data. It broadens CPD beyond abrupt transitions to gradual, trajectory-based regime changes.

title: RETROSPECT: RETROsynthesis via Sequential Prediction, and Chemically Transformed-ranking
comment: Accepted at the AI for Science workshop (ICML 2026)
summary: Single-step retrosynthesis needs both accurate first-ranked suggestions and candidate lists that are rich enough for downstream selection. We study this as a proposal-selection decomposition. Our system, RETROSPECT, combines a single Transformer proposal model, which we call the ChemAlign Transformer, with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, reaction-template, upstream-score, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. The generator is trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, exponential moving average weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss. On the full USPTO-50K test set of 5,007 reactions, the generator reaches 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy with 99.86% top-1 validity. On the merged candidate-pool benchmark used for reranking, which contains 5,007 test products and about 111 candidates per product, a LambdaMART model trained on the structural feature set reaches 59.4% top-1 with 0.7171 mean reciprocal rank. Feature ablations show that upstream proposal score and template-frequency statistics provide most of the reranking signal, while DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller and less consistent gains. These results support a modular view of retrosynthesis: stronger single-model proposal and learned candidate selection are complementary, and the proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems such as RetroChimera (Maziarz et al., 2024)

AI.tldr: A two-stage retrosynthesis system (RETROSPECT) combining a Transformer-based proposal generator (ChemAlign Transformer) with a LambdaMART reranker over structural, template, upstream-score, and DFT features. Demonstrates strong single-step performance on USPTO-50K and robust candidate-ranking on a large reranking pool; ablations highlight the central role of upstream scores and template statistics; supports modular, drop-in use in ensemble systems like RetroChimera.
AI.motivation: Address the need for both accurate top-1 proposals and rich, discriminative candidate lists for downstream selection in single-step retrosynthesis. Evaluate a proposed-selection decomposition and quantify the gains from a learned reranker on top of a strong proposal model.
AI.method: A two-component system: (1) a single Transformer proposal generator (ChemAlign Transformer) trained with hybrid root-aligned and random SMILES augmentation, Pre-LayerNorm, tied embeddings, EMA-averaged weights, and a differentiable atom-balance auxiliary loss; (2) a LambdaMART reranker using structural features, reaction-template information, upstream scores, and optional DFT-derived descriptors. Training and evaluation on USPTO-50K (5,007 reactions) and a merged candidate-pool benchmark (~5,007 test products with ~111 candidates each).
AI.result: Generator: 55.00% top-1 and 86.18% top-10 exact-match accuracy; 99.86% top-1 validity on USPTO-50K. Reranker: 59.4% top-1 and 0.7171 mean reciprocal rank on the candidate pool. Ablations: upstream proposal score and template-frequency dominate reranking signal; DFT and reaction-center DFT features provide smaller gains.
AI.conclusion: A modular retrosynthesis architecture where a strong proposal model and learned candidate selection are complementary. The proposal model can serve as a drop-in component for ensemble systems (e.g., RetroChimera), supporting flexible, scalable improvements in single-step retrosynthesis.

title: Reconstructing Multi-Decadal Forest Disturbances: A Spatio-Temporal Transformer Approach
summary: Accurate monitoring of forest disturbances is essential for understanding carbon dynamics and land management, yet traditional approaches typically rely on pixel-wise analysis of satellite time-series, ignoring spatial context. We present a deep learning framework that maps 38 years (1984-2022) of forest disturbance across the contiguous United States by modeling temporal trajectories and spatial neighborhoods simultaneously. By leveraging a vision transformer architecture, our approach effectively filters noise from weak supervision signals to produce spatially coherent disturbance maps. We perform exhaustive evaluations across multiple satellites (Landsat, Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2) and temporal windows (38 years and the more recent 6 years), validating performance against a novel, manually annotated validation dataset (n=300) and independent fire perimeter dataset (n=706). The results highlight the complexity of the task: while our spatio-temporal model demonstrates high precision (up to 98.2% for +-1 year detection on MTBS and up to 71.3% on the CONUS validation datasets, with F1-scores up to 75.8% and 47.3%, respectively) and effectively reduces spatial artifacts, it exhibits performance trade-offs across different disturbance regimes compared to pixel-wise baselines. Our method offers a promising foundation for consistent forest monitoring.

AI.tldr: A spatio-temporal Vision Transformer maps 38 years of CONUS forest disturbance by fusing multi-sensor time-series to produce spatially coherent maps, with high year-detection precision but some regime-dependent trade-offs compared to pixel-wise baselines.
AI.motivation: Overcoming loss of spatial context in pixel-wise time-series disturbance mapping and leveraging long temporal horizons with noisy weak supervision signals.
AI.method: A vision transformer that jointly models temporal trajectories and spatial neighborhoods using Landsat, Sentinel-1, and Sentinel-2 data over 38 years (1984–2022); filters noise from weak supervision to yield coherent disturbance maps.
AI.result: High precision (up to 98.2% for +-1 year on MTBS; 71.3% on CONUS validation); F1 up to 75.8% and 47.3%; reduced spatial artifacts; evaluated on manual-validation set (n=300) and fire perimeter set (n=706); demonstrates strong spatio-temporal coherence but with regime-dependent trade-offs vs pixel-wise baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a promising foundation for consistent forest monitoring, combining multi-sensor long-range data with spatio-temporal modeling to produce coherent disturbance maps, though more work is needed to balance performance across disturbance regimes and ensure broad generalization.

title: Structure-Preserving Correction Learning for Sparse Bayesian Inference in Brain Source Imaging
summary: Classical sparse Type-II Bayesian methods for M/EEG brain imaging support joint estimation of source and noise hyperparameters, but rely on fixed iterative update rules. Although these updates are principled and interpretable, their dynamics cannot be adapted from data. We propose to learn the update mechanism itself while preserving the underlying Bayesian structure by unfolding a classical joint hyperparameter-learning solver into a trainable neural architecture whose layers mirror the original iterations. The resulting framework is initialized to recover the classical solver exactly before training and is enriched through progressively more expressive correction-learning mechanisms, ranging from learnable biases to adaptive MLP and attention-based contextual refinements. In this way, training does not replace Bayesian inference with a black-box predictor, but instead learns structured correction terms while retaining the interpretability and model-based character of the original update dynamics. Structured correction learning therefore aims to improve empirical reconstruction performance without replacing the original model-based inference mechanism. Experimental results show that the learned correction variants improve reconstruction performance and convergence behavior over the baseline unfolded solver while preserving its algorithmic transparency.

AI.tldr: Unfolds a classical joint hyperparameter solver for M/EEG into a trainable, structure-preserving neural network that learns corrections to update rules, improving reconstruction and convergence while keeping interpretability.
AI.motivation: Classical Type-II Bayesian methods use fixed iterative updates that cannot adapt to data; there is a need for data-driven improvements that retain the Bayesian structure and interpretability.
AI.method: The solver is unfolded into a neural architecture whose layers correspond to the original iterations. It is initialized to reproduce the classical solver exactly, then extended with structured correction terms: learnable biases, adaptive MLP modules, and attention-based contextual refinements, all designed to preserve Bayesian inference dynamics while enabling data-driven improvements.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved reconstruction quality and faster/convergent behavior compared to the baseline unfolded solver, while maintaining the algorithmic transparency of the original model-based approach.
AI.conclusion: Structured correction learning can boost performance of principled Bayesian update schemes without abandoning interpretability, offering a principled path to data-driven refinement in model-based inference.

title: Geometric-Aware Hypergraph Reasoning for Novel Class Discovery in Point Cloud Segmentation
comment: Accepted to the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2026
summary: Novel class discovery in point cloud segmentation aims to transfer knowledge from known classes to automatically identify and segment unlabeled novel classes in point clouds. Existing methods mainly rely on pairwise associations for class assignment and novel class reasoning, which limits their ability to capture complex relationships among known and novel classes and may lead to inaccurate semantic segmentation. To address this issue, we introduce a hypergraph-based framework that models high-order associations among classes and enables collaborative reasoning from known classes to novel classes beyond traditional pairwise relations. Moreover, existing methods tend to focus on semantic feature extraction while paying insufficient attention to geometric information in point clouds. To better exploit spatial structure, we propose Geometric-Aware Prototypes to enhance the representation of class-level geometric cues. By propagating geometric information through hyperedges, the proposed method improves the understanding of spatial distributions across classes and leads to more accurate segmentation. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI and SemanticPOSS datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our method.
