AI.tldr: Hypergraph-based framework with Geometric-Aware Prototypes for novel class discovery in 3D point cloud segmentation, enabling high-order class reasoning and improved geometry-aware representations to segment known and unknown classes; validated on SemanticKITTI and SemanticPOSS with superior performance vs. pairwise-method baselines.
AI.motivation: Limitations of pairwise association in existing novel class discovery for point clouds: cannot capture complex inter-class relationships and may missegment novel classes; need to exploit geometric structure in 3D data to improve segmentation of unseen classes.
AI.method: Introduce a hypergraph that encodes high-order associations among class concepts and enables collaborative reasoning from known to novel classes. Develop Geometric-Aware Prototypes that incorporate class-level geometric cues and propagate geometric information through hyperedges to enhance spatial distributions across classes.
AI.result: Experimental results on SemanticKITTI and SemanticPOSS show the proposed method achieves superior segmentation performance over baselines, particularly in novel-class segmentation, demonstrating the benefit of high-order relational modeling and geometry-aware representations.
AI.conclusion: Hypergraph-based high-order reasoning combined with geometry-aware prototypes improves novel class discovery in point cloud segmentation, offering a robust framework that leverages both semantic and geometric information.

title: DEFINED: A Data-Efficient Computational Framework for Fine-Grained Creativity Assessment in Debate Scenarios
summary: Human creativity has emerged as a critical competency in the era of large language models. Assessing creativity in complex, open-ended environments is a grand challenge in data mining, currently hindered by a reliance on standardized simple tasks and the scarcity of fine-grained expert data. As an ecologically valid assessment context, debate reflects multiple dimensions of creativity, encompassing both divergent thinking and convergent thinking. Moreover, debate is a data-rich domain, with a large volume of publicly accessible materials. Current mainstream automated scoring methods are poorly suited to complex settings such as debate, and therefore still rely on costly human evaluation. To this end, this paper proposes DEFINED, a data-efficient computational framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate scenarios. DEFINED operationalizes debate creativity through a hierarchical eight-dimensional metric system, implemented via a pre-trained autoregressive language model with a hierarchical scoring head that supports both fine-grained and coarse-grained evaluation. Statements and their associated expert scores were obtained from authentic debate competitions, and a constrained data augmentation strategy was employed to address the elite bias inherent in the original data. DEFINED adopts a mixed-granularity training strategy enabling robust learning from limited fine-grained supervision annotated by trained graduate experts. To rigorously validate ecological validity beyond synthetic benchmarks, we incorporate an empirical study with debate-naive participants, utilizing these authentic data to serve as a qualitative case study for mid-to-low proficiency populations. Across our evaluation protocol, our scoring model achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming prompt-based large language model evaluators and existing debate scoring methods.

AI.tldr: Defines DEFINED, a data-efficient, hierarchical eight-dimensional framework for fine-grained creativity assessment in debate, using a pre-trained autoregressive LM with a hierarchical scoring head, data augmentation, and mixed-granularity training; validated on authentic debate data and with debate-naive participants, outperforming prompt-based LLM evaluators and prior methods.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of measuring creativity in open-ended, ecologically valid tasks where standardized benchmarks and limited expert data hinder robust evaluation; debate is a data-rich, multi-dimensional domain for creativity assessment.
AI.method: Eight-dimensional hierarchical scoring; autoregressive LM with hierarchical head; use authentic debate statements and expert scores; constrained data augmentation to mitigate elite bias; mixed-granularity training with limited fine-grained labels; empirical qualitative study with debate-naive participants.
AI.result: Model achieves accurate and stable scoring, outperforming prompt-based LLM evaluators and existing methods on the task; demonstrates data efficiency and ecological validity through real-data and novice-participant evaluation.
AI.conclusion: DEFINED provides a scalable, ecologically valid, data-efficient solution for fine-grained creativity assessment in complex open-ended domains, reducing reliance on costly human evaluation while maintaining accuracy.

title: ExMesh: EXplicit Mesh Reconstruction with Topology Adaptation
comment: Accepted at the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026 (CVPR 2026)
summary: Reconstructing surface meshes from multi-view images has remained a core challenge in recent years. Most existing methods, whether implicit or explicit, depend on intermediate representations and post-processing steps like Marching Cubes or TSDF fusion, often resulting in artifacts and fragmented geometry. Directly optimizing explicit meshes is a promising approach. However, it presents two critical challenges. The first is how to adaptively refine mesh topology to capture detail without introducing degenerate faces. The second is how to maintain consistent UV coordinates for high-fidelity texturing as the mesh structure evolves. To overcome these, we propose ExMesh, a novel framework that directly optimizes explicit meshes by integrating differentiable optimization with discrete topology updates. Specifically, we introduce an adaptive vertex splitting and merging strategy, along with real-time UV maintenance, to enable coarse-to-fine optimization while preserving geometric integrity. To our knowledge, ExMesh is the first framework to seamlessly integrate discrete topology operations into a continuous differentiable optimization pipeline. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ExMesh achieves a balance among accuracy, computational efficiency, and mesh conciseness.

AI.tldr: ExMesh directly optimizes explicit 3D meshes for multi-view reconstruction by adaptively refining topology (split/merge of vertices) and maintaining UVs in real time, integrating discrete topology updates into a differentiable optimization pipeline to improve accuracy, efficiency, and mesh conciseness over traditional implicit methods and post-processing.
AI.motivation: Conventional multi-view surface reconstruction relies on implicit representations or explicit meshes with post-processing (e.g., Marching Cubes, TSDF fusion), which introduce artifacts and fragmentation. There is a need to directly optimize explicit meshes with adaptive topology while preserving texture mapping during topology changes.
AI.method: Introduce ExMesh, a framework that optimizes explicit meshes via differentiable optimization augmented with discrete topology updates. Key components include adaptive vertex splitting and merging for coarse-to-fine refinement and real-time UV maintenance to preserve consistent texturing as topology evolves.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that ExMesh achieves a favorable balance among reconstruction accuracy, computational efficiency, and mesh conciseness, and is claimed to be the first framework to seamlessly integrate discrete topology operations into a continuous optimization pipeline.
AI.conclusion: ExMesh enables effective coarse-to-fine explicit-mesh optimization with maintained topology and UVs, reducing artifacts and achieving efficient, concise meshes compared to prior approaches.

title: Generative Molecular Morphing for Flexible-Size Design via Unbalanced Optimal Transport
summary: The success of generative molecular design hinges on a model's steerability toward high-reward samples. Because many molecular properties are intrinsically linked to molecular size, accurately capturing the joint distribution of properties and the number of atoms is essential. However, current diffusion and flow-based models fix the number of atoms, which ultimately limits their ability to navigate this complex relationship. To address this, we introduce Morph, a flexible-size generative model for conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design based on geometric graphs. By dynamically adapting size, Morph can seamlessly integrate existing structural priors, like scaffolds, and significantly enhances property steering. We show that Morph matches current fixed-size state-of-the-art models while offering the benefit of unparalleled sampling flexibility. We demonstrate out-of-distribution generation in regimes where previous models fail, paving the way for enhanced generative modeling for molecular design.

AI.tldr: Morph is a flexible-size 3D molecular generative model using geometric graphs that dynamically adjusts atom count, achieving competitive performance with fixed-size SOTA while enabling flexible sampling and OOD generation.
AI.motivation: Capture the joint distribution of molecular properties and molecule size; address the limitation of fixed-size diffusion/flow models; enable integration of structural priors (e.g., scaffolds) and improve steerability toward high-reward samples.
AI.method: A flexible-size generative framework for conditional and unconditional 3D molecular design based on geometric graphs that dynamically adapts molecular size and can incorporate structural priors, enabling improved property steering.
AI.result: Morph matches current fixed-size state-of-the-art models in performance while providing unprecedented sampling flexibility; enables out-of-distribution generation in regimes where fixed-size models fail.
AI.conclusion: Flexible-size, graph-based molecular design enhances steerability and space exploration, enabling integration of priors and OOD sampling, representing a meaningful advance in generative molecular design.

title: CULTURESCORE: Evaluating Cultural Faithfulness in Video Generation Models
summary: As video generation models like Veo 3.1 and LTX-2 advance, their ability to accurately represent diverse global cultures remains a critical yet understudied frontier. Current metrics, such as VideoScore, only measure visual quality but offer no mechanism for assessing cultural faithfulness. Consequently, a model that replaces a Namaste with a handshake receives the same score as one that generates the gesture correctly. We propose CultureScore, a compositional evaluation framework that decomposes cultural faithfulness into three granular dimensions: Identity (who is represented), Context (culturally localized background), and Behavior (normative gestures and interactions). We operationalize this framework through an evaluation suite spanning 10 countries, yielding 6,180 generated videos across three state-of-the-art models. Our evaluation reveals that no current model achieves culturally faithful video generation: the best-performing model reaches only 56.8\% overall CultureScore, with Behavior the most challenging dimension, which remains below 52\% across all models. Furthermore, human preference rankings align directionally with CultureScore but are inverted relative to VideoScore; the highest-scoring model on visual quality was ranked last by annotators, underscoring that cultural faithfulness is an essential criterion for equitable video generation.

AI.tldr: CultureScore is a compositional framework to measure cultural faithfulness in video generation (Identity, Context, Behavior). It shows current SOTA models underperform (best overall 56.8%), with Behavior hardest to get right; human prefs align with CultureScore but oppose VideoScore, highlighting culture as a crucial fairness criterion.
AI.motivation: Visual-quality metrics (e.g., VideoScore) ignore cultural fidelity. As video generation models expand globally, there is a need to assess and improve cultural faithfulness across diverse cultures to ensure equitable representations.
AI.method: Define CultureScore with three dimensions (Identity, Context, Behavior). Create an evaluation suite over 10 countries, producing 6,180 videos with three SOTA models. Compute dimension-wise and overall scores to assess cultural faithfulness.
AI.result: No model achieves high cultural faithfulness; best overall CultureScore is 56.8%. Behavior remains the most challenging dimension, consistently <52%. Human preferences correlate with CultureScore but are inversely related to VideoScore. Visual quality alone misaligns with user judgments on culture.
AI.conclusion: Cultural faithfulness is essential for equitable video generation; current models fail to achieve it, indicating a need for targeted improvements in identity representation, contextual localization, and behavioral norms.

title: A Held-Out Transition-Pair Falsifier for Long-Horizon Non-Abelian State Tracking
summary: State tracking exposes a sharp limitation of sequence models: the relevant signal is often not a summary of observed tokens, but an ordered latent state that evolves through non-commutative transformations. We introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier for finite non-Abelian group tracking. The protocol forbids selected ordered generator pairs during training and requires the same local patterns during evaluation, blocking one direct local-transition memorization pathway. In a controlled $S_3 \times S_3$ benchmark, a projected recurrent state model trained only on length-8 sequences produces error-free final-state predictions (perfect 250/250 per horizon) through evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens across five seeds. Matched native-readout baselines, including bag, GRU, and a single-configuration structured state-space model, remain near floor under the same protocol. Projection-matched GRU, structured SSM, and bag baselines equipped with analogous finite-group prototype readouts also remain near chance under the same split. Mechanism diagnostics show that hard projection coincides with low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation, while softened projection collapses final-state accuracy. Clean-split audits verify zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between training and evaluation partitions. The evidence is scoped to this controlled finite-group falsifier rather than to a general architecture ranking. Within that regime, explicit projected non-commutative state composition acts as a useful inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking.

AI.tldr: Explicitly projecting non-commutative state updates on finite non-Abelian groups yields a strong inductive bias for long-horizon state tracking, enabling perfect final-state prediction on a controlled S3×S3 benchmark under a held-out transition-pair falsifier, while native baselines fail under the same protocol.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation that sequence models often rely on summaries rather than evolving latent non-commutative states, and to evaluate whether enforcing group-structure-aligned projections improves long-horizon state tracking. The study isolates the effect with a controlled falsifier rather than architecture ranking.
AI.method: Introduce a held-out transition-pair falsifier that forbids selected ordered generator pairs during training and requires the same local patterns during evaluation. Evaluate on a finite non-Abelian group task (S3×S3) with a projected recurrent state model trained on length-8 sequences, testing horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens across five seeds. Compare with native-readout baselines (bag, GRU, single-configured structured SSM) and projection-matched baselines with analogous readouts.
AI.result: The projected model achieves perfect final-state predictions (250/250) across evaluation horizons up to 1,048,576 tokens, five seeds. Baselines remain near floor under the same protocol; projection-matched baselines also near chance. Mechanism diagnostics show low homomorphism error, low state-consistency drift, and non-trivial commutator separation when projection is hard; softened projection collapses accuracy. Clean-split audits show zero verbatim reduced-word overlap and zero structural-template overlap between training and evaluation. Findings are scoped to the controlled falsifier regime.
AI.conclusion: Explicit projected non-commutative state composition provides a useful inductive bias for long-horizon hidden-state tracking within the tested regime, demonstrating that withholding certain transition patterns can obstruct local memorization and enforce reliance on the non-Abelian state structure.

title: AnchorWorld: Embodied Egocentric World Simulation with View-based Evolution Customization
summary: Despite being a pivotal frontier, interactive world modeling remains underexplored in terms of the versatile controllability required by practical scenarios. To bridge this gap, we present AnchorWorld, a framework that advances egocentric simulation through enhanced interaction integrity and a flexible mechanism for world customization. First, we utilize 3D human motion as the primary interaction modality. To complement the out-of-view or truncated body parts in egocentric views, we introduce an auxiliary training supervision that incorporates exogenous viewpoints decoupled from the agent's first-person sensorium. It allows the model to observe the agent's full-body positioning relative to the environment, facilitating a more robust spatial grounding of human-world interactions. Furthermore, we propose a simple yet effective mechanism for customizing self-evolving worlds. This is achieved by defining anchor views within a unified world coordinate system, coupled with textual descriptions dictating the dynamic evolution of local scenes. Experimental results show that AnchorWorld significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, while ablation studies validate the effectiveness of our key designs. Notably, our customization scheme exhibits promising spatio-temporal geometric consistency and adheres strictly to the prescribed evolutionary dynamics.

AI.tldr: AnchorWorld introduces an egocentric simulation framework with exogenous viewpoint supervision and anchor-view driven world customization, enabling robust interaction grounding and consistent dynamic evolution, surpassing baselines.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in interactive world modeling, practical controllability remains limited in egocentric settings due to view truncation and weak spatial grounding. A need exists for training signals that capture full-body-environment relations and for a controllable, evolving world representation.
AI.method: 1) Use 3D human motion as the main interaction modality. 2) Incorporate auxiliary supervision from exogenous viewpoints decoupled from the agent's first-person sensorium to observe full-body positioning and improve spatial grounding. 3) Introduce anchor views within a unified world coordinate system to define a stable frame for customization. 4) Express local scene evolution via textual descriptions that drive dynamic changes in the environment. 5) Train and integrate these components into AnchorWorld to enable controllable egocentric simulation.
AI.result: AnchorWorld significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines; ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the exogenous viewpoint supervision and the anchor-view customization mechanism; the customization scheme shows promising spatio-temporal geometric consistency and adherence to the prescribed evolution dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The combination of exogenous viewpoint grounding and anchor-based world customization yields robust, controllable egocentric simulation with reliable spatial grounding and dynamic consistency, suggesting strong potential for practical interactive AI systems.

title: Where Rectified Flows Leak: Characterising Membership Signals Along the Interpolation Path
comment: ICML 2026 article, 9 main pages and 25 with annexes, 11 figures
summary: Understanding what generative models retain from training data remains challenging, with implications for copyright and privacy. Beyond verbatim reproduction, models can encode subtler traces of their training data that never surface in their outputs yet remain exploitable. We study this regime for Rectified Flows, which are increasingly used in deployed generative systems. We analyse the interpolation path $X_λ= (1-λ)X_0 + λX_1$ that defines the Rectified Flow training. We show that a gap exists between the reconstruction of train and test data that follows a bell-shaped curve over $λ$, wich accumulates during training, while the validation metrics remain stable. The signal has a maximum whose location we derive in closed form under Gaussian assumptions. We validate these predictions on both audio and images and show that the bell-shaped structure is universal, while the peak prediction holds when our assumptions are satisfied. As a proof of concept, we exploit this specific $λ$-resolved structure to perform a Membership Inference Attack, distinguishing members of the training set from non-members.

AI.tldr: Rectified Flows exhibit a bell-shaped reconstruction gap along the interpolation path, enabling a Gaussian-based peak location analysis and a membership inference attack.
AI.motivation: Understand how generative models retain training data beyond verbatim outputs, focusing on Rectified Flows to assess privacy/copyright risks.
AI.method: Analyze the interpolation path X_λ=(1−λ)X_0+λX_1 used during Rectified Flow training; show a bell-shaped reconstruction difference between train and test data accumulates during training; derive the peak location under Gaussian assumptions; validate on audio and images; demonstrate universality of the bell curve; perform a lambda-resolved membership inference attack.
AI.result: Demonstrates a train-test reconstruction gap that follows a bell-shaped curve over λ, with a maximum peak location derivable in closed form under Gaussian assumptions; validation across audio and image modalities; peak location prediction holds under the assumptions; establishes a proof-of-concept membership inference attack exploiting the λ-resolved structure.
AI.conclusion: There exists a universal, λ-resolved signal in Rectified Flows linking training data retention to a predictable, exploitable structure; this enables membership inference attacks and provides a framework for diagnosing training-data leakage in generative models.

title: Varifold Moment Invariants for Sustainable and Explainable Contour Feature Extraction
summary: We introduce Varifold Moments Invariants (VMI) as a unifying framework for many previously introduced Moment Invariants. These invariants are deeply related to other contour features that are invariant under translations and rotations, like Extended Gaussian Image, Elliptic Fourier Descriptors or Shape Distributions. The advantage of the varifold approach to moments consists in being able to combine the geometry of the region, its boundary, and the family of lines tangent to it, in order to create a substantial number of invariant features with high discriminating power and clear geometric meaning. By coupling our VMI feature extraction with the light feature classifiers Random Forest or Multi-Layer-Perceptron, we outperform state-of-the-art approaches based on contours, while decreasing drastically the computational cost to the point of allowing our algorithm to run on light devices. We tested our approach on classification tasks on a large number of widely-used datasets of various types (leaves, objects, cells) and achieved high accuracy with a low number of geometrically interpretable features.

AI.tldr: Varifold Moments Invariants (VMI) unify moment invariants via a varifold framework, relating to Extended Gaussian Image, Elliptic Fourier Descriptors, and Shape Distributions. They combine geometry of a region, its boundary, and tangent lines to produce many discriminative, interpretable invariants. Coupled with Random Forest or MLP classifiers, VMI achieves state-of-the-art performance on datasets with leaves, objects, and cells, while reducing computation and enabling deployment on light devices.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified, geometrically meaningful, and computationally efficient set of moment invariants by leveraging varifold representations that encode the region, boundary, and tangent lines, improving discrimination and enabling deployment on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: Define Varifold Moments Invariants by integrating region geometry, boundary information, and the family of tangent lines into the varifold representation to extract a large set of invariant features. Use VMI features with simple classifiers (Random Forest, Multilayer Perceptron) for classification. Evaluate on diverse datasets (leaves, objects, cells).
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art contour-based methods in accuracy while using a small, geometrically interpretable feature set. Achieves high accuracy with low computational cost, enabling real-time or light-device deployment.
AI.conclusion: VMI provides a unifying, efficient, and discriminative framework for moment invariants by leveraging varifolds to fuse region, boundary, and tangent information, yielding interpretable features that generalize across datasets and tasks.

title: Closed-Form Spectral Regularization for Multi-Task Model Merging
summary: Model merging combines several independently fine-tuned experts into a single multi-task model without any training data, reducing the storage, serving, and decentralized-development costs of large foundation models. State-of-the-art merging methods formulate merging as a layer-wise quadratic interference minimization problem. Although this problem admits an exact closed-form pseudoinverse solution, that solution underperforms hundreds of iterations of gradient descent in practice. The iterative loop dominates the cost of the pipeline, yet its effectiveness has remained unexplained. We revisit this regime and show that the iterative solver does not primarily act as an optimizer; rather, it serves as an implicit spectral regularizer for an ill-posed normal equation, where small-eigenvalue directions of the per-layer interference operator amplify proxy noise. Building on this finding, we formalize multi-task model merging as a noisy linear inverse problem and propose a spectral filtering estimator parameterized by a per-direction filter. We instantiate this estimator with SWUDI, a closed-form method that combines a soft exponential filter, which matches the gradient-flow trajectory of iterative descent, with a hard top-K truncation that suppresses noise-amplifying small-eigenvalue directions. Furthermore, we propose SWUDI-A, an adaptive variant that replaces the global rank hyperparameter with per-layer rank rules, further improving robustness across architectures. Both variants share a single symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer and require no training data or optimizer state. Across four general benchmarks and a multimodal merging benchmark spanning VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging, our proposed spectral solvers match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods. Crucially, they reduce wall-clock time by 28-72x and peak GPU memory by up to 50%.

AI.tldr: Spectral filtering solvers (SWUDI and SWUDI-A) for multi-task model merging treat per-layer interference as a noisy linear inverse problem; they use a spectral filter combining soft exponential decay with a hard top-K truncation to mimic gradient flow, achieving state-of-the-art results with large speedups and memory savings.
AI.motivation: Explain why iterative solvers outperform direct pseudoinverse in layer-wise model merging and leverage spectral regularization to enable fast, training-free, architecture-agnostic merging across tasks.
AI.method: Formalize multi-task model merging as a noisy linear inverse problem per layer. Introduce SWUDI, a closed-form spectral estimator using a soft exponential filter plus a hard top-K truncation, and SWUDI-A, an adaptive variant with per-layer rank rules. Reuse a single symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer; no training data or optimizer state. Evaluate on four general benchmarks plus a multimodal merging benchmark across VQA, Geometry, Chart, OCR, Grounding, and modality merging.
AI.result: SWUDI and SWUDI-A match or outperform state-of-the-art merging methods across four general benchmarks and a multimodal benchmark while delivering substantial efficiency gains: 28–72x wall-clock time reduction and up to 50% peak GPU memory savings. They also require only one symmetric eigendecomposition per linear layer.
AI.conclusion: Recasting model merging as a noisy linear inverse problem and applying spectral filtering yields robust, data-free merging that surpasses baselines in accuracy with large computational and memory benefits; per-layer adaptive rank further improves robustness across architectures.

title: VeriDrive: Verifiable Counterfactual Supervision for Cost-Efficient Vision-Language Planning
summary: Vision-language driving models increasingly use reasoning supervision to bridge perception, prediction, and planning, but existing driving rationales are often free-form and expensive to generate with frontier models. We present VeriDrive, a framework for constructing planning-oriented, verifiable counterfactual supervision. VeriDrive converts driving reasoning into a structured Perception-Evaluation-Revision chain that grounds key objects in future motion, evaluates alternative ego trajectories with rule-checkable evidence, revises risky intent toward expert behavior, and produces final planning targets. To scale data construction, VeriDrive combines local generation with validator-guided selective correction, escalating only invalid or difficult samples. We build the VeriDrive dataset on nuScenes and train under the Omni-Q protocol. Controlled open-loop experiments show that VeriDrive improves L2, Collision, and Intersection over OmniDrive while reducing logged token usage, generation time, and actual paid LLM/VLM cost. These results show that auditable intermediate fields and structured revision targets can improve vision-language planning supervision under realistic annotation budgets. Code, prompts, and validator scripts are coming soon and will be released after the review process.

AI.tldr: VeriDrive introduces a planning-oriented, verifiable supervision framework for vision-language driving models. It uses a Perception-Evaluation-Revision chain to ground objects in future motion, evaluate alternative ego trajectories with rule-checkable evidence, and revise toward expert behavior, producing final planning targets. Data construction combines local generation with validator-guided selective correction; evaluated on nuScenes under Omni-Q, showing faster, cheaper, and more accurate planning than OmniDrive.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between perception, prediction, and planning in driving models by providing auditable, structured supervision that can be scaled with efficient data construction, reducing reliance on free-form, expensive rationales.
AI.method: Convert driving reasoning into a structured Perception-Evaluation-Revision (PER) chain that grounds key objects in future motion, evaluates alternative ego trajectories against rule-based evidence, revises risky intent toward expert behavior, and outputs final planning targets. Data is built via a hybrid local generation and validator-guided selective correction pipeline, with VeriDrive dataset constructed on nuScenes and training under the Omni-Q protocol; validator scripts guide sample selection and correction.
AI.result: Controlled open-loop experiments show VeriDrive improves L2, Collision, and Intersection metrics over OmniDrive while reducing logged token usage, generation time, and actual paid LLM/VLM cost.
AI.conclusion: Auditable intermediate fields and structured revision targets can improve vision-language planning supervision under realistic annotation budgets; code, prompts, and validator scripts are forthcoming.

title: Trio: Learning Time-Series Forecasting with Temporal-Spatial-Sample Attention and Structural Causal Priors
summary: Multivariate time-series forecasting requires models to reason over temporal dynamics, cross-variable dependencies, and historical input-output correspondences. Recent Prior-Data Fitted Networks (PFNs) suggest that synthetic tasks can be useful for learning transferable inference behavior. However, directly transferring this paradigm to time-series forecasting remains difficult, since temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring historical patterns are not naturally captured by ordinary tabular priors. Motivated by this observation, we propose Trio, a sample-aware time-series forecasting architecture based on Temporal-Spatial-Sample attention. Temporal attention captures within-window dynamics, spatial attention models inter-variable dependencies, and sample attention retrieves relevant historical lookback-future pairs to guide the current prediction. Rather than claiming a fully general PFN-style forecaster, our goal is to study how historical input-output examples can be explicitly organized and reused within a forecasting model. We further introduce a Time-Series Structural Causal Model (TS-SCM) generator to create structured synthetic forecasting tasks with dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback, and distributional drift. Experiments on synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks show that the proposed architecture improves forecasting performance. Exploratory zero-shot experiments further suggest that TS-SCM-generated tasks may provide useful structural priors, while fully general PFN-style time-series forecasting remains an open problem.

AI.tldr: We propose Trio, a sample-aware forecasting model using Temporal-Spatial-Sample attention with a TS-SCM task generator; it improves multivariate forecasting and hints at useful structural priors, though fully general PFN-style forecasting remains unresolved.
AI.motivation: PFN-like transfer to time-series is difficult due to temporal order, dynamic lags, and recurring patterns; leveraging historical input-output examples could guide forecasting; need architecture that explicitly organizes and reuses these examples.
AI.method: Trio employs three attention streams: temporal attention for within-window dynamics; spatial attention for cross-variable dependencies; sample attention to retrieve relevant historical lookback–future pairs. A TS-SCM generator creates structured synthetic forecasting tasks with dynamic lags, cross-variable interactions, noise, feedback, and distribution drift.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic, industrial, and public benchmarks shows improved forecasting performance; zero-shot experiments suggest TS-SCM-generated tasks provide useful structural priors.
AI.conclusion: Trio advances forecasting accuracy and enables explicit reuse of historical I/O examples; but constructing a fully general PFN-style time-series forecaster remains an open problem, with TS-SCM providing a route for structural priors.

title: Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Adapter for Micro-gesture Online Recognition
comment: Technical Report. 1st Place in Micro-gesture Online Recognition in 4th MiGA at IJCAI 2026
summary: Micro-gesture online recognition aims to temporally localize and classify subtle gestures in untrimmed videos. Owing to their extremely short duration, low motion amplitude, and ambiguous visual cues, capturing discriminative spatiotemporal representations remains highly challenging. Existing parameter-efficient adapters typically employ a single branch to model spatial and temporal cues jointly, which may fail to capture the fine-grained patterns of micro-gestures. To address this limitation, we propose a Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Adapter that decomposes video adaptation into independent temporal and spatial branches via lightweight depthwise convolutions. In addition, to address the long-tail distribution problem in the benchmark dataset, we introduce Adaptive Soft Balanced Augmentation, which dynamically allocates augmentation intensity based on class rarity and learning difficulty, without manual thresholds. Our method achieves an F1 score of 0.43808, ranking 1st in Track 2 of the 4th EI-MiGA-IJCAI Challenge.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Adapter for micro-gesture recognition with independent temporal/spatial branches and Adaptive Soft Balanced Augmentation; achieves state-of-the-art performance (F1 0.43808) in EI-MiGA-IJCAI 4th Challenge.
AI.motivation: Micro-gestures have ultra-short durations and weak cues, making spatiotemporal modeling hard; existing adapters couple spatial and temporal information in a single branch, limiting discrimination.
AI.method: Introduce a Spatial-Temporal Decoupled Adapter using lightweight depthwise convolutions to decouple temporal and spatial adaptation; add Adaptive Soft Balanced Augmentation that dynamically adjusts augmentation intensity based on class rarity and learning difficulty without manual thresholds.
AI.result: Achieves F1 score of 0.43808 and ranks 1st in Track 2 of the 4th EI-MiGA-IJCAI Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled spatiotemporal adaptation plus adaptive augmentation improves micro-gesture recognition and provides competitive performance with less manual tuning; suggests decoupled design as effective for fine-grained, short-duration cues.

title: Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence: Explanatory Insufficiency as a Driver of Representation Learning and World Models
comment: 24 pages, 25 references. Theoretical framework relating representation learning, representational emergence, and world models
summary: Representation learning is central to modern machine learning, enabling transitions from handcrafted features to learned embeddings, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, and digital twins. Yet most research examines how representations are optimized after a representational framework has been selected, while less attention is given to when a new level of representation becomes necessary. We introduce the Bootstrap Theory of Representational Emergence (TBER), a framework describing how new representations arise when existing ones become explanatorily insufficient. In this view, representational innovation is not only driven by more data, larger models, or greater computational power, but also by persistent explanatory gaps: situations in which a representation can still describe observations but can no longer make their organization or transformations intelligible. TBER identifies explanatory insufficiency as a positive signal for representational transition. A representation becomes insufficient not because it is necessarily false, but because its explanatory domain has been exceeded. The bootstrap dynamic follows a recursive sequence: observations reveal anomalies; anomalies expose insufficiencies; insufficiencies motivate new representations; and these new representations generate further observations and possible new insufficiencies.We formalize this process through five stages: stabilized observation, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, and provisional stabilization. We discuss applications to representation learning, latent spaces, foundation models, world models, digital twins, adaptive biological systems, and scientific discovery. TBER suggests that future AI systems may benefit from mechanisms for detecting the explanatory limits of their own internal representations.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework (TBER) for representational emergence driven by explanatory insufficiency, outlining a five-stage bootstrap cycle and its relevance to AI, world models, and scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: To shift focus from optimizing representations within a fixed framework to understanding when new representations are necessary due to gaps in explainability.
AI.method: Theoretical construction of a bootstrap process: stabilized observations, anomaly detection, recognition of explanatory insufficiency, representational emergence, provisional stabilization; formalization and discussion of applications.
AI.result: A formal bootstrap dynamic that links anomalies to new representations and subsequent observations; identifies explanatory insufficiency as a positive signal for representation transition; practical implications for designing adaptive AI systems.
AI.conclusion: TBER provides a mechanism to detect the limits of internal representations, guiding future AI systems toward adaptive, self-aware representation evolution and broad applications in science and engineering.

title: TabSwift: An Efficient Tabular Foundation Model with Row-Wise Attention
summary: Tabular foundation models, exemplified by TabPFN, perform prediction via in-context learning, inferring test labels directly from labeled training examples. They have demonstrated competitive performance, particularly on small-to-medium datasets. However, recent tabular foundation models often improve accuracy with increasingly complex architectures, incurring higher inference cost and limiting practical deployment. In this work, we revisit the original TabPFN design and show that a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone can remain highly competitive with two simple enhancements: a gated attention stabilization mechanism and a small set of learnable register tokens that provide global context and improve pretraining quality. The resulting model, TabSwift, supports both classification and regression, and is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models (e.g., TabPFN v2 and TabICL) while being more efficient at inference. For latency-sensitive serving, we further introduce an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism that dynamically adjusts inference depth per sample. Overall, TabSwift enables efficient and anytime tabular in-context learning for practical deployments.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, row-wise attention-based tabular foundation model (TabSwift) achieves competitive performance with efficient inference by using gated attention stabilization, learnable register tokens, and adaptive layer-wise early-exits for anytime inference, preserving in-context learning on tabular data.
AI.motivation: Address the growing computational cost of modern tabular foundation models while preserving accuracy, by revisiting the original TabPFN design and enabling practical, latency-aware in-context learning.
AI.method: Use a lightweight row-wise attention-only backbone enhanced by (1) gated attention stabilization, (2) a small set of learnable register tokens providing global context, and (3) an adaptive layer-wise early-exit mechanism for per-sample inference depth; supports classification and regression; includes pretraining to improve quality.
AI.result: TabSwift is competitive with stronger tabular foundation models such as TabPFN v2 and TabICL and achieves higher inference efficiency, especially for latency-sensitive deployment.
AI.conclusion: TabSwift enables efficient, anytime tabular in-context learning suitable for practical deployments, balancing accuracy and inference cost.

title: Mitosis Detection in the Wild: Multi-Tumor and Context-Aware Generalization in the MIDOG 2025 Challenge
summary: Automated mitosis detection is a well-established task in computational pathology. While previous benchmarks focused on scanner-induced domain shift, clinical "real-world" application requires models to be robust across the vast variance to be expected in the histological landscape. The MItosis DOmain Generalization (MIDOG) 2025 challenge was designed to evaluate algorithmic performance across unprecedented biological and contextual diversity. We curated a test dataset of 365 cases, encompassing 12 distinct human, canine and feline tumor types, digitized across multiple scanning platforms. Moving beyond hand-selected hotspots, the challenge required detection also in random tissue areas (representative of the whole slide detection situation) and challenging areas (areas rich in hard negatives). In the second track, we introduced the classification of atypical mitotic figures (AMFs). There were 18 teams submitting to the detection track, with F1 scores ranging up to 0.740. In the AMF detection track, we had 21 submissions with balanced accuracy values up to 0.908. Our analysis reveals that while most models perform reliably in traditional hotspots, significant performance degradation occurs in challenging ROIs, where false positive rates tripled. Furthermore, performance varied significantly across the 12 tumor types, highlighting "blind spots" in current state-of-the-art architectures when encountering rare or highly pleomorphic malignancies. Moreover, we evaluated the effectiveness of ensembling and found a mean increases of 1.5 and 1.3 percentage points in F1 score and balanced accuracy, respectively. In contrast, TTA showed no relevant improvement. MIDOG 2025 demonstrates that "in the wild" mitosis detection remains a significant hurdle. The transition from hotspot-only evaluation to a multi-contextual framework provides a more realistic proxy for clinical reliability.

AI.tldr: MIDOG 2025 extends mitosis detection benchmarking to cross-context generalization with diverse species and scanners; ensembling yields small gains; TTA ineffective; hotspots robust but performance drops in challenging ROIs and across tumor types; highlights need for multi-context evaluation for clinical reliability.
AI.motivation: Assess robustness of mitosis detection models to real-world biological and contextual diversity beyond scanner-induced shifts; establish benchmarks that reflect whole-slide detection and atypical mitotic figure (AMF) classification in diverse tumor types.
AI.method: A 365-case test dataset spanning 12 tumor types across human, canine, and feline origins, scanned on multiple platforms. Two tracks: detection across hotspots and across random/challenging ROIs; an AMF classification track. 18 teams submitted for detection and 21 for AMF. Evaluated with F1 score and balanced accuracy; analyzed effects of ensembling and test-time augmentation (TTA).
AI.result: Detections achieved F1 up to 0.740 and balanced accuracy up to 0.908 in AMF; hotspots show reliable performance but challenging ROIs exhibit substantial degradation with false positives tripling. Performance varies markedly across tumor types, revealing blind spots for rare/pleomorphic cancers. Ensembling yielded mean gains of 1.5 pp in F1 and 1.3 pp in balanced accuracy; TTA provided no meaningful improvement.
AI.conclusion: In-the-wild mitosis detection remains challenging. Moving from hotspot-only evaluation to a multi-contextual framework yields a more clinically realistic reliability proxy, underscoring the need for further algorithms capable of generalizing across diverse histopathology contexts.

title: SleepExplain: Explainable Non-Rapid Eye Movement and Rapid Eye Movement Sleep Stage Classification from EEG Signal
comment: 6 pages, 7 figures, 2022 25th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
summary: Classification of sleep stages is one of the most important diagnostic approaches for a variety of sleep-related disorders. Electroencephalography (EEG) is regarded as a powerful tool for examining the association between neurological effects and sleep phases since it correctly identifies sleep-related neurological alterations. During Non-Rapid Eye Movement (NREM) and Rapid Eye Movement (REM) sleep phases, a number of nerve and bodily functions are affected and therefore hold an important role both in their functionalities. This work aims to classify NREM and REM sleep stages from sleep EEG data and present a noble SleepExplain model, an explainable NREM and REM sleep stage classification to explain its predictions. In this work, sleep stages were classified using Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting ensemble classification models. Overall, we obtained an accuracy of 92.54% (Random Forest), 94.25% (Gradient Boosting), and 94.30% (XGBoost). For explainable classification model, we utilized a game theoretic approach, SHAP (SHapley Addictive exPlanations) to offer a convincing explanation for the prediction.

AI.tldr: Proposes SleepExplain for explainable NREM/REM sleep-stage classification from EEG using ensemble models; achieves high accuracy (~92.5–94.3%) with SHAP-based explanations.
AI.motivation: Improve automated sleep stage classification and provide interpretable predictions for clinical trust, by combining EEG-based ensemble models with game-theoretic explanations (SHAP).
AI.method: Train Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost classifiers on EEG-derived features for binary NREM vs REM sleep classification; evaluate performance; introduce SleepExplain and apply SHAP to explain predictions.
AI.result: RF: 92.54% accuracy; Gradient Boosting: 94.25% accuracy; XGBoost: 94.30% accuracy. SHAP used to provide explanations for predictions.
AI.conclusion: SleepExplain achieves high accuracy with interpretable explanations; SHAP facilitates feature-level interpretation. Further detail on dataset, validation protocol, and clinical relevance recommended.

title: Mind the Gap: Disentangling Performance Bottlenecks in Video Instance Segmentation
summary: In Video Instance Segmentation (VIS), classification, segmentation, and tracking objectives are jointly evaluated, but their individual contributions to performance loss remain opaque. We introduce a diagnostic framework that formulates identity and class assignment as an Integer Linear Program (ILP), yielding a model-agnostic oracle that hierarchically isolates each error source. Applied to seven VIS methods spanning online and offline paradigms across YouTube-VIS 2019/2021 and a diagnostic subset of OVIS, our analysis reveals a consistent picture. Tracking instability is a critical bottleneck for online methods, with gaps exceeding 20 AP under heavy occlusion, and grows sharply with video length and instance density. While semantic classification contributes meaningfully on standard benchmarks, its impact becomes negligible where tracking fails most. Although stronger backbones substantially lift default scores, they leave AP tracking gaps largely intact, confirming that temporal fragility is algorithmic rather than purely representational. To complement the oracle, we introduce TrackLens, a visual tool that translates gap magnitude into observable, query-level failure modes. Together, these tools provide a systematic foundation for targeting VIS's core challenge: robust long-term temporal association.

AI.tldr: An ILP-based diagnostic framework acts as a model-agnostic oracle to isolate error sources in Video Instance Segmentation (VIS); tracking instability is the dominant bottleneck for online methods, with large AP gaps under occlusion and longer videos, while classification contributes mainly when tracking is reliable. Strong backbones raise overall scores but do not fix tracking gaps. TrackLens visualizes gap-related failure modes. Overall, robust long-term temporal association is the key VIS challenge.
AI.motivation: To disentangle how classification, segmentation, and tracking each contribute to VIS performance, and to identify the primary bottlenecks hindering robust long-term tracking across diverse VIS paradigms and datasets.
AI.method: Develop a diagnostic framework that formulates identity and class assignment as an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to create a model-agnostic oracle that hierarchically isolates error sources. Apply this oracle to seven VIS methods across online/offline paradigms and standard benchmarks (YouTube-VIS 2019/2021, a diagnostic subset of OVIS). Introduce TrackLens as a visual tool to map blame (gap magnitude) to observable failure modes.
AI.result: Tracking instability emerges as the critical bottleneck for online VIS, with AP gaps exceeding 20 under heavy occlusion and increasing with video length and instance density. Semantic classification adds measurable gains on standard benchmarks but becomes negligible where tracking falters most. Increasing backbone strength raises default scores but leaves AP tracking gaps largely intact, indicating temporal fragility is algorithmic rather than purely representational. TrackLens translates gap magnitudes into observable query-level failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Temporal fragility and long-term temporal association are the core challenges in VIS. The proposed ILP-based oracle and TrackLens provide a systematic framework to diagnose and target these challenges, guiding the development of more robust long-term tracking across VIS methods.

title: Breaking the Ice: Analyzing Cold Start Latency in vLLM
summary: As scalable inference services become popular, the cold start latency of an inference engine becomes important. Today, vLLM has evolved into the de facto inference engine of choice for many inference workloads. Although popular, due to its complexity and rapid evolution, there has not been a systematic study of its startup latency. With major architectural innovations such as the V1 API and the introduction of torch.compile, this paper presents the first detailed performance characterization of vLLM startup latency. We break down the startup process into six foundational steps and demonstrate that it is predominantly CPU bound. Each step exhibits consistent and interpretable scaling trends with respect to model-level and system-level parameters, enabling fine-grained attribution of latency sources. Building on these insights, we develop a lightweight analytical model that accurately predicts vLLM startup latency for a given hardware configuration, providing actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments. All benchmarking datasets, analysis tools, and prediction scripts are open sourced at https://github.com/upb-cn/vllm-startup-profiler.

AI.tldr: A systematic study of vLLM startup latency, breaking startup into six CPU-bound steps, with scaling trends, and a lightweight analytical model to predict latency for hardware configurations; includes open-source benchmarking and tooling.
AI.motivation: Industrial need to reduce cold-start latency in scalable inference services and the lack of a systematic study of vLLM startup amid architectural innovations (V1 API, torch.compile).
AI.method: Empirical breakdown of the startup process into six foundational steps, measurement of scaling with model- and system-level parameters, attribution of latency sources, and development of a lightweight analytical model; benchmarking datasets, tools, and scripts are open sourced.
AI.result: Found consistent scaling trends per step and clear attribution of latency sources; the model accurately predicts vLLM startup latency for a given hardware setup; provides actionable guidance for resource planning in large-scale inference environments.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a practical predictive model and empirical insights to optimize vLLM startup latency, enabling better resource provisioning and planning for scalable inference deployments.

title: RealDocBench: A Benchmark for Field-Level QA and Layout Understanding on Real-World Regulated Documents
summary: Document parsing systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, regulated workflows such as mortgage underwriting, financial reporting, supply-chain logistics, and clinical records. Yet most public benchmarks evaluate parsers on clean academic layouts or synthetic prose, and report a single OCR or markdown-level similarity score. Such documents and metrics correlate poorly with what downstream agents actually need: the correct value for a specific field on a messy real-world page. We introduce RealDocBench, a two-track benchmark built from real regulated documents. The QA track contains 1,356 field-level questions over 581 documents spanning four domains, where each question is paired with a typed gold_dict of key-to-value answers and parsers are scored on both per-field and strict per-question accuracy. The layout track contains 1,500 human-verified page images annotated with COCO-style bounding boxes under a nine-class public taxonomy, scored with a Hungarian matcher that includes adjacency-aware split/merge recovery. We evaluate eighteen systems, spanning commercial parsing APIs, general-purpose VLMs, and open-source OCR models, under a uniform extraction-and-scoring protocol, and report accuracy alongside per-page cost and cache-busted latency. RealDocBench exposes a wide performance spread that single-number benchmarks hide, a persistently hard medical sub-domain, and sharp cost/latency trade-offs across operating points. We release the datasets, parser adapters, and evaluation harness to support reproducible, field-level comparison of document parsing systems.

AI.tldr: RealDocBench is a two-track benchmark for real regulated documents withQA (field-level) and layout (bounding-box) tracks, enabling field-specific evaluation across multiple domains and measuring cost-latency; it reveals significant performance variation and a challenging medical subdomain, with datasets and tooling released for reproducible comparisons.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize clean layouts and generic OCR metrics, which poorly reflect downstream needs for field-value extraction on messy, real-world pages. There is a gap for field-accurate evaluation in regulated contexts.
AI.method: Two tracks: QA track with 1,356 field-level questions over 581 documents, each question paired with a typed gold_dict; per-field and per-question accuracy. Layout track with 1,500 page images annotated with COCO-style bounding boxes in a nine-class taxonomy; Hungarian matcher with adjacency-aware split/merge recovery. Evaluation across 18 systems including commercial APIs, general-purpose VLMs, and open OCR, using a unified extraction-and-scoring protocol; metrics include accuracy, per-page cost, and cache-busted latency.
AI.result: Demonstrates a wide performance spread across systems, persistent difficulty in medical subdomain, and notable cost-latency trade-offs. RealDocBench datasets and evaluation harness released to enable reproducible, field-level comparisons of document parsing systems.
AI.conclusion: A comprehensive, real-world benchmark that emphasizes field-level accuracy and end-to-end extraction, with practical evaluation metrics and tooling to support reproducible comparisons and to highlight trade-offs not captured by single-number benchmarks.

title: A robust PPG foundation model using multimodal physiological supervision
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG), a non-invasive measure of changes in blood volume, is widely used in both wearable devices and clinical settings. Recent PPG foundation models either use open-source ICU datasets with pretraining paradigms that require curated data and thus complicate generalization to field-like data, or use closed-source field-like PPG data. In contrast, we propose a PPG foundation model that does not require high-quality or field-like pretraining data, and instead leverages accompanying electrocardiogram and respiratory signals in ICU datasets to select contrastive samples during pretraining. Our approach allows the model to retain and learn from noisy PPG segments, improving robustness at inference. Our model, pretrained on 3x fewer subjects than existing state-of-the-art approaches, achieves performance improvements on 14 out of 15 diverse downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity and heart rate prediction. Our results demonstrate that multimodal supervision can integrate complementary physiological information to improve the robustness of PPG foundation models and enhance their generalization to consumer-grade data.

AI.tldr: A PPG foundation model trained with multimodal supervision from ECG and respiration signals to guide contrastive pretraining, enabling learning from noisy PPG data; pretrained on fewer subjects and achieving improvements on 14/15 downstream tasks, including field-like activities and HR prediction.
AI.motivation: Current PPG foundation models rely on either curated ICU data for pretraining or closed-field data, which hampers generalization to real-world consumer data.
AI.method: During pretraining, use accompanying ECG and respiratory signals to select contrastive samples, guiding learning from noisy PPG segments; achieves training with ~3x fewer subjects than prior state-of-the-art.
AI.result: Outperforms on 14 of 15 downstream tasks, including field-like daily activity and heart rate prediction; demonstrates improved robustness and generalization to consumer-grade data.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal supervision with ECG and respiration enhances the robustness and generalization of PPG foundation models without requiring high-quality or field-like pretraining data.

title: DisPOSE: Projected Polystochastic Diffusion for Self-Supervised Multi-View 3D Human Pose Estimation
summary: Recovering 3D human poses for multiple individuals from different camera views is a fundamental bottleneck for analyzing interacting behaviors. Existing self-supervised approaches leverage synthetic catalogues of 3D poses; however, this leads to poor generalization in real-world scenarios due to distribution shifts. We therefore introduce DisPOSE, a self-supervised framework that approximates the inherently discrete multi-view person-assignment problem as a generative diffusion process over the space of polystochastic tensors. By employing differentiable Sinkhorn projections during denoising, our model learns to guide solutions toward valid and feasible assignments based on 2D image priors. The complete 3D skeletons of localized individuals are then regressed using a Hypergraph-Convolutional Decoder that explicitly models relational structures and articulated joints across multiple views. The proposed approach outperforms current state-of-the-art self-supervised methods on standard datasets and demonstrates strong performance on a newly proposed benchmark featuring highly occluded scenes from surgical operating rooms. Our diffusion-based localization demonstrates high label efficiency, retaining 99% of its performance with only 10% of the pseudo-labels. Notably, disentangling the assignment and root regression components while maintaining differentiability makes DisPOSE nearly agnostic to different camera arrangements.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based, self-supervised multi-view 3D pose reconstruction (DisPOSE) using polystochastic tensors and Sinkhorn projections, with a Hypergraph-Convolutional Decoder; strong generalization and data efficiency, including occluded surgical scenes.
AI.motivation: Standard self-supervised approaches rely on synthetic pose catalogs that don't generalize to real-world view distributions; need a framework that handles discrete multi-view person assignment and relational pose structure without labeled data.
AI.method: Generative diffusion over polystochastic tensors to solve multi-view person assignment; differentiable Sinkhorn projections during denoising; regression of complete 3D skeletons via Hypergraph-Convolutional Decoder to capture relational joints across views.
AI.result: State-of-the-art among self-supervised methods on standard datasets; strong performance on a new highly occluded surgical operating-room dataset; high label efficiency (99% performance with 10% pseudo-labels).; component disentanglement (assignment vs root regression) yields camera-agnostic performance.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based localization with differentiable assignments offers data-efficient, generalizable multi-view 3D pose estimation with robust cross-view compatibility; reduces dependence on camera configuration.

title: Self-evolving LLM agents with in-distribution Optimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently emerged as powerful controllers for interactive agents in complex environments, yet training them to perform reliable long-horizon decision making remains a fundamental challenge. A key difficulty lies in credit assignment: agents often receive delayed rewards only at the end of episodes. In this paper, we propose Q-Evolve, a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that unifies automatic process-reward labeling and policy learning within a principled in-distribution reinforcement learning paradigm. In each evolving iteration, our method learns an in-distribution critic from a hybrid off-policy dataset that combines expert demonstrations with agent-generated trajectories, stabilizing Bellman backups in sparse-reward settings via a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective. The learned value function is then used to derive step-wise process rewards through advantage estimation, enabling dense and reliable supervision without environment backtracking or human annotation. Leveraging these signals, we perform behavior-proximal policy optimization that evolves the agent over the data used for process reward labeling, allowing iterative self-improvement without exacerbating distribution shift. We evaluate our method on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld, showing Q-Evolve outperforms strong baselines in sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance. Our results demonstrate that stable agent self-evolution is achievable through the co-evolution of process-level supervision and policy, both grounded within a shared in-distribution learning loop.

AI.tldr: Q-Evolve is a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that jointly learns an in-distribution critic and a policy in a unified RL loop to tackle long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks without environment backtracking or extra annotations. It uses a hybrid expert+agent dataset, a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective for stable Bellman backups, derives step-wise process rewards via advantage estimation, and optimizes the policy on the same data to enable iterative self-improvement. Evaluated on AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld, it shows improved sample efficiency, robustness, and task performance over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Credit assignment and learning stability for long-horizon decision making with LLM agents in sparse-reward environments. The goal is to enable reliable self-improvement without external supervision or env backtracking by grounding both supervision (process rewards) and policy in a shared in-distribution loop.
AI.method: Train an in-distribution critic using a hybrid off-policy dataset combining expert demonstrations and agent trajectories. Stabilize Bellman backups with a weighted Implicit Q-Learning objective. Derive step-wise process rewards from the learned value function via advantage estimation. Use these rewards to perform behavior-proximal policy optimization on the same data, enabling iterative self-improvement within the evolving data distribution.
AI.result: Q-Evolve achieves better sample efficiency, robustness, and overall task performance than strong baselines across AlfWorld, WebShop, and ScienceWorld.
AI.conclusion: Stable agent self-evolution is achievable by co-evolving process-level supervision and policy within a shared in-distribution learning loop, avoiding backtracking or extra supervision.

title: OpenGlass: Open-Source Smart Glasses for On-Device Event-Based Gesture Recognition
summary: Smart eyewear enables unobtrusive, context-aware interaction through multimodal sensors and on-device intelligence, but is severely limited by power, memory, and compute constraints in a compact form factor. Open-hardware platforms supporting event-based vision and embedded ML at this scale are rare. This work introduces an open-source smart glasses platform for rapid prototyping of novel sensors and algorithms. Its modular design uses a flexible FPC interposer to support both event-based and frame-based cameras without full PCB redesign. A hardware-software co-designed power management system combines a configurable PMIC with event-driven wake-up via an nRF5340 coordinator, keeping the GAP9 RISC-V SoC powered down between inferences. The prototype achieves up to 11.8 hours of continuous on-device ML from a 200 mAh battery. As a demonstration, an egocentric hand gesture recognition pipeline was evaluated on the LynX dataset using polarity-separated event histograms from a Prophesee GENX320 camera. R(2+1)D achieved the best cross-subject accuracy of 83.94\% (macro F1 = 0.781) under leave-two-subjects-out validation, with 33.9 ms end-to-end latency on the GAP9. Temporal augmentation and removal of ambiguous classes provided the largest gains (+8.9 pp). All hardware designs, firmware, and models are released open source.

AI.tldr: Open-source modular smart glasses platform enabling event-based and frame-based vision with on-device ML; demonstrates long battery life, low latency, and competitive egocentric gesture accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address power, memory, and compute constraints in a compact wearable form and provide an open hardware/software platform for rapid prototyping of sensors and algorithms.
AI.method: Modular design with a flexible FPC interposer supports both event-based and frame-based cameras without full PCB redesign. Hardware-software co-design combines a configurable PMIC with event-driven wake-up via an nRF5340 coordinator, keeping the GAP9 RISC-V SoC powered down between inferences. Evaluation used a 200 mAh battery to achieve up to 11.8 h of continuous on-device ML. Demonstration on LynX dataset for egocentric hand gestures using polarity-separated event histograms from a Prophesee GENX320 camera. Model: R(2+1)D; cross-subject validation with leave-two-subjects-out; end-to-end latency measured at 33.9 ms on GAP9.
AI.result: Prototype achieves up to 11.8 hours of continuous on-device ML from a 200 mAh battery. On the LynX dataset, R(2+1)D attains 83.94% cross-subject accuracy (macro F1 = 0.781) under leave-two-subjects-out validation; temporal augmentation and removing ambiguous classes yield the largest gains (+8.9 percentage points). End-to-end latency is 33.9 ms on GAP9. All hardware designs, firmware, and models are released open source.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility and utility of an open-source, modular smart-glasses platform for rapid prototyping of sensors and algorithms, achieving efficient on-device ML with low latency and substantial battery life, and providing a ready-to-use reference for community-driven hardware/software co-design.

title: Do Coding Agents Deceive Us? Detecting and Preventing Cheating via Capped Evaluation with Randomized Tests
summary: A growing failure mode in agent evaluation and training is that models can achieve high evaluation scores by exploiting shortcuts instead of solving the intended task, producing deceptive performance. This makes evaluation scores unreliable as measures of true task-solving ability. We propose CapCode, a framework for constructing coding datasets with randomized tests whose best achievable non-cheating performance is deliberately capped below one. This capped-performance design gives evaluation scores a clearer interpretation: scores substantially above the cap are implausible and therefore provide evidence of cheating. To prevent cheating, we propose CapReward, a reward design based on the CapCode principle to discourage optimization beyond the cap. Experiments across multiple datasets show that CapCode detects cheating while preserving performance ranking of models, and CapReward reduces cheating behavior, yielding models that better follow the intended task specification.

AI.tldr: CapCode introduces a coding-dake framework of randomized tests with a cap on non-cheating performance, plus CapReward to discourage beyond-cap optimization; experiments show improved cheating detection and reduced cheating while maintaining model ranking.
AI.motivation: Evaluation of AI models is vulnerable to shortcut exploitation, yielding deceptive scores that do not reflect true task-solving ability. A principled, interpretable evaluation with safeguards is needed.
AI.method: CapCode constructs coding datasets with randomized tests and a deliberately capped best non-cheating performance. CapReward adjusts training rewards to penalize strategies that push results beyond the cap. Experiments across multiple datasets assess cheating detection and impact on task-aligned performance.
AI.result: CapCode effectively detects cheating while preserving model ranking; CapReward reduces cheating behavior, producing models that better follow intended specs.
AI.conclusion: A principled framework (CapCode) paired with a reward design (CapReward) improves evaluation reliability and task alignment by capping non-cheating performance and discouraging cheating behavior.

title: Watch, Remember, Reason: Human-View Video Understanding with MLLMs
summary: Video understanding is being rapidly transformed by multimodal large language models (MLLMs), as research moves from short clips to long, multimodal, and knowledge-intensive video scenarios. These scenarios require models to handle sparse evidence, long-range dependencies, multimodal alignment, and reliable inference under limited computational budgets. This work presents a human-view perspective on LLM-based video understanding, organized around three functional abilities: watching, remembering, and reasoning. Rather than treating video tasks as isolated benchmarks, this view provides a unified structure for analyzing how video MLLMs acquire evidence, preserve context, and produce grounded outputs. We introduce a formulation that characterizes video understanding systems by their perceptual representations, memory states, reasoning traces, and final predictions. Based on this formulation, we identify challenges in spatio-temporal perception, efficient long-video processing, memory modeling, streaming understanding, and faithful reasoning. Representative methods are organized by their roles in video MLLM systems. Watching covers fine-grained, comprehensive, audio-visual, and efficient perception. Remembering includes offline and streaming memory, while reasoning covers text-only reasoning and thinking with videos. We further examine application domains such as egocentric, sports, instructional, medical, and narrative videos, and cover training datasets and evaluation benchmarks across task types, supervision formats, modalities, and capability dimensions. Finally, we outline open problems and future directions for scalable, memory-aware, and evidence-grounded video intelligence. Related works will be continuously traced at https://github.com/marinero4972/Awesome-HumanView-VideoUnderstanding.

AI.tldr: A human-centered framework for video understanding with watching, remembering, and reasoning, offering a unified perceptual–memory–reasoning formulation to analyze multimodal LLM-based video systems and to identify challenges and future directions.
AI.motivation: To move beyond isolated benchmarks and provide a unified lens to study how video MLLMs acquire evidence, maintain context, and produce grounded outputs across long, multimodal, and knowledge-intensive videos.
AI.method: Proposes a unified formalism for video understanding systems parameterized by perceptual representations, memory states, reasoning traces, and final predictions; organizes methods by roles (watching, remembering, reasoning) and surveys domains, datasets, and benchmarks; discusses challenges and potential solutions.
AI.result: A structured taxonomy and analytical framework that clarifies the roles of perception, memory, and reasoning in video MLLMs; identifies core challenges (spatio-temporal perception, long-video processing, memory modeling, streaming understanding, faithful reasoning); aggregates methods and datasets across domains; provides a reference survey (including a living GitHub resource).
AI.conclusion: Outlines open problems and future directions toward scalable, memory-aware, and evidence-grounded video intelligence, inviting ongoing development and community contributions.

title: Covariance Shrinkage via Stochastic Interpolation
summary: We recast classical shrinkage of high-dimensional covariance estimators as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between a source and a target distribution. This formalism recovers known shrinkage estimators as special cases and reveals three distinct mechanisms for reducing statistical risk: (i) Scheduling: the interpolant schedule determines the class of admissible covariances, and hence the achievable risk. (ii) Flow maps and couplings: whereas naive constructions amount to assuming independence between the distributions, specific coupling structures (e.g., solutions of optimal transport problems) can lower the empirical risk. Moreover, non-linear flow maps realizing such couplings free the interpolant covariance from the eigenbasis of the empirical estimate, enabling eigenvector regularization. (iii) Early stopping: estimators defined by integrating a regressed vector field afford an additional bias-variance trade-off through approximation of the true interpolant distribution. We then propose a neural estimator of the interpolant, together with an upper bound on its quadratic risk in terms of the interpolant approximation error, and validate both on synthetic experiments. Finally, we apply the estimator to real neuroimaging data, demonstrating the additional regularization power this approach offers in practice.

AI.tldr: Recast shrinkage of high-dimensional covariance estimators as empirical risk minimization over a parametric stochastic interpolant between a source and a target distribution, unifying known shrinkage methods and revealing three risk-reduction mechanisms (scheduling, flow maps/couplings, early stopping). Proposes a neural interpolant estimator with a bound on quadratic risk, validated on synthetic data and real neuroimaging data.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional covariance estimation suffers high variance; shrinkage is essential but lacks a unified interpretative framework. Casting shrinkage as ERM over a stochastic interpolant clarifies mechanisms to reduce risk and links to optimal transport and eigenvector regularization.
AI.method: Introduce a parametric stochastic interpolant between a source and a target distribution. Decompose risk reduction into three mechanisms: (i) scheduling of the interpolant, which constrains the admissible covariances; (ii) flow maps and couplings (including solutions of optimal transport) to capture nontrivial dependencies and enable eigenvector regularization via non-linear flows; (iii) early stopping by integrating a regressed vector field to trade bias-variance. Propose a neural estimator of the interpolant and derive an upper bound on quadratic risk in terms of the interpolation error. Validate on synthetic experiments and apply to real neuroimaging data.
AI.result: Shows that classical shrinkage estimators emerge as special cases within the interpolant framework. Identifies three distinct risk-reduction mechanisms and demonstrates that non-linear flow maps can break dependence on the empirical eigenbasis, enabling eigenvector regularization.
