Provides a risk bound linking estimator performance to interpolation error. Empirical validation confirms benefits on synthetic data and improved regularization on neuroimaging data.
AI.conclusion: A unified ERM-based framework for shrinkage in high-dimensional covariance estimation, clarifying three mechanisms to reduce risk and introducing a learnable interpolant estimator with theoretical guarantees and practical benefits in neuroimaging.

title: The Lipreading Gap: Do VSR Models Perceive Visual Speech Like Human Lipreaders?
summary: Visual speech recognition (VSR) models now surpass human lipreaders on benchmarks, but do such gains establish human-like visual speech perception? To explore this, we compare three VSR systems with human baselines on the MaFI word-level lipreading dataset using word, character, phoneme, and viseme-level metrics. Although models achieve higher overall accuracy, they succeed and fail on different words than humans. A text-only n-gram baseline given only a few initial phonemes rivals human lipreading. VSR word-level errors are consistently better explained by training word frequency than by the visual informativeness of words. Viseme accuracies, confusion matrices and human-model correlations further show that models gain most on visemes humans find hardest, and show much weaker dependence on visual clarity. Our work demonstrates that VSR systems rely primarily on language cues from training data rather than visual perception, failing to bind visual features into meaningful words.

AI.tldr: VSR models improve over humans in overall accuracy but rely on language priors rather than visual information, revealing a disparity between machine perception and human lipreading.
AI.motivation: Assess whether state-of-the-art VSR systems exhibit human-like visual speech perception beyond aggregate accuracy by conducting a fine-grained comparison with human baselines across multiple representational levels.
AI.method: Compare three VSR systems with human baselines on the MaFI word-level lipreading dataset using word, character, phoneme, and viseme-level metrics; include a text-only n-gram baseline that uses only initial phonemes; analyze error explanations via word frequency, viseme confusion matrices, and human–model correlations.
AI.result: Models achieve higher overall accuracy but succeed and fail on different words relative to humans. A simple text-only n-gram baseline using only initial phonemes rivals human lipreading. Word-level errors align more with word frequency than with visual informativeness. Viseme accuracies and confusion patterns show models gain most on visemes that humans find hardest and display weak dependence on visual clarity.
AI.conclusion: VSR systems largely rely on language cues from training data rather than robust visual-perception to bind visual features into words, indicating a gap from human-like visual speech understanding.

title: Making the Most of Limited Data: Score-Aware Training for Text-to-Music Generation
summary: State-of-the-art text-to-music generation systems rely on massive proprietary datasets and industrial-scale compute, making it impossible to disentangle architectural contributions from resource advantages. We propose \textit{score-aware training}, which treats audio-caption alignment score as a direct supervision signal throughout the pipeline. Rather than discarding low-scoring segments, we repurpose them via a CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule that routes them to high-noise training regimes, acting as an effective implicit regularizer. Complementarily, segment-level filtering removes the most misaligned examples, and a two-stage caption procedure bridges the distribution gap between verbose training captions and concise inference prompts. A REPA auxiliary loss further transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without additional data. Our 450M-parameter FluxAudio-based system, submitted to the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge Efficiency Track, ranked 2nd across both tracks in the objective evaluation and 3rd in the Efficiency Track in the final MOS evaluation.

AI.tldr: A resource-efficient, score-aware training framework for text-to-music generation using alignment-guided supervision, Beta-noise regularization, segment filtering, two-stage captions, and REPA loss; achieves competitive efficiency results with FluxAudio-450M on ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge.
AI.motivation: Disentangle architectural contributions from data/compute advantages and improve efficiency in text-to-music generation without relying on massive proprietary datasets.
AI.method: Score-aware training uses audio-caption alignment scores as supervision. Low-scoring segments are repurposed via a CLAP-conditioned Beta noise timestep schedule to emphasize high-noise training regimes. Segment-level filtering removes misaligned examples. A two-stage caption procedure bridges verbose training captions and concise inference prompts. A REPA auxiliary loss transfers structured semantic knowledge from pretrained CLAP and MuQ encoders without extra data. The system uses a 450M-parameter FluxAudio model.
AI.result: The model ranked 2nd across both objective tracks and 3rd in the Efficiency Track for final MOS evaluation at the ICME 2026 ATTM Grand Challenge.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that efficiency-focused training strategies—score-aware supervision, Beta-noise regularization, data filtering, caption bridging, and cross-encoder knowledge transfer—can achieve strong performance without relying on large proprietary datasets or extreme compute.

title: Skill-3D: Evolving Scene-Aware Skills for Agentic 3D Spatial Reasoning
summary: This paper explores agentic 3D spatial understanding, i.e., MLLM agents performing 3D reasoning through tool use. Existing methods often misuse tools and exhibit biased tool preferences under 3D scenarios, leaving the agentic paradigm with only marginal gains over non-agentic strategies. We reveal that 3D spatial reasoning tasks are heterogeneous across scenes, while these agents apply a uniform tool-use strategy to all scenes rather than selecting tools according to the specific scene and task. To address this, we propose Skill-3D, a framework that learns self-evolving scene-aware skills. Specifically, Skill-3D identifies the task scene and records the agent's tool-use trajectory into a Scene Memory, where successful trajectories from similar scenes are aggregated and distilled into a reusable scene-aware skill, with failed ones attached to the skill as lessons. During training, once a similar scene recurs, the corresponding skill is injected to guide the agent, producing new trajectories whose successes and failures further refine the skill, forming a loop in which the memory and the skill library co-evolve. Experiments show that Skill-3D substantially improves tool utilization in 3D spatial reasoning (from 39% to 78% on VSI-Bench), driving the agent toward correct and sufficient tool use. For instance, it improves Gemini-3-Flash by 67% on MMSI-Bench. Furthermore, we conduct agentic post-training over skill-guided trajectories, which boosts Qwen3-VL-8B by 43% on VSI-Bench.

AI.tldr: Skill-3D introduces self-evolving Scene Memory and scene-aware skills to tailor tool-use in 3D spatial reasoning, significantly improving tool utilization and performance on VSI-Bench, MMSI-Bench, and Qwen3-VL-8B.
AI.motivation: 3D spatial reasoning with MLLMs suffers from heterogeneous tasks across scenes; fixed tool-use strategies lead to biased tool preferences and limited gains.
AI.method: Skill-3D identifies the task scene, records the agent's tool-use trajectory into a Scene Memory, aggregates successful trajectories from similar scenes, and distills them into reusable scene-aware skills. Failed trajectories are attached as lessons. During training, when a similar scene recurs, the corresponding skill is injected to guide the agent, creating a loop where memory and skill co-evolve.
AI.result: Tool utilization on VSI-Bench improves from 39% to 78%. Gemini-3-Flash improves by 67% on MMSI-Bench. Agentic post-training over skill-guided trajectories boosts Qwen3-VL-8B by 43% on VSI-Bench.
AI.conclusion: The memory–skill co-evolution framework yields substantial improvements in 3D spatial reasoning through scene-aware tool use, validating the approach and enabling more effective agentic behavior.

title: Generative Modeling of Discrete Latent Structures via Dynamic Policy Gradients
summary: Many scientific problems require inferring unobserved mechanistic latent states from indirect observations. While classical approaches, including expectation maximization, do not scale to combinatorially large spaces, deep learning approaches such as variational autoencoders typically form artificial latent states rather than reconstructing the mechanistic ground-truth states. Here, we introduce GReinSS, a policy learning framework that uses dynamically rescaled rewards to learn latent state distributions that maximize the observed data likelihood. We show that GReinSS accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative policy learning and generative modeling baselines. Additionally, GReinSS reconstructs isoforms from real short-read RNA sequencing data that better match isoforms detected by orthogonal long-read sequencing than the standard RSEM algorithm. Overall, GReinSS is a principled and practically effective approach for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations.

AI.tldr: GReinSS is a policy-learning framework that dynamically rescales rewards to infer mechanistic latent state distributions from indirect observations, achieving accurate reconstruction of latent structures and improved RNA isoform inference over baselines.
AI.motivation: Inferring unobserved mechanistic latent states from indirect observations is challenging and existing methods either do not scale to combinatorially large latent spaces or produce artificial latent representations. A principled method that recovers ground-truth mechanistic latents is needed.
AI.method: GReinSS formulates latent-state inference as policy learning with dynamically rescaled rewards to maximize the data likelihood. It reconstructs latent sets and graphs from simulated data and applies to real short-read RNA-seq to infer isoforms, comparing against standard baselines such as other policy learning methods, generative models, and RSEM.
AI.result: The method accurately reconstructs simulated latent sets and latent graphs, outperforming alternative baselines. On real data, it reconstructs isoforms from short-read RNA sequencing that align better with isoforms detected by long-read sequencing than the RSEM algorithm.
AI.conclusion: GReinSS provides a principled, effective approach for generative modeling and inference of combinatorial latent states from indirect observations, with demonstrated applicability to complex biological latent structures like transcript isoforms.

title: TEVI: Text-Conditioned Editing of Visual Representations via Sparse Autoencoders for Improved Vision-Language Alignment
summary: Vision-language models such as CLIP are highly useful for diverse tasks due to their shared image-text embedding space. Despite this, the image and text embeddings are often poorly aligned, affecting downstream performance. Recent work has shown that this can be attributed to an information imbalance: images contain more information than their captions describe. In this work, we propose TEVI, a framework that uses captions as a signal for what to retain from image embeddings. Specifically, we use sparse autoencoders to disentangle image embeddings and train a masking module to selectively reconstruct the embedding based on a given caption. In a controlled setup with synthetic captions, we show that TEVI is effective at preserving caption-described attributes while discarding others. By applying TEVI to CLIP models trained on natural images, we further achieve improved retrieval performance across coarse-grained short-caption (MS COCO, Flickr) and fine-grained long-caption (IIW, DOCCI) benchmarks, with stronger gains on richer captions, and improved robustness on the RoCOCO benchmark.

AI.tldr: Caption-guided embedding refinement (TEVI) improves image–text alignment by selectively reconstructing image embeddings with a caption-conditioned mask learned via sparse autoencoders, boosting CLIP retrieval across varied caption granularities.
AI.motivation: Vision–language models exhibit information imbalance: images contain more information than captions describe, leading to misalignment and degraded downstream performance. TEVI targets preserving caption-described attributes while discarding non-described content.
AI.method: TEVI uses sparse autoencoders to disentangle image embeddings and trains a caption-conditioned masking module to selectively reconstruct embeddings. In a controlled setup with synthetic captions, TEVI preserves caption-described attributes. Applied to CLIP models trained on natural images, it improves retrieval on both coarse-grained short captions (MS COCO, Flickr) and fine-grained long captions (IIW, DOCCI), with larger gains for richer captions, and improves robustness on RoCOCO.
AI.result: TEVI yields improved retrieval performance across both coarse- and fine-grained caption benchmarks, with stronger gains as captions become richer, and enhanced robustness on RoCOCO; it effectively preserves caption-described attributes while discarding others.
AI.conclusion: Caption-guided selective reconstruction can mitigate information imbalance in vision–language models, improving alignment and robustness across datasets, and is compatible with existing CLIP frameworks.

title: Reversible Foundations: Training a 120B Sparse MoE through State-Preserving Scaling
comment: 58 pages, 9 figures, 37 tables. Code: https://github.com/The-School-of-AI/LLM. Released models: huggingface.co/theschoolofai/LightningLM-0.1V-{2B, 5B-MoE, 9B-MoE, 120B-MoE}. Companion work: arXiv:2605.29379 (BrahmicTokenizer-131K), arXiv:2605.29459 (Kronecker Embeddings)
summary:
This paper reports on training a hundred-billion-parameter sparse mixture of experts on a single eight-GPU node, end to end. LightningLM 0.1V is a recurrence-backbone language model family grown in four stages from a small dense seed, through a 5B and a 9B mixture of experts, to a 120B model with 460 routed experts under top-12 routing. Each larger model is grown from the trained weights of the smaller one; active parameters rise monotonically from 1.78B at the dense seed to 5.93B at 120B (about 5% of the 118.67B stored). The full lineage runs on single nodes, the larger stages at 8K context, reaching a released training loss of 1.78 at 120B scale.
  This is a systems and experience report. It is organized around three disciplines. Reversibility: a reversible recurrence stack reconstructs activations in the backward pass instead of storing them, holding activation memory flat as the model grows. State-preserving growth: each expansion (dense to MoE, shallow to deep, few experts to many) is given as a reproducible principle paired with the failure that results from getting it wrong; several failures are silent. Single-node economics: the 120B trains through TQP, a strategy of quantized base expert weights and trained low-rank adapters that carries optimizer state on 2.26B adapter parameters rather than 100B+ resident in routed experts, cutting expert-path optimizer state by a factor of ~45.
  What is new is the integration of known primitives, not any primitive in isolation: one grown lineage running end to end on a single node, documented at practitioner level, with per-domain held-out loss as evidence that targeted capabilities (multilingual Indic competence, code) were learned by construction. Model family, tokenizer, and training code are released.

AI.tldr: A 120B sparse MoE LLM trained end-to-end on a single 8-GPU node, using reversible recurrence, state-preserving growth, and efficient optimization, achieving 1.78 loss and releasing model, tokenizer, and code.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate end-to-end growth and practical training of a very large sparse model on modest hardware, while controlling memory, optimizer state, and reproducibility.
AI.method: Grow a Lighti ngLM lineage from a dense seed through 5B and 9B MoE to a 120B model with 460 routed experts using top-12 routing. Key techniques include a reversible recurrence stack to reconstruct activations, state-preserving growth across expansions (dense→MoE, shallow→deep, few→many), and single-node economics via TQP: quantized base expert weights and trained low-rank adapters that carry optimizer state on 2.26B adapter parameters instead of 100B+ in routed experts.
AI.result: The 120B model trains with 8K context, reaches a released training loss of 1.78. Active parameters grow from 1.78B (dense seed) to 5.93B (120B), about 5% of the 118.67B stored. The system maintains a single-node lineage, releases model, tokenizer, and training code, and demonstrates learned capabilities such as multilingual Indic competence and code.
AI.conclusion: The work emphasizes assembling known primitives into an end-to-end, practitioner-level workflow on a single node rather than introducing new primitives; it provides a reproducible lineage with per-domain held-out loss evidence and highlights practical efficiency gains via reversible memory and adapter-based optimization.

title: Implicit Data Synthesis for Contrastive Unsupervised Data Augmentation
summary: Scientific observations generate large quantities of unlabeled data which is laborious to hand-label, making unsupervised learning techniques valuable for processing datasets. Among these approaches, contrastive learning provides a convenient mechanism for extracting structural representations from unannotated datasets. For natural imagery, the general approach is to use a variety of data-space augmentation methods in order to generate synthetic samples; however, for scientific observations data-space perturbations can fundamentally alter the underlying data. Our proposed method is to generate contrastive samples by perturbing the network weights rather than the underlying data, thus more closely preserving the structure of the data. We demonstrate this technique using a SimCLR-based pipeline applied over radar observations of meteors, and show performance gains under matched protocols.

AI.tldr: Proposes a weight-perturbation based contrastive learning method to preserve data structure in scientific observations, applied within a SimCLR framework to radar meteor data, yielding performance gains under matched protocols.
AI.motivation: Unlabeled data in scientific observations makes labeling labor-intensive. In such data, standard data-space perturbations can alter the underlying data structure; thus there is a need for augmentation methods that preserve domain structure.
AI.method: Introduce contrastive learning by perturbing neural network weights rather than data samples within a SimCLR pipeline; evaluate on radar observations of meteors with matched evaluation protocols.
AI.result: The approach yields performance gains over baseline methods under the same evaluation setup.
AI.conclusion: Weight perturbation as a data-structure-preserving augmentation is a viable alternative to data-space perturbations for unsupervised learning in scientific datasets and can improve representation quality.

title: A Comprehensive Anatomy of Human and DeepSeek-R1 LLM Mathematical Reasoning
summary: The emergence of "Aha moments" in large language models, particularly DeepSeek-R1-0120, has raised the question of whether these systems genuinely reason or merely imitate the appearance of reasoning. We conduct a comprehensive empirical comparison between model and human reasoning across all 30 problems from AIME 2025, exhaustively annotating 10,247 reasoning steps into five functional categories: Analysis, Inference, Branch, Backtrace, and Reflection. We find a clear structural difference. Human solutions maintain a compact alternation between analysis and deduction, whereas DeepSeek-R1 frequently revisits intermediate results, performs shallow and often unnecessary verification, and loops through local checks without meaningful logical progress. We describe this as topological mimicry: reproducing the surface form of reasoning without its functional role. Despite this, we identify two signals of genuine reasoning. First, successful traces exhibit stable use of branching and backtracking, while failed traces either underuse or overuse exploratory actions. Second, reflection is only effective when placed within deductive inference; reflections trapped in analysis loops focus on local numerical details while missing global logical errors. These findings suggest that current long-CoT models may be rewarded more for the appearance of reasoning than for genuine deductive progress. We discuss directions for improving evaluation and training, including measuring cross-trace stability, penalising "spinning-wheel" traces, encouraging deeper logical correction, and reallocating inference-time compute toward deduction and backtracking. Overall, reasoning quality depends not simply on how much reflection occurs, but on whether reflection appears consistently and at the appropriate logical scale.

AI.tldr: Large language models exhibit topological mimicry of reasoning (surface structure without functional reasoning); two genuine signals identified: stable branching/backtracking and context-appropriate reflection within deduction; implications for evaluation and training of long-CoT models.
AI.motivation: Assess whether current LLM reasoning is genuine or superficial, using a rigorous, trace-level comparison against human solutions on a standard math benchmark.
AI.method: Annotate 10,247 reasoning steps from 30 AIME 2025 problems into five functional categories (Analysis, Inference, Branch, Backtrace, Reflection); compare model vs human traces; analyze recurrence of intermediate results, verification, loops; examine effects of reflection and deductive inference.
AI.result: Humans maintain compact analysis-deduction alternation; DeepSeek-R1 loops, revisits intermediate results, shallow checks; topological mimicry. Two signals of genuine reasoning identified: (1) traces with stable branching/backtracking correlate with success; (2) reflection effective when within deductive inference; otherwise misleads. Conclusion: current long-CoT models prioritize appearance of reasoning; propose evaluation/training changes: measuring cross-trace stability, penalizing spinning-wheel traces, encouraging deeper logical correction, reallocating compute toward deduction and backtracking.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning quality depends on consistency and appropriate logical scale of reflection; future work to improve evaluation and training.

title: Differences in Detection: Explainability Where it Matters
comment: Accepted to IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Workshops 2026 - How Do Vision Models Work? (HOW)
summary: We propose Differences in Detection (DnD), an intuitive method to compare two object detection models. Based on the same matching algorithm, it complements the standard metrics of mean Average Precision ($mAP$) and TIDE error analysis with the ability to compare two models directly. More specifically, we calculate the intersection of ground truth labels that are recognized by both models, followed by the corresponding difference sets and the complement set of ground truth labels that are missed by both models. The resulting comparison is more direct and intuitive than a comparison of independent summary statistics. It reveals individual and shared mistakes and becomes particularly interesting when combined with error types. In this case, the differences in detection errors can be analyzed naturally in a standard confusion matrix. While valuable in itself, we believe that one of the best applications of DnD is to guide explainability methods such as ODAM towards metric-relevant examples, grounded in structured subsets. The code for our method is available here: https://github.com/JohannesTheo/differences-in-detection

AI.tldr: DnD is a method to directly compare two object detectors by analyzing shared and differing ground-truth detections, complementing mAP and TIDE.
AI.motivation: To enable direct, intuitive comparison between two detectors, revealing individual and shared mistakes and guiding explainability.
AI.method: Using the same matching algorithm as standard metrics, compute the intersection of GTs detected by both models, derive the corresponding difference sets and the complement of GTs missed by both; analyze errors within a confusion-matrix framework and relate to error types; enables metric-relevant explanations (ODAM).
AI.result: Claims that DnD yields a more direct, intuitive comparison, highlights individual/shared mistakes, and supports explainability workflows; provides code link for implementation.
AI.conclusion: DnD offers a practical, explainability-friendly tool for model comparison in object detection, augmenting existing metrics and error analysis.

title: Sparsely gated tiny linear experts
summary: Sparsity allows scaling model parameters without proportionally increasing computational cost. While mixture of experts (MoE) models are made increasingly sparse, individual experts typically remain large and dense. Here, we demonstrate that further increasing sparsity by shrinking each expert to consist of a single neuron and selecting a tiny fraction of many available neurons can improve compute efficiency and interpretability. Counterintuitively, the key to achieving both is removing the nonlinearity typically applied to the experts, resulting in a network of sparsely gated linear neurons (sgatlin). In an isoflop comparison, we find that replacing all transformer feedforward layers with sgatlin improves perplexity in language models across different compute budgets. At the same time, the sparsity and linearity of the resulting feedforward circuits present new opportunities for model interpretability. In a small-scale case study, we demonstrate that feedforward circuits in sgatlin can be interpreted without having to train additional replacement models. We find that they form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall. Our findings paint a possible path towards compute-efficient and interpretable transformer feedforward layers.

AI.tldr: A sparsely gated linear neuron (sgatlin) replaces dense transformer feedforward layers by shrinking each expert to a single neuron and removing nonlinearity, achieving compute efficiency and interpretability while improving perplexity across budgets.
AI.motivation: To make transformer FFNs more compute-efficient and interpretable by extreme sparsity and linearity, addressing both efficiency and interpretability gaps in MoE-based models.
AI.method: Replace transformer feedforward layers with sparsely gated linear neurons (sgatlin): shrink each expert to a single neuron, select a tiny fraction of neurons, remove nonlinearity; implement across transformer layers; benchmark in iso-flop style across compute budgets; include a small-scale case study for interpretability.
AI.result: sgatlin yields perplexity improvements over standard FFNs across compute budgets; its sparsity and linearity enable interpretable FFN circuits, which form semantically structured clusters and are causally implicated in factual recall.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a path to compute-efficient and interpretable transformer feedforward layers via sgatlin, offering gains in language modeling performance and new avenues for interpretability.

title: Streaming Video Generation with Streaming Force Control
summary: We introduce StreamForce, a streaming video generation framework that enables physically grounded control through continuous force inputs. Unlike prior video models that train separate models for different force types, assume fixed forces, or rely on non-causal processing, StreamForce is a causal and unified model that responds instantly and coherently to both local and global, time-varying forces. To achieve this, we design a unified force representation as a control signal and develop a distillation pipeline for force-controllable video generation. Our model combines autoregressive efficiency with force responsiveness, sustaining stable photometric and dynamic realism. StreamForce runs at up to 16.6 FPS on a single GPU, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both force adherence and motion realism. Project website: https://neu-vi.github.io/StreamForce/

AI.tldr: A causal, unified streaming video generator that uses a continuous force control signal to drive realistic motion and photometry, running at ~16.6 FPS on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Enable physically grounded, responsive video generation without needing multiple force-specific models, fixed-force assumptions, or non-causal processing.
AI.method: Introduce a unified force representation as a control signal; build a distillation pipeline for force-controllable video generation; combine autoregressive efficiency with real-time force responsiveness; ensure stability in photometric and dynamic realism; operate as a single causal model.
AI.result: Achieves up to 16.6 FPS on a single GPU; claims state-of-the-art performance in force adherence and motion realism.
AI.conclusion: StreamForce provides real-time, coherent force-controlled video generation with strong force adherence and realistic motion via a unified causal framework.

title: Video-Based Prediction of In-Flight Particle Characteristics in Atmospheric Plasma Spraying
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Applied Data Science Track)
summary: Atmospheric plasma spraying (APS) is a widely used coating process in which in-flight particle temperature and velocity strongly influence coating quality. However, these particle characteristics are difficult to monitor continuously during operation, motivating the development of non-invasive data-driven diagnostic methods. In this work, we investigate the predictive potential of high-speed video observations of the plasma plume for estimating in-flight particle characteristics in APS. We introduce three different video-derived feature representations and evaluate them using Tabular Prior-Data Fitted Networks (TabPFN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and classical regression baselines including Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, Support Vector Regression, and XGBoost. Experiments are conducted using grouped leave-one-out cross-validation on 126 labeled pre- and post-spray video recordings from 63 APS spray runs. Across the engineered feature experiments, TabPFN achieves the most consistent performance for temperature prediction, reaching R2 = 0.86 using the combined feature representation. CNN models particularly perform stronger for velocity prediction, achieving R2 of 0.81. In addition, we evaluate models operating directly on raw video frames using pretrained CNNs and find that the highest performance is achieved by a pretrained CNN with a regression head with R2 of 0.90 and 0.82 for temperature and velocity, respectively. The results demonstrate that video-derived plume information provides a promising and scalable foundation for non-invasive APS diagnostics and real-time process monitoring.

AI.tldr: Video-derived plume features enable non-invasive estimation of APS in-flight particle temperature and velocity; TabPFN best for temperature, CNN best for velocity; leveraging raw frames with pretrained CNNs yields highest R2 (0.90 for temperature, 0.82 for velocity).
AI.motivation: APS requires monitoring in-flight particle properties for coating quality, but direct measurement is invasive. Non-invasive video-based diagnostics from plasma plume offers scalable, real-time insights.
AI.method: Three video-derived feature representations are constructed from high-speed plume videos. Evaluations use TabPFN, CNN, and classical regressors (RF, GB, SVR, XGBoost). Dataset: 126 labeled video recordings from 63 spray runs with grouped leave-one-out cross-validation. Additional experiments with models operating directly on raw frames using pretrained CNNs.
AI.result: Engineered features: TabPFN achieves R^2 = 0.86 for temperature prediction with combined features. CNNs perform better for velocity with R^2 = 0.81. End-to-end on raw frames with a pretrained CNN and regression head yields the highest performance: R^2 = 0.90 for temperature and 0.82 for velocity.
AI.conclusion: Video-derived plume information provides a promising, scalable basis for non-invasive APS diagnostics and real-time process monitoring; potential for practical deployment with caveats on dataset size and generalization.

title: MemDreamer: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Long Video Understanding via Hierarchical Graph Memory and Agentic Retrieval Mechanism
summary: Current Vision-Language Models struggle with hours-long videos because processing full-length visual sequences induces prohibitive token explosion and attention dilution. To overcome this, we introduce MemDreamer to decouple perception and reasoning, shifting long-video understanding into an agentic exploration process. As a plug-and-play framework, it incrementally streams videos to construct a Hierarchical Graph Memory, a top-down three-tier architecture for semantic abstraction, anchored by a foundational graph capturing spatiotemporal and causal relations. During inference, the reasoning model employs agentic tool-augmented retrieval, navigating hierarchies, searching nodes, and traversing logical edges via an Observation-Reason-Action loop. Experiments show MemDreamer achieves SOTA results across four mainstream benchmarks, narrowing the gap with human experts to only 3.7 points. It constrains the reasoning context window to merely 2% of full-context ingestion while delivering a 12.5 point absolute accuracy gain. Furthermore, statistical analysis uncovers a strong positive linear correlation between an VLM's performance on logic reasoning and long-video understanding benchmarks, establishing agentic capability scaling as a new paradigm for multimodal comprehension.

AI.tldr: MemDreamer decouples perception and reasoning for long videos by streaming data into a Hierarchical Graph Memory and using agentic, tool-augmented retrieval to perform reasoning over a three-tier semantic graph; it achieves SOTA on four benchmarks with far reduced context window and shows a strong link between logic and long-video understanding.
AI.motivation: Long-form videos cause token explosion and attention dilution in vision-language models. A scalable solution requires decoupling perception from reasoning and introducing an efficient, hierarchical memory and agentic exploration for long-sequence understanding.
AI.method: Incremental streaming of video into a Hierarchical Graph Memory with a top-down three-tier architecture anchored by a foundational graph of spatiotemporal and causal relations. At inference, a reasoning model uses agentic, tool-augmented retrieval to navigate nodes and edges through an Observation-Reason-Action loop.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on four mainstream benchmarks, narrowing the gap to human experts to 3.7 points. Reduces the reasoning context window to 2% of full-context ingestion while delivering a 12.5-point absolute accuracy gain. A linear correlation is found between a VLM’s logic reasoning performance and long-video understanding, supporting agentic capability scaling as a new paradigm for multimodal comprehension.
AI.conclusion: MemDreamer demonstrates that decoupling perception and reasoning with a hierarchical memory and agentic, tool-augmented search yields strong long-video understanding with efficient context usage, suggesting a scalable path for multimodal reasoning in long sequences.

title: Discovering Multiscale Deep Formulas in Complex Systems via Neural-Guided Lambda Calculus
comment: 35 pages, 5 figures; Supplementary Information available as an ancillary file (79 pages)
summary: A fundamental problem in science is identifying underlying patterns of complex systems in the form of concise mathematical formulas. Current Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based methods have shown strong performance in single-scale systems, yet remain limited in identifying scale-specific formulas in multiscale complex systems. We present Deflex, an end-to-end AI method to automatically extract multiscale formulas with potentially different forms, including invariants and distributions, from complex systems. Deflex consists of two subsystems named Deflexformer and Deflexpressor. Deflexpressor is a lambda-calculus symbolic regression model for higher-order formulas. Deflexformer is a decomposable deep energy model for learning unified representations across scales. Deflexpressor generates synthetic data to pre-train Deflexformer, which then guides formula discovery by decoupling multiscale latent relationships. Across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors, Deflex achieves up to 7-fold higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art methods while enabling automated multiscale discovery. Our work could be a useful tool for scientific discovery across disciplines.

AI.tldr: Deflex: an end-to-end AI framework for multiscale formula discovery using a two-component system: Deflexformer (unified multiscale representations) and Deflexressor (lambda-calculus symbolic regression). Synthetic-data pretraining guides cross-scale latent disentanglement; achieves up to 7x efficiency gain over state-of-the-art across six complex systems, enabling automated multiscale discovery.
AI.motivation: Identify concise, scale-specific mathematical formulas in multiscale complex systems, addressing limitations of single-scale AI methods and enabling automated discovery across disciplines.
AI.method: Two-subsystem architecture: Deflexformer, a decomposable deep energy model that learns unified, scale-spanning representations; Deflexressor, a lambda-calculus-based symbolic regression model for higher-order formulas. Deflexressor generates synthetic data to pre-train Deflexformer; the pretrained Deflexformer then guides formula discovery by decoupling multiscale latent relationships.
AI.result: Demonstrated up to 7-fold higher efficiency versus state-of-the-art methods across six representative complex systems with diverse behaviors; supports automated multiscale discovery of formulas and invariants/distributions.
AI.conclusion: Deflex provides a scalable, cross-scale symbolic-discovery framework with potential broad applicability in scientific discovery, enabling efficient extraction of multiscale formulas and invariants across disciplines.

title: UniSHARP: Universal Sharp Monocular View Synthesis
summary: In this work, we focus on extending SHARP, the popular photorealistic view synthesis method, for universal monocular rendering across a continuum of camera systems, from conventional perspective cameras to wide-field-of-view, fisheye and omnidirectional panoramic settings. To overcome the pinhole-specific assumptions of SHARP, our key idea is to align various images in a unified omnidirectional latent space. Thus, we propose UniSHARP, which performs implicit alignment in both feature and Gaussian spaces. Specifically, Gaussian primitives are arranged along rays and radial distances in a ray-based universal representation, while 2D semantic and 3D spatial features extracted from UniK3D-inspired encoders are jointly decoded to generate the complete Gaussian cloud. To comprehensively evaluate our method, we construct a benchmark covering diverse imaging systems across various scenes. The benchmark is further stratified by field of view (FoV) to enable fine-grained assessment of the universal monocular rendering task. Extensive experiments on the proposed benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of UniSHARP, outperforming alternative methods by a large margin. The project page can be found at: https://insta360-research-team.github.io/Unisharp-website/

AI.tldr: UniSHARP extends SHARP to universal monocular rendering across diverse camera systems by aligning inputs in a unified omnidirectional latent space with Gaussian primitives and UniK3D-inspired encoders, achieving state-of-the-art results on a FoV-stratified benchmark.
AI.motivation: Photorealistic view synthesis is limited by pinhole assumptions; there is a need to support conventional and wide/FOV cameras in a single framework.
AI.method: Introduce UniSHARP: implicit alignment in feature and Gaussian spaces. Use a ray-based universal representation with Gaussian primitives along rays and radial distances. Decode 2D semantic and 3D spatial features from UniK3D-inspired encoders to form a complete Gaussian cloud. Construct a FoV-stratified benchmark to evaluate universal monocular rendering.
AI.result: Experiments on the proposed benchmark show UniSHARP significantly outperforms competing methods across diverse imaging systems, demonstrating strong generalization and photorealism.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates universal monocular rendering across camera systems, introduces a unified omnidirectional latent space and Gaussian-based representation, and provides a dedicated benchmark and resources.

title: Time series Foundation Models based on Physics-Informed Synthetic Histories for Cold-Start Photovoltaic Forecasting
comment: To be published in the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: At commissioning time, Photovoltaic (PV) operators must forecast production before target-site observations are available, limiting the direct use of standard supervised forecasters. This cold-start setting is addressed with a zero-shot pipeline that generates a synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates, enabling time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast through inference-time conditioning. Five TSFMs are benchmarked against classical baselines under strict Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback strategies. The evaluation spans $440$ PV sites across four datasets and diverse climate regimes. Covariate-aware foundation models outperform baselines by approximately $1.7-2\times$: TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE $0.514$, RMSE $0.721$ $kWh$ ${kWp}^{-1}$ ${d}^{-1}$), while Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source, indicating that accuracy is driven more by the availability of plausible temporal context than by the specific generator.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot PV forecasting using synthetic histories and covariate-aware time-series foundation models (TSFMs) outperforms baselines by ~1.7–2× across 440 sites; TabPFN-TS leads under Real Feedback, Chronos-2 best under Self-Forecast Feedback; synthetic-history source has limited impact.
AI.motivation: Address cold-start commissioning in PV forecasting where site-specific historical data are unavailable; enable accurate short-term forecasts by leveraging synthetic histories derived from plant metadata and meteorological covariates.
AI.method: Construct a zero-shot pipeline that generates a synthetic production history from plant metadata and meteorological covariates, then use five covariate-aware time-series foundation models (TSFMs) to forecast with inference-time conditioning. Benchmark under Cold-Start Baseline, Real Feedback, and Self-Forecast Feedback across 440 PV sites in four datasets.
AI.result: Covariate-aware TSFMs outperform baselines by approximately 1.7–2×. TabPFN-TS achieves the lowest error under Real Feedback (MAE 0.514, RMSE 0.721 kWh kWp^-1 d^-1). Chronos-2 is most robust under Self-Forecast Feedback. Performance is largely insensitive to the synthetic-history source.
AI.conclusion: In cold-start PV forecasting, incorporating covariate-aware TSFMs with synthetic histories yields strong, robust performance across climates; the choice of synthetic-history generator matters less than providing plausible temporal context.

title: Unsupervised Continual Clustering via Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026 (Research Track). arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2405.19234
summary: Unsupervised Continual Learning (UCL) aims to enable neural networks to learn sequential tasks without labels or access to past data. A major challenge in this setting is Catastrophic Forgetting, where models forget previously learned tasks upon learning new ones. This challenge is amplified in UCL due to the absence of labels to guide learning and memory retention. Existing mitigation strategies, such as knowledge distillation and replay buffers, often raise memory and privacy concerns. Moreover, current UCL methods largely overlook clustering-specific objectives. To fill this gap, we introduce Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) and propose Forward-Backward Knowledge Distillation for Continual Clustering (FBCC). FBCC employs a continual teacher network with a clustering projector and lightweight task-specific students. Through a dual-phase forward-backward distillation process, the teacher learns new clusters while preserving previously discovered cluster structure without storing past data. FBCC represents a pioneering approach to UCC, demonstrating improved clustering performance across sequential tasks. Experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that FBCC consistently outperforms existing continual learning baselines in clustering accuracy while significantly reducing catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: Introduces Unsupervised Continual Clustering (UCC) and FBCC to tackle catastrophic forgetting in unsupervised continual clustering via a continual teacher with a clustering projector and dual-phase forward-backward distillation; achieves state-of-the-art clustering accuracy without storing past data.
AI.motivation: Fill gap in UCL where clustering objectives are overlooked and memory/privacy constraints hinder replay/distillation-based methods; address forgetting without labeled data.
AI.method: A continual teacher network with a clustering projector guides lightweight task-specific students; dual-phase forward-backward distillation preserves previously discovered cluster structure while learning new clusters, without replay.
AI.result: On four benchmarks, FBCC consistently outperforms baselines in clustering accuracy and significantly reduces forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Presents a pioneering approach to UCC; demonstrates effective unsupervised continual clustering with memory-efficient knowledge transfer; opens avenues for scalable, private UCC methods.

title: Graph Neural Network leveraging Higher-order Class Label Connectivity for Heterophilous Graphs
summary: Node classification in graph neural networks (GNNs) has been widely applied in various fields of graph analysis. GNNs achieve high-accuracy node classification in homophilous graphs, where nodes with the same class label tend to be connected. However, their performance remains limited in heterophilous graphs, where nodes with different class labels are more likely to be connected. In particular, current GNNs derived from graph convolutional networks cannot capture higher-order class label connectivity, which is frequently observed in real-world heterophilous graphs. To address this issue, we propose a novel classifier, Label Context Classifier (LCC), designed to capture higher-order class label connectivity in directed graphs. LCC estimates the class label of a target node by leveraging label context embeddings that are generated through four distinct types of walks. In addition, our approach allows the integration of LCC and any GNN by adaptively learning their importance. Experimental results demonstrate that GNNs integrated with LCC outperform SOTA methods and the label context embeddings improve the node classification performance in heterophilous directed graphs.

AI.tldr: A novel Label Context Classifier (LCC) is proposed to capture higher-order class label connectivity in directed graphs, enhancing GNNs for heterophily. It uses four types of walks to generate label context embeddings and can be integrated with any GNN by learning their importance, achieving state-of-the-art results on heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.motivation: GNNs perform well on homophilous graphs but struggle with heterophily; standard GCNs miss higher-order class label connectivity often present in real-world heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.method: Introduce Label Context Classifier (LCC) that estimates a target node's class using label-context embeddings derived from four distinct walk types. Integrate LCC with arbitrary GNNs by adaptively learning the relative importance of LCC and the GNN components.
AI.result: GNNs integrated with LCC outperform state-of-the-art methods. The label-context embeddings improve node classification performance in heterophilous directed graphs.
AI.conclusion: LCC effectively captures higher-order class-label connectivity in directed graphs and can be flexibly integrated with existing GNNs to boost performance in heterophilous settings.

title: Drifting Models for Surrogate Flow Modeling
comment: Accepted to the 2nd International Symposium AI and Fluid Mechanics 2026
summary: While Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) provides high-fidelity flow fields for optimizing indoor environments, its computational cost limits rapid exploration. To solve this problem generative surrogates offer better distribution modeling than deterministic networks, but iterative sampling is slow. To enable high-quality, single-pass generation, we adapt the novel generative drifting framework to fluid mechanics. We introduce a conditional architecture that performs drifting in a learned VAE latent space and uses label-aware masking to align generated samples with their boundary conditions. Our label-conditioned model matches iterative diffusion in accuracy and flow consistency while running two orders of magnitude faster. Additionally, we propose a spatial-conditioning variant that establishes a promising path towards generalization to unseen geometries. Ultimately, conditional drifting serves as a highly efficient alternative to diffusion based approaches, unlocking real-time CFD surrogates where inference speed is critical.

AI.tldr: Conditional drifting in VAE latent space offers single-pass, boundary-conditioned CFD surrogates that rival diffusion accuracy with ~100x speedup; spatial conditioning enables generalization to unseen geometries.
AI.motivation: CFD provides high-fidelity data but is computationally expensive; diffusion-based surrogates require iterative sampling; need fast, real-time capable surrogates for design/optimization.
AI.method: Propose a conditional drifting framework operating in a learned VAE latent space; incorporate label-aware masking to enforce boundary condition alignment; develop a label-conditioned model; introduce spatial-conditioning variant to support unseen geometries; compare to iterative diffusion.
AI.result: Achieves accuracy and flow consistency comparable to iterative diffusion; 100x faster inference; spatial conditioning demonstrates potential for generalization to unseen geometries.
AI.conclusion: Conditional drifting is an efficient alternative to diffusion-based methods, enabling real-time CFD surrogates for speed-critical applications.

title: Network Recovery from Cascade Data: A Debiased Jacobian-Based Machine Learning Approach
summary: Many important outcomes unfold as dynamic cascades, including product adoption, disease spread, financial distress, and information diffusion. A central challenge is to recover the hidden influence network behind these cascades. Existing methods typically assume a specific diffusion model, and their performance degrades substantially when that assumption is misspecified. We propose CascadeNet, a Jacobian-based machine learning framework for network recovery that does not require specifying a diffusion mechanism. The key idea is that the underlying influence structure can be characterized by the Jacobian of the one-step transition function. CascadeNet first constructs a flexible estimator of the transition function, and further applies Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer, so that the debiased Jacobian is $\sqrt{n}$-consistent and asymptotically normal, enabling formal inference on the network structure. We validate CascadeNet in both a simulation exercise and a real-world empirical application. In simulations, where the data-generating process is known, CascadeNet achieves the highest network recovery accuracy across nine common data-generating processes. In an empirical application to COVID-19 transmission across Spain's 52 provinces, CascadeNet recovers transmission networks that are significantly correlated with the true inter-province mobility network, whereas networks recovered by baseline methods show no significant alignment with the ground truth.

AI.tldr: CascadeNet recovers hidden influence networks from cascades without assuming a diffusion model, using a Jacobian-based estimator with Neyman-orthogonal debiasing to enable valid inference.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-network recovery methods rely on specifying a diffusion mechanism; misspecification degrades performance. A diffusion-agnostic, inference-capable approach is needed.
AI.method: Estimate a flexible one-step transition function, compute its Jacobian to capture the influence structure, and apply Neyman-orthogonal debiasing via the Riesz representer to obtain a debiased, sqrt(n)-consistent, asymptotically normal Jacobian for network inference.
AI.result: Simulation studies show CascadeNet achieves the highest network recovery accuracy across nine data-generating processes; in a COVID-19 application across Spain's 52 provinces, recovered networks align significantly with the true inter-province mobility network, outperforming baselines which show no significant alignment.
AI.conclusion: CascadeNet provides a model-free yet statistically valid framework for recovering and inferring diffusion networks from cascades, with strong empirical performance in both synthetic and real-world epidemic data.

title: CoMetaPNS: Continually Meta-learning Personalized Neural Surrogates for Cardiac Electrophysiology Simulations
summary: Personalized virtual heart simulations face challenges in model personalization and computational cost. While neural surrogates offer state-of-the-art solutions, they typically address either efficient personalization or training generalizable models. Recent work reframes this by learning the process of personalizing a surrogate using limited subject-specific context data, through few-shot generative modeling with set-conditioned surrogates and meta-learned amortized inference. These methods, however, assume a static and diverse training distribution with known task identifiers. When new data becomes available, they require costly retraining with all prior data to avoid catastrophic forgetting - a phenomena where the model forgets earlier tasks when trained on new ones. This is a major limitation in clinical settings where often unlabeled data arrives sequentially and full retraining is infeasible. This paper presents a new continual meta-learning framework to achieve personalized neural surrogates able to not only continually integrate information but also identify whether incoming data stems from a known or unknown dynamics source. By leveraging a continual Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model over a memory buffer, our framework can infer the identifiers and relationships of data over time - required for effective meta-learning. Empirical results on synthetic cardiac data demonstrate superior simulation forecasting, computational scalability, and resilience to catastrophic forgetting compared to existing baselines.

AI.tldr: A continual meta-learning framework for personalized neural surrogates that can integrate new data online and detect unknown dynamics using a memory-buffered continual Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model, improving forecasting, scalability, and resistance to forgetting on synthetic cardiac data.
AI.motivation: Personalized neural surrogates must be efficiently adapted to new subjects and evolving data streams without expensive retraining. Existing few-shot/meta-learning approaches assume static, labeled task distributions and suffer catastrophic forgetting when updated with new data, which is impractical in clinical settings.
AI.method: The proposed approach introduces a continual meta-learning framework with a memory buffer. It uses a Bayesian Gaussian Mixture Model over stored data to infer data identifiers and relationships across time, enabling online meta-learning and identification of whether new data comes from known or unknown dynamics. This allows continual integration of information and selective updating without retraining on all past data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic cardiac data shows improved long-horizon simulation forecasting, better computational scalability, and reduced catastrophic forgetting relative to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables continual personalization of neural surrogates for cardiac simulations, handling non-stationary data and unseen dynamics by leveraging memory-based probabilistic clustering to guide meta-learning, with promising potential for clinical deployment.

title: Second-Order Path Kernel Interpolation Formulas in Machine Learning
summary: Understanding how training data shape neural network predictions is a central problem in modern learning theory. In 2020, Pedro Domingos proposed an interpolation formula valid for every model learned by deterministic gradient descent. It expresses the model's prediction as an integral, along the optimization path, of a data-dependent kernel that aligns the model's gradients at the test and training data. Such a first-order characterization remains valid for models trained with batch-based stochastic optimization. In this paper, we develop second-order forms of these interpolation formulas. We show that the leading path-kernel interpolation is supplemented by a curvature-weighted interpolation term. For stochastic gradient descent, an additional sampling-induced component appears, coupling the curvature of the prediction with the covariance of mini-batch gradient noise. We also extend the representation to stochastic gradient descent with momentum, where the interpolation structure is preserved but with the weights modified by a memory-related factor. Moreover, we establish a concentration estimate for the terminal prediction, identifying the fluctuation scale around the expected second-order representation. Together, these results provide a refinement of the path-kernel interpretation of neural network prediction.

AI.tldr: Second-order path-kernel interpolation for neural networks extends Domingos’ first-order formula by adding a curvature-weighted term; for SGD, a sampling-induced component coupling curvature with gradient-noise covariance; SGD with momentum introduces a memory factor; a concentration estimate for terminal predictions is established. This refines the path-kernel interpretation of predictions.
AI.motivation: To extend the interpolation framework from a first-order (gradient-path) description to second-order dynamics, capturing curvature effects and stochastic training dynamics (SGD and momentum) to better understand how training data shape predictions.
AI.method: Derive second-order interpolation formulas along the optimization path. Decompose the prediction into a leading path-kernel term plus a curvature-weighted term; for SGD, include a sampling-induced component that couples curvature with minibatch gradient noise covariance; extend to SGD with momentum by incorporating a memory-related factor; prove a concentration estimate for the terminal prediction to describe fluctuations around the mean second-order representation.
AI.result: A refined, second-order path-kernel representation: the leading term is supplemented by a curvature-weighted interpolation; SGD introduces an additional noise-covariance-driven term; momentum adds a memory-factor modification; a concentration bound for the terminal prediction is established.
AI.conclusion: The path-kernel interpretation is strengthened by second-order terms, explicitly accounting for curvature and stochasticity in training; the results provide a more complete theoretical description of how training data and optimization dynamics shape neural network predictions.

title: Sparse Subspace-to-Expert Sharing for Task-Agnostic Continual Learning
comment: 19 pages. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2601.17616
summary: Continual learning in Large Language Models (LLMs) is hindered by the plasticity-stability dilemma, where acquiring new capabilities often leads to catastrophic forgetting of previous knowledge. Existing methods typically treat parameters uniformly, failing to distinguish between specific task knowledge and shared capabilities. We introduce Mixture of Sparse Experts for Task Agnostic Continual Learning (SETA), a framework that resolves the plasticity-stability conflict through adaptive sparse subspace decomposition into task-specific expert modules. Unlike standard updates, where tasks compete for the same parameters, SETA separates knowledge into unique experts, designed to isolate task-specific patterns, and shared experts, responsible for capturing common features. This structure is maintained through adaptive elastic anchoring and a routing-aware regularization that jointly protect shared knowledge at both the weight and routing levels and enable a unified gating network to automatically retrieve the correct expert combination during inference. Extensive experiments across diverse domain-specific benchmarks demonstrate that SETA achieves competitive or superior overall performance relative to state-of-the-art continual learning baselines, with particularly strong retention of early-task knowledge and improved backward transfer on LLaMA-2 7B and Qwen3-4B.

AI.tldr: SETA employs a mixture of sparse experts with adaptive routing to separate task-specific from shared knowledge, improving continual learning for LLMs by preserving earlier tasks and boosting backward transfer.
AI.motivation: To address the plasticity-stability dilemma in LLM continual learning by distinguishing task-specific knowledge from shared capabilities and avoiding uniform parameter updates.
AI.method: SETA decomposes knowledge into task-specific experts and shared experts via adaptive sparse subspace decomposition. It uses elastic anchoring to maintain task-specificity and a routing-aware regularization to protect shared knowledge at both weight and routing levels. A unified gating network automatically retrieves the appropriate expert combination during inference.
AI.result: Across diverse domain-specific benchmarks, SETA matches or surpasses state-of-the-art continual learning baselines, with notably better retention of early tasks and improved backward transfer on LLaMA-2 7B and Qwen3-4B.
AI.conclusion: SETA effectively mitigates the plasticity-stability trade-off by modularizing knowledge into task-specific and shared components, guarded by routing-aware regularization, enabling robust, scalable continual learning for large language models.

title: Accelerated Decentralized Stochastic Gradient Descent for Strongly Convex Optimization
summary:
Decentralized stochastic optimization is a fundamental paradigm for large-scale learning over networks, where agents communicate only with their neighbors and no central coordinator is required. For strongly convex problems, communication efficiency is mainly determined by the condition number \(κ=L/μ\) and the network spectral gap \(1-β\). Although deterministic decentralized methods can simultaneously achieve accelerated \(\sqrtκ\) and \(1/\sqrt{1-β}\) dependences, no existing stochastic method attains both improvements at once. In this paper, we propose \emph{Multi-Gossip Accelerated DSGD} (MG-ADSGD), a decentralized stochastic algorithm that combines Nesterov-type primal--dual extrapolation with multi-round fast gossip averaging. The key idea is to couple the gossip depth with the mini-batch size so that additional communication rounds simultaneously improve consensus accuracy and reduce gradient variance. We show that MG-ADSGD achieves the communication complexity \[ \widetilde{\mathcal O}\!\left( \frac{σ^2}{μnε}\log\frac{1}ε +
\sqrt{\fracκ{1-β}}\log\frac{1}ε \right), \] where \(ε\) denotes the target accuracy, \(n\) is the number of nodes, and \(σ^2\) is the gradient variance. To the best of our knowledge, this bound yields the best currently available communication complexity for decentralized stochastic strongly convex optimization, up to logarithmic factors that are independent of $ε$.

AI.tldr: MG-ADSGD introduces multi-round gossip within an accelerated DSGD framework, coupling gossip depth with mini-batch size to simultaneously improve consensus and reduce gradient variance, achieving near-optimal communication complexity for decentralized stochastic strongly convex optimization.
AI.motivation: Decentralized stochastic optimization faces a trade-off between accelerated convergence (√κ) and improved consensus (1/√(1−β)); existing methods either achieve one but not both. The work aims to jointly optimize both factors by leveraging multi-round gossip and variance reduction through larger mini-batches.
AI.method: MG-ADSGD combines Nesterov-type primal–dual extrapolation with multi-round fast gossip averaging. The key idea is to couple the gossip depth with mini-batch size so that additional communication rounds improve both consensus accuracy and gradient variance reduction.
AI.result: Derived communication complexity bound: ~ O( (σ^2/(μ n ε)) log(1/ε) + sqrt( κ/(1−β) ) log(1/ε) ). This matches or improves the best known bounds for decentralized stochastic strongly convex optimization up to ε-independent logarithmic factors.
AI.conclusion: MG-ADSGD achieves the best currently available communication complexity for decentralized stochastic strongly convex optimization up to ε-independent log factors. The method offers a unified mechanism to trade off communication with variance reduction via joint control of gossip depth and mini-batch size; applicability to non-strongly convex or non-convex settings and practical constants remain to be explored.
